subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}},{/tex} if {tex}y = {x^{\tan x}} + \sqrt {\frac{{{x^2} + 1}}{2}} {/tex}
question_text: Find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}},{/tex} if {tex}y = {x^{\tan x}} + \sqrt {\frac{{{x^2} + 1}}{2}} {/tex}
explanation: We have, {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}},{/tex} if {tex}y = {x^{\tan x}} + \sqrt {\frac{{{x^2} + 1}}{2}} {/tex} Taking, {tex}u = {x^{\tan x}}{/tex} and {tex}v = \sqrt {\frac{{{x^2} + 1}}{2}} {/tex} {tex}\log u = \tan x\log x{/tex} ...(ii) and {tex}{v^2} = \frac{{{x^2} + 1}}{2}{/tex} ...(iii) On, differentiating Eq. (ii) w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{1}{u}.\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = \tan x.\frac{1}{x} + \log x{\sec ^2}x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{du}}{{dx}} = u\left[ {\frac{{\tan x}}{x} + \log x.{{\sec }^2}x} \right]{/tex} {tex} = {x^{\tan x}}\left[ {\frac{{\tan x}}{x} + \log x.{{\sec }^2}x} \right]{/tex} ...(iv) also, differentiating Eq. (iii) w.r.t. x, we get {tex}2v.\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{2}\left( {2x} \right) \Rightarrow \frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{4v}}.\left( {2x} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{4.\sqrt {\frac{{{x^2} + 1}}{2}} }}.2x = \frac{{x.\sqrt 2 }}{{2\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{x}{{\sqrt {2\left( {{x^2} + 1} \right)} }}{/tex} ...(v) Now, {tex}y = u + v{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{du}}{{dx}} + \frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex}= {x^{\tan x}}\left[ {\frac{{\tan x}}{x} + \log x.{{\sec }^2}x} \right] + \frac{x}{{\sqrt {2\left( {{x^2} + 1} \right)} }}{/tex}
solution: We have, {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}},{/tex} if {tex}y = {x^{\tan x}} + \sqrt {\frac{{{x^2} + 1}}{2}} {/tex} Taking, {tex}u = {x^{\tan x}}{/tex} and {tex}v = \sqrt {\frac{{{x^2} + 1}}{2}} {/tex} {tex}\log u = \tan x\log x{/tex} ...(ii) and {tex}{v^2} = \frac{{{x^2} + 1}}{2}{/tex} ...(iii) On, differentiating Eq. (ii) w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{1}{u}.\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = \tan x.\frac{1}{x} + \log x{\sec ^2}x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{du}}{{dx}} = u\left[ {\frac{{\tan x}}{x} + \log x.{{\sec }^2}x} \right]{/tex} {tex} = {x^{\tan x}}\left[ {\frac{{\tan x}}{x} + \log x.{{\sec }^2}x} \right]{/tex} ...(iv) also, differentiating Eq. (iii) w.r.t. x, we get {tex}2v.\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{2}\left( {2x} \right) \Rightarrow \frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{4v}}.\left( {2x} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{4.\sqrt {\frac{{{x^2} + 1}}{2}} }}.2x = \frac{{x.\sqrt 2 }}{{2\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{x}{{\sqrt {2\left( {{x^2} + 1} \right)} }}{/tex} ...(v) Now, {tex}y = u + v{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{du}}{{dx}} + \frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex}= {x^{\tan x}}\left[ {\frac{{\tan x}}{x} + \log x.{{\sec }^2}x} \right] + \frac{x}{{\sqrt {2\left( {{x^2} + 1} \right)} }}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}},{/tex}if {tex}y = {x^{\tan x}} + \sqrt {\frac{{{x^2} + 1}}{2}} {/tex}

Taking, {tex}u = {x^{\tan x}}{/tex} and {tex}v = \sqrt {\frac{{{x^2} + 1}}{2}} {/tex}

{tex}\log u = \tan x\log x{/tex} ...(ii)

and {tex}{v^2} = \frac{{{x^2} + 1}}{2}{/tex}...(iii)

On, differentiating Eq. (ii) w.r.t. x, we get

{tex}\frac{1}{u}.\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = \tan x.\frac{1}{x} + \log x{\sec ^2}x{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{du}}{{dx}} = u\left[ {\frac{{\tan x}}{x} + \log x.{{\sec }^2}x} \right]{/tex}

{tex} = {x^{\tan x}}\left[ {\frac{{\tan x}}{x} + \log x.{{\sec }^2}x} \right]{/tex} ...(iv)

also, differentiating Eq. (iii) w.r.t. x, we get

{tex}2v.\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{2}\left( {2x} \right) \Rightarrow \frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{4v}}.\left( {2x} \right){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{4.\sqrt {\frac{{{x^2} + 1}}{2}} }}.2x = \frac{{x.\sqrt 2 }}{{2\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \frac{x}{{\sqrt {2\left( {{x^2} + 1} \right)} }}{/tex} ...(v)

Now, {tex}y = u + v{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{du}}{{dx}} + \frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex}

{tex}= {x^{\tan x}}\left[ {\frac{{\tan x}}{x} + \log x.{{\sec }^2}x} \right] + \frac{x}{{\sqrt {2\left( {{x^2} + 1} \right)} }}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}x = {e^{\frac{x}{y}}}{/tex}, prove that {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{x - y}}{{x\log x}}{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}x = {e^{\frac{x}{y}}}{/tex} , prove that {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{x - y}}{{x\log x}}{/tex}
explanation: We have, {tex}x = {e^{\frac{x}{y}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d}{{dx}}x = \frac{d}{{dx}}{e^{x/y}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 1 = {e^{x/y}}.\frac{d}{{dx}}(\frac{x}{y}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 1 = {e^{x/y}}.\left[ {\frac{{y.1 - x.dy/dx}}{{{y^2}}}} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {y^2} = y.{e^{x/y}} - x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}.{e^{x/y}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}.{e^{x/y}} = y{e^{x/y}} - {y^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{y\left( {{e^{x/y}} - y} \right)}}{{x.{e^{x/y}}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\left( {{e^{x/y}} - y} \right)}}{{{e^{x/y}}.\frac{x}{y}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{x - y}}{{x.\log x}}{/tex} {tex}\left[\therefore x=e^{\frac{x}{y}}\Rightarrow logx=\frac{x}{y}\right]{/tex} .￼￼ Hence Proved.
solution: We have, {tex}x = {e^{\frac{x}{y}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d}{{dx}}x = \frac{d}{{dx}}{e^{x/y}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 1 = {e^{x/y}}.\frac{d}{{dx}}(\frac{x}{y}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 1 = {e^{x/y}}.\left[ {\frac{{y.1 - x.dy/dx}}{{{y^2}}}} \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {y^2} = y.{e^{x/y}} - x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}.{e^{x/y}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}.{e^{x/y}} = y{e^{x/y}} - {y^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{y\left( {{e^{x/y}} - y} \right)}}{{x.{e^{x/y}}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\left( {{e^{x/y}} - y} \right)}}{{{e^{x/y}}.\frac{x}{y}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{x - y}}{{x.\log x}}{/tex} {tex}\left[\therefore x=e^{\frac{x}{y}}\Rightarrow logx=\frac{x}{y}\right]{/tex} .￼￼ Hence Proved.
solution_html:
We have, {tex}x = {e^{\frac{x}{y}}}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \frac{d}{{dx}}x = \frac{d}{{dx}}{e^{x/y}}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow 1 = {e^{x/y}}.\frac{d}{{dx}}(\frac{x}{y}){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow 1 = {e^{x/y}}.\left[ {\frac{{y.1 - x.dy/dx}}{{{y^2}}}} \right]{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow {y^2} = y.{e^{x/y}} - x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}.{e^{x/y}}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}.{e^{x/y}} = y{e^{x/y}} - {y^2}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{y\left( {{e^{x/y}} - y} \right)}}{{x.{e^{x/y}}}}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{\left( {{e^{x/y}} - y} \right)}}{{{e^{x/y}}.\frac{x}{y}}}{/tex}

{tex} = \frac{{x - y}}{{x.\log x}}{/tex} {tex}\left[\therefore x=e^{\frac{x}{y}}\Rightarrow logx=\frac{x}{y}\right]{/tex}.￼￼

Hence Proved.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}x={a}\left(\cos \theta+\log \tan \frac{\theta}{2}\right){/tex} and {tex}{y}=\sin \theta{/tex}, then find {tex}\frac{{d}^{2} {y}}{{d} x^{2}}{/tex} at {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}x={a}\left(\cos \theta+\log \tan \frac{\theta}{2}\right){/tex} and {tex}{y}=\sin \theta{/tex} , then find {tex}\frac{{d}^{2} {y}}{{d} x^{2}}{/tex} at {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex} x=a\left(\cos \theta+\log \tan \frac{\theta}{2}\right) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d \theta}=a\left(-\sin \theta+\frac{1}{\tan \frac{\theta}{2}} \times \sec ^2 \frac{\theta}{2} \times \frac{1}{2}\right) {/tex} {tex} =a\left(-\sin \theta+\frac{1}{\sin \theta}\right)=a\left(\frac{1-\sin ^2 \theta}{\sin \theta}\right) {/tex} {tex} \frac{d x}{d \theta}=a \cot \theta \cos \theta {/tex} {tex} \text { Also, } y=\sin \theta \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d \theta}=\cos \theta {/tex} {tex} \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\tan \theta}{a} {/tex} Differetiating wrt {tex}x{/tex} , {tex} \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=\frac{\sec ^2 \theta}{a} \times \frac{d \theta}{d x} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\sec ^3 \theta \tan \theta}{a^2} {/tex} {tex} \left.\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right]_{a t \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}}=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{a^2} {/tex}
solution: {tex} x=a\left(\cos \theta+\log \tan \frac{\theta}{2}\right) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d \theta}=a\left(-\sin \theta+\frac{1}{\tan \frac{\theta}{2}} \times \sec ^2 \frac{\theta}{2} \times \frac{1}{2}\right) {/tex} {tex} =a\left(-\sin \theta+\frac{1}{\sin \theta}\right)=a\left(\frac{1-\sin ^2 \theta}{\sin \theta}\right) {/tex} {tex} \frac{d x}{d \theta}=a \cot \theta \cos \theta {/tex} {tex} \text { Also, } y=\sin \theta \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d \theta}=\cos \theta {/tex} {tex} \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\tan \theta}{a} {/tex} Differetiating wrt {tex}x{/tex} , {tex} \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=\frac{\sec ^2 \theta}{a} \times \frac{d \theta}{d x} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\sec ^3 \theta \tan \theta}{a^2} {/tex} {tex} \left.\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right]_{a t \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}}=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{a^2} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} x=a\left(\cos \theta+\log \tan \frac{\theta}{2}\right) {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d \theta}=a\left(-\sin \theta+\frac{1}{\tan \frac{\theta}{2}} \times \sec ^2 \frac{\theta}{2} \times \frac{1}{2}\right) {/tex}
{tex} =a\left(-\sin \theta+\frac{1}{\sin \theta}\right)=a\left(\frac{1-\sin ^2 \theta}{\sin \theta}\right) {/tex}
{tex} \frac{d x}{d \theta}=a \cot \theta \cos \theta {/tex}
{tex} \text { Also, } y=\sin \theta \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d \theta}=\cos \theta {/tex}
{tex} \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\tan \theta}{a} {/tex}
Differetiating wrt {tex}x{/tex},
{tex} \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=\frac{\sec ^2 \theta}{a} \times \frac{d \theta}{d x} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{\sec ^3 \theta \tan \theta}{a^2} {/tex}
{tex} \left.\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right]_{a t \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}}=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{a^2} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}\sqrt{1-x^{2}}+\sqrt{1-y^{2}}=a(x-y){/tex}, then prove that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\sqrt{\frac{1-y^{2}}{1-x^{2}}}{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}\sqrt{1-x^{2}}+\sqrt{1-y^{2}}=a(x-y){/tex} , then prove that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\sqrt{\frac{1-y^{2}}{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} .
explanation: Let {tex}x=\sin A, y=\sin B \Rightarrow A=\sin ^{-1} x, B=\sin ^{-1} y{/tex} {tex} \therefore \sqrt{1-x^2}+\sqrt{1-y^2}=a(x-y) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cos A+\cos B=a(\sin A-\sin B) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 2 \cos \left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)=2 a \cos \left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cot \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)=a \Rightarrow A-B=2 \cot ^{-1} a {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \sin ^{-1} x-\sin ^{-1} y=2 \cot ^{-1} a {/tex} differentiate both sides wrt {tex}x{/tex} , {tex} \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-y^2}} \frac{d y}{d x}=0 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\sqrt{\frac{1-y^2}{1-x^2}} {/tex}
solution: Let {tex}x=\sin A, y=\sin B \Rightarrow A=\sin ^{-1} x, B=\sin ^{-1} y{/tex} {tex} \therefore \sqrt{1-x^2}+\sqrt{1-y^2}=a(x-y) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cos A+\cos B=a(\sin A-\sin B) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 2 \cos \left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)=2 a \cos \left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cot \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)=a \Rightarrow A-B=2 \cot ^{-1} a {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \sin ^{-1} x-\sin ^{-1} y=2 \cot ^{-1} a {/tex} differentiate both sides wrt {tex}x{/tex} , {tex} \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-y^2}} \frac{d y}{d x}=0 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\sqrt{\frac{1-y^2}{1-x^2}} {/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}x=\sin A, y=\sin B \Rightarrow A=\sin ^{-1} x, B=\sin ^{-1} y{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \sqrt{1-x^2}+\sqrt{1-y^2}=a(x-y) {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \cos A+\cos B=a(\sin A-\sin B) {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 2 \cos \left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)=2 a \cos \left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right) {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \cot \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)=a \Rightarrow A-B=2 \cot ^{-1} a {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \sin ^{-1} x-\sin ^{-1} y=2 \cot ^{-1} a {/tex}
differentiate both sides wrt {tex}x{/tex},
{tex} \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-y^2}} \frac{d y}{d x}=0 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\sqrt{\frac{1-y^2}{1-x^2}} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find k so that {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \frac{x^{2}-2 x-3}{x+1}, & x \neq-1 \\ k, & x=-1 \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at {tex}{x}=-1{/tex}.
question_text: Find k so that {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \frac{x^{2}-2 x-3}{x+1}, & x \neq-1 \\ k, & x=-1 \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at {tex}{x}=-1{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow-1} \frac{x^2-2 x-3}{x+1}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow-1} \frac{(x-3)(x+1)}{x+1}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow-1}(x-3)=-4 {/tex} Also, {tex}f(-1)=k{/tex} as {tex}f{/tex} is continuous, {tex}k=-4{/tex}
solution: {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow-1} \frac{x^2-2 x-3}{x+1}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow-1} \frac{(x-3)(x+1)}{x+1}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow-1}(x-3)=-4 {/tex} Also, {tex}f(-1)=k{/tex} as {tex}f{/tex} is continuous, {tex}k=-4{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow-1} \frac{x^2-2 x-3}{x+1}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow-1} \frac{(x-3)(x+1)}{x+1}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow-1}(x-3)=-4 {/tex}
Also, {tex}f(-1)=k{/tex} as {tex}f{/tex} is continuous, {tex}k=-4{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)=a^{2}{/tex}, then find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)=a^{2}{/tex} , then find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(x^2+y^2\right)=a^2 \Rightarrow x^2+y^2=\tan a^2{/tex} Differentiate both sides wrt {tex}x{/tex} , {tex}2 x+2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{x}{y}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(x^2+y^2\right)=a^2 \Rightarrow x^2+y^2=\tan a^2{/tex} Differentiate both sides wrt {tex}x{/tex} , {tex}2 x+2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{x}{y}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(x^2+y^2\right)=a^2 \Rightarrow x^2+y^2=\tan a^2{/tex}
Differentiate both sides wrt {tex}x{/tex},
{tex}2 x+2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{x}{y}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Differentiate {tex}2^{\cos ^{2} x}{/tex} w.r.t {tex}\cos ^{2} x{/tex}.
question_text: Differentiate {tex}2^{\cos ^{2} x}{/tex} w.r.t {tex}\cos ^{2} x{/tex} .
explanation: Let {tex}u=2^{\cos ^2 x} \Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=2^{\cos ^2 x}(-2 \cos x \sin x) \log 2{/tex} Let {tex}v=\cos ^2 x \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=-2 \cos x \sin x{/tex} Now {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{\left(\frac{d u}{d x}\right)}{\left(\frac{d v}{d x}\right)}=2^{\cos ^2 x} \log 2{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}u=2^{\cos ^2 x} \Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=2^{\cos ^2 x}(-2 \cos x \sin x) \log 2{/tex} Let {tex}v=\cos ^2 x \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=-2 \cos x \sin x{/tex} Now {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{\left(\frac{d u}{d x}\right)}{\left(\frac{d v}{d x}\right)}=2^{\cos ^2 x} \log 2{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}u=2^{\cos ^2 x} \Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=2^{\cos ^2 x}(-2 \cos x \sin x) \log 2{/tex}
Let {tex}v=\cos ^2 x \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=-2 \cos x \sin x{/tex}
Now {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{\left(\frac{d u}{d x}\right)}{\left(\frac{d v}{d x}\right)}=2^{\cos ^2 x} \log 2{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}(x)^{y}=(y)^{x}{/tex}, then find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}(x)^{y}=(y)^{x}{/tex} , then find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} .
explanation: Taking {tex}\log{/tex} on both sides, we get {tex}y \log x=x \log y{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t. {tex}x{/tex} , we get {tex}\frac{y}{x .}+\log x \frac{d y}{d x}= \frac{x}{y .} \frac{d y}{d x}+\log y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y(x \log y-y)}{x(y \log x-x)}{/tex}
solution: Taking {tex}\log{/tex} on both sides, we get {tex}y \log x=x \log y{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t. {tex}x{/tex} , we get {tex}\frac{y}{x .}+\log x \frac{d y}{d x}= \frac{x}{y .} \frac{d y}{d x}+\log y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y(x \log y-y)}{x(y \log x-x)}{/tex}
solution_html:
Taking {tex}\log{/tex} on both sides, we get {tex}y \log x=x \log y{/tex}
Differentiating both sides w.r.t. {tex}x{/tex}, we get
{tex}\frac{y}{x .}+\log x \frac{d y}{d x}= \frac{x}{y .} \frac{d y}{d x}+\log y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y(x \log y-y)}{x(y \log x-x)}{/tex}

question_html: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} if {tex}y^{x}+x^{y}+x^{x}=a^{b}{/tex}, where {tex}a{/tex} and {tex}b{/tex} are constants.
question_text: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} if {tex}y^{x}+x^{y}+x^{x}=a^{b}{/tex} , where {tex}a{/tex} and {tex}b{/tex} are constants.
explanation: Let {tex}{u}={y}^{{x}}, {v}={x}^{{y}}{/tex} and {tex}{w}={x}^{{x}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}+\frac{d w}{d x}=0 .. .(i){/tex} {tex}u=y^{x} \Rightarrow \log u=x \cdot \log y \Rightarrow \frac{1}{u} \cdot \frac{d u}{d x}=\frac{x}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}+\log y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=y^{x}\left(\frac{x}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}+\log y\right)=x y^{x-1} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}+y^{x} \log y{/tex} {tex}v=x^{y} \Rightarrow \log v=y \cdot \log x \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v} \cdot \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}{/tex} {tex}+\log x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=x^{y}\left(\frac{y}{x}+\log x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}\right)=y x^{y-1}{/tex} {tex}+x^{y} \log x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}w=x^{x} \Rightarrow \log w=x \cdot \log x \Rightarrow \frac{1}{w} \cdot \frac{d w}{d x}=1{/tex} {tex}+\log x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d w}{d x}=x^{x} \cdot(1+\log x){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} From (i), we get {tex}x y^{x-1} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}+y^{x} \cdot \log y+y x^{y-1}+x^{y} \cdot \log x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}+x^{x} \cdot(1+\log x)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{x^x \cdot(1+\log x)+y^x \cdot \log y+y x^{y-1}}{x \cdot y^{x-1}+x^y \cdot \log x}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}{u}={y}^{{x}}, {v}={x}^{{y}}{/tex} and {tex}{w}={x}^{{x}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}+\frac{d w}{d x}=0 .. .(i){/tex} {tex}u=y^{x} \Rightarrow \log u=x \cdot \log y \Rightarrow \frac{1}{u} \cdot \frac{d u}{d x}=\frac{x}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}+\log y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=y^{x}\left(\frac{x}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}+\log y\right)=x y^{x-1} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}+y^{x} \log y{/tex} {tex}v=x^{y} \Rightarrow \log v=y \cdot \log x \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v} \cdot \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}{/tex} {tex}+\log x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=x^{y}\left(\frac{y}{x}+\log x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}\right)=y x^{y-1}{/tex} {tex}+x^{y} \log x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}w=x^{x} \Rightarrow \log w=x \cdot \log x \Rightarrow \frac{1}{w} \cdot \frac{d w}{d x}=1{/tex} {tex}+\log x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d w}{d x}=x^{x} \cdot(1+\log x){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} From (i), we get {tex}x y^{x-1} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}+y^{x} \cdot \log y+y x^{y-1}+x^{y} \cdot \log x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}+x^{x} \cdot(1+\log x)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{x^x \cdot(1+\log x)+y^x \cdot \log y+y x^{y-1}}{x \cdot y^{x-1}+x^y \cdot \log x}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}{u}={y}^{{x}}, {v}={x}^{{y}}{/tex} and {tex}{w}={x}^{{x}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}+\frac{d w}{d x}=0 .. .(i){/tex}
{tex}u=y^{x} \Rightarrow \log u=x \cdot \log y \Rightarrow \frac{1}{u} \cdot \frac{d u}{d x}=\frac{x}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}+\log y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=y^{x}\left(\frac{x}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}+\log y\right)=x y^{x-1} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}+y^{x} \log y{/tex}
{tex}v=x^{y} \Rightarrow \log v=y \cdot \log x \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v} \cdot \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}{/tex} {tex}+\log x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=x^{y}\left(\frac{y}{x}+\log x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}\right)=y x^{y-1}{/tex} {tex}+x^{y} \log x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}w=x^{x} \Rightarrow \log w=x \cdot \log x \Rightarrow \frac{1}{w} \cdot \frac{d w}{d x}=1{/tex} {tex}+\log x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d w}{d x}=x^{x} \cdot(1+\log x){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} From (i), we get
{tex}x y^{x-1} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}+y^{x} \cdot \log y+y x^{y-1}+x^{y} \cdot \log x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}+x^{x} \cdot(1+\log x)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{x^x \cdot(1+\log x)+y^x \cdot \log y+y x^{y-1}}{x \cdot y^{x-1}+x^y \cdot \log x}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: For a positive constant 'a', differentiate {tex}{a}^{{t}+\frac{1}{{t}}}{/tex} with respect to {tex}\left({t}+\frac{1}{{t}}\right)^{{a}}{/tex}, where t is a non-zero real number.
question_text: For a positive constant 'a', differentiate {tex}{a}^{{t}+\frac{1}{{t}}}{/tex} with respect to {tex}\left({t}+\frac{1}{{t}}\right)^{{a}}{/tex} , where t is a non-zero real number.
explanation: Let {tex}u=a^{t+\frac{1}{t}} \Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d t}=a^{t+\frac{1}{t}} \cdot \log a .\left(1-\frac{1}{t^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}v=\left(t+\frac{1}{t}\right)^{a} \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d t}=a\left(t+\frac{1}{t}\right)^{a-1} \cdot\left(1-\frac{1}{t^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{d u / d t}{d v / d t}=\frac{a^{t+\frac{1}{t}} \cdot \log a}{a\left(t+\frac{1}{t}\right)^{a-1}}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}u=a^{t+\frac{1}{t}} \Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d t}=a^{t+\frac{1}{t}} \cdot \log a .\left(1-\frac{1}{t^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}v=\left(t+\frac{1}{t}\right)^{a} \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d t}=a\left(t+\frac{1}{t}\right)^{a-1} \cdot\left(1-\frac{1}{t^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{d u / d t}{d v / d t}=\frac{a^{t+\frac{1}{t}} \cdot \log a}{a\left(t+\frac{1}{t}\right)^{a-1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}u=a^{t+\frac{1}{t}} \Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d t}=a^{t+\frac{1}{t}} \cdot \log a .\left(1-\frac{1}{t^{2}}\right){/tex}
{tex}v=\left(t+\frac{1}{t}\right)^{a} \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d t}=a\left(t+\frac{1}{t}\right)^{a-1} \cdot\left(1-\frac{1}{t^{2}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{d u / d t}{d v / d t}=\frac{a^{t+\frac{1}{t}} \cdot \log a}{a\left(t+\frac{1}{t}\right)^{a-1}}{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}-2 x^{2}-5 x y+y^{3}=76{/tex}, then find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}-2 x^{2}-5 x y+y^{3}=76{/tex} , then find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} .
explanation: Differentiating {tex}-2 x^{2}-5 x y+y^{3}=76{/tex} , with respect to ' {tex}x{/tex} ', {tex}-4 x-5 y-5 x \frac{d y}{d x}+3 y^{2} \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{4 x+5 y}{3 y^{2}-5 x}{/tex}
solution: Differentiating {tex}-2 x^{2}-5 x y+y^{3}=76{/tex} , with respect to ' {tex}x{/tex} ', {tex}-4 x-5 y-5 x \frac{d y}{d x}+3 y^{2} \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{4 x+5 y}{3 y^{2}-5 x}{/tex}
solution_html:
Differentiating {tex}-2 x^{2}-5 x y+y^{3}=76{/tex}, with respect to '{tex}x{/tex}',
{tex}-4 x-5 y-5 x \frac{d y}{d x}+3 y^{2} \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{4 x+5 y}{3 y^{2}-5 x}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Differentiate {tex}\left(\frac{5^{{x}}}{{x}^{5}}\right){/tex} with respect to x.
question_text: Differentiate {tex}\left(\frac{5^{{x}}}{{x}^{5}}\right){/tex} with respect to x.
explanation: Let, {tex}y=\frac{5^{x}}{x^{5}}=5^{x} \cdot x^{-5} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\left(5^{x}\right)^{\prime} \cdot x^{-5}+5^{x} \cdot\left(x^{-5}\right)^{\prime}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{5^{x}}{x^{5}} \log 5-\frac{5^{x+1}}{x^{6}}{/tex}
solution: Let, {tex}y=\frac{5^{x}}{x^{5}}=5^{x} \cdot x^{-5} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\left(5^{x}\right)^{\prime} \cdot x^{-5}+5^{x} \cdot\left(x^{-5}\right)^{\prime}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{5^{x}}{x^{5}} \log 5-\frac{5^{x+1}}{x^{6}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let, {tex}y=\frac{5^{x}}{x^{5}}=5^{x} \cdot x^{-5} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\left(5^{x}\right)^{\prime} \cdot x^{-5}+5^{x} \cdot\left(x^{-5}\right)^{\prime}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{5^{x}}{x^{5}} \log 5-\frac{5^{x+1}}{x^{6}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}x \sqrt{1+{y}}+{y} \sqrt{1+x}=0,-1 \lt x \lt 1, x \neq {y}{/tex}, then prove that {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{d} x}=\frac{-1}{(1+x)^{2}}{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}x \sqrt{1+{y}}+{y} \sqrt{1+x}=0,-1 \lt x \lt 1, x \neq {y}{/tex} , then prove that {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{d} x}=\frac{-1}{(1+x)^{2}}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}x \sqrt{1+y}+y \sqrt{1+x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \sqrt{1+y}=-y \sqrt{1+x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^2(1+y)=y^2(1+x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(x-y)(x+y)+x y(x-y)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(x-y)(x+y+x y)=0{/tex} {tex}x \neq y \Rightarrow x+y+x y=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{-x}{1+x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-1}{(1+x)^2}{/tex}
solution: {tex}x \sqrt{1+y}+y \sqrt{1+x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \sqrt{1+y}=-y \sqrt{1+x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^2(1+y)=y^2(1+x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(x-y)(x+y)+x y(x-y)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(x-y)(x+y+x y)=0{/tex} {tex}x \neq y \Rightarrow x+y+x y=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{-x}{1+x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-1}{(1+x)^2}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}x \sqrt{1+y}+y \sqrt{1+x}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \sqrt{1+y}=-y \sqrt{1+x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^2(1+y)=y^2(1+x){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(x-y)(x+y)+x y(x-y)=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(x-y)(x+y+x y)=0{/tex}
{tex}x \neq y \Rightarrow x+y+x y=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{-x}{1+x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-1}{(1+x)^2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}{y}=\log \left(\sqrt{x}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}}\right)^{2}{/tex}, then show that {tex}x(x+1)^{2} {y}_{2}+(x+1)^{2} {y}_{1}=2{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}{y}=\log \left(\sqrt{x}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}}\right)^{2}{/tex} , then show that {tex}x(x+1)^{2} {y}_{2}+(x+1)^{2} {y}_{1}=2{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}y=2 \log (x+1)-\log x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y_{1}=\frac{2}{x+1}-\frac{1}{x}=\frac{x-1}{x(x+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(x+1) y_{1}=\frac{x-1}{x}=1-\frac{1}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(x+1) y_{2}+y_{1}=\frac{1}{x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x(x+1)^{2} y_{2}+x(x+1) y_{1}=1+\frac{1}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x(x+1)^{2} y_{2}+x(x+1) y_{1}=1+1-(x+1) y_{1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x(x+1)^{2} y_{2}+(x+1)^{2} y_{1}=2{/tex}
solution: {tex}y=2 \log (x+1)-\log x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y_{1}=\frac{2}{x+1}-\frac{1}{x}=\frac{x-1}{x(x+1)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(x+1) y_{1}=\frac{x-1}{x}=1-\frac{1}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(x+1) y_{2}+y_{1}=\frac{1}{x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x(x+1)^{2} y_{2}+x(x+1) y_{1}=1+\frac{1}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x(x+1)^{2} y_{2}+x(x+1) y_{1}=1+1-(x+1) y_{1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x(x+1)^{2} y_{2}+(x+1)^{2} y_{1}=2{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}y=2 \log (x+1)-\log x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y_{1}=\frac{2}{x+1}-\frac{1}{x}=\frac{x-1}{x(x+1)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(x+1) y_{1}=\frac{x-1}{x}=1-\frac{1}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow(x+1) y_{2}+y_{1}=\frac{1}{x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x(x+1)^{2} y_{2}+x(x+1) y_{1}=1+\frac{1}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x(x+1)^{2} y_{2}+x(x+1) y_{1}=1+1-(x+1) y_{1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x(x+1)^{2} y_{2}+(x+1)^{2} y_{1}=2{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}{y}=5 \cos {x}-3 \sin {x}{/tex}, prove that {tex}\frac{{d}^{2} {y}}{{dx}^{2}}+{y}=0{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}{y}=5 \cos {x}-3 \sin {x}{/tex} , prove that {tex}\frac{{d}^{2} {y}}{{dx}^{2}}+{y}=0{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}y=5 \cos x-3 \sin x{/tex} , then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-5 \cdot \sin x-3 \cdot \cos x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}= -5 \cdot \cos x+3 \cdot \sin x=-y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+y=0{/tex}
solution: {tex}y=5 \cos x-3 \sin x{/tex} , then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-5 \cdot \sin x-3 \cdot \cos x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}= -5 \cdot \cos x+3 \cdot \sin x=-y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+y=0{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}y=5 \cos x-3 \sin x{/tex}, then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-5 \cdot \sin x-3 \cdot \cos x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}= -5 \cdot \cos x+3 \cdot \sin x=-y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+y=0{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Differentiate {tex}\frac{\sin {x}}{\sqrt{\cos {x}}}{/tex} with respect to x.
question_text: Differentiate {tex}\frac{\sin {x}}{\sqrt{\cos {x}}}{/tex} with respect to x.
explanation: Let {tex}y=\frac{\sin x}{\sqrt{\cos x}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sqrt{\cos x} \cdot \cos x-\sin x \cdot\left(\frac{-\sin x}{2 \sqrt{\cos x}}\right)}{\cos x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x} =\frac{2 \cos ^{2} x+\sin ^{2} x}{2(\cos x)^{3 / 2}} \text { or } \frac{1+\cos ^{2} x}{2(\cos x)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}y=\frac{\sin x}{\sqrt{\cos x}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sqrt{\cos x} \cdot \cos x-\sin x \cdot\left(\frac{-\sin x}{2 \sqrt{\cos x}}\right)}{\cos x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x} =\frac{2 \cos ^{2} x+\sin ^{2} x}{2(\cos x)^{3 / 2}} \text { or } \frac{1+\cos ^{2} x}{2(\cos x)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}y=\frac{\sin x}{\sqrt{\cos x}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sqrt{\cos x} \cdot \cos x-\sin x \cdot\left(\frac{-\sin x}{2 \sqrt{\cos x}}\right)}{\cos x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x} =\frac{2 \cos ^{2} x+\sin ^{2} x}{2(\cos x)^{3 / 2}} \text { or } \frac{1+\cos ^{2} x}{2(\cos x)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}

question_html:
If {tex}{f}(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl}2 x-3, & -3 \leq x \leq-2 \\ x+1, & -2 \lt x \leq 0\end{array}\right.{/tex}
Check the differentiability of {tex}{f}(x){/tex} at {tex}x=-2{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}{f}(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl}2 x-3, & -3 \leq x \leq-2 \\ x+1, & -2 \lt x \leq 0\end{array}\right.{/tex} Check the differentiability of {tex}{f}(x){/tex} at {tex}x=-2{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}L f^{\prime}(-2)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(-2-h)-f(-2)}{-h} (h \gt 0){/tex} {tex}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{2(-2-h)-3-(-7)}{-h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} 2=2{/tex} {tex}R f^{\prime}(-2)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(-2+h)-f(-2)}{h} (h \gt 0){/tex} {tex}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{-2+h+1-(-7)}{h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{6+h}{h}{/tex} , which does not exist, i.e., RHD does not exist. Therefore, the function is not differentiable at -2.
solution: {tex}L f^{\prime}(-2)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(-2-h)-f(-2)}{-h} (h \gt 0){/tex} {tex}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{2(-2-h)-3-(-7)}{-h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} 2=2{/tex} {tex}R f^{\prime}(-2)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(-2+h)-f(-2)}{h} (h \gt 0){/tex} {tex}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{-2+h+1-(-7)}{h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{6+h}{h}{/tex} , which does not exist, i.e., RHD does not exist. Therefore, the function is not differentiable at -2.
solution_html:
{tex}L f^{\prime}(-2)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(-2-h)-f(-2)}{-h} (h \gt 0){/tex}
{tex}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{2(-2-h)-3-(-7)}{-h}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} 2=2{/tex}
{tex}R f^{\prime}(-2)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(-2+h)-f(-2)}{h} (h \gt 0){/tex}
{tex}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{-2+h+1-(-7)}{h}{/tex}
{tex}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{6+h}{h}{/tex}, which does not exist, i.e., RHD does not exist.
Therefore, the function is not differentiable at -2.

explanation: Put {tex}x=\cos \theta \Rightarrow \theta=\cos ^{-1} x{/tex} Let {tex}u=\tan ^{-1} \frac{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{x}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}(\tan \theta)=\theta=\cos ^{-1} x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} Let {tex}v=\cos ^{-1}\left(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\cos ^{-1}(\sin 2 \theta)=\cos ^{-1}\left(\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-2 \theta\right)\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}-2 \cos ^{-1} x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{d u / d x}{d v / d x}=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
solution: Put {tex}x=\cos \theta \Rightarrow \theta=\cos ^{-1} x{/tex} Let {tex}u=\tan ^{-1} \frac{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{x}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}(\tan \theta)=\theta=\cos ^{-1} x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} Let {tex}v=\cos ^{-1}\left(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\cos ^{-1}(\sin 2 \theta)=\cos ^{-1}\left(\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-2 \theta\right)\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}-2 \cos ^{-1} x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{d u / d x}{d v / d x}=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

{tex}\frac{d y}{d x} a t\left(\frac{1}{9}, \frac{1}{9}\right)=-1{/tex}

explanation: {tex}\frac{d}{d x} \log \left(x^{x}+\operatorname{cosec}^{2} x\right) {/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{x^{x}+\operatorname{cosec}^{2} x} \frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x \log x}+\operatorname{cosec}^{2} x\right) {/tex} {tex}\left(\because x^{x}=e^{x \log x}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{x^{x}+\operatorname{cosec}^{2} x}\left[e^{x \log x}(1+\log x)-2 \operatorname{cosec}^{2} x \cot x\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{x^{x}+\operatorname{cosec}^{2} x}\left[x^{x}(1+\log x)-2 \operatorname{cosec}^{2} x \cot x\right]{/tex}

question_html: Show that the derivative of {tex}\tan ^{-1}(\sec {x}+\tan {x}),\left[-\frac{\pi}{2} \lt {x} \lt \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex} with respect to x is equal to {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}.
question_text: Show that the derivative of {tex}\tan ^{-1}(\sec {x}+\tan {x}),\left[-\frac{\pi}{2} \lt {x} \lt \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex} with respect to x is equal to {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\tan ^{-1}(\sec x+\tan x){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1+\sin x}{\cos x}\right)= \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1+\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \cos ^2\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{x}{2}\right)}{2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{x}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{x}{2}\right)}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\cot \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{x}{2}\right)\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}\right)\right)=\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore\left(\tan ^{-1}(\sec x+\tan x)\right)^{\prime}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\tan ^{-1}(\sec x+\tan x){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1+\sin x}{\cos x}\right)= \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1+\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \cos ^2\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{x}{2}\right)}{2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{x}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{x}{2}\right)}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\cot \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{x}{2}\right)\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}\right)\right)=\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore\left(\tan ^{-1}(\sec x+\tan x)\right)^{\prime}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\tan ^{-1}(\sec x+\tan x){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1+\sin x}{\cos x}\right)= \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1+\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \cos ^2\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{x}{2}\right)}{2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{x}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{x}{2}\right)}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\cot \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{x}{2}\right)\right){/tex}
{tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}\right)\right)=\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore\left(\tan ^{-1}(\sec x+\tan x)\right)^{\prime}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

question_html: Check the differentiability of function {tex}{f}({x})={x}|{x}|{/tex} at {tex}{x}=0{/tex}.
question_text: Check the differentiability of function {tex}{f}({x})={x}|{x}|{/tex} at {tex}{x}=0{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}f(x)=x|x|=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}-x^{2} & , x \leq 0 \\ x^{2} & , x \gt 0\end{array}\right.{/tex} {tex}{LHD}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0-h)-f(0)}{-h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{-h^{2}-0}{-h}=0{/tex} RHD {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0+h)-f(0)}{h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{h^{2}-0}{h}=0{/tex} Since LHD {tex}={/tex} RHD, {tex}f{/tex} is differentiable at {tex}x=0{/tex}
solution: {tex}f(x)=x|x|=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}-x^{2} & , x \leq 0 \\ x^{2} & , x \gt 0\end{array}\right.{/tex} {tex}{LHD}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0-h)-f(0)}{-h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{-h^{2}-0}{-h}=0{/tex} RHD {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0+h)-f(0)}{h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{h^{2}-0}{h}=0{/tex} Since LHD {tex}={/tex} RHD, {tex}f{/tex} is differentiable at {tex}x=0{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}f(x)=x|x|=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}-x^{2} & , x \leq 0 \\ x^{2} & , x \gt 0\end{array}\right.{/tex}
{tex}{LHD}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0-h)-f(0)}{-h}{/tex}{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{-h^{2}-0}{-h}=0{/tex}
RHD {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0+h)-f(0)}{h}{/tex}{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{h^{2}-0}{h}=0{/tex}
Since LHD {tex}={/tex} RHD,{tex}f{/tex} is differentiable at {tex}x=0{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}e^{y}(x+1)=1{/tex}, prove that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-e^{y}{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}e^{y}(x+1)=1{/tex} , prove that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-e^{y}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}e^{y}(x+1)=1 \Rightarrow e^{y}=\frac{1}{x+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=-\log (x+1){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{1}{x+1}{/tex} {tex}=-e^{y} \left[\because \frac{1}{x+1}=e^{y}\right]{/tex}
solution: {tex}e^{y}(x+1)=1 \Rightarrow e^{y}=\frac{1}{x+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=-\log (x+1){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{1}{x+1}{/tex} {tex}=-e^{y} \left[\because \frac{1}{x+1}=e^{y}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}e^{y}(x+1)=1 \Rightarrow e^{y}=\frac{1}{x+1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=-\log (x+1){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{1}{x+1}{/tex}
{tex}=-e^{y} \left[\because \frac{1}{x+1}=e^{y}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If xy = ex-y, then prove that {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \log x } { ( 1 + \log x ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}.
question_text: If x y = e x-y , then prove that {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \log x } { ( 1 + \log x ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} .
explanation: According to the question, {tex}x^y = e^{x-y}{/tex} Taking log both sides , {tex}\Rightarrow y log_ex = (x - y)log_ee{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y log_ex = (x - y){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y(1 + logx) = x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = \frac { x } { 1 + \log x }{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { ( 1 + \log x ) \frac { d } { d x } ( x ) - x \frac { d } { d x } ( 1 + \log x ) } { ( 1 + \log x ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} [ Using quotient rule of derivative] {tex}= \frac { 1 + \log x - x \cdot \frac { 1 } { x } } { ( 1 + \log x ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { 1 + \log x - 1 } { ( 1 + \log x ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \log x } { ( 1 + \log x ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Hence Proved
solution: According to the question, {tex}x^y = e^{x-y}{/tex} Taking log both sides , {tex}\Rightarrow y log_ex = (x - y)log_ee{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y log_ex = (x - y){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y(1 + logx) = x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = \frac { x } { 1 + \log x }{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { ( 1 + \log x ) \frac { d } { d x } ( x ) - x \frac { d } { d x } ( 1 + \log x ) } { ( 1 + \log x ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} [ Using quotient rule of derivative] {tex}= \frac { 1 + \log x - x \cdot \frac { 1 } { x } } { ( 1 + \log x ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { 1 + \log x - 1 } { ( 1 + \log x ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \log x } { ( 1 + \log x ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Hence Proved
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}x^y = e^{x-y}{/tex}
Taking log both sides ,
{tex}\Rightarrow y log_ex = (x - y)log_ee{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y log_ex = (x - y){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow y(1 + logx) = x{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow y = \frac { x } { 1 + \log x }{/tex}
Differentiating both sides w.r.t x,
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { ( 1 + \log x ) \frac { d } { d x } ( x ) - x \frac { d } { d x } ( 1 + \log x ) } { ( 1 + \log x ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}[ Using quotient rule of derivative]
{tex}= \frac { 1 + \log x - x \cdot \frac { 1 } { x } } { ( 1 + \log x ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}= {tex}\frac { 1 + \log x - 1 } { ( 1 + \log x ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \log x } { ( 1 + \log x ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
Hence Proved

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If y = {tex}e^{a \cos ^{-1} x}{/tex}, show that {tex}\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d^{2} x}-x \frac{d y}{d x}-a^{2} y=0{/tex}.
question_text: If y = {tex}e^{a \cos ^{-1} x}{/tex} , show that {tex}\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d^{2} x}-x \frac{d y}{d x}-a^{2} y=0{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}y=e^{a \cos ^{-1} x} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{a \cos ^{-1} x} \frac{(-a)}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\sqrt{1-x^{2}} \frac{d y}{d x}=-a y{/tex} ... (i) Differentiating again w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\sqrt{1-x^{2}} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{a d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}=-a \sqrt{1-x^{2}} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = – a (-ay) [from i] Hence {tex}\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}-a^{2} y=0{/tex}
solution: {tex}y=e^{a \cos ^{-1} x} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{a \cos ^{-1} x} \frac{(-a)}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\sqrt{1-x^{2}} \frac{d y}{d x}=-a y{/tex} ... (i) Differentiating again w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\sqrt{1-x^{2}} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{a d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}=-a \sqrt{1-x^{2}} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = – a (-ay) [from i] Hence {tex}\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}-a^{2} y=0{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}y=e^{a \cos ^{-1} x} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{a \cos ^{-1} x} \frac{(-a)}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\sqrt{1-x^{2}} \frac{d y}{d x}=-a y{/tex} ... (i)
Differentiating again w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\sqrt{1-x^{2}} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{a d y}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}=-a \sqrt{1-x^{2}} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
= – a (-ay) [from i]
Hence {tex}\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}-a^{2} y=0{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}\sqrt{1-x^2}+\sqrt{1-y^2}{/tex} = a(x - y), then prove that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sqrt{1-y^2}}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}\sqrt{1-x^2}+\sqrt{1-y^2}{/tex} = a(x - y), then prove that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sqrt{1-y^2}}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\sqrt{1-x^2}+\sqrt{1-y^2}=a(x-y){/tex} Let x = sin A, y = sin B {tex}\sqrt{1-\sin ^2 A}+\sqrt{1-\sin ^2 B}{/tex} = a(sin A - sin B) cos A + cos B = a(sin A - sin B) {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \cos \left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=2 a \cos \left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)=a \sin \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cot \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)=a{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{A-B}{2}=\cot ^{-1} a{/tex} A - B = 2 cot -1 a {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin -1 x - sin -1 y = 2 cot -1 a {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Differentiating w.r.t. x {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-y^2}} \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sqrt{1-y^2}}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\sqrt{1-x^2}+\sqrt{1-y^2}=a(x-y){/tex} Let x = sin A, y = sin B {tex}\sqrt{1-\sin ^2 A}+\sqrt{1-\sin ^2 B}{/tex} = a(sin A - sin B) cos A + cos B = a(sin A - sin B) {tex}\Rightarrow 2 \cos \left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=2 a \cos \left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)=a \sin \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cot \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)=a{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{A-B}{2}=\cot ^{-1} a{/tex} A - B = 2 cot -1 a {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin -1 x - sin -1 y = 2 cot -1 a {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Differentiating w.r.t. x {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-y^2}} \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sqrt{1-y^2}}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\sqrt{1-x^2}+\sqrt{1-y^2}=a(x-y){/tex}
Let x = sin A, y = sin B
{tex}\sqrt{1-\sin ^2 A}+\sqrt{1-\sin ^2 B}{/tex} = a(sin A - sin B)
cos A + cos B = a(sin A - sin B)
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \cos \left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}=2 a \cos \left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \cos \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)=a \sin \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \cot \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)=a{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{A-B}{2}=\cot ^{-1} a{/tex}
A - B = 2 cot-1 a
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin-1 x - sin-1y = 2 cot-1 a
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Differentiating w.r.t. x
{tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-y^2}} \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sqrt{1-y^2}}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Check whether the function f(x) = x2 |x| is differentiable at x = 0 or not.
question_text: Check whether the function f(x) = x 2 |x| is differentiable at x = 0 or not.
explanation: {tex} f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} x^3, x \geq 0 \\ -x^3, x \leq 0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} {tex} {RHD}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0+h)-f(0)}{h}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} h^2=0{/tex} {tex} {LHD}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0-h)-f(0)}{-h}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}\left(-h^2\right)=0{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} RHD = LHD = 0, So f(x) is differentiable at x = 0
solution: {tex} f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} x^3, x \geq 0 \\ -x^3, x \leq 0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} {tex} {RHD}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0+h)-f(0)}{h}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} h^2=0{/tex} {tex} {LHD}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0-h)-f(0)}{-h}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}\left(-h^2\right)=0{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} RHD = LHD = 0, So f(x) is differentiable at x = 0
solution_html:
{tex} f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} x^3, x \geq 0 \\ -x^3, x \leq 0 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
{tex} {RHD}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0+h)-f(0)}{h}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} h^2=0{/tex}
{tex} {LHD}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0-h)-f(0)}{-h}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}\left(-h^2\right)=0{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} RHD = LHD = 0,
So f(x) is differentiable at x = 0

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If y = {tex}\sqrt{\tan \sqrt{x}}{/tex}, prove that {tex}\sqrt{x} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1+y^4}{4 y}{/tex}.
question_text: If y = {tex}\sqrt{\tan \sqrt{x}}{/tex} , prove that {tex}\sqrt{x} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1+y^4}{4 y}{/tex} .
explanation: y = {tex} \sqrt{\tan \sqrt{x}}{/tex} {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sec ^2 \sqrt{x}}{2 \sqrt{\tan \sqrt{x}}} \times \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}}{/tex} {tex} \sqrt{x} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sec ^2 \sqrt{x}}{4 \sqrt{\tan \sqrt{x}}}{/tex} {tex} =\frac{1+(\tan \sqrt{x})^2}{4 \sqrt{\tan \sqrt{x}}}=\frac{1+y^4}{4 y}{/tex}
solution: y = {tex} \sqrt{\tan \sqrt{x}}{/tex} {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sec ^2 \sqrt{x}}{2 \sqrt{\tan \sqrt{x}}} \times \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}}{/tex} {tex} \sqrt{x} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sec ^2 \sqrt{x}}{4 \sqrt{\tan \sqrt{x}}}{/tex} {tex} =\frac{1+(\tan \sqrt{x})^2}{4 \sqrt{\tan \sqrt{x}}}=\frac{1+y^4}{4 y}{/tex}
solution_html:
y = {tex} \sqrt{\tan \sqrt{x}}{/tex}
{tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sec ^2 \sqrt{x}}{2 \sqrt{\tan \sqrt{x}}} \times \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}}{/tex}
{tex} \sqrt{x} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sec ^2 \sqrt{x}}{4 \sqrt{\tan \sqrt{x}}}{/tex}
{tex} =\frac{1+(\tan \sqrt{x})^2}{4 \sqrt{\tan \sqrt{x}}}=\frac{1+y^4}{4 y}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}\sqrt{1-{x}^2}+\sqrt{1-{y}^2}{/tex} = a(x - y), prove that {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex} \sqrt{\frac{1-{y}^2}{1-{x}^2}}{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}\sqrt{1-{x}^2}+\sqrt{1-{y}^2}{/tex} = a(x - y), prove that {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex} \sqrt{\frac{1-{y}^2}{1-{x}^2}}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex} \sqrt{1-x^2}+\sqrt{1-y^2}=a(x-y) {/tex} {tex} \text { Put } {x}=\sin \theta, {y}=\sin \phi {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cos \theta+\cos \phi={a}(\sin \theta-\sin \phi) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 2 \cos \left(\frac{\theta+\phi}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right)=2 {a} \sin \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{\theta+\phi}{2}\right) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cot \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right)={a} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \theta-\phi=2 \cot ^{-1} a {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \sin ^{-1} x-\sin ^{-1} y=2 \cot ^{-1} a {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-y^2}} \frac{d y}{d x}=0 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\sqrt{\frac{1-y^2}{1-x^2}}{/tex}
solution: {tex} \sqrt{1-x^2}+\sqrt{1-y^2}=a(x-y) {/tex} {tex} \text { Put } {x}=\sin \theta, {y}=\sin \phi {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cos \theta+\cos \phi={a}(\sin \theta-\sin \phi) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 2 \cos \left(\frac{\theta+\phi}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right)=2 {a} \sin \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{\theta+\phi}{2}\right) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cot \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right)={a} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \theta-\phi=2 \cot ^{-1} a {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \sin ^{-1} x-\sin ^{-1} y=2 \cot ^{-1} a {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-y^2}} \frac{d y}{d x}=0 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\sqrt{\frac{1-y^2}{1-x^2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \sqrt{1-x^2}+\sqrt{1-y^2}=a(x-y) {/tex}
{tex} \text { Put } {x}=\sin \theta, {y}=\sin \phi {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \cos \theta+\cos \phi={a}(\sin \theta-\sin \phi) {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 2 \cos \left(\frac{\theta+\phi}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right)=2 {a} \sin \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{\theta+\phi}{2}\right) {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \cot \left(\frac{\theta-\phi}{2}\right)={a} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \theta-\phi=2 \cot ^{-1} a {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \sin ^{-1} x-\sin ^{-1} y=2 \cot ^{-1} a {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-y^2}} \frac{d y}{d x}=0 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\sqrt{\frac{1-y^2}{1-x^2}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If y = (tan x)x, then find {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}.
question_text: If y = (tan x) x , then find {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} .
explanation: y = (tan x) x log y = x log(tan x) {tex} \frac{1}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=x\left(\frac{\sec ^2 x}{\tan x}\right)+\log (\tan x) {/tex} {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=(\tan x)^x\left[\left(\frac{x \sec ^2 x}{\tan x}\right)+\log (\tan x)\right]{/tex}
solution: y = (tan x) x log y = x log(tan x) {tex} \frac{1}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=x\left(\frac{\sec ^2 x}{\tan x}\right)+\log (\tan x) {/tex} {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=(\tan x)^x\left[\left(\frac{x \sec ^2 x}{\tan x}\right)+\log (\tan x)\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
y = (tan x)x
log y = x log(tan x)
{tex} \frac{1}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=x\left(\frac{\sec ^2 x}{\tan x}\right)+\log (\tan x) {/tex}

question_html: The number of points of discontinuity of f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{aligned} |x|+3, & \text { if } x \leq-3 \\ -2 x, & \text { if }-3<x<3 \\ 6 x+2, & \text { if } x \geq 3 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex} is:
question_text: The number of points of discontinuity of f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{aligned} |x|+3, & \text { if } x \leq-3 \\ -2 x, & \text { if }-3<x<3 \\ 6 x+2, & \text { if } x \geq 3 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex} is:

options.explanation: we check the points where the pieces meet, specifically at {tex}x=-3{/tex} and {tex}x=3{/tex} . At {tex}x=-3{/tex} : - {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow-3^{-}} f(x)=|-3|+3=6{/tex} - {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow-3^{+}} f(x)=-2(-3)=6{/tex} - {tex}f(-3)=6{/tex} - Since both limits and the function value agree, {tex}f{/tex} is continuous at {tex}x={/tex} {tex}-3{/tex} . At {tex}x=3{/tex} : - {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 3^{-}} f(x)=-2(3)=-6{/tex} - {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 3^{+}} f(x)=6(3)+2=20{/tex} - {tex}f(3)=6(3)+2=20{/tex} - The left and right limits do not agree, indicating a discontinuity at {tex}x={/tex} 3. The function {tex}f(x){/tex} has potential discontinuities at {tex}x=-3{/tex} and {tex}x=3{/tex} . At {tex}x={/tex} -3 , it is continuous; however, at {tex}x=3{/tex} , the left and right limits differ. Thus, there is 1 point of discontinuity. The answer is 1.

At {tex}x={/tex} -3 , it is continuous; however, at {tex}x=3{/tex} , the left and right limits differ. Thus, there is 1 point of discontinuity. The answer is 1.

question_html: If x = {tex}e^{\cos 3 t}{/tex} t and y = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\sin 3 \mathrm{t}}{/tex} prove that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{y \log x}{x \log y}{/tex}.
question_text: If x = {tex}e^{\cos 3 t}{/tex} t and y = {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\sin 3 \mathrm{t}}{/tex} prove that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{y \log x}{x \log y}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex} \frac{d x}{d t}=e^{\cos 3 t} \times(-\sin 3 t) \times 3 {/tex} {tex} \frac{d y}{d t}=e^{\sin 3 t} \times(\cos 3 t) \times 3 {/tex} {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d t}}{\frac{d x}{d t}}=\frac{e^{\sin 3 t} \times(\cos 3 t)}{-e^{\cos 3 t} \times(\sin 3 t)} {/tex} {tex} x=e^{\cos 3 t} \Rightarrow \cos 3 t=\log x {/tex} {tex} y=e^{\sin 3 t} \Rightarrow \sin 3 t=\log y {/tex} {tex} \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-y \log x}{x \log y} {/tex}
solution: {tex} \frac{d x}{d t}=e^{\cos 3 t} \times(-\sin 3 t) \times 3 {/tex} {tex} \frac{d y}{d t}=e^{\sin 3 t} \times(\cos 3 t) \times 3 {/tex} {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d t}}{\frac{d x}{d t}}=\frac{e^{\sin 3 t} \times(\cos 3 t)}{-e^{\cos 3 t} \times(\sin 3 t)} {/tex} {tex} x=e^{\cos 3 t} \Rightarrow \cos 3 t=\log x {/tex} {tex} y=e^{\sin 3 t} \Rightarrow \sin 3 t=\log y {/tex} {tex} \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-y \log x}{x \log y} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \frac{d x}{d t}=e^{\cos 3 t} \times(-\sin 3 t) \times 3 {/tex}
{tex} \frac{d y}{d t}=e^{\sin 3 t} \times(\cos 3 t) \times 3 {/tex}
{tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d t}}{\frac{d x}{d t}}=\frac{e^{\sin 3 t} \times(\cos 3 t)}{-e^{\cos 3 t} \times(\sin 3 t)} {/tex}
{tex} x=e^{\cos 3 t} \Rightarrow \cos 3 t=\log x {/tex}
{tex} y=e^{\sin 3 t} \Rightarrow \sin 3 t=\log y {/tex}
{tex} \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-y \log x}{x \log y} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Show that the function f(x) = |x|3 is differentiable at all points of its domain.
question_text: Show that the function f(x) = |x| 3 is differentiable at all points of its domain.
explanation: {tex}{f}({x})=\left\{\begin{array}{rr}x^3, & x \geq 0 \\ -x^3, & x \leq 0\end{array}\right.{/tex} At {tex}{x}=0{/tex} LHD {tex} =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0-h)-f(0)}{-h}={/tex} {tex} \lim _{h \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{h^3}{-h}\right)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}\left(-h^2\right)=0{/tex} {tex} {RHD}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0+h)-f(0)}{h}={/tex} {tex} \lim _{h \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{h^3}{h}\right)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}\left(h^2\right)=0{/tex} {tex}\because \operatorname{LHD}={/tex} RHD at {tex}{x}=0{/tex} ; when {tex}{x} \neq 0, {f}({x}){/tex} is a polynomial and hence differentiable. {tex}\therefore {f}({x}){/tex} is differentiable at all points.
solution: {tex}{f}({x})=\left\{\begin{array}{rr}x^3, & x \geq 0 \\ -x^3, & x \leq 0\end{array}\right.{/tex} At {tex}{x}=0{/tex} LHD {tex} =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0-h)-f(0)}{-h}={/tex} {tex} \lim _{h \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{h^3}{-h}\right)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}\left(-h^2\right)=0{/tex} {tex} {RHD}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0+h)-f(0)}{h}={/tex} {tex} \lim _{h \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{h^3}{h}\right)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}\left(h^2\right)=0{/tex} {tex}\because \operatorname{LHD}={/tex} RHD at {tex}{x}=0{/tex} ; when {tex}{x} \neq 0, {f}({x}){/tex} is a polynomial and hence differentiable. {tex}\therefore {f}({x}){/tex} is differentiable at all points.
solution_html:
{tex}{f}({x})=\left\{\begin{array}{rr}x^3, & x \geq 0 \\ -x^3, & x \leq 0\end{array}\right.{/tex}
At {tex}{x}=0{/tex}
LHD {tex} =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0-h)-f(0)}{-h}={/tex} {tex} \lim _{h \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{h^3}{-h}\right)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}\left(-h^2\right)=0{/tex}
{tex} {RHD}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0+h)-f(0)}{h}={/tex} {tex} \lim _{h \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{h^3}{h}\right)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}\left(h^2\right)=0{/tex}
{tex}\because \operatorname{LHD}={/tex} RHD at {tex}{x}=0{/tex}; when {tex}{x} \neq 0, {f}({x}){/tex} is a polynomial and hence differentiable.
{tex}\therefore {f}({x}){/tex} is differentiable at all points.

question_html: The number of points, where {tex}\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=[\mathrm{x}], 0<\mathrm{x}<3([\cdot]{/tex} denotes greatest integer function) is not differentiable is:
question_text: The number of points, where {tex}\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=[\mathrm{x}], 0<\mathrm{x}<3([\cdot]{/tex} denotes greatest integer function) is not differentiable is:

options.explanation: The greatest integer function, denoted by [x], is discontinuous at every integer point. Therefore, f(x) = [x] is not differentiable at any integer point within the interval 0 < x < 3. There are three integer points within this interval: 1, 2, and 3. However, since the interval is strictly between 0 and 3, we only consider the points 1 and 2. Therefore, f(x) is not differentiable at 2 points.

Therefore, f(x) is not differentiable at 2 points.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: {tex}\text { If } x=a t, y=\frac{a}{t} \text {, then } \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} is:
question_text: {tex}\text { If } x=a t, y=\frac{a}{t} \text {, then } \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} is:

options.explanation: To find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} , we can use the chain rule: {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d t} \cdot \frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} First, let's find {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}{/tex} : {tex} \frac{d y}{d t}=-\frac{a}{t^2}{/tex} Next, let's find {tex}\frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} : {tex} \frac{d t}{d x}=\frac{1}{a}{/tex} Substituting these values into the chain rule equation: {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\left(-\frac{a}{t^2}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{1}{a}\right){/tex} Simplifying: {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{1}{t^2}{/tex} Therefore, the correct answer is {tex} -\frac{1}{t^2}{/tex} .

explanation: To find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} , we can use the chain rule: {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d t} \cdot \frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} First, let's find {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}{/tex} : {tex} \frac{d y}{d t}=-\frac{a}{t^2}{/tex} Next, let's find {tex}\frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} : {tex} \frac{d t}{d x}=\frac{1}{a}{/tex} Substituting these values into the chain rule equation: {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\left(-\frac{a}{t^2}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{1}{a}\right){/tex} Simplifying: {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{1}{t^2}{/tex} Therefore, the correct answer is {tex} -\frac{1}{t^2}{/tex} .

question_html: Check the differentiability of function f(x) = [x] at x = -3, where [{tex}\cdot{/tex}] denotes greatest integer function.
question_text: Check the differentiability of function f(x) = [x] at x = -3, where [ {tex}\cdot{/tex} ] denotes greatest integer function.
explanation: {tex} f(x)=[x] \text { at } x=-3 {/tex} {tex} {RHD}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(-3+h)-f(-3)}{h} {/tex} {tex} =\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{-3-(-3)}{h}=0 {/tex} {tex} {LHD}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(-3-h)-f(-3)}{-h} {/tex} {tex} =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{-4-(-3)}{h}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{-1}{h}\right) {/tex} {tex} =\text { not defined } {/tex} {tex} \because \text { LHD } \neq \text { RHD } {/tex} {tex} \text { So } {f} \text { is not differentiable at } {x}=-3 {/tex}
solution: {tex} f(x)=[x] \text { at } x=-3 {/tex} {tex} {RHD}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(-3+h)-f(-3)}{h} {/tex} {tex} =\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{-3-(-3)}{h}=0 {/tex} {tex} {LHD}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(-3-h)-f(-3)}{-h} {/tex} {tex} =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{-4-(-3)}{h}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{-1}{h}\right) {/tex} {tex} =\text { not defined } {/tex} {tex} \because \text { LHD } \neq \text { RHD } {/tex} {tex} \text { So } {f} \text { is not differentiable at } {x}=-3 {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} f(x)=[x] \text { at } x=-3 {/tex}
{tex} {RHD}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(-3+h)-f(-3)}{h} {/tex}
{tex} =\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{-3-(-3)}{h}=0 {/tex}
{tex} {LHD}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(-3-h)-f(-3)}{-h} {/tex}
{tex} =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{-4-(-3)}{h}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{-1}{h}\right) {/tex}
{tex} =\text { not defined } {/tex}
{tex} \because \text { LHD } \neq \text { RHD } {/tex}
{tex} \text { So } {f} \text { is not differentiable at } {x}=-3 {/tex}

question_html: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x} \text {, if } 5^x+5^y=5^{x+y}{/tex}.
question_text: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x} \text {, if } 5^x+5^y=5^{x+y}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex} \text { Differentiating both sides w.r.t. } {x} \text {, we get } {/tex} {tex} 5^x \log 5+5^y \log 5 \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}=5^{x+y} \log 5\left(1+\frac{d y}{d x}\right) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 5^x+5^y \frac{d y}{d x}=\left(5^x+5^y\right)\left(1+\frac{d y}{d x}\right) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 5^x+5^y \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}=5^x+5^x \frac{d y}{d x}+5^y+5^y \frac{d y}{d x} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{5^y}{5^x}=-5^{y-x} {/tex}
solution: {tex} \text { Differentiating both sides w.r.t. } {x} \text {, we get } {/tex} {tex} 5^x \log 5+5^y \log 5 \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}=5^{x+y} \log 5\left(1+\frac{d y}{d x}\right) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 5^x+5^y \frac{d y}{d x}=\left(5^x+5^y\right)\left(1+\frac{d y}{d x}\right) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 5^x+5^y \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}=5^x+5^x \frac{d y}{d x}+5^y+5^y \frac{d y}{d x} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{5^y}{5^x}=-5^{y-x} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \text { Differentiating both sides w.r.t. } {x} \text {, we get } {/tex}
{tex} 5^x \log 5+5^y \log 5 \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}{tex}=5^{x+y} \log 5\left(1+\frac{d y}{d x}\right) {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 5^x+5^y \frac{d y}{d x}=\left(5^x+5^y\right)\left(1+\frac{d y}{d x}\right) {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 5^x+5^y \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}{tex}=5^x+5^x \frac{d y}{d x}+5^y+5^y \frac{d y}{d x} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{5^y}{5^x}=-5^{y-x} {/tex}

question_html: Check the differentiability of f(x) = {tex}|\cos x| \text { at } x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}.
question_text: Check the differentiability of f(x) = {tex}|\cos x| \text { at } x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex} {f}({x})=|\cos {x}|=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \cos x & 0 \leq x \leq \frac{\pi}{2} \\ -\cos x & \frac{\pi}{2} \leq x \leq \pi \end{array}\right.{/tex} {tex} \text { LHD at } \frac{\pi}{2}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-h\right)-f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)}{-h}={/tex} {tex} \lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-h\right)-0}{-h}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sinh }{-h}=-1{/tex} {tex} \text { RHD at } \frac{\pi}{2}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}+h\right)-f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)}{h}{/tex} {tex} =\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{-\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}+h\right)-0}{h}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sinh }{h}=1{/tex} {tex} \text { LHD } \neq \text { RHD } {/tex} {tex} \therefore {f} \text { is not differentiable at } {x}=\frac{\pi}{2} {/tex}

{tex} {f}({x})=|\cos {x}|=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \cos x & 0 \leq x \leq \frac{\pi}{2} \\ -\cos x & \frac{\pi}{2} \leq x \leq \pi \end{array}\right.{/tex}
{tex} \text { LHD at } \frac{\pi}{2}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-h\right)-f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)}{-h}={/tex}{tex} \lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-h\right)-0}{-h}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sinh }{-h}=-1{/tex}
{tex} \text { RHD at } \frac{\pi}{2}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}+h\right)-f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)}{h}{/tex}{tex} =\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{-\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}+h\right)-0}{h}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sinh }{h}=1{/tex}
{tex} \text { LHD } \neq \text { RHD } {/tex}
{tex} \therefore {f} \text { is not differentiable at } {x}=\frac{\pi}{2} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}{x^y} = {e^{x - y}},{/tex} prove that {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{\log x}}{{{{\left( {1 + \log x} \right)}^2}}}{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}{x^y} = {e^{x - y}},{/tex} prove that {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{\log x}}{{{{\left( {1 + \log x} \right)}^2}}}{/tex}
explanation: We have {tex}{x^y} = {e^{x - y}}{/tex} Taking logarithm on both sides, we get {tex}y\log x = x - y{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y\left( {1 + \log x} \right) = x{/tex} i.e. {tex}y = \frac{x}{{1 + \log x}}{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{\left( {1 + \log x} \right).1 - x\left( {\frac{1}{x}} \right)}}{{{{\left( {1 + \log x} \right)}^2}}} = \frac{{\log x}}{{{{\left( {1 + \log x} \right)}^2}}}{/tex}
solution: We have {tex}{x^y} = {e^{x - y}}{/tex} Taking logarithm on both sides, we get {tex}y\log x = x - y{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y\left( {1 + \log x} \right) = x{/tex} i.e. {tex}y = \frac{x}{{1 + \log x}}{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{\left( {1 + \log x} \right).1 - x\left( {\frac{1}{x}} \right)}}{{{{\left( {1 + \log x} \right)}^2}}} = \frac{{\log x}}{{{{\left( {1 + \log x} \right)}^2}}}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have {tex}{x^y} = {e^{x - y}}{/tex} 

Taking logarithm on both sides, we get

{tex}y\log x = x - y{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow y\left( {1 + \log x} \right) = x{/tex}

i.e. {tex}y = \frac{x}{{1 + \log x}}{/tex}

Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{\left( {1 + \log x} \right).1 - x\left( {\frac{1}{x}} \right)}}{{{{\left( {1 + \log x} \right)}^2}}} = \frac{{\log x}}{{{{\left( {1 + \log x} \right)}^2}}}{/tex}

question_html:
Find the values of a and b so that the following function is differentiable for all values of {tex}x{/tex} :
{tex}{f}(x)= \begin{cases}{a} x+{b}, & x \gt -1 \\ {~b} x^{2}-3, & x \leq-1\end{cases}{/tex}
question_text: Find the values of a and b so that the following function is differentiable for all values of {tex}x{/tex} : {tex}{f}(x)= \begin{cases}{a} x+{b}, & x \gt -1 \\ {~b} x^{2}-3, & x \leq-1\end{cases}{/tex}
explanation: Since Differentiability ⇒ Continuity {tex}f(x){/tex} is continuous at {tex}x=-1 \Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow-^{-}}\left(b x^{2}-3\right)=\lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{+}}(a x+b){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow b-3=-a+b, \therefore a=3{/tex} {tex}f(x){/tex} is differentiable at {tex}x=-1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{-}} \frac{\left(b x^{2}-3\right)-(b-3)}{x+1}=\lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{+}} \frac{(a x+b)-(b-3)}{x+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{-}} b(x-1)=\lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{+}} \frac{3(x+1)}{x+1}, \therefore b=-\frac{3}{2}{/tex} Alternately, {tex}L H D=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(-1)-f(-1-h)}{h}={/tex} {tex}\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{(b-3)-\left(b(-1-h)^{2}-3\right)}{h}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}(-b h-2 b)=-2 b{/tex} {tex}{R H D}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(-1)-f(-1-h)}{h}={/tex} {tex}\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{[a(-1+h)+b]-(b-3)}{h}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{(-a+3)+a h}{h}=a{/tex} {tex}\text { and }-a+3=0 \Rightarrow a=3,-2 b=a \Rightarrow b=-3 / 2{/tex}
solution: Since Differentiability ⇒ Continuity {tex}f(x){/tex} is continuous at {tex}x=-1 \Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow-^{-}}\left(b x^{2}-3\right)=\lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{+}}(a x+b){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow b-3=-a+b, \therefore a=3{/tex} {tex}f(x){/tex} is differentiable at {tex}x=-1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{-}} \frac{\left(b x^{2}-3\right)-(b-3)}{x+1}=\lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{+}} \frac{(a x+b)-(b-3)}{x+1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{-}} b(x-1)=\lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{+}} \frac{3(x+1)}{x+1}, \therefore b=-\frac{3}{2}{/tex} Alternately, {tex}L H D=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(-1)-f(-1-h)}{h}={/tex} {tex}\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{(b-3)-\left(b(-1-h)^{2}-3\right)}{h}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}(-b h-2 b)=-2 b{/tex} {tex}{R H D}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(-1)-f(-1-h)}{h}={/tex} {tex}\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{[a(-1+h)+b]-(b-3)}{h}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{(-a+3)+a h}{h}=a{/tex} {tex}\text { and }-a+3=0 \Rightarrow a=3,-2 b=a \Rightarrow b=-3 / 2{/tex}
solution_html:
Since Differentiability ⇒ Continuity
{tex}f(x){/tex} is continuous at {tex}x=-1 \Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow-^{-}}\left(b x^{2}-3\right)=\lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{+}}(a x+b){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow b-3=-a+b, \therefore a=3{/tex}
{tex}f(x){/tex} is differentiable at {tex}x=-1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{-}} \frac{\left(b x^{2}-3\right)-(b-3)}{x+1}=\lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{+}} \frac{(a x+b)-(b-3)}{x+1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{-}} b(x-1)=\lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{+}} \frac{3(x+1)}{x+1}, \therefore b=-\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
Alternately,
{tex}L H D=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(-1)-f(-1-h)}{h}={/tex} {tex}\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{(b-3)-\left(b(-1-h)^{2}-3\right)}{h}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}(-b h-2 b)=-2 b{/tex}
{tex}{R H D}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(-1)-f(-1-h)}{h}={/tex} {tex}\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{[a(-1+h)+b]-(b-3)}{h}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{(-a+3)+a h}{h}=a{/tex}
{tex}\text { and }-a+3=0 \Rightarrow a=3,-2 b=a \Rightarrow b=-3 / 2{/tex}

question_html: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}, if {tex}y=x^{\cos x}-2^{\sin x}{/tex}.
question_text: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} , if {tex}y=x^{\cos x}-2^{\sin x}{/tex} .
explanation: Let {tex}y=u-v \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}-\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} , where {tex}u=x^{\cos x}, v=2^{\sin x}{/tex} {tex} \log u=\cos x \log x \Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} {tex}=x^{\cos x}\left[\frac{\cos x}{x}-\sin x \log x\right], {/tex} {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=2^{\sin x} \cos x \log 2 {/tex} {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=x^{\cos x}\left[\frac{\cos x}{x}-\sin x \log x\right]{/tex} {tex}-2^{\sin x} \cos x \log 2 {/tex}
solution: Let {tex}y=u-v \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}-\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} , where {tex}u=x^{\cos x}, v=2^{\sin x}{/tex} {tex} \log u=\cos x \log x \Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} {tex}=x^{\cos x}\left[\frac{\cos x}{x}-\sin x \log x\right], {/tex} {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=2^{\sin x} \cos x \log 2 {/tex} {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=x^{\cos x}\left[\frac{\cos x}{x}-\sin x \log x\right]{/tex} {tex}-2^{\sin x} \cos x \log 2 {/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}y=u-v \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}-\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}, where {tex}u=x^{\cos x}, v=2^{\sin x}{/tex}
{tex} \log u=\cos x \log x \Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} {tex}=x^{\cos x}\left[\frac{\cos x}{x}-\sin x \log x\right], {/tex} {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=2^{\sin x} \cos x \log 2 {/tex}
{tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=x^{\cos x}\left[\frac{\cos x}{x}-\sin x \log x\right]{/tex}{tex}-2^{\sin x} \cos x \log 2 {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Find the value of a and b so that function f defined as:
{tex}f(x)= \begin{cases}\frac{x-2}{|x-2|}+a, & \text { if } x \lt 2 \\ a+b, & \text { if } x=2 \\ \frac{x-2}{|x-2|}+b, & \text { if } x \gt 2\end{cases}{/tex} is a continuous function.
question_text: Find the value of a and b so that function f defined as: {tex}f(x)= \begin{cases}\frac{x-2}{|x-2|}+a, & \text { if } x \lt 2 \\ a+b, & \text { if } x=2 \\ \frac{x-2}{|x-2|}+b, & \text { if } x \gt 2\end{cases}{/tex} is a continuous function.
explanation: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \frac{x-2}{-(x-2)}+a & ; x<2 \\ a+b & ; x=2 \\ \frac{x-2}{(x-2)}+b & ; x>2 \end{array}\right.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} -1+a & ; x<2 \\ a+b & ; x=2 \\ 1+b & ; x>2 \end{array}\right.{/tex} {tex} \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2^{-}} f(x)=-1+a, \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2^{+}} f(x){/tex} {tex}=1+b \text { and } f(2)=a+b {/tex} as f is continous at x = 2 {tex} \therefore-1+a=1+b=a+b{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow a=1, b=-1 {/tex}
solution: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \frac{x-2}{-(x-2)}+a & ; x<2 \\ a+b & ; x=2 \\ \frac{x-2}{(x-2)}+b & ; x>2 \end{array}\right.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} -1+a & ; x<2 \\ a+b & ; x=2 \\ 1+b & ; x>2 \end{array}\right.{/tex} {tex} \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2^{-}} f(x)=-1+a, \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2^{+}} f(x){/tex} {tex}=1+b \text { and } f(2)=a+b {/tex} as f is continous at x = 2 {tex} \therefore-1+a=1+b=a+b{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow a=1, b=-1 {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \frac{x-2}{-(x-2)}+a & ; x<2 \\ a+b & ; x=2 \\ \frac{x-2}{(x-2)}+b & ; x>2 \end{array}\right.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} -1+a & ; x<2 \\ a+b & ; x=2 \\ 1+b & ; x>2 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
{tex} \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2^{-}} f(x)=-1+a, \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 2^{+}} f(x){/tex} {tex}=1+b \text { and } f(2)=a+b {/tex}
as f is continous at x = 2
{tex} \therefore-1+a=1+b=a+b{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow a=1, b=-1 {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}x \cos (p+y)+\cos p \sin (p+y)=0{/tex}, prove that {tex}\cos p \frac{d y}{d x}=-\cos ^{2}(p+y){/tex}, where {tex}p{/tex} is a constant.
question_text: If {tex}x \cos (p+y)+\cos p \sin (p+y)=0{/tex} , prove that {tex}\cos p \frac{d y}{d x}=-\cos ^{2}(p+y){/tex} , where {tex}p{/tex} is a constant.
explanation: {tex} x \cos (p+y)+\cos p \sin (p+y)=0 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x=\frac{-\cos p \sin (p+y)}{\cos (p+y)} \Rightarrow x=-\cos p \cdot \tan (p+y) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}=-\cos p \cdot \sec ^2(p+y) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-1}{\cos p \cdot \sec ^2(p+y)} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cos p \frac{d y}{d x}=-\cos ^2(p+y) {/tex}
solution: {tex} x \cos (p+y)+\cos p \sin (p+y)=0 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x=\frac{-\cos p \sin (p+y)}{\cos (p+y)} \Rightarrow x=-\cos p \cdot \tan (p+y) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}=-\cos p \cdot \sec ^2(p+y) {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-1}{\cos p \cdot \sec ^2(p+y)} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \cos p \frac{d y}{d x}=-\cos ^2(p+y) {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} x \cos (p+y)+\cos p \sin (p+y)=0 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x=\frac{-\cos p \sin (p+y)}{\cos (p+y)} \Rightarrow x=-\cos p \cdot \tan (p+y) {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}=-\cos p \cdot \sec ^2(p+y) {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-1}{\cos p \cdot \sec ^2(p+y)} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \cos p \frac{d y}{d x}=-\cos ^2(p+y) {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Check the differentiability of {tex}{f}({x})=\left\{\begin{array}{cr}{x}^{2}+1, & 0 \leq {x} \lt 1 \\ 3-{x}, & 1 \leq {x} \leq 2\end{array}\right.{/tex} at {tex}{x}=1{/tex}.
question_text: Check the differentiability of {tex}{f}({x})=\left\{\begin{array}{cr}{x}^{2}+1, & 0 \leq {x} \lt 1 \\ 3-{x}, & 1 \leq {x} \leq 2\end{array}\right.{/tex} at {tex}{x}=1{/tex} .
explanation: LHD at {tex}x=1{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1-h)-f(1)}{-h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left[(1-h)^{2}+1\right]-2}{-h}=2{/tex} RHD at {tex}x=1{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1+h)-f(1)}{h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{[3-(1+h)]-2}{h}=-1{/tex} as {tex}{LHD} \neq {RHD}{/tex} , so {tex}f(x){/tex} is not differentiable at {tex}x=1{/tex}
solution: LHD at {tex}x=1{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1-h)-f(1)}{-h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left[(1-h)^{2}+1\right]-2}{-h}=2{/tex} RHD at {tex}x=1{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1+h)-f(1)}{h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{[3-(1+h)]-2}{h}=-1{/tex} as {tex}{LHD} \neq {RHD}{/tex} , so {tex}f(x){/tex} is not differentiable at {tex}x=1{/tex}
solution_html:
LHD at {tex}x=1{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1-h)-f(1)}{-h}{/tex}{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left[(1-h)^{2}+1\right]-2}{-h}=2{/tex}
RHD at {tex}x=1{/tex}
{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1+h)-f(1)}{h}{/tex}{tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{[3-(1+h)]-2}{h}=-1{/tex}
as {tex}{LHD} \neq {RHD}{/tex}, so {tex}f(x){/tex} is not differentiable at {tex}x=1{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}y=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}\right), 0 \lt x \lt 1{/tex}, then find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}y=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-x^{2}}{1+x^{2}}\right), 0 \lt x \lt 1{/tex} , then find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}y=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-x^2}{1+x^2}\right) {/tex} {tex}\text {, Put } x=\tan \theta \Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1} x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\cos ^{-1}(\cos 2 \theta)={/tex} {tex}2 \theta=2 \tan ^{-1} x, {/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2}{1+x^2}{/tex}
solution: {tex}y=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-x^2}{1+x^2}\right) {/tex} {tex}\text {, Put } x=\tan \theta \Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1} x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\cos ^{-1}(\cos 2 \theta)={/tex} {tex}2 \theta=2 \tan ^{-1} x, {/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2}{1+x^2}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}y=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-x^2}{1+x^2}\right) {/tex}{tex}\text {, Put } x=\tan \theta \Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1} x{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}x=a \sin ^{3} \theta, y=b \cos ^{3} \theta{/tex}, then find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} at {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}x=a \sin ^{3} \theta, y=b \cos ^{3} \theta{/tex} , then find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} at {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}=3 a \sin ^{2} \theta \cos \theta, \frac{d y}{d \theta}=-3 b \cos ^{2} \theta \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-3 b \cos ^{2} \theta \sin \theta}{3 a \sin ^{2} \theta \cos \theta}=-\frac{b}{a} \cot \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{b}{a} \operatorname{cosec}^{2} \theta \frac{d \theta}{d x}=\frac{b}{a} \operatorname{cosec}^{2} \theta \cdot \frac{1}{3 a \sin ^{2} \theta \cos \theta}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{b}{3 a^{2}} \sec \theta \operatorname{cosec}^{4} \theta{/tex} {tex}\left.\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right]_{\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}}=\frac{4 \sqrt{2} b}{3 a^{2}}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}=3 a \sin ^{2} \theta \cos \theta, \frac{d y}{d \theta}=-3 b \cos ^{2} \theta \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-3 b \cos ^{2} \theta \sin \theta}{3 a \sin ^{2} \theta \cos \theta}=-\frac{b}{a} \cot \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{b}{a} \operatorname{cosec}^{2} \theta \frac{d \theta}{d x}=\frac{b}{a} \operatorname{cosec}^{2} \theta \cdot \frac{1}{3 a \sin ^{2} \theta \cos \theta}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{b}{3 a^{2}} \sec \theta \operatorname{cosec}^{4} \theta{/tex} {tex}\left.\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right]_{\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}}=\frac{4 \sqrt{2} b}{3 a^{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}=3 a \sin ^{2} \theta \cos \theta, \frac{d y}{d \theta}=-3 b \cos ^{2} \theta \sin \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-3 b \cos ^{2} \theta \sin \theta}{3 a \sin ^{2} \theta \cos \theta}=-\frac{b}{a} \cot \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{b}{a} \operatorname{cosec}^{2} \theta \frac{d \theta}{d x}=\frac{b}{a} \operatorname{cosec}^{2} \theta \cdot \frac{1}{3 a \sin ^{2} \theta \cos \theta}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{b}{3 a^{2}} \sec \theta \operatorname{cosec}^{4} \theta{/tex}
{tex}\left.\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right]_{\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}}=\frac{4 \sqrt{2} b}{3 a^{2}}{/tex}
