question_html: Find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex}, if {tex}{y}=(\cos x)^{x}+\cos ^{-1} \sqrt{x}{/tex} is given.
question_text: Find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} , if {tex}{y}=(\cos x)^{x}+\cos ^{-1} \sqrt{x}{/tex} is given.
explanation: Let {tex}{u}=(\cos x)^{x} \Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\cos x)^{x}(-x \tan x+\log (\cos x)){/tex} , {tex}{v}=\cos ^{-1} \sqrt{x} \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-1}{2 \sqrt{x-x^{2}}}{/tex} Since, {tex}y=u+v \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}=(\cos x)^{x}(-x \tan x+\log (\cos x))+\frac{-1}{2 \sqrt{x-x^{2}}}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}{u}=(\cos x)^{x} \Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\cos x)^{x}(-x \tan x+\log (\cos x)){/tex} , {tex}{v}=\cos ^{-1} \sqrt{x} \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-1}{2 \sqrt{x-x^{2}}}{/tex} Since, {tex}y=u+v \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}=(\cos x)^{x}(-x \tan x+\log (\cos x))+\frac{-1}{2 \sqrt{x-x^{2}}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}{u}=(\cos x)^{x} \Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\cos x)^{x}(-x \tan x+\log (\cos x)){/tex},
{tex}{v}=\cos ^{-1} \sqrt{x} \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-1}{2 \sqrt{x-x^{2}}}{/tex}
Since, {tex}y=u+v \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}=(\cos x)^{x}(-x \tan x+\log (\cos x))+\frac{-1}{2 \sqrt{x-x^{2}}}{/tex}

question_html: Check for differentiability of the function f defined by {tex}{f}(x)=|x-5|{/tex}, at the point {tex}x=5{/tex}.
question_text: Check for differentiability of the function f defined by {tex}{f}(x)=|x-5|{/tex} , at the point {tex}x=5{/tex} .
explanation: LHD {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 5^{-}} \frac{\|x-5\|-0}{x-5}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 5^{-}} \frac{-(x-5)}{x-5}=-1{/tex} {tex}R H D=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 5^{+}} \frac{\|x-5\|-0}{x-5}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 5^{+}} \frac{(x-5)}{x-5}=1{/tex} LHD {tex}\neq{/tex} {tex}\boldsymbol{} {R H D ,} \therefore {f}{/tex} is not differentiable at {tex}{x}={5}{/tex}
solution: LHD {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 5^{-}} \frac{\|x-5\|-0}{x-5}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 5^{-}} \frac{-(x-5)}{x-5}=-1{/tex} {tex}R H D=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 5^{+}} \frac{\|x-5\|-0}{x-5}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 5^{+}} \frac{(x-5)}{x-5}=1{/tex} LHD {tex}\neq{/tex} {tex}\boldsymbol{} {R H D ,} \therefore {f}{/tex} is not differentiable at {tex}{x}={5}{/tex}
solution_html:
LHD {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 5^{-}} \frac{\|x-5\|-0}{x-5}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 5^{-}} \frac{-(x-5)}{x-5}=-1{/tex}
{tex}R H D=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 5^{+}} \frac{\|x-5\|-0}{x-5}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 5^{+}} \frac{(x-5)}{x-5}=1{/tex}
LHD {tex}\neq{/tex} {tex}\boldsymbol{} {R H D ,} \therefore {f}{/tex} is not differentiable at {tex}{x}={5}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex}, if {tex}(\cos x)^{{y}}=(\cos {y})^{x}{/tex}.
question_text: Find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} , if {tex}(\cos x)^{{y}}=(\cos {y})^{x}{/tex} .
explanation: Taking 'log' on both sides of {tex}(\cos {x})^{{y}}=(\cos {y})^{\boldsymbol{x}}{/tex} , we get {tex}{y} \log \cos {x}={x} \log \cos {y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x} \log \cos x+y(-\tan x){/tex} {tex}=\log \cos y+x(-\tan y) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}=\frac{\boldsymbol{\operatorname { l o g }} \cos {y}+{y} \tan {x}}{\boldsymbol{\operatorname { l o g }} \cos {x}+{x} \tan {y}}{/tex}
solution: Taking 'log' on both sides of {tex}(\cos {x})^{{y}}=(\cos {y})^{\boldsymbol{x}}{/tex} , we get {tex}{y} \log \cos {x}={x} \log \cos {y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x} \log \cos x+y(-\tan x){/tex} {tex}=\log \cos y+x(-\tan y) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}=\frac{\boldsymbol{\operatorname { l o g }} \cos {y}+{y} \tan {x}}{\boldsymbol{\operatorname { l o g }} \cos {x}+{x} \tan {y}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Taking 'log' on both sides of {tex}(\cos {x})^{{y}}=(\cos {y})^{\boldsymbol{x}}{/tex}, we get
{tex}{y} \log \cos {x}={x} \log \cos {y}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x} \log \cos x+y(-\tan x){/tex} {tex}=\log \cos y+x(-\tan y) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}=\frac{\boldsymbol{\operatorname { l o g }} \cos {y}+{y} \tan {x}}{\boldsymbol{\operatorname { l o g }} \cos {x}+{x} \tan {y}}{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}{y}=\left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)^{2}{/tex}, show that {tex}\left(x^{2}+1\right)^{2} \frac{{~d}^{2} {y}}{{d} x^{2}}+2 x\left(x^{2}+1\right) \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}=2{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}{y}=\left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)^{2}{/tex} , show that {tex}\left(x^{2}+1\right)^{2} \frac{{~d}^{2} {y}}{{d} x^{2}}+2 x\left(x^{2}+1\right) \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}=2{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}y=\left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)^{2} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 \tan ^{-1} x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=2 \tan ^{-1} x,{/tex} differentiating with respect to 'x' {tex}\Rightarrow\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+2 x\left(x^{2}+1\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=2{/tex}
solution: {tex}y=\left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)^{2} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 \tan ^{-1} x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=2 \tan ^{-1} x,{/tex} differentiating with respect to 'x' {tex}\Rightarrow\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+2 x\left(x^{2}+1\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=2{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}y=\left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)^{2} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 \tan ^{-1} x}{1+x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=2 \tan ^{-1} x,{/tex} differentiating with respect to 'x'
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(1+x^{2}\right)^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+2 x\left(x^{2}+1\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=2{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}, if {tex}y=(\cos x)^{x}+\cos ^{-1} \sqrt{x}{/tex}.
question_text: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} , if {tex}y=(\cos x)^{x}+\cos ^{-1} \sqrt{x}{/tex} .
explanation: Let {tex}y=u+v \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex} u=(\cos x)^x \Rightarrow \log u=x \log \cos x {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\cos x)^x[\log \cos x-x \tan x] {/tex} {tex} v=\cos ^{-1} \sqrt{x} \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x} \sqrt{1-x}} {/tex} {tex} \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=(\cos x)^x[\log \cos x-x \tan x]-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x} \sqrt{1-x}} {/tex}
solution: Let {tex}y=u+v \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex} u=(\cos x)^x \Rightarrow \log u=x \log \cos x {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\cos x)^x[\log \cos x-x \tan x] {/tex} {tex} v=\cos ^{-1} \sqrt{x} \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x} \sqrt{1-x}} {/tex} {tex} \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=(\cos x)^x[\log \cos x-x \tan x]-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x} \sqrt{1-x}} {/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}y=u+v \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex} u=(\cos x)^x \Rightarrow \log u=x \log \cos x {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\cos x)^x[\log \cos x-x \tan x] {/tex}
{tex} v=\cos ^{-1} \sqrt{x} \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x} \sqrt{1-x}} {/tex}
{tex} \therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=(\cos x)^x[\log \cos x-x \tan x]-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x} \sqrt{1-x}} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}x \sin (a+y)-\sin y=0{/tex}, prove that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sin ^{2}(a+y)}{\sin a}{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}x \sin (a+y)-\sin y=0{/tex} , prove that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sin ^{2}(a+y)}{\sin a}{/tex}
explanation: {tex} x=\frac{\sin y}{\sin (a+y)}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{\sin (a+y) \cos y-\sin y \cos (a+y)}{\sin ^2(a+y)} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{\sin a}{\sin ^2(a+y)}, {/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sin ^2(a+y)}{\sin a} {/tex}
solution: {tex} x=\frac{\sin y}{\sin (a+y)}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{\sin (a+y) \cos y-\sin y \cos (a+y)}{\sin ^2(a+y)} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{\sin a}{\sin ^2(a+y)}, {/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sin ^2(a+y)}{\sin a} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} x=\frac{\sin y}{\sin (a+y)}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{\sin (a+y) \cos y-\sin y \cos (a+y)}{\sin ^2(a+y)} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{\sin a}{\sin ^2(a+y)}, {/tex} 
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sin ^2(a+y)}{\sin a} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Given that {tex}{y}=(\sin x)^{x} \cdot x^{\sin x}+{a}^{x}{/tex}, find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{d} x}{/tex}.
question_text: Given that {tex}{y}=(\sin x)^{x} \cdot x^{\sin x}+{a}^{x}{/tex} , find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{d} x}{/tex} .
explanation: As, {tex}y=(\sin x)^{x} \cdot x^{\sin x}+a^{x}=u+a^{x} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d\left(a^{x}\right)}{d x}{/tex} where {tex}u=(\sin x)^{x} . x^{\sin x} \Rightarrow \log u{/tex} {tex}=x \log (\sin x)+\sin x . \log x{/tex} on differentiating both sides with respect to {tex}x{/tex} , we get {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\sin x)^{x} \cdot x^{\sin x}{/tex} {tex}\left[\log (\sin x)+x \cot x+\frac{\sin x}{x}+\log x \cdot \cos x\right]{/tex} Thus, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=(\sin x)^{x} \cdot x^{\sin x}{/tex} {tex}\left[\log (\sin x)+x \cot x+\frac{\sin x}{x}+\log x \cdot \cos x\right]{/tex} {tex}+a^{x} \log a{/tex}
solution: As, {tex}y=(\sin x)^{x} \cdot x^{\sin x}+a^{x}=u+a^{x} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d\left(a^{x}\right)}{d x}{/tex} where {tex}u=(\sin x)^{x} . x^{\sin x} \Rightarrow \log u{/tex} {tex}=x \log (\sin x)+\sin x . \log x{/tex} on differentiating both sides with respect to {tex}x{/tex} , we get {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\sin x)^{x} \cdot x^{\sin x}{/tex} {tex}\left[\log (\sin x)+x \cot x+\frac{\sin x}{x}+\log x \cdot \cos x\right]{/tex} Thus, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=(\sin x)^{x} \cdot x^{\sin x}{/tex} {tex}\left[\log (\sin x)+x \cot x+\frac{\sin x}{x}+\log x \cdot \cos x\right]{/tex} {tex}+a^{x} \log a{/tex}
solution_html:
As, {tex}y=(\sin x)^{x} \cdot x^{\sin x}+a^{x}=u+a^{x} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d\left(a^{x}\right)}{d x}{/tex}
where {tex}u=(\sin x)^{x} . x^{\sin x} \Rightarrow \log u{/tex} {tex}=x \log (\sin x)+\sin x . \log x{/tex}
on differentiating both sides with respect to {tex}x{/tex}, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\sin x)^{x} \cdot x^{\sin x}{/tex} {tex}\left[\log (\sin x)+x \cot x+\frac{\sin x}{x}+\log x \cdot \cos x\right]{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=(\sin x)^{x} \cdot x^{\sin x}{/tex} {tex}\left[\log (\sin x)+x \cot x+\frac{\sin x}{x}+\log x \cdot \cos x\right]{/tex} {tex}+a^{x} \log a{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}{y}^{{x}}={x}^{{y}}{/tex}, then find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}{y}^{{x}}={x}^{{y}}{/tex} , then find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}y^x=x^y \Rightarrow x \log y=y \log x{/tex} Differentiating with respect to ' {tex}x{/tex} ', {tex} \frac{x}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}+\log y=\frac{y}{x}+\frac{d y}{d x} \log x {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}\left(\frac{y-x \log y}{x-y \log x}\right) {/tex}
solution: {tex}y^x=x^y \Rightarrow x \log y=y \log x{/tex} Differentiating with respect to ' {tex}x{/tex} ', {tex} \frac{x}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}+\log y=\frac{y}{x}+\frac{d y}{d x} \log x {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}\left(\frac{y-x \log y}{x-y \log x}\right) {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}y^x=x^y \Rightarrow x \log y=y \log x{/tex}
Differentiating with respect to '{tex}x{/tex}',
{tex} \frac{x}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}+\log y=\frac{y}{x}+\frac{d y}{d x} \log x {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y}{x}\left(\frac{y-x \log y}{x-y \log x}\right) {/tex}

question_html: If {tex}y=\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)^{2}{/tex}, then find {tex}\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}y=\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)^{2}{/tex} , then find {tex}\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 \sin ^{-1} x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \Rightarrow\left(1-x^2\right)\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2=4 y{/tex} Differentiating with respect to ' {tex}x{/tex} ', we get {tex} \left(1-x^2\right) 2 \frac{d y}{d x} \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-2 x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2=4 \frac{d y}{d x} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left(1-x^2\right) \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-x \frac{d y}{d x}=2 {/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 \sin ^{-1} x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \Rightarrow\left(1-x^2\right)\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2=4 y{/tex} Differentiating with respect to ' {tex}x{/tex} ', we get {tex} \left(1-x^2\right) 2 \frac{d y}{d x} \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-2 x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2=4 \frac{d y}{d x} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left(1-x^2\right) \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-x \frac{d y}{d x}=2 {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 \sin ^{-1} x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} \Rightarrow\left(1-x^2\right)\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2=4 y{/tex}
Differentiating with respect to '{tex}x{/tex}', we get
{tex} \left(1-x^2\right) 2 \frac{d y}{d x} \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-2 x\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)^2=4 \frac{d y}{d x} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \left(1-x^2\right) \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-x \frac{d y}{d x}=2 {/tex}

question_html: Given that {tex}x^{y}+y^{x}=a^{b}{/tex}, where {tex}a{/tex} and {tex}b{/tex} are positive constants, find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}.
question_text: Given that {tex}x^{y}+y^{x}=a^{b}{/tex} , where {tex}a{/tex} and {tex}b{/tex} are positive constants, find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} .
explanation: Let {tex}{u}=x^{y} \Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=x^{y}\left(\frac{y}{x}+\log x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}\right), {/tex} {tex}{v}={y}^{x} \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=y^{x}\left(\frac{x}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}+\log y\right){/tex} Since, {tex}{u}+{v}=a^{b} \Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}=0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-\left(x^{y-1} y+y^{x} \log y\right)}{\left(x^{y} \log x+y^{x-1} x\right)}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}{u}=x^{y} \Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=x^{y}\left(\frac{y}{x}+\log x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}\right), {/tex} {tex}{v}={y}^{x} \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=y^{x}\left(\frac{x}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}+\log y\right){/tex} Since, {tex}{u}+{v}=a^{b} \Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}=0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-\left(x^{y-1} y+y^{x} \log y\right)}{\left(x^{y} \log x+y^{x-1} x\right)}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}{u}=x^{y} \Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=x^{y}\left(\frac{y}{x}+\log x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}\right), {/tex} {tex}{v}={y}^{x} \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=y^{x}\left(\frac{x}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}+\log y\right){/tex}
Since, {tex}{u}+{v}=a^{b} \Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}=0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-\left(x^{y-1} y+y^{x} \log y\right)}{\left(x^{y} \log x+y^{x-1} x\right)}{/tex}

question_html: If (x2 + y2)2 = xy, then find {tex}\frac {\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}{/tex}.
question_text: If (x 2 + y 2 ) 2 = xy, then find {tex}\frac {\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} .
explanation: Given, (x 2 + y 2 ) {tex}^2{/tex} = xy............(i) Therefore,on differentiating both sides of Eq.(i) w.r.t x, we get, 2(x 2 + y 2 ) {tex}\left[ 2 x + 2 y \frac { d y } { d x } \right] = x \frac { d y } { d x } + y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x(x 2 + y 2 ) + 4y(x 2 + y 2 ) {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = x \frac { d y } { d x } + y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4 y \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } - x \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = y - 4x(x 2 + y 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} [4y(x 2 + y 2 ) - x] = y - 4x(x 2 + y 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y - 4 x \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) } { 4 y \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) - x }{/tex}
solution: Given, (x 2 + y 2 ) {tex}^2{/tex} = xy............(i) Therefore,on differentiating both sides of Eq.(i) w.r.t x, we get, 2(x 2 + y 2 ) {tex}\left[ 2 x + 2 y \frac { d y } { d x } \right] = x \frac { d y } { d x } + y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4x(x 2 + y 2 ) + 4y(x 2 + y 2 ) {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = x \frac { d y } { d x } + y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4 y \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } - x \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = y - 4x(x 2 + y 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} [4y(x 2 + y 2 ) - x] = y - 4x(x 2 + y 2 ) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y - 4 x \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) } { 4 y \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) - x }{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, (x2 + y2){tex}^2{/tex} = xy............(i)
Therefore,on differentiating both sides of Eq.(i) w.r.t x, we get,
2(x2 + y2){tex}\left[ 2 x + 2 y \frac { d y } { d x } \right] = x \frac { d y } { d x } + y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}4x(x2 + y2) + 4y(x2 + y2){tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = x \frac { d y } { d x } + y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 4 y \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } - x \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = y - 4x(x2 + y2)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}[4y(x2 + y2) - x] = y - 4x(x2 + y2)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y - 4 x \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) } { 4 y \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } \right) - x }{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If the function f(x) given by f(x) ={tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { c l } { 3 a x + b , } & { \text { if } x > 1 } \\ { 11 , } & { \text { if } x = 1 } \\ { 5 a x - 2 b , } & { \text { if } x < 1 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 1, then find the values of a and b.
question_text: If the function f(x) given by f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { c l } { 3 a x + b , } & { \text { if } x > 1 } \\ { 11 , } & { \text { if } x = 1 } \\ { 5 a x - 2 b , } & { \text { if } x < 1 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 1, then find the values of a and b.
explanation: Given, f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { c l } { 3 a x + b , } & { \text { if } x > 1 } \\ { 11 , } & { \text { if } x = 1 } \\ { 5 a x - 2 b , } & { \text { if } x < 1 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} Since f(x) is continuos at x=1, therefore, LHL = RHL = f(1) ...(i) Now, LHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 1 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 1 ^ { - } } ( 5 a x - 2 b ){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ 5 a ( 1 - h ) - 2 b ]{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( 5 a - 5 a h - 2 b ){/tex} = 5a - 2b and RHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 1 ^ { + } } ( 3 a x + b ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ 3 a ( 1 + h ) + b ]{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( 3 a + 3 a h + b ) = 3 a + b{/tex} Also, given that f(1) = 11 On substituting these values in Eq. (i), we get 5a - 2b = 3a + b = 11 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3a + b = 11 ...(ii) and 5a - 2b = 11 ...(iii) On subtracting 3 {tex}\times{/tex} Eq. (iii) from 5 {tex}\times{/tex} Eq. (ii), we get 15a + 5b - 15a + 6b = 55 - 33 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 11b = 22 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 2 On putting the value of b in Eq. (ii), we get, 3a + 2 = 11 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3a = 9 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 3 Hence, a = 3 and b = 2
solution: Given, f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { c l } { 3 a x + b , } & { \text { if } x > 1 } \\ { 11 , } & { \text { if } x = 1 } \\ { 5 a x - 2 b , } & { \text { if } x < 1 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} Since f(x) is continuos at x=1, therefore, LHL = RHL = f(1) ...(i) Now, LHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 1 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 1 ^ { - } } ( 5 a x - 2 b ){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ 5 a ( 1 - h ) - 2 b ]{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( 5 a - 5 a h - 2 b ){/tex} = 5a - 2b and RHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 1 ^ { + } } ( 3 a x + b ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ 3 a ( 1 + h ) + b ]{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( 3 a + 3 a h + b ) = 3 a + b{/tex} Also, given that f(1) = 11 On substituting these values in Eq. (i), we get 5a - 2b = 3a + b = 11 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3a + b = 11 ...(ii) and 5a - 2b = 11 ...(iii) On subtracting 3 {tex}\times{/tex} Eq. (iii) from 5 {tex}\times{/tex} Eq. (ii), we get 15a + 5b - 15a + 6b = 55 - 33 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 11b = 22 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 2 On putting the value of b in Eq. (ii), we get, 3a + 2 = 11 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3a = 9 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 3 Hence, a = 3 and b = 2
solution_html:
Given, f(x) ={tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { c l } { 3 a x + b , } & { \text { if } x > 1 } \\ { 11 , } & { \text { if } x = 1 } \\ { 5 a x - 2 b , } & { \text { if } x < 1 } \end{array} \right.{/tex}

Since f(x) is continuos at x=1, therefore,
LHL = RHL = f(1) ...(i)
Now, LHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 1 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 1 ^ { - } } ( 5 a x - 2 b ){/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ 5 a ( 1 - h ) - 2 b ]{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( 5 a - 5 a h - 2 b ){/tex}= 5a - 2b
and RHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 1 ^ { + } } ( 3 a x + b ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ 3 a ( 1 + h ) + b ]{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( 3 a + 3 a h + b ) = 3 a + b{/tex}
Also, given that f(1) = 11
On substituting these values in Eq. (i), we get
5a - 2b = 3a + b = 11
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3a + b = 11 ...(ii)
and  5a - 2b = 11 ...(iii)
On subtracting 3 {tex}\times{/tex} Eq. (iii) from 5 {tex}\times{/tex} Eq. (ii), we get
15a + 5b - 15a + 6b = 55 - 33
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 11b = 22 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 2
On putting the value of b in Eq. (ii), we get,
3a + 2 = 11 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3a = 9 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 3
Hence, a = 3 and b = 2

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If y = tan x + sec x, prove that {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\cos x}{(1-\sin x)^{2}}{/tex}
question_text: If y = tan x + sec x, prove that {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\cos x}{(1-\sin x)^{2}}{/tex}
explanation: We have y = tan x + sec x. Differentiating w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\sec ^{2} x+\sec x \tan x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\cos ^{2} x}+\frac{\sin x}{\cos ^{2} x}=\frac{1+\sin x}{\cos ^{2} x}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1+\sin x}{(1+\sin x)(1-\sin x)}{/tex} Thus, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{1-\sin x}{/tex} Now, differentiating again w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{-(-\cos x)}{(1-\sin x)^{2}}=\frac{\cos x}{(1-\sin x)^{2}}{/tex}
solution: We have y = tan x + sec x. Differentiating w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\sec ^{2} x+\sec x \tan x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\cos ^{2} x}+\frac{\sin x}{\cos ^{2} x}=\frac{1+\sin x}{\cos ^{2} x}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1+\sin x}{(1+\sin x)(1-\sin x)}{/tex} Thus, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{1-\sin x}{/tex} Now, differentiating again w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{-(-\cos x)}{(1-\sin x)^{2}}=\frac{\cos x}{(1-\sin x)^{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have y = tan x + sec x. Differentiating w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\sec ^{2} x+\sec x \tan x{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{\cos ^{2} x}+\frac{\sin x}{\cos ^{2} x}=\frac{1+\sin x}{\cos ^{2} x}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{1+\sin x}{(1+\sin x)(1-\sin x)}{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{1-\sin x}{/tex}
Now, differentiating again w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{-(-\cos x)}{(1-\sin x)^{2}}=\frac{\cos x}{(1-\sin x)^{2}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{lll} \mathrm{a} x+\mathrm{b} & ; & 0<x \leq 1 \\ 2 x^2-x & ; & 1<x<2 \end{array}\right.{/tex} is a differentiable function in (0, 2), then find the values of a and b.
question_text: If f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{lll} \mathrm{a} x+\mathrm{b} & ; & 0<x \leq 1 \\ 2 x^2-x & ; & 1<x<2 \end{array}\right.{/tex} is a differentiable function in (0, 2), then find the values of a and b.
explanation: f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{lll} \mathrm{a} x+\mathrm{b} & ; & 0<x \leq 1 \\ 2 x^2-x & ; & 1<x<2 \end{array}\right.{/tex} LHD at x=1 = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 1^{-}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{f(x)-f(1)}{x-1}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}\frac{f(1-h)-f(1)}{1-h-1}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}\frac{[a(1-h)+b]-[a+b]}{-h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}\frac{[a-ah+b-a-b]}{-h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}\frac{[-ah]}{-h}{/tex} = a RHD at x=1 = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 1^{+}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{f(x)-f(1)}{x-1}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}\frac{f(1+h)-f(1)}{1+h-1}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}\frac{[2(1+h)^2+(1+h)]-[2-1]}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}\frac{[2(1+h^2+2h)-1-h]-1}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}\frac{[2+2h^2+4h-1-h-1]}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}\frac{[2h^2+3h]}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}{[2h+3]}{/tex} =3 Since f(x) is differentiable, so LHD=RHD a=3 Now LHL = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 1^{-}}f(x){/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}{f(1-h)}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}{a(1-h)+b}{/tex} = a+b Now, RHL = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 1^{-}}f(x){/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}{f(1+h)}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}{2(1+h)^2-(1+h)}{/tex} =2-1=1 f(x) is differentiable in (0, 2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) is continuous on (0, 2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) is continuous at x = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a + b = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 1-a b=1-3 b=-2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} a = 3 & b = -2
solution: f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{lll} \mathrm{a} x+\mathrm{b} & ; & 0<x \leq 1 \\ 2 x^2-x & ; & 1<x<2 \end{array}\right.{/tex} LHD at x=1 = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 1^{-}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{f(x)-f(1)}{x-1}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}\frac{f(1-h)-f(1)}{1-h-1}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}\frac{[a(1-h)+b]-[a+b]}{-h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}\frac{[a-ah+b-a-b]}{-h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}\frac{[-ah]}{-h}{/tex} = a RHD at x=1 = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 1^{+}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{f(x)-f(1)}{x-1}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}\frac{f(1+h)-f(1)}{1+h-1}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}\frac{[2(1+h)^2+(1+h)]-[2-1]}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}\frac{[2(1+h^2+2h)-1-h]-1}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}\frac{[2+2h^2+4h-1-h-1]}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}\frac{[2h^2+3h]}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}{[2h+3]}{/tex} =3 Since f(x) is differentiable, so LHD=RHD a=3 Now LHL = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 1^{-}}f(x){/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}{f(1-h)}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}{a(1-h)+b}{/tex} = a+b Now, RHL = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 1^{-}}f(x){/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}{f(1+h)}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}{2(1+h)^2-(1+h)}{/tex} =2-1=1 f(x) is differentiable in (0, 2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) is continuous on (0, 2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) is continuous at x = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a + b = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 1-a b=1-3 b=-2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} a = 3 & b = -2
solution_html:
f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{lll} \mathrm{a} x+\mathrm{b} & ; & 0<x \leq 1 \\ 2 x^2-x & ; & 1<x<2 \end{array}\right.{/tex}

LHD at x=1
={tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 1^{-}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{f(x)-f(1)}{x-1}{/tex}

= {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}\frac{f(1-h)-f(1)}{1-h-1}{/tex}
= {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}\frac{[a(1-h)+b]-[a+b]}{-h}{/tex}

= {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}\frac{[a-ah+b-a-b]}{-h}{/tex}
= {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}\frac{[-ah]}{-h}{/tex}
= a
RHD at x=1

={tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 1^{+}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{f(x)-f(1)}{x-1}{/tex}

= {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}\frac{f(1+h)-f(1)}{1+h-1}{/tex}
= {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}\frac{[2(1+h)^2+(1+h)]-[2-1]}{h}{/tex}

= {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}\frac{[2(1+h^2+2h)-1-h]-1}{h}{/tex}
={tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}\frac{[2+2h^2+4h-1-h-1]}{h}{/tex}
= {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}\frac{[2h^2+3h]}{h}{/tex}

={tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}{[2h+3]}{/tex}
=3

Since f(x) is differentiable, so LHD=RHD
a=3
Now LHL
= {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 1^{-}}f(x){/tex}
= {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}{f(1-h)}{/tex}
= {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}{a(1-h)+b}{/tex}

= a+b
Now, RHL
= {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 1^{-}}f(x){/tex}
= {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}{f(1+h)}{/tex}
= {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0}{2(1+h)^2-(1+h)}{/tex}
=2-1=1
f(x) is differentiable in (0, 2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) is continuous on (0, 2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) is continuous at x = 1
 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a + b = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = 1-a
b=1-3
b=-2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a = 3 & b = -2

question_html: Find the value(s) of {tex}\lambda{/tex}, if the function f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{cl} \frac{\sin ^2 \lambda x}{x^2}, & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\ 1, & \text { if } x=0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0.
question_text: Find the value(s) of {tex}\lambda{/tex} , if the function f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{cl} \frac{\sin ^2 \lambda x}{x^2}, & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\ 1, & \text { if } x=0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0.
explanation: f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{cl} \frac{\sin ^2 \lambda x}{x^2}, & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\ 1, & \text { if } x=0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} Given function is continuous, {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 0^-}{/tex} f(x) = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 0^+}{/tex} f(x) = f(0) {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{\sin ^2 \lambda \text x}{\text x^2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{\sin ^2 \lambda \text x}{(\lambda \text x)^2} \cdot \lambda^2\right]{/tex} = {tex}\lambda^2{/tex} Since f(x) is continuous at x = 0 {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 0}{/tex} f(x) = f(0) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda^2{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\pm 1{/tex}
solution: f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{cl} \frac{\sin ^2 \lambda x}{x^2}, & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\ 1, & \text { if } x=0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} Given function is continuous, {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 0^-}{/tex} f(x) = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 0^+}{/tex} f(x) = f(0) {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{\sin ^2 \lambda \text x}{\text x^2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{\sin ^2 \lambda \text x}{(\lambda \text x)^2} \cdot \lambda^2\right]{/tex} = {tex}\lambda^2{/tex} Since f(x) is continuous at x = 0 {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 0}{/tex} f(x) = f(0) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda^2{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\pm 1{/tex}
solution_html:
f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{cl} \frac{\sin ^2 \lambda x}{x^2}, & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\ 1, & \text { if } x=0 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
Given function is continuous,
{tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 0^-}{/tex} f(x) = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 0^+}{/tex} f(x) = f(0)
 {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{\sin ^2 \lambda \text x}{\text x^2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{\sin ^2 \lambda \text x}{(\lambda \text x)^2} \cdot \lambda^2\right]{/tex} = {tex}\lambda^2{/tex}
Since f(x) is continuous at x = 0
{tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 0}{/tex}f(x) = f(0)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda^2{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\pm 1{/tex}

question_text: If f(x) = {tex}\begin{cases}x^2, & \text { if } x \geq 1 \\ x, & \text { if } x<1\end{cases}{/tex} , then show that f is not differentiable at x = 1.
explanation: Here RHD = {tex}\lim\limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1+h)-\mathrm{f}(1)}{h}{/tex} = 2 LHD = {tex}\lim\limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{f(1-h)-f(1)}{-h}\right]{/tex} = 1 Since RHD {tex}\neq{/tex} LHD {tex}\therefore{/tex} f is not differentiable at x = 1.
solution: Here RHD = {tex}\lim\limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1+h)-\mathrm{f}(1)}{h}{/tex} = 2 LHD = {tex}\lim\limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{f(1-h)-f(1)}{-h}\right]{/tex} = 1 Since RHD {tex}\neq{/tex} LHD {tex}\therefore{/tex} f is not differentiable at x = 1.
solution_html:
Here
RHD = {tex}\lim\limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1+h)-\mathrm{f}(1)}{h}{/tex} = 2
LHD = {tex}\lim\limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{f(1-h)-f(1)}{-h}\right]{/tex} = 1
Since RHD {tex}\neq{/tex} LHD
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f is not differentiable at x = 1.

U = sec-1{tex}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\sin ^2 \theta}}\right){/tex} = sec-1{tex}\left(\frac{1}{\cos \theta}\right){/tex}
= sec-1 (sec {tex}\theta{/tex}) = {tex}\theta{/tex} = sin-1x

question_html:
Function f is defined as f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{ccc} 2 \text x+2, & \text { if } & \text x<2 \\ \text k, & \text { if } & \text x=2 \\ 3\text x, & \text { if } & \text x>2 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
Find the value of k for which the function f is continuous at x = 2.
question_text: Function f is defined as f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{ccc} 2 \text x+2, & \text { if } & \text x<2 \\ \text k, & \text { if } & \text x=2 \\ 3\text x, & \text { if } & \text x>2 \end{array}\right.{/tex} Find the value of k for which the function f is continuous at x = 2.
explanation: As f is continuous at x = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 2^{+}}{/tex} f(x) = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 2^{-}}{/tex} f(x) = f(2) {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 2^{+}}{/tex} 3x = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 2^{-}}{/tex} (2x + 2) = k {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 6
solution: As f is continuous at x = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 2^{+}}{/tex} f(x) = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 2^{-}}{/tex} f(x) = f(2) {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 2^{+}}{/tex} 3x = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 2^{-}}{/tex} (2x + 2) = k {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 6
solution_html:
As f is continuous at x = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 2^{+}}{/tex}f(x) = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 2^{-}}{/tex}f(x) = f(2)
{tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 2^{+}}{/tex}3x = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text x \rightarrow 2^{-}}{/tex}(2x + 2) = k
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 6

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Let f(x) be a real valued function. Then its

  • Left Hand Derivative (L.H.D.): Lf'(a) = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\text f(\text a-\text h)-\text f(\text a)}{-\text h}{/tex}
  • Right Hand Derivative (R.H.D.): Rf'(a) = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\text f(\text a+\text h)-\text f(\text a)}{\text h}{/tex}

Also, a function f(x) is said to be differentiable at x = a if its L.H.D. and R.H.D. at x = a exist and both are equal.
For the function f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{l} |\text x-3|, \text x \geq 1 \\ \frac{\text x^2}{4}-\frac{3 \text x}{2}+\frac{13}{4}, \text x<1 \end{array}\right.{/tex}

  1. What is R.H.D. of f(x) at x = 1? (1)
  2. What is L.H.D. of f(x) at x = 1? (1)
  3. Check if the function f(x) is differentiable at x = 1. (2)
    OR
    Find f'(2) and f'(-1). (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Let f(x) be a real valued function. Then its Left Hand Derivative (L.H.D.): Lf'(a) = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\text f(\text a-\text h)-\text f(\text a)}{-\text h}{/tex} Right Hand Derivative (R.H.D.): Rf'(a) = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\text f(\text a+\text h)-\text f(\text a)}{\text h}{/tex} Also, a function f(x) is said to be differentiable at x = a if its L.H.D. and R.H.D. at x = a exist and both are equal. For the function f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{l} |\text x-3|, \text x \geq 1 \\ \frac{\text x^2}{4}-\frac{3 \text x}{2}+\frac{13}{4}, \text x<1 \end{array}\right.{/tex} What is R.H.D. of f(x) at x = 1? (1) What is L.H.D. of f(x) at x = 1? (1) Check if the function f(x) is differentiable at x = 1. (2) OR Find f'(2) and f'(-1). (2)
explanation: R.H.D. of f(x) at x = 1 = {tex}\lim\limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1+h)-f(1)}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\boldsymbol{h} \rightarrow \mathbf{0}} \frac{|1+h-3|-|-2|}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\boldsymbol{h} \rightarrow \mathbf{0}} \frac{2-h-2}{h}{/tex} = -1 L.H.D. of f(x) at x = 1 = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1-h)-f(1)}{-h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left[\frac{(1-h)^2}{4}-\frac{3(1-h)}{2}+\frac{13}{4}-2\right]}{-h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{h^2-2 h+1-6+6 h+13-8}{-4 h}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{h^2+4 h}{-4 h}\right]{/tex} = -1. Since L.H.D. of f(x) at x = 1 is same as R.H.D. of f(x) at x = 1, f(x) is differentiable at x = 1. OR f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{l} \text x-3, \text x \geq 3 \\ 3-\text x, 1 \leq \text x<3 \\ \frac{\text x^2}{4}-\frac{3 \text x}{2}+\frac{13}{4}, \mathrm{x}<1 \end{array}\right.{/tex} [f'(x)] x=2 = 0 - 1 = -1 [f'(x)] x=–1 = {tex}\frac{2(-1)}{4}-\frac{3}{2}{/tex} = -2
solution: R.H.D. of f(x) at x = 1 = {tex}\lim\limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1+h)-f(1)}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\boldsymbol{h} \rightarrow \mathbf{0}} \frac{|1+h-3|-|-2|}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\boldsymbol{h} \rightarrow \mathbf{0}} \frac{2-h-2}{h}{/tex} = -1 L.H.D. of f(x) at x = 1 = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1-h)-f(1)}{-h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left[\frac{(1-h)^2}{4}-\frac{3(1-h)}{2}+\frac{13}{4}-2\right]}{-h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{h^2-2 h+1-6+6 h+13-8}{-4 h}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{h^2+4 h}{-4 h}\right]{/tex} = -1. Since L.H.D. of f(x) at x = 1 is same as R.H.D. of f(x) at x = 1, f(x) is differentiable at x = 1. OR f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{l} \text x-3, \text x \geq 3 \\ 3-\text x, 1 \leq \text x<3 \\ \frac{\text x^2}{4}-\frac{3 \text x}{2}+\frac{13}{4}, \mathrm{x}<1 \end{array}\right.{/tex} [f'(x)] x=2 = 0 - 1 = -1 [f'(x)] x=–1 = {tex}\frac{2(-1)}{4}-\frac{3}{2}{/tex} = -2
solution_html:
1. R.H.D. of f(x) at x = 1 = {tex}\lim\limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1+h)-f(1)}{h}{/tex}
    = {tex}\lim\limits_{\boldsymbol{h} \rightarrow \mathbf{0}} \frac{|1+h-3|-|-2|}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\lim\limits_{\boldsymbol{h} \rightarrow \mathbf{0}} \frac{2-h-2}{h}{/tex} = -1

  2. L.H.D. of f(x) at x = 1 = {tex}\lim\limits_{\text h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(1-h)-f(1)}{-h}{/tex}
    = {tex}\lim\limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left[\frac{(1-h)^2}{4}-\frac{3(1-h)}{2}+\frac{13}{4}-2\right]}{-h}{/tex}
    = {tex}\lim\limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{h^2-2 h+1-6+6 h+13-8}{-4 h}\right]{/tex}
    = {tex}\lim\limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{h^2+4 h}{-4 h}\right]{/tex} = -1.

  3. Since L.H.D. of f(x) at x = 1
    is same as R.H.D. of f(x) at x = 1,
    f(x) is differentiable at x = 1.
    OR
    f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{l} \text x-3, \text x \geq 3 \\ 3-\text x, 1 \leq \text x<3 \\ \frac{\text x^2}{4}-\frac{3 \text x}{2}+\frac{13}{4}, \mathrm{x}<1 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
    [f'(x)]x=2 = 0 - 1 = -1
    [f'(x)]x=–1 = {tex}\frac{2(-1)}{4}-\frac{3}{2}{/tex} = -2

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} x}[\mathrm{~F}(x)]=\frac{\sec ^4 x}{\operatorname{cosec}^4 x}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{F}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}, then find F(x).
question_text: If {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} x}[\mathrm{~F}(x)]=\frac{\sec ^4 x}{\operatorname{cosec}^4 x}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{F}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} , then find F(x).
explanation: F(x) = {tex}\int \tan ^4 x d x=\int \tan ^2 x \cdot\left(\sec ^2 x-1\right) d x{/tex} {tex}=\int\left(\tan ^2 x \sec ^2 x-\sec ^2 x+1\right) d x{/tex} F(x) = {tex}\frac{\tan ^3 x}{3}-\tan x+x+C{/tex} x = {tex}\frac {\pi}{4}{/tex} , F(x) = {tex}\frac {\pi}{4}{/tex} gives C = {tex}\frac {2}{3}{/tex} F(x) = {tex}\frac{\tan ^3 x}{3}-\tan x+x+\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
solution: F(x) = {tex}\int \tan ^4 x d x=\int \tan ^2 x \cdot\left(\sec ^2 x-1\right) d x{/tex} {tex}=\int\left(\tan ^2 x \sec ^2 x-\sec ^2 x+1\right) d x{/tex} F(x) = {tex}\frac{\tan ^3 x}{3}-\tan x+x+C{/tex} x = {tex}\frac {\pi}{4}{/tex} , F(x) = {tex}\frac {\pi}{4}{/tex} gives C = {tex}\frac {2}{3}{/tex} F(x) = {tex}\frac{\tan ^3 x}{3}-\tan x+x+\frac{2}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
F(x) = {tex}\int \tan ^4 x d x=\int \tan ^2 x \cdot\left(\sec ^2 x-1\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int\left(\tan ^2 x \sec ^2 x-\sec ^2 x+1\right) d x{/tex}
F(x) = {tex}\frac{\tan ^3 x}{3}-\tan x+x+C{/tex}
x = {tex}\frac {\pi}{4}{/tex}, F(x) = {tex}\frac {\pi}{4}{/tex} gives C = {tex}\frac {2}{3}{/tex}
F(x) = {tex}\frac{\tan ^3 x}{3}-\tan x+x+\frac{2}{3}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Differentiate sin2 x w.r.t. ecos x.
question_text: Differentiate sin 2 x w.r.t. e cos x .
explanation: Let u (x) = sin 2 x and v (x) = e cos x . We want to find {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{d u / d x}{d v / d x}{/tex} . Clearly {tex}\frac{d u}{d x} {/tex} = 2 sin x cos x and {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = e cos x (– sin x) = – (sin x) e cos x {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{2 \sin x \cos x}{-\sin x e^{\cos x}}=-\frac{2 \cos x}{e^{\cos x}}{/tex}
solution: Let u (x) = sin 2 x and v (x) = e cos x . We want to find {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{d u / d x}{d v / d x}{/tex} . Clearly {tex}\frac{d u}{d x} {/tex} = 2 sin x cos x and {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = e cos x (– sin x) = – (sin x) e cos x {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{2 \sin x \cos x}{-\sin x e^{\cos x}}=-\frac{2 \cos x}{e^{\cos x}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let u (x) = sin2 x and v (x) = ecos x . We want to find {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{d u / d x}{d v / d x}{/tex}.
Clearly {tex}\frac{d u}{d x} {/tex} = 2 sin x cos x
and      {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = ecos x (– sin x) = – (sin x) ecos x
{tex}\therefore{/tex}         {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{2 \sin x \cos x}{-\sin x e^{\cos x}}=-\frac{2 \cos x}{e^{\cos x}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the relationship between a and b so that the function f defined by {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{lll} a x+1 & \text { if } & x \leq 3 \\ b x+3 & \text { if } & x>3 \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 3.
question_text: Find the relationship between a and b so that the function f defined by {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{lll} a x+1 & \text { if } & x \leq 3 \\ b x+3 & \text { if } & x>3 \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 3.
explanation: f is continuous at x = 3 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { - } } (a x +1) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { +} } ( b x + 1 ){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 3a + 1 = 3b + 1 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 3a - 3b = 2 {tex}\left(\text { or } a-b=\frac{2}{3}\right){/tex}
solution: f is continuous at x = 3 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { - } } (a x +1) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { +} } ( b x + 1 ){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 3a + 1 = 3b + 1 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 3a - 3b = 2 {tex}\left(\text { or } a-b=\frac{2}{3}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
f is continuous at x = 3 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex}{tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { - } } (a x +1) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { +} } ( b x + 1 ){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 3a + 1 = 3b + 1
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 3a - 3b = 2{tex}\left(\text { or } a-b=\frac{2}{3}\right){/tex}

question_html: If ey - x = yx, prove that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y(1+\log y)}{x \log y}{/tex}
question_text: If e y - x = y x , prove that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y(1+\log y)}{x \log y}{/tex}
explanation: Taking log on both side of e y-x = y x y - x = x log y...(i) Differentiate with respect to x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-1{/tex} = log y + {tex}\frac{x}{y}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y(1+\log y)}{y-x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y(1+\log y)}{x \log y}{/tex} ... using (i)
solution: Taking log on both side of e y-x = y x y - x = x log y...(i) Differentiate with respect to x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-1{/tex} = log y + {tex}\frac{x}{y}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y(1+\log y)}{y-x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y(1+\log y)}{x \log y}{/tex} ... using (i)
solution_html:
Taking log on both side of ey-x = yx
y - x = x log y...(i)
Differentiate with respect to x
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}-1{/tex} = log y + {tex}\frac{x}{y}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y(1+\log y)}{y-x}{/tex}
 = {tex}\frac{y(1+\log y)}{x \log y}{/tex}... using (i)

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Check the differentiability of f(x) = |x - 3| at x = 3.
question_text: Check the differentiability of f(x) = |x - 3| at x = 3.
explanation: The given function is f(x) = |x - 3| {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = x - 3 if x {tex}\geqslant{/tex} 3 . = 3 - x if x < 3 Checking the differentiability at x = 3 At x = 3 L.H.D = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3} \frac{f(x)-f(3)}{x-3}{/tex} = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3} \frac{(3-x)-0}{x-3}{/tex} = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3}(-1){/tex} = (-1). At the same point RHD = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})-\mathrm{f}(3)}{\mathrm{x}-3}{/tex} = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3} \frac{(x-3)-0}{x-3}{/tex} = 1 So at x = 3, LHD {tex}\neq{/tex} RHD. Hence, f(x) = |x - 3| at x = 3 is not differentiable.
solution: The given function is f(x) = |x - 3| {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = x - 3 if x {tex}\geqslant{/tex} 3 . = 3 - x if x < 3 Checking the differentiability at x = 3 At x = 3 L.H.D = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3} \frac{f(x)-f(3)}{x-3}{/tex} = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3} \frac{(3-x)-0}{x-3}{/tex} = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3}(-1){/tex} = (-1). At the same point RHD = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})-\mathrm{f}(3)}{\mathrm{x}-3}{/tex} = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3} \frac{(x-3)-0}{x-3}{/tex} = 1 So at x = 3, LHD {tex}\neq{/tex} RHD. Hence, f(x) = |x - 3| at x = 3 is not differentiable.
solution_html:
The given function is f(x) = |x - 3|
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(x) = x - 3 if x {tex}\geqslant{/tex} 3 .
= 3 - x if x < 3
Checking the differentiability at x = 3
At x = 3 L.H.D = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3} \frac{f(x)-f(3)}{x-3}{/tex}
= {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3} \frac{(3-x)-0}{x-3}{/tex}
= {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3}(-1){/tex}
= (-1).
At the same point RHD = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})-\mathrm{f}(3)}{\mathrm{x}-3}{/tex}
= {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3} \frac{(x-3)-0}{x-3}{/tex}
= 1
So at x = 3, LHD {tex}\neq{/tex} RHD.
Hence, f(x) = |x - 3| at x = 3 is not differentiable.

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
If x = a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + b sin {tex}\theta{/tex}, y = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - b cos {tex}\theta{/tex}, then show that {tex}\frac {dy} {dx} = -\frac {x} {y}{/tex} and hence show that
{tex}y^2 \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-x \frac{d y}{d x}+y{/tex} = 0.
question_text: If x = a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + b sin {tex}\theta{/tex} , y = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - b cos {tex}\theta{/tex} , then show that {tex}\frac {dy} {dx} = -\frac {x} {y}{/tex} and hence show that {tex}y^2 \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-x \frac{d y}{d x}+y{/tex} = 0.
explanation: x = a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + b sin {tex}\theta{/tex} , y = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - b cos {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}{/tex} = -a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} + b sin {tex}\theta{/tex} , {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}{/tex} = a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{d y}{d \theta}}{\frac{d x}{ d \theta}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{a \cos \theta+b \sin \theta}{-a \sin \theta+b \cos \theta}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{x}{y}{/tex} Differentiate {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{x}{y}{/tex} with respect to 'x' {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1 . y-x \frac{d y}{d x}}{y^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y 2 {tex}\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}{/tex} = -y + x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y 2 {tex}\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}{/tex} - x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y = 0
solution: x = a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + b sin {tex}\theta{/tex} , y = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - b cos {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}{/tex} = -a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} + b sin {tex}\theta{/tex} , {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}{/tex} = a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{d y}{d \theta}}{\frac{d x}{ d \theta}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{a \cos \theta+b \sin \theta}{-a \sin \theta+b \cos \theta}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{x}{y}{/tex} Differentiate {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{x}{y}{/tex} with respect to 'x' {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1 . y-x \frac{d y}{d x}}{y^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y 2 {tex}\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}{/tex} = -y + x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y 2 {tex}\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}{/tex} - x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y = 0
solution_html:
x = a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + b sin {tex}\theta{/tex}, y = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - b cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}{/tex} = -a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} + b sin {tex}\theta{/tex}, {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}{/tex} = a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + sin {tex}\theta{/tex}

{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{d y}{d \theta}}{\frac{d x}{ d \theta}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{a \cos \theta+b \sin \theta}{-a \sin \theta+b \cos \theta}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{x}{y}{/tex}
Differentiate {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{x}{y}{/tex} with respect to 'x'
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1 . y-x \frac{d y}{d x}}{y^2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y2 {tex}\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}{/tex} = -y + x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y2 {tex}\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}{/tex} - x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + y = 0

question_html: If sin y = x cos (a + y), then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} is equal to
question_text: If sin y = x cos (a + y), then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} is equal to

options.explanation: sin y = x cos(a + y) {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{\sin y}{\cos (a+y)}{/tex} Differentiating with respect to x, {tex}\cos y \frac{d y}{d x}=-x \sin (a+y) \frac{d y}{d x}+\cos (a+y){/tex} {tex}\cos y \frac{d y}{d x}+x \sin (a+y) \frac{d y}{d x}=\cos (a+y){/tex} {tex}[\cos y+x \sin (a+y)] \frac{d y}{d x}=\cos (a+y){/tex} {tex}\left[\cos y+\frac{\sin y}{\cos (a+y)} \sin (a+y)\right] \frac{d y}{d x}=\cos (a+y){/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{\cos (a+y) \cos y+\sin y \sin (a+y)}{\cos (a+y)}\right] \frac{d y}{d x}=\cos (a+y){/tex} {tex}\frac{\cos a}{\cos (a+y)} \frac{d y}{d x}=\cos (a+y){/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\cos ^{2}(a+y)}{\cos a}{/tex}

explanation: sin y = x cos(a + y) {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{\sin y}{\cos (a+y)}{/tex} Differentiating with respect to x, {tex}\cos y \frac{d y}{d x}=-x \sin (a+y) \frac{d y}{d x}+\cos (a+y){/tex} {tex}\cos y \frac{d y}{d x}+x \sin (a+y) \frac{d y}{d x}=\cos (a+y){/tex} {tex}[\cos y+x \sin (a+y)] \frac{d y}{d x}=\cos (a+y){/tex} {tex}\left[\cos y+\frac{\sin y}{\cos (a+y)} \sin (a+y)\right] \frac{d y}{d x}=\cos (a+y){/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{\cos (a+y) \cos y+\sin y \sin (a+y)}{\cos (a+y)}\right] \frac{d y}{d x}=\cos (a+y){/tex} {tex}\frac{\cos a}{\cos (a+y)} \frac{d y}{d x}=\cos (a+y){/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\cos ^{2}(a+y)}{\cos a}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: The function {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \frac{e^{3 x}-e^{-5 x}}{x}, & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\ k & \text { if } x=0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0 for the value of k, as:
question_text: The function {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \frac{e^{3 x}-e^{-5 x}}{x}, & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\ k & \text { if } x=0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0 for the value of k, as:

options.explanation: Since, f(x) is continuous at x = 0, then LHL = RHL = f(0) or LHL = RHL = k Now, {tex}\mathrm{LHL}=\lim\limits _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^{3(0-h)}-e^{-5(0-h)}}{0-h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim\limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^{-3 h}-e^{5 h}}{-h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{e^{-3 h}-1}{-h}\right)+\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{e^{5 h}-1}{h}\right){/tex} {tex}=3 \lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{e^{-3 k}-1}{-3 h}\right)+5 \lim\limits _{h \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{e^{5 h}-1}{5 h}\right){/tex} = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 8 Thus, k = 8

explanation: Since, f(x) is continuous at x = 0, then LHL = RHL = f(0) or LHL = RHL = k Now, {tex}\mathrm{LHL}=\lim\limits _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^{3(0-h)}-e^{-5(0-h)}}{0-h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim\limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^{-3 h}-e^{5 h}}{-h}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{e^{-3 h}-1}{-h}\right)+\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{e^{5 h}-1}{h}\right){/tex} {tex}=3 \lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{e^{-3 k}-1}{-3 h}\right)+5 \lim\limits _{h \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{e^{5 h}-1}{5 h}\right){/tex} = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 8 Thus, k = 8

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Differential of log[log(log x5)] w.r.t x is:
question_text: Differential of log[log(log x 5 )] w.r.t x is:

options.explanation: Let y = log[log(log x 5 )] {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{\log \left(\log x^5\right)} \frac{d y}{d x}\left[\log \left(\log x^5\right)\right]{/tex} (By Chain Rule) {tex}=\frac{1}{\log \left(\log x^5\right)} \cdot \frac{1}{\log x^5} \frac{d}{d x} \log x^5{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\log \left(x^5\right) \log \left(\log x^5\right)} \frac{1}{x^5} \frac{d}{d x}\left(x^5\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{5}{x \log \left(x^5\right) \log \left(\log x^5\right)}{/tex}

explanation: Let y = log[log(log x 5 )] {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{\log \left(\log x^5\right)} \frac{d y}{d x}\left[\log \left(\log x^5\right)\right]{/tex} (By Chain Rule) {tex}=\frac{1}{\log \left(\log x^5\right)} \cdot \frac{1}{\log x^5} \frac{d}{d x} \log x^5{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\log \left(x^5\right) \log \left(\log x^5\right)} \frac{1}{x^5} \frac{d}{d x}\left(x^5\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{5}{x \log \left(x^5\right) \log \left(\log x^5\right)}{/tex}

question_html: If (x2 + y2)2 = xy, then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} is:
question_text: If (x 2 + y 2 ) 2 = xy, then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} is:

options.explanation: Given, (x 2 + y 2 ) 2 = xy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 4 + 2x 2 y 2 + y 4 - xy = 0 Differentiating w.r.t. x, we get {tex}4 x^3+2\left[2 x y^2+2 x^2 y \frac{d y}{d x}\right]{/tex} {tex}+4 y^3 \frac{d y}{d x}-\left[y+x \frac{d y}{d x}\right]=0{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}\left[4 x^2 y+4 y^3-x\right]{/tex} {tex}+\left[4 x^3+4 x y^2-y\right]=0{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-\left[4 x^3+4 x y^2-y\right]}{\left[4 x^2 y+4 y^3-x\right]}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y-4 x\left(x^2+y^2\right)}{4 y\left(x^2+y^2\right)-x}{/tex}

explanation: Given, (x 2 + y 2 ) 2 = xy {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 4 + 2x 2 y 2 + y 4 - xy = 0 Differentiating w.r.t. x, we get {tex}4 x^3+2\left[2 x y^2+2 x^2 y \frac{d y}{d x}\right]{/tex} {tex}+4 y^3 \frac{d y}{d x}-\left[y+x \frac{d y}{d x}\right]=0{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}\left[4 x^2 y+4 y^3-x\right]{/tex} {tex}+\left[4 x^3+4 x y^2-y\right]=0{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{-\left[4 x^3+4 x y^2-y\right]}{\left[4 x^2 y+4 y^3-x\right]}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y-4 x\left(x^2+y^2\right)}{4 y\left(x^2+y^2\right)-x}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If y = sin(m sin-1x), then which one of the following equations is true?
question_text: If y = sin(m sin -1 x), then which one of the following equations is true?

options.html: {tex}\left(1-x^2\right) \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+x \frac{d y}{d x}+m^2 y=0{/tex}
{tex}\left(1-x^2\right) \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-x \frac{d y}{d x}+m^2 y=0{/tex}
options.explanation: Given, y = sin(m(sin -1 x)) ...(i) Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\cos \left(m \sin ^{-1} x\right) \times \frac{m}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{m \cos \left(m \sin ^{-1} x\right)}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\Rightarrow y^{\prime}=\frac{m \cos \left(m \sin ^{-1} x\right)}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{/tex} ...(iii) {tex}\Rightarrow\left(\sqrt{1-x^2}\right) y^{\prime}=m \cos \left(m \sin ^{-1} x\right){/tex} Differentiating again w.r.t. 'x', we get {tex}y^{\prime \prime}\left(\sqrt{1-x^2}\right)+y^{\prime} \frac{(-2 x)}{2 \sqrt{1-x^2}}{/tex} {tex}=-m^2 \sin \left(m \sin ^{-1} x\right) \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y"(1 - x 2 ) - xy' = -m 2 y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y"(1 - x 2 ) - xy' + m 2 y = 0 or {tex}\left(1-x^2\right) \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-x \frac{d y}{d x}+m^2 y=0{/tex}

options.html: {tex}\left(1+x^2\right) \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-x \frac{d y}{d x}-m^2 y=0{/tex}
{tex}\left(1+x^2\right) \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+x \frac{d y}{d x}-m^2 y=0{/tex}

explanation: Given, y = sin(m(sin -1 x)) ...(i) Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\cos \left(m \sin ^{-1} x\right) \times \frac{m}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{m \cos \left(m \sin ^{-1} x\right)}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\Rightarrow y^{\prime}=\frac{m \cos \left(m \sin ^{-1} x\right)}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{/tex} ...(iii) {tex}\Rightarrow\left(\sqrt{1-x^2}\right) y^{\prime}=m \cos \left(m \sin ^{-1} x\right){/tex} Differentiating again w.r.t. 'x', we get {tex}y^{\prime \prime}\left(\sqrt{1-x^2}\right)+y^{\prime} \frac{(-2 x)}{2 \sqrt{1-x^2}}{/tex} {tex}=-m^2 \sin \left(m \sin ^{-1} x\right) \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y"(1 - x 2 ) - xy' = -m 2 y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y"(1 - x 2 ) - xy' + m 2 y = 0 or {tex}\left(1-x^2\right) \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-x \frac{d y}{d x}+m^2 y=0{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If x = 2 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} - cos 2{tex}\theta{/tex} and y = 2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - sin 2{tex}\theta{/tex}, then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} is:
question_text: If x = 2 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} - cos 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} and y = 2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - sin 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} , then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} is:

options.explanation: Given, x = 2 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} - cos 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} and y = 2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - sin 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}=-2 \sin \theta+2 \sin 2 \theta{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}=2 \cos \theta-2 \cos 2 \theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 \cos \theta-2 \cos 2 \theta}{-2 \sin \theta+2 \sin 2 \theta}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\cos \theta-\cos 2 \theta}{\sin 2 \theta-\sin \theta}{/tex}

explanation: Given, x = 2 cos {tex}\theta{/tex} - cos 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} and y = 2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - sin 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}=-2 \sin \theta+2 \sin 2 \theta{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}=2 \cos \theta-2 \cos 2 \theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 \cos \theta-2 \cos 2 \theta}{-2 \sin \theta+2 \sin 2 \theta}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\cos \theta-\cos 2 \theta}{\sin 2 \theta-\sin \theta}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}y = {e^{a{{\cos }^{ - 1}}x}}{/tex} Show that {tex}\left( {1 - {x^2}} \right)\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} - x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - {a^2}y = 0{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}y = {e^{a{{\cos }^{ - 1}}x}}{/tex} Show that {tex}\left( {1 - {x^2}} \right)\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} - x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - {a^2}y = 0{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}y = {e^{a{{\cos }^{ - 1}}x}}{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - a.{e^{a{{\cos }^{ - 1}}x}}.\frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }}{/tex}

{tex}\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - {e^{a{{\cos }^{ - 1}}x}}.a{/tex}

{tex}\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} \frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} + \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} \times \frac{1}{{2\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} \times \left( { - 2x} \right) = - a{e^{a{{\cos }^{ - 1}}x}} \times \frac{{ - a}}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }}{/tex}

{tex}\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} \frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} - \frac{x}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{a^2}{e^{a{{\cos }^{ - 1}}x}}}}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }}{/tex}

{tex}\left( {1 - {x^2}} \right)\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} - x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - {a^2}y = 0{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} when y = xlog x + (log x)x
question_text: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} when y = x log x + (log x) x
explanation: Let y = x logx + (log x) x Also, let u = (log x) x and v = x log x {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = v + u {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d v}{d x}+\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} ...(i) Now, u = (log x) x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = log[(log x) x ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = x log(log x) Differentiating both sides with respect to x, {tex}\frac{1}{u} \frac{d u}{d x}=\log (\log x) \frac{d}{d x}(x)+x \frac{d}{d x}[\log (\log x)]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=u\left[\log (\log x)+x \frac{1}{\log x} \frac{d}{d x}(\log x)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\log x)^{x}\left[\log (\log x)+\frac{x}{\log x} \times \frac{1}{x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\log x)^{x}\left[\log (\log x)+\frac{1}{\log x}\right]{/tex} ...(ii) Also, v = x log x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log v = log x log x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log v = log x log x = (log x) 2 Differentiating both sides with respect to x, {tex}\frac{1}{v} \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left[(\log x)^{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{v} \frac{d v}{d x}=2(\log x) \frac{d}{d x}(\log x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=2 v(\log x) \frac{1}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=2 x^{\log x} \frac{\log x}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=2 x^{\log x} \frac{\log x}{x}{/tex} ....(iii) From (i), (ii) and (iii), we obtain {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=2 x^{\log x} \frac{\log x}{x}+(\log x)^{x}\left[\log (\log x)+\frac{1}{\log x}\right]{/tex}
solution: Let y = x logx + (log x) x Also, let u = (log x) x and v = x log x {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = v + u {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d v}{d x}+\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} ...(i) Now, u = (log x) x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = log[(log x) x ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = x log(log x) Differentiating both sides with respect to x, {tex}\frac{1}{u} \frac{d u}{d x}=\log (\log x) \frac{d}{d x}(x)+x \frac{d}{d x}[\log (\log x)]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=u\left[\log (\log x)+x \frac{1}{\log x} \frac{d}{d x}(\log x)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\log x)^{x}\left[\log (\log x)+\frac{x}{\log x} \times \frac{1}{x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\log x)^{x}\left[\log (\log x)+\frac{1}{\log x}\right]{/tex} ...(ii) Also, v = x log x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log v = log x log x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log v = log x log x = (log x) 2 Differentiating both sides with respect to x, {tex}\frac{1}{v} \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left[(\log x)^{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{v} \frac{d v}{d x}=2(\log x) \frac{d}{d x}(\log x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=2 v(\log x) \frac{1}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=2 x^{\log x} \frac{\log x}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=2 x^{\log x} \frac{\log x}{x}{/tex} ....(iii) From (i), (ii) and (iii), we obtain {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=2 x^{\log x} \frac{\log x}{x}+(\log x)^{x}\left[\log (\log x)+\frac{1}{\log x}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Let y = xlogx + (log x)x
Also, let u = (log x)x and v = xlog x
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y = v + u
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d v}{d x}+\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} ...(i)
Now, u = (log x)x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = log[(log x)x]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = x log(log x)
Differentiating both sides with respect to x,
{tex}\frac{1}{u} \frac{d u}{d x}=\log (\log x) \frac{d}{d x}(x)+x \frac{d}{d x}[\log (\log x)]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=u\left[\log (\log x)+x \frac{1}{\log x} \frac{d}{d x}(\log x)\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\log x)^{x}\left[\log (\log x)+\frac{x}{\log x} \times \frac{1}{x}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\log x)^{x}\left[\log (\log x)+\frac{1}{\log x}\right]{/tex} ...(ii)
Also, v = xlog x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log v = log xlog x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log v = log x log x = (log x)2
Differentiating both sides with respect to x,
{tex}\frac{1}{v} \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left[(\log x)^{2}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{v} \frac{d v}{d x}=2(\log x) \frac{d}{d x}(\log x){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=2 v(\log x) \frac{1}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=2 x^{\log x} \frac{\log x}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=2 x^{\log x} \frac{\log x}{x}{/tex} ....(iii)
From (i), (ii) and (iii), we obtain
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=2 x^{\log x} \frac{\log x}{x}+(\log x)^{x}\left[\log (\log x)+\frac{1}{\log x}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}x=a \sec ^{3} \theta, y=a \tan ^{3} \theta,{/tex} then find {tex} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}} \text { at } \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}x=a \sec ^{3} \theta, y=a \tan ^{3} \theta,{/tex} then find {tex} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}} \text { at } \theta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
explanation: Here we have, x = a sec 3 {tex}\theta{/tex} and y = a tan 3 {tex}\theta{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}=3 a \tan ^{2} \theta \frac{d}{d \theta}(\tan \theta){/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}=3 a \tan ^{2} \theta \sec ^{2} \theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}=3 a \sec ^{2} \theta \frac{d}{d \theta}(\sec \theta) \frac{d x}{d \theta}{/tex} = {tex}3 a \sec ^{2} \theta \sec \theta \tan \theta=3 a \sec ^{3} \theta \tan \theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d \theta}}{\frac{d x}{d \theta}}=\frac{3 a \tan ^{2} \theta \sec ^{2} \theta}{3 a \sec ^{3} \theta \tan \theta}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\tan \theta}{\sec \theta}=\sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\sin \theta{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\frac{d}{d \theta}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)}{\frac{d x}{d \theta}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{d}{d \theta}(\sin \theta)}{3 a \sec ^{3} \theta \tan \theta}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\cos ^{5} \theta}{3 a \sin \theta}{/tex} At {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{5}}{3 a\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)}=\frac{1}{12 a}{/tex}
solution: Here we have, x = a sec 3 {tex}\theta{/tex} and y = a tan 3 {tex}\theta{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}=3 a \tan ^{2} \theta \frac{d}{d \theta}(\tan \theta){/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}=3 a \tan ^{2} \theta \sec ^{2} \theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}=3 a \sec ^{2} \theta \frac{d}{d \theta}(\sec \theta) \frac{d x}{d \theta}{/tex} = {tex}3 a \sec ^{2} \theta \sec \theta \tan \theta=3 a \sec ^{3} \theta \tan \theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d \theta}}{\frac{d x}{d \theta}}=\frac{3 a \tan ^{2} \theta \sec ^{2} \theta}{3 a \sec ^{3} \theta \tan \theta}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\tan \theta}{\sec \theta}=\sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\sin \theta{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\frac{d}{d \theta}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)}{\frac{d x}{d \theta}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\frac{d}{d \theta}(\sin \theta)}{3 a \sec ^{3} \theta \tan \theta}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\cos ^{5} \theta}{3 a \sin \theta}{/tex} At {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{5}}{3 a\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)}=\frac{1}{12 a}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here we have, x = a sec3{tex}\theta{/tex} and y = a tan3{tex}\theta{/tex}
Now, {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}=3 a \tan ^{2} \theta \frac{d}{d \theta}(\tan \theta){/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}=3 a \tan ^{2} \theta \sec ^{2} \theta{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}=3 a \sec ^{2} \theta \frac{d}{d \theta}(\sec \theta) \frac{d x}{d \theta}{/tex}  =  {tex}3 a \sec ^{2} \theta \sec \theta \tan \theta=3 a \sec ^{3} \theta \tan \theta{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d \theta}}{\frac{d x}{d \theta}}=\frac{3 a \tan ^{2} \theta \sec ^{2} \theta}{3 a \sec ^{3} \theta \tan \theta}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\tan \theta}{\sec \theta}=\sin \theta{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\sin \theta{/tex}
Now, {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\frac{d}{d \theta}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)}{\frac{d x}{d \theta}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\frac{d}{d \theta}(\sin \theta)}{3 a \sec ^{3} \theta \tan \theta}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\cos ^{5} \theta}{3 a \sin \theta}{/tex}
At {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{5}}{3 a\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)}=\frac{1}{12 a}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If x = a sec3 {tex}\theta{/tex}, y = a tan3 {tex}\theta{/tex}, then find {tex}\frac{\text d^{2} \text y}{\text {dx}^{2}}{/tex} at {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac \pi 4{/tex}.
question_text: If x = a sec 3 {tex}\theta{/tex} , y = a tan 3 {tex}\theta{/tex} , then find {tex}\frac{\text d^{2} \text y}{\text {dx}^{2}}{/tex} at {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}\frac \pi 4{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}=\frac{\frac {\text {dy}} {\text d \theta}}{\frac {\text {dx}}{\text d \theta}}=\frac{3 \text a \tan ^2 \theta \sec ^2 \theta}{3 \text a \sec ^3 \theta \tan \theta}{/tex} = sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{\text d^2 \text y}{\text {dx}^2}{/tex} = {tex}\cos \theta \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3 a \sec ^4 \theta \cdot \tan \theta}{/tex} {tex}\left.\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^2}\right|_{\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}}=\frac{1}{12 \mathrm{a}}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}=\frac{\frac {\text {dy}} {\text d \theta}}{\frac {\text {dx}}{\text d \theta}}=\frac{3 \text a \tan ^2 \theta \sec ^2 \theta}{3 \text a \sec ^3 \theta \tan \theta}{/tex} = sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{\text d^2 \text y}{\text {dx}^2}{/tex} = {tex}\cos \theta \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3 a \sec ^4 \theta \cdot \tan \theta}{/tex} {tex}\left.\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^2}\right|_{\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}}=\frac{1}{12 \mathrm{a}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}=\frac{\frac {\text {dy}} {\text d \theta}}{\frac {\text {dx}}{\text d \theta}}=\frac{3 \text a \tan ^2 \theta \sec ^2 \theta}{3 \text a \sec ^3 \theta \tan \theta}{/tex} = sin{tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\text d^2 \text y}{\text {dx}^2}{/tex} = {tex}\cos \theta \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3 a \sec ^4 \theta \cdot \tan \theta}{/tex}
{tex}\left.\frac{\mathrm{d}^2 \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^2}\right|_{\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}}=\frac{1}{12 \mathrm{a}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the differential sin2 x  w.r.t  ecosx.
question_text: Find the differential sin 2 x w.r.t e cosx .
explanation: {tex}u = {\sin ^2}x{/tex} {tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = 2\sin x.\cos x{/tex} {tex}v = {e^{\cos x}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = {e^{\cos x}}( - \sin x){/tex} {tex}\frac{{du}}{{dv}} = \frac{{2\sin x.\cos x}}{{ - {e^{\cos x}}.\sin x}}{/tex} = {tex}- \frac{{2\cos x}}{{{e^{\cos x}}}}{/tex}
solution: {tex}u = {\sin ^2}x{/tex} {tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = 2\sin x.\cos x{/tex} {tex}v = {e^{\cos x}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = {e^{\cos x}}( - \sin x){/tex} {tex}\frac{{du}}{{dv}} = \frac{{2\sin x.\cos x}}{{ - {e^{\cos x}}.\sin x}}{/tex} = {tex}- \frac{{2\cos x}}{{{e^{\cos x}}}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}u = {\sin ^2}x{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = 2\sin x.\cos x{/tex}
{tex}v = {e^{\cos x}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = {e^{\cos x}}( - \sin x){/tex}
{tex}\frac{{du}}{{dv}} = \frac{{2\sin x.\cos x}}{{ - {e^{\cos x}}.\sin x}}{/tex}
= {tex}- \frac{{2\cos x}}{{{e^{\cos x}}}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If x = a cos {tex}\theta{/tex}; y = b sin {tex}\theta{/tex}, then find {tex}\frac{\text d^{2} \text y}{\text {dx}^{2}}{/tex}.
question_text: If x = a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ; y = b sin {tex}\theta{/tex} , then find {tex}\frac{\text d^{2} \text y}{\text {dx}^{2}}{/tex} .
explanation: Here we are given that x = a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ; y = b sin {tex}\theta{/tex} , Now x = a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}=\frac{d}{d \theta} a \cos \theta=-a \sin \theta{/tex} y = b sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}=\frac{\frac{{dy}}{{d} \theta}}{\frac{{dx}}{{d} \theta}}=-\frac{{b} \cos \theta}{\operatorname{asin} \theta}=-\frac{{b}}{{a}} \cot \theta{/tex} Differentiating again w.r.t x: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(-\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{a}} \cot \theta\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{b}{a} \ c osec ^{2} \theta \frac{d \theta}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}=-\operatorname{asin} \theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d \theta}{d x}=\frac{-1}{a \sin \theta}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=-\frac{b}{a} \ cosec ^{2} \theta \frac{1}{a \sin \theta}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{-b}{a^{2} \sin ^{3} \theta}{/tex}
solution: Here we are given that x = a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ; y = b sin {tex}\theta{/tex} , Now x = a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}=\frac{d}{d \theta} a \cos \theta=-a \sin \theta{/tex} y = b sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}=\frac{\frac{{dy}}{{d} \theta}}{\frac{{dx}}{{d} \theta}}=-\frac{{b} \cos \theta}{\operatorname{asin} \theta}=-\frac{{b}}{{a}} \cot \theta{/tex} Differentiating again w.r.t x: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(-\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{a}} \cot \theta\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{b}{a} \ c osec ^{2} \theta \frac{d \theta}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}=-\operatorname{asin} \theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d \theta}{d x}=\frac{-1}{a \sin \theta}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=-\frac{b}{a} \ cosec ^{2} \theta \frac{1}{a \sin \theta}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{-b}{a^{2} \sin ^{3} \theta}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here we are given that x = a cos{tex}\theta{/tex}; y = b sin{tex}\theta{/tex},
Now x = a cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}=\frac{d}{d \theta} a \cos \theta=-a \sin \theta{/tex}
y = b sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}=\frac{\frac{{dy}}{{d} \theta}}{\frac{{dx}}{{d} \theta}}=-\frac{{b} \cos \theta}{\operatorname{asin} \theta}=-\frac{{b}}{{a}} \cot \theta{/tex}
Differentiating again w.r.t x:
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(-\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{a}} \cot \theta\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{b}{a} \ c osec ^{2} \theta \frac{d \theta}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}=-\operatorname{asin} \theta{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{d \theta}{d x}=\frac{-1}{a \sin \theta}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=-\frac{b}{a} \ cosec ^{2} \theta \frac{1}{a \sin \theta}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{-b}{a^{2} \sin ^{3} \theta}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If x = at2, y = 2at, then find {tex}\frac{\text d^{2} \text y}{\text {dx}^{2}}{/tex}.
question_text: If x = at 2 , y = 2at, then find {tex}\frac{\text d^{2} \text y}{\text {dx}^{2}}{/tex} .
explanation: We have, x = at 2 and y = 2 at {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}=2 a t{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=2 a{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac {dy}{dt}}{\frac {dx}{dt}}=\frac{2 a}{2 a t}=\frac{1}{t}{/tex} Differentiating both sides with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{1}{t}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=-\frac{1}{t^{2}} \frac{d t}{d x}=-\frac{1}{t^{2}} \times \frac{1}{2 a t}{/tex} {tex}\left[\text { From (i) }, \frac{d x}{d t}=2 at \therefore \frac{1}{\frac{d x}{d t}}=\frac{1}{2 a t}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=-\frac{1}{2 a t^{3}}{/tex}
solution: We have, x = at 2 and y = 2 at {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}=2 a t{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=2 a{/tex} ...(i) {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac {dy}{dt}}{\frac {dx}{dt}}=\frac{2 a}{2 a t}=\frac{1}{t}{/tex} Differentiating both sides with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{1}{t}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=-\frac{1}{t^{2}} \frac{d t}{d x}=-\frac{1}{t^{2}} \times \frac{1}{2 a t}{/tex} {tex}\left[\text { From (i) }, \frac{d x}{d t}=2 at \therefore \frac{1}{\frac{d x}{d t}}=\frac{1}{2 a t}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=-\frac{1}{2 a t^{3}}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have,
x = at2 and y = 2 at
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}=2 a t{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=2 a{/tex} ...(i)
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac {dy}{dt}}{\frac {dx}{dt}}=\frac{2 a}{2 a t}=\frac{1}{t}{/tex}
Differentiating both sides with respect to x, we get
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{1}{t}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=-\frac{1}{t^{2}} \frac{d t}{d x}=-\frac{1}{t^{2}} \times \frac{1}{2 a t}{/tex}  {tex}\left[\text { From (i) }, \frac{d x}{d t}=2 at \therefore \frac{1}{\frac{d x}{d t}}=\frac{1}{2 a t}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=-\frac{1}{2 a t^{3}}{/tex}

question_html: If y = Ae5x + Be-5x, then {tex}\frac{\text d^{2} \text y}{\text {dx}^{2}}{/tex} is equal to
question_text: If y = Ae 5x + Be -5x , then {tex}\frac{\text d^{2} \text y}{\text {dx}^{2}}{/tex} is equal to

options.explanation: We have, y = ae 5x + be −5x On differentiating w.r.t x, we get {tex}d^2y \over dx^2{/tex} = 5ae 5x − 5be −5x {tex}d^2y \over dx^2{/tex} = 25(ae 5x +be −5x ) {tex}d^2y \over dx^2{/tex} = 25 y Hence, this is the answer.

question_html: If f(x) = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{\sec x-1}{\sec x+1}}{/tex}, find {tex}f^{\prime}\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right){/tex}
question_text: If f(x) = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{\sec x-1}{\sec x+1}}{/tex} , find {tex}f^{\prime}\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right){/tex}
explanation: {tex}f(x) = \sqrt {\frac{{\sec x - 1}}{{\sec x + 1}}} {/tex} {tex}f(x) = \sqrt {\frac{{1 - \cos x}}{{1 + \cos x}}} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {\frac{{2{{\sin }^2}\frac{x}{2}}}{{2{{\cos }^2}\frac{x}{2}}}} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {{{\tan }^2}\frac{x}{2}} {/tex} f(x) = {tex} \tan \frac{x}{2}{/tex} f'(x) = {tex} {\sec ^2}\frac{x}{2}.\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}f'\left( {\frac{\pi }{3}} \right) = {\sec ^2}\frac{ {tex}\pi{/tex} }{6}.\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex} = {4 \over 3}.\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = {tex}2 \over 3{/tex}
solution: {tex}f(x) = \sqrt {\frac{{\sec x - 1}}{{\sec x + 1}}} {/tex} {tex}f(x) = \sqrt {\frac{{1 - \cos x}}{{1 + \cos x}}} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {\frac{{2{{\sin }^2}\frac{x}{2}}}{{2{{\cos }^2}\frac{x}{2}}}} {/tex} {tex} = \sqrt {{{\tan }^2}\frac{x}{2}} {/tex} f(x) = {tex} \tan \frac{x}{2}{/tex} f'(x) = {tex} {\sec ^2}\frac{x}{2}.\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}f'\left( {\frac{\pi }{3}} \right) = {\sec ^2}\frac{ {tex}\pi{/tex} }{6}.\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex} = {4 \over 3}.\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = {tex}2 \over 3{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}f(x) = \sqrt {\frac{{\sec x - 1}}{{\sec x + 1}}} {/tex} 
{tex}f(x) = \sqrt {\frac{{1 - \cos x}}{{1 + \cos x}}} {/tex} 
{tex} = \sqrt {\frac{{2{{\sin }^2}\frac{x}{2}}}{{2{{\cos }^2}\frac{x}{2}}}} {/tex} 
{tex} = \sqrt {{{\tan }^2}\frac{x}{2}} {/tex} 
f(x) = {tex} \tan \frac{x}{2}{/tex}
f'(x) = {tex} {\sec ^2}\frac{x}{2}.\frac{1}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}f'\left( {\frac{\pi }{3}} \right) = {\sec ^2}\frac{{tex}\pi{/tex}}{6}.\frac{1}{2}{/tex} 
{tex} = {4 \over 3}.\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = {tex}2 \over 3{/tex}
