question_html: If x = a sec3 {tex}\theta{/tex} and y = a tan3 {tex}\theta{/tex}, find {tex}\frac{\text d^{2} \text y}{\text {dx}^{2}}{/tex}.
question_text: If x = a sec 3 {tex}\theta{/tex} and y = a tan 3 {tex}\theta{/tex} , find {tex}\frac{\text d^{2} \text y}{\text {dx}^{2}}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex} \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{d} \theta} {/tex} = 3a sec 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} sec {tex}\theta{/tex} tan {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} \theta} {/tex} = 3a tan 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} sec 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex} \frac{\left(\frac{d y}{d \theta}\right)}{\left(\frac{d x}{d \theta}\right)}{/tex} = sin {tex}\theta{/tex} Also, {tex}\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}{/tex} = cos {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex} \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dx}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\cos \theta}{3 \mathrm{a} \tan \theta \sec ^3 \theta} {/tex} or {tex} \frac{\cos ^5 \theta}{3 \mathrm{a} \sin \theta} {/tex}
solution: {tex} \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{d} \theta} {/tex} = 3a sec 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} sec {tex}\theta{/tex} tan {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} \theta} {/tex} = 3a tan 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} sec 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex} \frac{\left(\frac{d y}{d \theta}\right)}{\left(\frac{d x}{d \theta}\right)}{/tex} = sin {tex}\theta{/tex} Also, {tex}\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}{/tex} = cos {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex} \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dx}} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{\cos \theta}{3 \mathrm{a} \tan \theta \sec ^3 \theta} {/tex} or {tex} \frac{\cos ^5 \theta}{3 \mathrm{a} \sin \theta} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{d} \theta} {/tex} = 3a sec2 {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} sec {tex}\theta{/tex} tan {tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} \theta} {/tex} =  3a tan2 {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} sec2 {tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex} \frac{\left(\frac{d y}{d \theta}\right)}{\left(\frac{d x}{d \theta}\right)}{/tex} = sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
Also, {tex}\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}{/tex} = cos {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\cdot{/tex} {tex} \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dx}} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{\cos \theta}{3 \mathrm{a} \tan \theta \sec ^3 \theta} {/tex} or {tex} \frac{\cos ^5 \theta}{3 \mathrm{a} \sin \theta} {/tex}

question_html: If y = sin-1 x - cos-1 x, show that {tex} \left(1-x^2\right) \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-x \frac{d y}{d x} {/tex} = 0.
question_text: If y = sin -1 x - cos -1 x, show that {tex} \left(1-x^2\right) \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-x \frac{d y}{d x} {/tex} = 0.
explanation: y = sin -1 x - cos -1 x {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}-\left(\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right)=\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \sqrt{1-x^2} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=2 {/tex} differentiate again wrt x, {tex} \sqrt{1-x^2} \cdot \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+\frac{d y}{d x} \cdot \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{1-x^2}}(-2 x)=0 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left(1-x^2\right) \cdot \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-x \frac{d y}{d x}=0 {/tex}
solution: y = sin -1 x - cos -1 x {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}-\left(\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right)=\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \sqrt{1-x^2} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=2 {/tex} differentiate again wrt x, {tex} \sqrt{1-x^2} \cdot \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+\frac{d y}{d x} \cdot \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{1-x^2}}(-2 x)=0 {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left(1-x^2\right) \cdot \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-x \frac{d y}{d x}=0 {/tex}
solution_html:
y = sin-1 x - cos-1 x
{tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}-\left(\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right)=\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \sqrt{1-x^2} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=2 {/tex}
differentiate again wrt x,
{tex} \sqrt{1-x^2} \cdot \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+\frac{d y}{d x} \cdot \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{1-x^2}}(-2 x)=0 {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \left(1-x^2\right) \cdot \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}-x \frac{d y}{d x}=0 {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If y = sin (sin x), prove that {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\tan x \frac{d y}{d x}+y \cos ^{2} x=0{/tex}
question_text: If y = sin (sin x), prove that {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\tan x \frac{d y}{d x}+y \cos ^{2} x=0{/tex}
explanation: Given, y = sin (sin x) ...(i) To prove: {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\tan x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}+y \cos ^{2} x=0{/tex} To find the above equation we will find the derivative twice. As {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} So, lets first find dy/dx {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x} \sin (\sin x){/tex} Using chain rule, we will differentiate the above expression Let t = sinx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d t}{d x}=\cos x{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d t} \frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{d} x}{/tex} = cos t cos x = cos(sinx) cosx ...(ii) Again differentiating with respect to x applying product rule: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\cos \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \cos (\sin \mathrm{x})+\cos (\sin \mathrm{x}) \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \cos \mathrm{x}{/tex} Using chain rule again in the next step- {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -cos x cos x sin (sinx) - sin x cos (sin x) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -y cos 2 x - tan x cos x cos (sin x) [using equation (i) : y =sin (sin x)] And using equation (ii) we have: {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=-y \cos ^{2} x-\tan x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+y \cos ^{2} x+\tan x \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex} Hence proved.
solution: Given, y = sin (sin x) ...(i) To prove: {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\tan x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}+y \cos ^{2} x=0{/tex} To find the above equation we will find the derivative twice. As {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} So, lets first find dy/dx {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x} \sin (\sin x){/tex} Using chain rule, we will differentiate the above expression Let t = sinx {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d t}{d x}=\cos x{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d t} \frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{d} x}{/tex} = cos t cos x = cos(sinx) cosx ...(ii) Again differentiating with respect to x applying product rule: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\cos \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \cos (\sin \mathrm{x})+\cos (\sin \mathrm{x}) \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \cos \mathrm{x}{/tex} Using chain rule again in the next step- {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -cos x cos x sin (sinx) - sin x cos (sin x) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -y cos 2 x - tan x cos x cos (sin x) [using equation (i) : y =sin (sin x)] And using equation (ii) we have: {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=-y \cos ^{2} x-\tan x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+y \cos ^{2} x+\tan x \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex} Hence proved.
solution_html:
Given,
y = sin (sin x) ...(i)
To prove: {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\tan x \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}+y \cos ^{2} x=0{/tex}
To find the above equation we will find the derivative twice.
As {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex}
So, lets first find dy/dx
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x} \sin (\sin x){/tex}
Using chain rule, we will differentiate the above expression
Let t = sinx
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d t}{d x}=\cos x{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d t} \frac{d t}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{d} x}{/tex} = cos t cos x = cos(sinx) cosx ...(ii)
Again differentiating with respect to x applying product rule:
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\cos \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \cos (\sin \mathrm{x})+\cos (\sin \mathrm{x}) \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \cos \mathrm{x}{/tex}
Using chain rule again in the next step-
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -cos x cos x sin (sinx) - sin x cos (sin x)
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -y cos2x - tan x cos x cos (sin x)
[using equation (i) : y =sin (sin x)]
And using equation (ii) we have:
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=-y \cos ^{2} x-\tan x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+y \cos ^{2} x+\tan x \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex}
Hence proved.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If (sin x)y = x + y, prove that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1-(x+y) y \cot x}{(x+y) \log \sin x-1}{/tex}
question_text: If (sin x) y = x + y, prove that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1-(x+y) y \cot x}{(x+y) \log \sin x-1}{/tex}
explanation: ATQ, (sin x) y = x + y Taking log on both the sides, log(sin x) y = log (x + y) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y log (sin x) = log (x + y) Differentiating with respect to x using chain rule, {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\{y \log (\sin x)\}=\frac{d}{d x}\{\log (x+y)\}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \frac{d}{d x} \log \sin x+\log \sin x \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{x+y} \frac{d}{d x}(x+y){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{\sin x} \frac{d}{d x}(\sin x)+\log \sin x \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{(x+y)}\left[1+\frac{d y}{d x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y(\cos x)}{(\sin x)}+\log \sin x \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{(x+y)}+\frac{1}{(x+y)} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}\left(\log \sin x-\frac{1}{x+y}\right)=\frac{1}{(x+y)}-y \cot x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}\left\{\frac{(x+y) \log \sin x-1}{(x+y)}\right\}=\frac{1-y(x+y) \cot x}{x+y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\left\{\frac{1-y(x+y) \cot x}{(x+y) \log \sin x-1}\right\}{/tex} LHS=RHS Hence Proved.
solution: ATQ, (sin x) y = x + y Taking log on both the sides, log(sin x) y = log (x + y) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y log (sin x) = log (x + y) Differentiating with respect to x using chain rule, {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\{y \log (\sin x)\}=\frac{d}{d x}\{\log (x+y)\}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y \frac{d}{d x} \log \sin x+\log \sin x \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{x+y} \frac{d}{d x}(x+y){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{\sin x} \frac{d}{d x}(\sin x)+\log \sin x \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{(x+y)}\left[1+\frac{d y}{d x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y(\cos x)}{(\sin x)}+\log \sin x \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{(x+y)}+\frac{1}{(x+y)} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}\left(\log \sin x-\frac{1}{x+y}\right)=\frac{1}{(x+y)}-y \cot x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}\left\{\frac{(x+y) \log \sin x-1}{(x+y)}\right\}=\frac{1-y(x+y) \cot x}{x+y}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\left\{\frac{1-y(x+y) \cot x}{(x+y) \log \sin x-1}\right\}{/tex} LHS=RHS Hence Proved.
solution_html:
ATQ, (sin x)y = x + y
Taking log on both the sides,
log(sin x)y = log (x + y)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y log (sin x) = log (x + y)
Differentiating with respect to x using chain rule,
{tex}\frac{d}{d x}\{y \log (\sin x)\}=\frac{d}{d x}\{\log (x+y)\}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y \frac{d}{d x} \log \sin x+\log \sin x \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{x+y} \frac{d}{d x}(x+y){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y}{\sin x} \frac{d}{d x}(\sin x)+\log \sin x \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{(x+y)}\left[1+\frac{d y}{d x}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{y(\cos x)}{(\sin x)}+\log \sin x \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{(x+y)}+\frac{1}{(x+y)} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}\left(\log \sin x-\frac{1}{x+y}\right)=\frac{1}{(x+y)}-y \cot x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}\left\{\frac{(x+y) \log \sin x-1}{(x+y)}\right\}=\frac{1-y(x+y) \cot x}{x+y}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\left\{\frac{1-y(x+y) \cot x}{(x+y) \log \sin x-1}\right\}{/tex}
LHS=RHS
Hence Proved.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Differentiate the function with respect to x: {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left\{\frac{\cos x+\sin x}{\cos x-\sin x}\right\},-\frac{\pi}{4}<x<\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}.
question_text: Differentiate the function with respect to x: {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left\{\frac{\cos x+\sin x}{\cos x-\sin x}\right\},-\frac{\pi}{4}<x<\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} .
explanation: Let, {tex}\mathrm{y}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\cos x+\sin x}{\cos x-\sin x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\frac{\cos x+\sin x}{\cos x}}{\frac{\cos x-\sin x}{\cos x}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\frac{\cos x}{\cos x}+\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}}{\frac{\cos x}{\cos x}-\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{1+\tan x}{1-\tan x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\tan \frac{\pi}{4}+\tan x}{1-\tan \frac{\pi}{4} \tan x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\tan ^{-1}\left[\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+x\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{\pi}{4}+x{/tex} Differentiate it with respect to x, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=0+1{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=1{/tex} The differentiation of the given function y is as above.
solution: Let, {tex}\mathrm{y}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\cos x+\sin x}{\cos x-\sin x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\frac{\cos x+\sin x}{\cos x}}{\frac{\cos x-\sin x}{\cos x}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\frac{\cos x}{\cos x}+\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}}{\frac{\cos x}{\cos x}-\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{1+\tan x}{1-\tan x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\tan \frac{\pi}{4}+\tan x}{1-\tan \frac{\pi}{4} \tan x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\tan ^{-1}\left[\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+x\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{\pi}{4}+x{/tex} Differentiate it with respect to x, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=0+1{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=1{/tex} The differentiation of the given function y is as above.
solution_html:
Let, {tex}\mathrm{y}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\cos x+\sin x}{\cos x-\sin x}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\frac{\cos x+\sin x}{\cos x}}{\frac{\cos x-\sin x}{\cos x}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\frac{\cos x}{\cos x}+\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}}{\frac{\cos x}{\cos x}-\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{1+\tan x}{1-\tan x}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\tan \frac{\pi}{4}+\tan x}{1-\tan \frac{\pi}{4} \tan x}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=\tan ^{-1}\left[\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+x\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{\pi}{4}+x{/tex}
Differentiate it with respect to x,
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=0+1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=1{/tex}
The differentiation of the given function y is as above.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If sin y = x cos (a + y), then show that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\cos ^{2}(a+y)}{\cos a}{/tex}. Also, show that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}={/tex} cos a, when x = 0.
question_text: If sin y = x cos (a + y), then show that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\cos ^{2}(a+y)}{\cos a}{/tex} . Also, show that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}={/tex} cos a, when x = 0.
explanation: Given, x = {tex}\frac{\sin y}{\cos (a+y)}{/tex} gives {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{\cos (a+y) \cos y+\sin y \sin (a+y)}{\cos ^{2}(a+y)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\cos ^{2}(a+y)}{\cos (a+y-y)}=\frac{\cos ^{2}(a+y)}{\cos a}{/tex} therefore, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} =cos a when x = 0 i.e. y = 0
solution: Given, x = {tex}\frac{\sin y}{\cos (a+y)}{/tex} gives {tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{\cos (a+y) \cos y+\sin y \sin (a+y)}{\cos ^{2}(a+y)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\cos ^{2}(a+y)}{\cos (a+y-y)}=\frac{\cos ^{2}(a+y)}{\cos a}{/tex} therefore, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} =cos a when x = 0 i.e. y = 0
solution_html:
Given, x = {tex}\frac{\sin y}{\cos (a+y)}{/tex}gives
{tex}\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{\cos (a+y) \cos y+\sin y \sin (a+y)}{\cos ^{2}(a+y)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\cos ^{2}(a+y)}{\cos (a+y-y)}=\frac{\cos ^{2}(a+y)}{\cos a}{/tex} therefore,
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}=cos a when x = 0 i.e. y = 0

question_html: If x = a sec3 {tex}\theta{/tex} and y = tan3 {tex}\theta{/tex}, find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}} \text { at } \theta=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
question_text: If x = a sec 3 {tex}\theta{/tex} and y = tan 3 {tex}\theta{/tex} , find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}} \text { at } \theta=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}
explanation: y = tan 3 θ and x = a sec 3 θ differentiating x and y w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{d}{\theta}}{/tex} =3a tan 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} sec 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} and {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{d}{\theta}}{/tex} =3a sec 3 {tex}\theta{/tex} tan {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{d}{\theta}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\tan \theta}{\sec \theta}{/tex} =sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d}{d \theta}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right) \frac{d \theta}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\cos \theta \times \frac{1}{3 a \sec ^{3} \theta \tan \theta}{/tex} {tex}\left.\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right]_{\theta=\frac{\pi}{3}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2}}{3 a \times 8 \times \sqrt{3}}=\frac{1}{48a \sqrt{3 }}{/tex}
solution: y = tan 3 θ and x = a sec 3 θ differentiating x and y w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{d}{\theta}}{/tex} =3a tan 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} sec 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} and {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{d}{\theta}}{/tex} =3a sec 3 {tex}\theta{/tex} tan {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{d}{\theta}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\tan \theta}{\sec \theta}{/tex} =sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d}{d \theta}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right) \frac{d \theta}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\cos \theta \times \frac{1}{3 a \sec ^{3} \theta \tan \theta}{/tex} {tex}\left.\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right]_{\theta=\frac{\pi}{3}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2}}{3 a \times 8 \times \sqrt{3}}=\frac{1}{48a \sqrt{3 }}{/tex}
solution_html:
y = tan3 θ and x = a sec3 θ

differentiating x and y w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex}

 {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{d}{\theta}}{/tex}=3a tan2{tex}\theta{/tex} sec2{tex}\theta{/tex}  and 
{tex}\frac{{dx}}{{d}{\theta}}{/tex} =3a sec3{tex}\theta{/tex} tan{tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{d}{\theta}}{/tex}= {tex}\frac{\tan \theta}{\sec \theta}{/tex}=sin{tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}={tex}\frac{d}{d \theta}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right) \frac{d \theta}{d x}{/tex}={tex}\cos \theta \times \frac{1}{3 a \sec ^{3} \theta \tan \theta}{/tex}
{tex}\left.\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right]_{\theta=\frac{\pi}{3}}{/tex}={tex}\frac{\frac{1}{2}}{3 a \times 8 \times \sqrt{3}}=\frac{1}{48a \sqrt{3 }}{/tex}

question_html: If y = etan-1 x, prove that (1 + x2) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+(2 x-1) \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex}
question_text: If y = e tan-1 x , prove that (1 + x 2 ) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+(2 x-1) \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex}
explanation: y = e tan-1 x differentiating both sides w.r.t. x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} x}\left(\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{y}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+2 x \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+(2 x-1) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} =0
solution: y = e tan-1 x differentiating both sides w.r.t. x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} x}\left(\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{y}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+2 x \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+(2 x-1) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} =0
solution_html:
y = etan-1 x

differentiating both sides w.r.t. x
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}={tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} x}\left(\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}\right){/tex}={tex}\frac{y}{1+x^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}= y
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+2 x \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+(2 x-1) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}=0

question_html: If the function {tex}\mathrm{f}(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl} \frac{\left(3^{\sin x}-1\right)^{2}}{x \log (1+5 x)}, & x \neq 0 \\ \mathrm{k} & , x=0 \end{array}\right.{/tex}is continuous at x = 0, find k.
question_text: If the function {tex}\mathrm{f}(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl} \frac{\left(3^{\sin x}-1\right)^{2}}{x \log (1+5 x)}, & x \neq 0 \\ \mathrm{k} & , x=0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0, find k.
explanation: function is continuous at x = 0, so, f(0) = {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} f(x){/tex} => k = {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left(3^{\sin x}-1\right)^{2}}{x \log (1+5 x)}{/tex} => k= {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left(3^{\sin x}-1\right)^{2}}{\sin ^{2} x}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin ^{2} x}{x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\times {/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{5} \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{\frac{\log (1+5 x)}{5 x}}{/tex} => k= {tex}\frac{1}{5}(\log 3)^{2}{/tex}
solution: function is continuous at x = 0, so, f(0) = {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} f(x){/tex} => k = {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left(3^{\sin x}-1\right)^{2}}{x \log (1+5 x)}{/tex} => k= {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left(3^{\sin x}-1\right)^{2}}{\sin ^{2} x}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin ^{2} x}{x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\times {/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{5} \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{\frac{\log (1+5 x)}{5 x}}{/tex} => k= {tex}\frac{1}{5}(\log 3)^{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
function is continuous at x = 0, so,

f(0) = {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} f(x){/tex}

=> k = {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left(3^{\sin x}-1\right)^{2}}{x \log (1+5 x)}{/tex}
=> k= {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\left(3^{\sin x}-1\right)^{2}}{\sin ^{2} x}{/tex}{tex}\times{/tex}{tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin ^{2} x}{x^{2}}{/tex}{tex}\times {/tex}{tex}\frac{1}{5} \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{\frac{\log (1+5 x)}{5 x}}{/tex}
=> k= {tex}\frac{1}{5}(\log 3)^{2}{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}y=e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex}, prove that (1 + x2) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+(2 x-1) \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}y=e^{\tan ^{-1} x}{/tex} , prove that (1 + x 2 ) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+(2 x-1) \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex}
explanation: y = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1}} x{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} x}\left(\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{y}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} [y = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1}} x{/tex} ] {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = y differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+2 x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+(2 x-1) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 0
solution: y = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1}} x{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} x}\left(\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{y}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} [y = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1}} x{/tex} ] {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = y differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+2 x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+(2 x-1) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 0
solution_html:
y = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1}} x{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}={tex}e^{\tan ^{-1} x}\left(\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}\right){/tex}={tex}\frac{y}{1+x^{2}}{/tex}     [y = {tex}e^{\tan ^{-1}} x{/tex}]
{tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}= y

differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+2 x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}={tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+(2 x-1) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 0

question_html: Differentiate the function with respect to x: {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1+\cos x}{\sin x}\right){/tex}.
question_text: Differentiate the function with respect to x: {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1+\cos x}{\sin x}\right){/tex} .
explanation: Let u = tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x}\right){/tex} Let u = tan-1 {tex}\left[\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{x}{2}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{x}{2}{/tex} Differentiating it with respect to x {tex}\frac{du}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}0-\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{d u}{d x}=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ......(i) Let v = sec -1 x Differentiating it with respect to x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{x \sqrt{x^{2}-1}}{/tex} ......(ii) Dividing equation (i) by (ii), {tex}\frac{\frac{d u}{d x}}{\frac{d v}{d x}}=-\frac{1}{2} \times{/tex} {tex}\frac{x \sqrt{x^{2}-1}}{1} \quad \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-x \sqrt{x^{2}-1}}{2}{/tex} .
solution: Let u = tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x}\right){/tex} Let u = tan-1 {tex}\left[\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{x}{2}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{x}{2}{/tex} Differentiating it with respect to x {tex}\frac{du}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}0-\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{d u}{d x}=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ......(i) Let v = sec -1 x Differentiating it with respect to x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{x \sqrt{x^{2}-1}}{/tex} ......(ii) Dividing equation (i) by (ii), {tex}\frac{\frac{d u}{d x}}{\frac{d v}{d x}}=-\frac{1}{2} \times{/tex} {tex}\frac{x \sqrt{x^{2}-1}}{1} \quad \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-x \sqrt{x^{2}-1}}{2}{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let u = tan-1 {tex}\left(\frac{\cos x}{1+\sin x}\right){/tex} 
Let u = tan-1 {tex}\left[\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{x}{2}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{x}{2}{/tex} 
Differentiating it with respect to x
{tex}\frac{du}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}0-\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \frac{d u}{d x}=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}  ......(i)
Let v = sec-1 x
Differentiating it with respect to x
{tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{x \sqrt{x^{2}-1}}{/tex} ......(ii)
Dividing equation (i) by (ii), {tex}\frac{\frac{d u}{d x}}{\frac{d v}{d x}}=-\frac{1}{2} \times{/tex} {tex}\frac{x \sqrt{x^{2}-1}}{1} \quad \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-x \sqrt{x^{2}-1}}{2}{/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Differentiate the function w.r.t. x: tan-1 {tex}\left(\frac{\cos x-\sin x}{\cos x+\sin x}\right){/tex}.
question_text: Differentiate the function w.r.t. x: tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{\cos x-\sin x}{\cos x+\sin x}\right){/tex} .
explanation: Let y = tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{\cos x-\sin x}{\cos x+\sin x}\right){/tex} = tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{1-\tan x}{1+\tan x}\right){/tex} [On dividing num. and denom. by cos x] = tan -1 {tex}\left\{\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-x\right)\right\}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-x\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-x\right){/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get Hence, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-x\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)-\frac{d}{d x}(x){/tex} = (0 - 1) = -1.
solution: Let y = tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{\cos x-\sin x}{\cos x+\sin x}\right){/tex} = tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{1-\tan x}{1+\tan x}\right){/tex} [On dividing num. and denom. by cos x] = tan -1 {tex}\left\{\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-x\right)\right\}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-x\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-x\right){/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get Hence, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-x\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)-\frac{d}{d x}(x){/tex} = (0 - 1) = -1.
solution_html:
Let y = tan-1 {tex}\left(\frac{\cos x-\sin x}{\cos x+\sin x}\right){/tex} = tan-1 {tex}\left(\frac{1-\tan x}{1+\tan x}\right){/tex} 
[On dividing num. and denom. by cos x]
= tan-1 {tex}\left\{\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-x\right)\right\}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-x\right){/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y = {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-x\right){/tex} 
Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
Hence, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-x\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)-\frac{d}{d x}(x){/tex} = (0 - 1) = -1.

question_html: If y = (sin x)x + sin-1{tex} \sqrt { x }{/tex}, then find {tex} \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}..
question_text: If y = (sin x) x + sin -1 {tex} \sqrt { x }{/tex} , then find {tex} \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} ..
explanation: According to the question, {tex}y= ( \sin x ) ^ { x } + \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x }{/tex} .......(i) Let u = {tex} ( \sin x ) ^ { x }{/tex} ...............................(ii) Then, Equation.(i) becomes, y = u + {tex} \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x }{/tex} ............(iii) Differentiating both sides of (iii) w.r.t x,we get, {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{du}{dx}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-(\sqrt x)^2}}×\frac{d}{dx}(\sqrt x){/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{du}{dx}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x}}×\frac{1}{2\sqrt x}{/tex} .............(iv) Taking log on both sides of Equation.(ii), log u = x log sinx Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = x \frac { d } { d x } ( \log \sin x ) + \log \sin x \frac { d } { d x } ( x ){/tex} [ Using product rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d u } { d x }{/tex} = {tex}u \left[ x \times \frac { 1 } { \sin x } \frac { d } { d x } ( \sin x ) + \log \sin x ( 1 ) \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d u } { d x }{/tex} = (sin x) x {tex} \left[ \frac { x } { \sin x } \times \cos x + \log \sin x \right]{/tex} [ From Eq(ii)] From (iv),we get, {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = (sin x)^x[x\ cot\ x + log\ sin x] {/tex} {tex} + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x } } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { x } }{/tex}
solution: According to the question, {tex}y= ( \sin x ) ^ { x } + \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x }{/tex} .......(i) Let u = {tex} ( \sin x ) ^ { x }{/tex} ...............................(ii) Then, Equation.(i) becomes, y = u + {tex} \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x }{/tex} ............(iii) Differentiating both sides of (iii) w.r.t x,we get, {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{du}{dx}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-(\sqrt x)^2}}×\frac{d}{dx}(\sqrt x){/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{du}{dx}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x}}×\frac{1}{2\sqrt x}{/tex} .............(iv) Taking log on both sides of Equation.(ii), log u = x log sinx Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = x \frac { d } { d x } ( \log \sin x ) + \log \sin x \frac { d } { d x } ( x ){/tex} [ Using product rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d u } { d x }{/tex} = {tex}u \left[ x \times \frac { 1 } { \sin x } \frac { d } { d x } ( \sin x ) + \log \sin x ( 1 ) \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d u } { d x }{/tex} = (sin x) x {tex} \left[ \frac { x } { \sin x } \times \cos x + \log \sin x \right]{/tex} [ From Eq(ii)] From (iv),we get, {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = (sin x)^x[x\ cot\ x + log\ sin x] {/tex} {tex} + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x } } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { x } }{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}y= ( \sin x ) ^ { x } + \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x }{/tex}.......(i)
Let u = {tex} ( \sin x ) ^ { x }{/tex}...............................(ii)
Then, Equation.(i) becomes, y = u + {tex} \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x }{/tex}............(iii)
Differentiating both sides of (iii) w.r.t x,we get,
{tex}\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{du}{dx}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-(\sqrt x)^2}}×\frac{d}{dx}(\sqrt x){/tex}
{tex}\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{du}{dx}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x}}×\frac{1}{2\sqrt x}{/tex}.............(iv)
Taking log on both sides of Equation.(ii), 
log u = x log sinx
Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = x \frac { d } { d x } ( \log \sin x ) + \log \sin x \frac { d } { d x } ( x ){/tex}[ Using product rule of derivative]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d u } { d x }{/tex}= {tex}u \left[ x \times \frac { 1 } { \sin x } \frac { d } { d x } ( \sin x ) + \log \sin x ( 1 ) \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d u } { d x }{/tex} = (sin x)x{tex} \left[ \frac { x } { \sin x } \times \cos x + \log \sin x \right]{/tex}[ From Eq(ii)]
From (iv),we get,
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = (sin x)^x[x\ cot\ x + log\ sin x] {/tex} {tex} + \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x } } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { x } }{/tex}

question_html: If xm yn = (x + y)m + n, prove that {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x }{/tex}.
question_text: If x m y n = (x + y) m + n , prove that {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x }{/tex} .
explanation: According to the question, x m y n = (x + y) m + n On taking log both sides, we get log(x m y n )= log (x + y) m + n {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} log(x m ) + log(y n )= (m + n)log (x + y) {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} m log x + n log y = (m + n) log(x + y) On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex} \frac { m } { x } + \frac { n } { y } \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { m + n } { x + y } \left( 1 + \frac { d y } { d x } \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { m } { x } + \frac { n } { y } \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { m + n } { x + y } + \frac { m + n } { x + y } \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { m } { x } - \frac { ( m + n ) } { x + y }{/tex} {tex} = \left( \frac { m + n } { x + y } - \frac { n } { y } \right) \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \frac { m y + n y - n x - n y } { y ( x + y ) } \right] \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { m x + m y - m x - n x } { x ( x + y ) }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } \left[ \frac { m y - n x } { y } \right] = \frac { m y - n x } { x }{/tex} Hence, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x }{/tex}
solution: According to the question, x m y n = (x + y) m + n On taking log both sides, we get log(x m y n )= log (x + y) m + n {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} log(x m ) + log(y n )= (m + n)log (x + y) {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} m log x + n log y = (m + n) log(x + y) On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex} \frac { m } { x } + \frac { n } { y } \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { m + n } { x + y } \left( 1 + \frac { d y } { d x } \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { m } { x } + \frac { n } { y } \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { m + n } { x + y } + \frac { m + n } { x + y } \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { m } { x } - \frac { ( m + n ) } { x + y }{/tex} {tex} = \left( \frac { m + n } { x + y } - \frac { n } { y } \right) \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \frac { m y + n y - n x - n y } { y ( x + y ) } \right] \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { m x + m y - m x - n x } { x ( x + y ) }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } \left[ \frac { m y - n x } { y } \right] = \frac { m y - n x } { x }{/tex} Hence, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x }{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, xm yn = (x + y)m + n
On taking log both sides, we get
log(xm yn)= log (x + y)m + n
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex}log(xm) + log(yn)= (m + n)log (x + y)
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} m log x + n log y = (m + n) log(x + y)
On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get
{tex} \frac { m } { x } + \frac { n } { y } \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { m + n } { x + y } \left( 1 + \frac { d y } { d x } \right){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { m } { x } + \frac { n } { y } \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}{tex} = \frac { m + n } { x + y } + \frac { m + n } { x + y } \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { m } { x } - \frac { ( m + n ) } { x + y }{/tex}{tex} = \left( \frac { m + n } { x + y } - \frac { n } { y } \right) \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \frac { m y + n y - n x - n y } { y ( x + y ) } \right] \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}= {tex}\frac { m x + m y - m x - n x } { x ( x + y ) }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } \left[ \frac { m y - n x } { y } \right] = \frac { m y - n x } { x }{/tex}
Hence, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y } { x }{/tex}

question_html: If x = a (cos{tex}\theta{/tex} + {tex}\theta{/tex} sin{tex}\theta{/tex}), y = a (sin{tex}\theta{/tex} - {tex}\theta{/tex}cos{tex}\theta{/tex}), prove that {tex}\frac{d^{2} x}{d \theta^{2}}=a(\cos \theta-\theta \sin \theta), \frac{d^{2} y}{d \theta^{2}}=a(\sin \theta+\theta \cos \theta){/tex} and {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\sec ^{3} \theta}{a \theta}{/tex}
question_text: If x = a (cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ), y = a (sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ), prove that {tex}\frac{d^{2} x}{d \theta^{2}}=a(\cos \theta-\theta \sin \theta), \frac{d^{2} y}{d \theta^{2}}=a(\sin \theta+\theta \cos \theta){/tex} and {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\sec ^{3} \theta}{a \theta}{/tex}
explanation: Given, x = a (cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ) ...(1) y = a (sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ) ...(2) To prove: (i) {tex}\frac{d^{2} x}{d \theta^{2}}=a(\cos \theta-\theta \sin \theta),{/tex} (ii) {tex} \frac{d^{2} y}{d \theta^{2}}=a(\sin \theta+\theta \cos \theta){/tex} (iii) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\sec ^{3} \theta}{a \theta}{/tex} We notice a second order derivative in the expression to be proved so first take the step to find the second order derivative. Let's find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} As {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}=\frac{d}{d \theta} a(\cos \theta+\theta \sin \theta){/tex} = a (-sin {tex}\theta{/tex} + {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ) [differentiated using product rule for {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ] = a {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ...(3) Again differentiating w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex} using product rule: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{d} \theta^{2}}=\mathrm{a}(-\theta \sin \theta+\cos \theta){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{d} \theta^{2}}=\mathrm{a}(\cos \theta-\theta \sin \theta){/tex} ...proved (i) Similarly, {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} \theta}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} \theta} \mathrm{a}(\sin \theta-\theta \cos \theta)=\mathrm{a} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} \theta} \sin \theta-\mathrm{a} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} \theta}(\theta \cos \theta){/tex} = a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + a {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d \theta}=a \theta \sin \theta{/tex} ...(5) Again differentiating w.r.t {tex}\theta{/tex} using product rule: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{d} \theta^{2}}=\mathrm{a}(\theta \cos \theta+\sin \theta){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{d} \theta^{2}}=\mathrm{a}(\sin \theta+\theta \cos \theta){/tex} ...proved (ii) {tex}\because \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d \theta}}{\frac{d x}{d \theta}}{/tex} Using equations (4) and (5) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{a \theta \sin \theta}{a \theta \cos \theta}=\tan \theta{/tex} As {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Again differentiating w.r.t x: {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x} \tan \theta{/tex} {tex}=\sec ^{2} \theta \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{d} x}{/tex} [using chain rule] {tex}\because \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{d} \theta}=\mathrm{a} \theta \cos \theta=>\frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{a} \theta \cos \theta}{/tex} Putting a value in the above equation We have: {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\sec ^{2} \theta \times \frac{1}{a \theta \cos \theta}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\sec ^{3} \theta}{a \theta}{/tex} ...proved (iii)
solution: Given, x = a (cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ) ...(1) y = a (sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ) ...(2) To prove: (i) {tex}\frac{d^{2} x}{d \theta^{2}}=a(\cos \theta-\theta \sin \theta),{/tex} (ii) {tex} \frac{d^{2} y}{d \theta^{2}}=a(\sin \theta+\theta \cos \theta){/tex} (iii) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\sec ^{3} \theta}{a \theta}{/tex} We notice a second order derivative in the expression to be proved so first take the step to find the second order derivative. Let's find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} As {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}=\frac{d}{d \theta} a(\cos \theta+\theta \sin \theta){/tex} = a (-sin {tex}\theta{/tex} + {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ) [differentiated using product rule for {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ] = a {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ...(3) Again differentiating w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex} using product rule: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{d} \theta^{2}}=\mathrm{a}(-\theta \sin \theta+\cos \theta){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{d} \theta^{2}}=\mathrm{a}(\cos \theta-\theta \sin \theta){/tex} ...proved (i) Similarly, {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} \theta}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} \theta} \mathrm{a}(\sin \theta-\theta \cos \theta)=\mathrm{a} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} \theta} \sin \theta-\mathrm{a} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} \theta}(\theta \cos \theta){/tex} = a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + a {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d \theta}=a \theta \sin \theta{/tex} ...(5) Again differentiating w.r.t {tex}\theta{/tex} using product rule: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{d} \theta^{2}}=\mathrm{a}(\theta \cos \theta+\sin \theta){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{d} \theta^{2}}=\mathrm{a}(\sin \theta+\theta \cos \theta){/tex} ...proved (ii) {tex}\because \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d \theta}}{\frac{d x}{d \theta}}{/tex} Using equations (4) and (5) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{a \theta \sin \theta}{a \theta \cos \theta}=\tan \theta{/tex} As {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Again differentiating w.r.t x: {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x} \tan \theta{/tex} {tex}=\sec ^{2} \theta \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{d} x}{/tex} [using chain rule] {tex}\because \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{d} \theta}=\mathrm{a} \theta \cos \theta=>\frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{a} \theta \cos \theta}{/tex} Putting a value in the above equation We have: {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\sec ^{2} \theta \times \frac{1}{a \theta \cos \theta}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\sec ^{3} \theta}{a \theta}{/tex} ...proved (iii)
solution_html:
Given,
x = a (cos{tex}\theta{/tex} + {tex}\theta{/tex} sin{tex}\theta{/tex}) ...(1)
y = a (sin{tex}\theta{/tex} - {tex}\theta{/tex} cos{tex}\theta{/tex}) ...(2)
To prove:
(i) {tex}\frac{d^{2} x}{d \theta^{2}}=a(\cos \theta-\theta \sin \theta),{/tex}
(ii)  {tex} \frac{d^{2} y}{d \theta^{2}}=a(\sin \theta+\theta \cos \theta){/tex}
(iii) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\sec ^{3} \theta}{a \theta}{/tex}
We notice a second order derivative in the expression to be proved so first take the step to find the second order derivative.
Let's find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}
As {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}=\frac{d}{d \theta} a(\cos \theta+\theta \sin \theta){/tex}
= a (-sin{tex}\theta{/tex} + {tex}\theta{/tex} cos{tex}\theta{/tex} + sin{tex}\theta{/tex})
[differentiated using product rule for {tex}\theta{/tex} sin{tex}\theta{/tex}]
= a{tex}\theta{/tex} cos{tex}\theta{/tex} ...(3)
Again differentiating w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex} using product rule:
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{d} \theta^{2}}=\mathrm{a}(-\theta \sin \theta+\cos \theta){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{d} \theta^{2}}=\mathrm{a}(\cos \theta-\theta \sin \theta){/tex} ...proved (i)
Similarly,
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} \theta}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} \theta} \mathrm{a}(\sin \theta-\theta \cos \theta)=\mathrm{a} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} \theta} \sin \theta-\mathrm{a} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} \theta}(\theta \cos \theta){/tex}
= a cos{tex}\theta{/tex} + a {tex}\theta{/tex} sin{tex}\theta{/tex} - a cos{tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d \theta}=a \theta \sin \theta{/tex} ...(5)
Again differentiating w.r.t {tex}\theta{/tex} using product rule:
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{d} \theta^{2}}=\mathrm{a}(\theta \cos \theta+\sin \theta){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{d} \theta^{2}}=\mathrm{a}(\sin \theta+\theta \cos \theta){/tex} ...proved (ii)
{tex}\because \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d \theta}}{\frac{d x}{d \theta}}{/tex}
Using equations (4) and (5)
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{a \theta \sin \theta}{a \theta \cos \theta}=\tan \theta{/tex}
As {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Again differentiating w.r.t x:
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x} \tan \theta{/tex}
{tex}=\sec ^{2} \theta \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{d} x}{/tex} [using chain rule]
{tex}\because \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{d} \theta}=\mathrm{a} \theta \cos \theta=>\frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{a} \theta \cos \theta}{/tex}
Putting a value in the above equation
We have:
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\sec ^{2} \theta \times \frac{1}{a \theta \cos \theta}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\sec ^{3} \theta}{a \theta}{/tex} ...proved (iii)

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the value of the constant k so that the function f defined below is continuous at {tex}x = 0,{/tex} Where {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{1 - \cos 4x}}{{8{x^2}}},x \ne 0 \hfill \\ k,\,\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}
question_text: Find the value of the constant k so that the function f defined below is continuous at {tex}x = 0,{/tex} Where {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{1 - \cos 4x}}{{8{x^2}}},x \ne 0 \hfill \\ k,\,\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}
explanation: It is given that the function f is continuous at x = 0. Therefore, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} f\left( x \right) = f\left( 0 \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{1 - \cos 4x}}{{8{x^2}}} = k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{2{{\sin }^2}2x}}{{8{x^2}}} = k\ \left(\therefore {1 - \cos 2x = 2{{\sin }^2}x} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} {\left( {\frac{{\sin 2x}}{{2x}}} \right)^2}=k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow k = 1{/tex} {tex}{/tex}
solution: It is given that the function f is continuous at x = 0. Therefore, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} f\left( x \right) = f\left( 0 \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{1 - \cos 4x}}{{8{x^2}}} = k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{2{{\sin }^2}2x}}{{8{x^2}}} = k\ \left(\therefore {1 - \cos 2x = 2{{\sin }^2}x} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} {\left( {\frac{{\sin 2x}}{{2x}}} \right)^2}=k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow k = 1{/tex} {tex}{/tex}
solution_html:
It is given that the function f is continuous at x = 0.
Therefore, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} f\left( x \right) = f\left( 0 \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{1 - \cos 4x}}{{8{x^2}}} = k{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{2{{\sin }^2}2x}}{{8{x^2}}} = k\ \left(\therefore {1 - \cos 2x = 2{{\sin }^2}x} \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} {\left( {\frac{{\sin 2x}}{{2x}}} \right)^2}=k{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow k = 1{/tex}{tex}{/tex}

question_html: Find the value of k for which the function {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl} \frac{x^{2}+3 x-10}{x-2}, & x \neq 2 \\ k & , x=2 \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 2
question_text: Find the value of k for which the function {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl} \frac{x^{2}+3 x-10}{x-2}, & x \neq 2 \\ k & , x=2 \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 2
explanation: Given funcion is, {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl} \frac{x^{2}+3 x-10}{x-2}, & x \neq 2 \\ k & , x=2 \end{array}\right.{/tex} {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2^{-}}\left(\frac{x^{2}+3 x-10}{x-2}\right)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2^{-}}(x+5)=7{/tex} {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2^{+}}\left(\frac{x^{2}+3 x-10}{x-2}\right)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2^{+}}(x+5)=7{/tex} If f(x) is continuous at x = 2, then {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2^{-}} f(x)=f(2)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2^{+}} f(x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 7 As f(2) = k
solution: Given funcion is, {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl} \frac{x^{2}+3 x-10}{x-2}, & x \neq 2 \\ k & , x=2 \end{array}\right.{/tex} {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2^{-}}\left(\frac{x^{2}+3 x-10}{x-2}\right)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2^{-}}(x+5)=7{/tex} {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2^{+}}\left(\frac{x^{2}+3 x-10}{x-2}\right)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2^{+}}(x+5)=7{/tex} If f(x) is continuous at x = 2, then {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2^{-}} f(x)=f(2)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2^{+}} f(x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 7 As f(2) = k
solution_html:
Given funcion is, {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl} \frac{x^{2}+3 x-10}{x-2}, & x \neq 2 \\ k & , x=2 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
{tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2^{-}}\left(\frac{x^{2}+3 x-10}{x-2}\right)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2^{-}}(x+5)=7{/tex}
{tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2^{+}}\left(\frac{x^{2}+3 x-10}{x-2}\right)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2^{+}}(x+5)=7{/tex}
If f(x) is continuous at x = 2, then
{tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2^{-}} f(x)=f(2)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 2^{+}} f(x){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 7   As f(2) = k

question_html: Determine the value of 'k' for which the following function is continuous at x = 3 : f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { l } { \frac { ( x + 3 ) ^ { 2 } - 36 } { x - 3 } , x \neq 3 } \\ { k \quad , x = 3 } \end{array} \right.{/tex}.
question_text: Determine the value of 'k' for which the following function is continuous at x = 3 : f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { l } { \frac { ( x + 3 ) ^ { 2 } - 36 } { x - 3 } , x \neq 3 } \\ { k \quad , x = 3 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} .
explanation: Given, f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \frac { ( x + 3 ) ^ { 2 } - 36 } { x - 3 } , } & { x \neq 3 } \\ { k, } & { x = 3 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} We shall use the definition of continuity to find the value of k. If f(x) is continuous at x = 3, Then, we have {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 3} f ( x ) = f ( 3 ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 3} \frac { ( x + 3 ) ^ { 2 } - 36 } { x - 3 } = k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 3} \frac { ( x + 3 ) ^ { 2 } - 6 ^ { 2 } } { x - 3 } = k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 3} \frac { ( x + 3 - 6 ) ( x + 3 + 6 ) } { x - 3 } = k{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} a 2 - b 2 = (a - b)(a + b)] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 3} \frac { ( x - 3 ) ( x + 9 ) } { ( x - 3 ) } = k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 3} ( x + 9 ) = k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 + 9 = k {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 12
solution: Given, f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \frac { ( x + 3 ) ^ { 2 } - 36 } { x - 3 } , } & { x \neq 3 } \\ { k, } & { x = 3 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} We shall use the definition of continuity to find the value of k. If f(x) is continuous at x = 3, Then, we have {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 3} f ( x ) = f ( 3 ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 3} \frac { ( x + 3 ) ^ { 2 } - 36 } { x - 3 } = k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 3} \frac { ( x + 3 ) ^ { 2 } - 6 ^ { 2 } } { x - 3 } = k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 3} \frac { ( x + 3 - 6 ) ( x + 3 + 6 ) } { x - 3 } = k{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} a 2 - b 2 = (a - b)(a + b)] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 3} \frac { ( x - 3 ) ( x + 9 ) } { ( x - 3 ) } = k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 3} ( x + 9 ) = k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 + 9 = k {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 12
solution_html:
Given, f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \frac { ( x + 3 ) ^ { 2 } - 36 } { x - 3 } , } & { x \neq 3 } \\ { k, } & { x = 3 } \end{array} \right.{/tex}

We shall use the definition of continuity to find the value of k.
If f(x) is continuous at x = 3,
Then, we have {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 3} f ( x ) = f ( 3 ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 3} \frac { ( x + 3 ) ^ { 2 } - 36 } { x - 3 } = k{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 3} \frac { ( x + 3 ) ^ { 2 } - 6 ^ { 2 } } { x - 3 } = k{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 3} \frac { ( x + 3 - 6 ) ( x + 3 + 6 ) } { x - 3 } = k{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} a2 - b2 = (a - b)(a + b)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 3} \frac { ( x - 3 ) ( x + 9 ) } { ( x - 3 ) } = k{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 3} ( x + 9 ) = k{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3 + 9 = k {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 12

question_html: Determine the value of the constant 'k' so that the function f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \frac { k x } { | x | } , } & { \text { if } x < 0 } \\ { 3 , } & { \text { if } x \geq 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x= 0.
question_text: Determine the value of the constant 'k' so that the function f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \frac { k x } { | x | } , } & { \text { if } x < 0 } \\ { 3 , } & { \text { if } x \geq 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x= 0.
explanation: Let f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \frac { k x } { | x | } , } & { \text { if } x < 0 } \\ { 3 , } & { \text { if } x \geq 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} be continuous at x = 0 Then, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = f ( 0 ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f ( 0 + h ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f ( 0 - h ) = f ( 0 ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { k ( - h ) } { |- h | } = 3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \left( \frac { - k h } { h } \right) = 3{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( - k ) = 3{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} k = - 3
solution: Let f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \frac { k x } { | x | } , } & { \text { if } x < 0 } \\ { 3 , } & { \text { if } x \geq 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} be continuous at x = 0 Then, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = f ( 0 ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f ( 0 + h ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f ( 0 - h ) = f ( 0 ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { k ( - h ) } { |- h | } = 3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \left( \frac { - k h } { h } \right) = 3{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( - k ) = 3{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} k = - 3
solution_html:
Let f(x) ={tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \frac { k x } { | x | } , } & { \text { if } x < 0 } \\ { 3 , } & { \text { if } x \geq 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} be continuous at x = 0

Then,{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = f ( 0 ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f ( 0 + h ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f ( 0 - h ) = f ( 0 ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 3 = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { k ( - h ) } { |- h | } = 3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \left( \frac { - k h } { h } \right) = 3{/tex}
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( - k ) = 3{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} k = - 3

question_html: If sin2y + cosxy = k, find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} at x = 1, {tex}y=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}.
question_text: If sin 2 y + cosxy = k, find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} at x = 1, {tex}y=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} .
explanation: We have, sin 2 y + cosxy = K Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, {tex}\frac { d } { d x } \left( \sin ^ { 2 } y + \cos x y \right) = \frac { d } { d x } ( k ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d } { d x } \left( \sin ^ { 2 } y + \frac { d } { d x } ( \cos x y ) = 0\right.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \sin y \cos y \frac { d y } { d x } + ( - \sin x y ) \frac { d } { d x } ( x y ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\sin 2 y \frac { d y } { d x } - \sin x y \left( x \frac { d y } { d x } + y \cdot 1 \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin 2 y \frac { d y } { d x } - x \sin x y \frac { d y } { d x } = y \sin x y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y \sin ( x y ) } { \sin 2 y - x \sin ( x y ) }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right){/tex} at x = 1, y = {tex}\frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \frac { \pi } { 4 } \sin \left( 1 \cdot \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) } { \sin \left( 2 \cdot \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) - 1 \sin \left( 1 \cdot \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \frac { \pi } { 4 } \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) } { \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) - \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \frac { \pi } { 4 } \left( \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right) } { 1 - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \frac { \pi } { 4 \sqrt { 2 } } } { ( \sqrt { 2 } - 1 ) / \sqrt { 2 } } {/tex} {tex}= \frac { \pi } { 4 \sqrt { 2 } } \times \frac { \sqrt { 2 } } { ( \sqrt { 2 } - 1 ) } {/tex} {tex}= \frac { \pi } { 4 ( \sqrt { 2 } - 1 ) }{/tex}
solution: We have, sin 2 y + cosxy = K Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get, {tex}\frac { d } { d x } \left( \sin ^ { 2 } y + \cos x y \right) = \frac { d } { d x } ( k ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d } { d x } \left( \sin ^ { 2 } y + \frac { d } { d x } ( \cos x y ) = 0\right.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \sin y \cos y \frac { d y } { d x } + ( - \sin x y ) \frac { d } { d x } ( x y ) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\sin 2 y \frac { d y } { d x } - \sin x y \left( x \frac { d y } { d x } + y \cdot 1 \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin 2 y \frac { d y } { d x } - x \sin x y \frac { d y } { d x } = y \sin x y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y \sin ( x y ) } { \sin 2 y - x \sin ( x y ) }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right){/tex} at x = 1, y = {tex}\frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \frac { \pi } { 4 } \sin \left( 1 \cdot \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) } { \sin \left( 2 \cdot \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) - 1 \sin \left( 1 \cdot \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \frac { \pi } { 4 } \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) } { \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) - \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \frac { \pi } { 4 } \left( \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right) } { 1 - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \frac { \pi } { 4 \sqrt { 2 } } } { ( \sqrt { 2 } - 1 ) / \sqrt { 2 } } {/tex} {tex}= \frac { \pi } { 4 \sqrt { 2 } } \times \frac { \sqrt { 2 } } { ( \sqrt { 2 } - 1 ) } {/tex} {tex}= \frac { \pi } { 4 ( \sqrt { 2 } - 1 ) }{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, sin2 y + cosxy = K
Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get,
{tex}\frac { d } { d x } \left( \sin ^ { 2 } y + \cos x y \right) = \frac { d } { d x } ( k ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d } { d x } \left( \sin ^ { 2 } y + \frac { d } { d x } ( \cos x y ) = 0\right.{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 \sin y \cos y \frac { d y } { d x } + ( - \sin x y ) \frac { d } { d x } ( x y ) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\sin 2 y \frac { d y } { d x } - \sin x y \left( x \frac { d y } { d x } + y \cdot 1 \right) = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \sin 2 y \frac { d y } { d x } - x \sin x y \frac { d y } { d x } = y \sin x y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { y \sin ( x y ) } { \sin 2 y - x \sin ( x y ) }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right){/tex} at x = 1, y = {tex}\frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \frac { \pi } { 4 } \sin \left( 1 \cdot \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) } { \sin \left( 2 \cdot \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) - 1 \sin \left( 1 \cdot \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \frac { \pi } { 4 } \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) } { \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) - \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \frac { \pi } { 4 } \left( \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } \right) } { 1 - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \frac { \pi } { 4 \sqrt { 2 } } } { ( \sqrt { 2 } - 1 ) / \sqrt { 2 } } {/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \pi } { 4 \sqrt { 2 } } \times \frac { \sqrt { 2 } } { ( \sqrt { 2 } - 1 ) } {/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \pi } { 4 ( \sqrt { 2 } - 1 ) }{/tex}

question_html: If y = {tex}\sin ^ { - 1 } \left( 6 x \sqrt { 1 - 9 x ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex},{tex}- \frac { 1 } { 3 \sqrt { 2 } } < x < \frac { 1 } { 3 \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex}, then find {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}.
question_text: If y = {tex}\sin ^ { - 1 } \left( 6 x \sqrt { 1 - 9 x ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} , {tex}- \frac { 1 } { 3 \sqrt { 2 } } < x < \frac { 1 } { 3 \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex} , then find {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} .
explanation: Given, {tex}y = \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( 6 x \sqrt { 1 - 9 x ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( 2 \cdot 3 x \sqrt { 1 - ( 3 x ) ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} We shall make use of trignometric substitution to find {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} . Put 3x = sin {tex}\theta{/tex} , then {tex}y = \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( 2 \sin \theta \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin ^ { - 1 } ( 2 \sin \theta \cdot \cos \theta ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin ^ { - 1 } ( \sin 2 \theta ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = 2 \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = 2 \sin ^ { - 1 } ( 3 x ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 1 - 9 x ^ { 2 } } } ( 3 ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 6 } { \sqrt { 1 - 9 x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
solution: Given, {tex}y = \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( 6 x \sqrt { 1 - 9 x ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( 2 \cdot 3 x \sqrt { 1 - ( 3 x ) ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} We shall make use of trignometric substitution to find {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} . Put 3x = sin {tex}\theta{/tex} , then {tex}y = \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( 2 \sin \theta \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin ^ { - 1 } ( 2 \sin \theta \cdot \cos \theta ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin ^ { - 1 } ( \sin 2 \theta ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = 2 \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = 2 \sin ^ { - 1 } ( 3 x ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 1 - 9 x ^ { 2 } } } ( 3 ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 6 } { \sqrt { 1 - 9 x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, {tex}y = \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( 6 x \sqrt { 1 - 9 x ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( 2 \cdot 3 x \sqrt { 1 - ( 3 x ) ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex}

We shall make use of trignometric substitution to find {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}.
Put 3x = sin{tex}\theta{/tex}, then
{tex}y = \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( 2 \sin \theta \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin ^ { - 1 } ( 2 \sin \theta \cdot \cos \theta ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin ^ { - 1 } ( \sin 2 \theta ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = 2 \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = 2 \sin ^ { - 1 } ( 3 x ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 1 - 9 x ^ { 2 } } } ( 3 ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 6 } { \sqrt { 1 - 9 x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}

question_html: If x = 3 cos t - 2 cos3t,  y = 3 sin t - 2 sin3 t, find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}
question_text: If x = 3 cos t - 2 cos 3 t, y = 3 sin t - 2 sin 3 t, find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}
explanation: Here, x = 3 cot t - 2cos 3 t, y = 3 sin t - 2sin 3 t Now, differentiating x and y with respect to t {tex}\frac{dy}{dt}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d}{dt}{/tex} (3 sin t - 2 sin 3 t) = 3 cos t - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 sin 2 t {tex}\times{/tex} cos t = 3 cos t(1 - 2 sin 2 t) = 3 cos t cos 2t Also, {tex}\frac{dx}{dt}{/tex} = -3 sin t - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 cos 2 t {tex}\times{/tex} (-sin t) = -3 sint (1- 2 cos 2 t) = 3 sin t cos 2t {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{dy}{dt}}{\frac{dx}{dt}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 cos \ t \ cos \ 2t}{3 sin \ t \ cos \ 2t}{/tex} = cot t Differentiating Again w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex} (cot t) = {tex}\frac{d}{dt}{/tex} cot t {tex}\times \frac{1}{\frac{dx}{dt}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-cosec^2 \ t}{3 sin \ t \ cos \ 2t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-cosec^2 \ t}{3 sin \ t \ cos \ 2t}{/tex}
solution: Here, x = 3 cot t - 2cos 3 t, y = 3 sin t - 2sin 3 t Now, differentiating x and y with respect to t {tex}\frac{dy}{dt}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d}{dt}{/tex} (3 sin t - 2 sin 3 t) = 3 cos t - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 sin 2 t {tex}\times{/tex} cos t = 3 cos t(1 - 2 sin 2 t) = 3 cos t cos 2t Also, {tex}\frac{dx}{dt}{/tex} = -3 sin t - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 cos 2 t {tex}\times{/tex} (-sin t) = -3 sint (1- 2 cos 2 t) = 3 sin t cos 2t {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{dy}{dt}}{\frac{dx}{dt}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 cos \ t \ cos \ 2t}{3 sin \ t \ cos \ 2t}{/tex} = cot t Differentiating Again w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex} (cot t) = {tex}\frac{d}{dt}{/tex} cot t {tex}\times \frac{1}{\frac{dx}{dt}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-cosec^2 \ t}{3 sin \ t \ cos \ 2t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-cosec^2 \ t}{3 sin \ t \ cos \ 2t}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, x = 3 cot t - 2cos3 t, y = 3 sin t - 2sin3 t
Now, differentiating x and y with respect to t
{tex}\frac{dy}{dt}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d}{dt}{/tex}(3 sin t - 2 sin3 t)
= 3 cos t - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 sin2 t {tex}\times{/tex} cos t 
= 3 cos t(1 - 2 sin2 t)
= 3 cos t cos 2t
Also,
{tex}\frac{dx}{dt}{/tex} = -3 sin t - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 cos2 t {tex}\times{/tex} (-sin t)
= -3 sint (1- 2 cos2 t)
= 3 sin t cos 2t
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{dy}{dt}}{\frac{dx}{dt}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 cos \ t \ cos \ 2t}{3 sin \ t \ cos \ 2t}{/tex} = cot t
Differentiating  Again w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex} (cot t) = {tex}\frac{d}{dt}{/tex}cot t {tex}\times \frac{1}{\frac{dx}{dt}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{-cosec^2 \ t}{3 sin \ t \ cos \ 2t}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-cosec^2 \ t}{3 sin \ t \ cos \ 2t}{/tex}

question_html: If y = (cos x)x + sin-1{tex}\sqrt3x{/tex}, find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
question_text: If y = (cos x) x + sin -1 {tex}\sqrt3x{/tex} , find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
explanation: Let u = (cos x) x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = x log cos x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = (cos x) x [-x tan x + log cos x] .......(1) {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = (cos x) x + sin -1 {tex}\sqrt{3x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = u + sin -1 {tex}\sqrt{3x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-3 x}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2 \sqrt{x}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = (cos x) [-x tan x log cos x] + {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2 \sqrt{x}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-3 x}}{/tex} {from (1)}
solution: Let u = (cos x) x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = x log cos x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = (cos x) x [-x tan x + log cos x] .......(1) {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = (cos x) x + sin -1 {tex}\sqrt{3x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = u + sin -1 {tex}\sqrt{3x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-3 x}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2 \sqrt{x}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = (cos x) [-x tan x log cos x] + {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2 \sqrt{x}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-3 x}}{/tex} {from (1)}
solution_html:
Let u = (cos x)x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = x log cos x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex}= (cos x)x [-x tan x + log cos x]  .......(1)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y = (cos x)x + sin-1 {tex}\sqrt{3x}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}y = u + sin-1{tex}\sqrt{3x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}={tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex}+{tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-3 x}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2 \sqrt{x}}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = (cos x) [-x tan x log cos x] + {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2 \sqrt{x}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-3 x}}{/tex}    {from (1)}

question_text: If the following function f(x) is continuous at x = 0, then write the value of k. {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \frac{\sin \frac{3 x}{2}}{x} & , x \neq 0 \\ k & , x=0 \end{array}\right.{/tex}

Find the value of p for which the function

question_text: Find the value of p for which the function {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \frac{1-\cos 4 x}{x^2} & , x \neq 0 \\ p & , x=0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0.
explanation: {tex}\lim\limits _{x \rightarrow 0} {/tex} f(x) = f(0) {tex}\lim\limits _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{4 \times 2 \sin ^2 2 x}{4 x^2}=p{/tex} {tex}8 \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{\sin 2 x}{2 x}\right)^2=p{/tex} p = 8
solution: {tex}\lim\limits _{x \rightarrow 0} {/tex} f(x) = f(0) {tex}\lim\limits _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{4 \times 2 \sin ^2 2 x}{4 x^2}=p{/tex} {tex}8 \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{\sin 2 x}{2 x}\right)^2=p{/tex} p = 8
