subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the value of k for which the function {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \frac{\sin x-\cos x}{4 x-\pi} & , x \neq \frac{\pi}{4} \\ k & , x=\frac{\pi}{4} \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at {tex}x=\frac{x}{4}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the value of k for which the function {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \frac{\sin x-\cos x}{4 x-\pi} & , x \neq \frac{\pi}{4} \\ k & , x=\frac{\pi}{4} \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at {tex}x=\frac{x}{4}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\lim\limits _{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sin x-\cos x}{4 x-\pi}=f\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex} {tex}\lim\limits _{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sqrt{2}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sin x-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cos x\right)}{4 x-\pi}=k{/tex} {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sqrt{2}\left(\sin x \cos \frac{\pi}{4}-\cos x \cdot \sin \frac{\pi}{4}\right)}{4 x-\pi}=k{/tex} {tex}\lim\limits _{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sqrt{2} \sin \left(x-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)}{4\left(x-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)}=k{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2}}{4}=k{/tex} {tex}k=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\lim\limits _{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sin x-\cos x}{4 x-\pi}=f\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex} {tex}\lim\limits _{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sqrt{2}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sin x-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cos x\right)}{4 x-\pi}=k{/tex} {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sqrt{2}\left(\sin x \cos \frac{\pi}{4}-\cos x \cdot \sin \frac{\pi}{4}\right)}{4 x-\pi}=k{/tex} {tex}\lim\limits _{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sqrt{2} \sin \left(x-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)}{4\left(x-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)}=k{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2}}{4}=k{/tex} {tex}k=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\lim\limits _{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sin x-\cos x}{4 x-\pi}=f\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex}
{tex}\lim\limits _{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sqrt{2}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sin x-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cos x\right)}{4 x-\pi}=k{/tex}
{tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sqrt{2}\left(\sin x \cos \frac{\pi}{4}-\cos x \cdot \sin \frac{\pi}{4}\right)}{4 x-\pi}=k{/tex}
{tex}\lim\limits _{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{4}} \frac{\sqrt{2} \sin \left(x-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)}{4\left(x-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)}=k{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\sqrt{2}}{4}=k{/tex}
{tex}k=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}x = a\sin 2t\left( {1 + \cos 2t} \right){/tex} and {tex}y = b\cos 2t\left( {1 - \cos 2t} \right){/tex}, show that at {tex}t=\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{b}{a}{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}x = a\sin 2t\left( {1 + \cos 2t} \right){/tex} and {tex}y = b\cos 2t\left( {1 - \cos 2t} \right){/tex} , show that at {tex}t=\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{b}{a}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\because x = a\sin 2t\left( {1 + \cos 2t} \right){/tex} and {tex}y = b\cos 2t\left( {1 - \cos 2t} \right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = a\left[ {\sin 2t.\frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {1 + \cos 2t} \right) + \left( {1 + \cos 2t} \right).\frac{d}{{dt}}\sin 2t} \right]{/tex} {tex}= a\left[ {\sin 2t.\left( { - \sin 2t} \right).\frac{d}{{dt}}2t + \left( {1 + \cos 2t} \right).\cos 2t.\frac{d}{{dt}}2t} \right]{/tex} {tex}= - 2a{\sin ^2}2t + 2a\cos 2t\left( {1 + \cos 2t} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = - 2a\left[ {{{\sin }^2}2t - \cos 2t\left( {1 + \cos 2t} \right)} \right]{/tex} ...(i) and {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = b\left[ {\cos 2t.\frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {1 - \cos 2t} \right) + \left( {1 - \cos 2t} \right).\frac{d}{{dt}}\cos 2t} \right]{/tex} {tex}= b\left[ {\cos 2t.\left( {\sin 2t} \right)\frac{d}{{dt}}2t + \left( {1 - \cos 2t} \right)\left( { - \sin 2t} \right).\frac{d}{{dt}}2t} \right]{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex}= b\left[ {2\sin 2t.\cos 2t + 2\left( {1 - \cos 2t} \right)\left( { - \sin 2t} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}= -2b\left[ -{\sin 2t.\cos 2t + \left( {1 - \cos 2t} \right)\sin 2t} \right]{/tex} ..(ii) {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{dy/dt}}{{dx/dt}} = \frac{{ - 2b\left[ { - \sin 2t.\cos 2t + \left( {1 - \cos 2t} \right)\sin 2t} \right]}}{{ - 2a\left[ {{{\sin }^2}2t - \cos 2t\left( {1 + \cos 2t} \right)} \right]}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right)_{t =\pi /4}} = \frac{b}{a}\frac{{\left[ { - \sin \frac{\pi }{2}.\cos \frac{\pi }{2} + \left( {1 - \cos \frac{\pi }{2}} \right)\sin \frac{\pi }{2}} \right]}}{{ \left[ {{{\sin }^2}\frac{\pi }{2} - \cos \frac{\pi }{2}\left( {1 + \cos \frac{\pi }{2}} \right)} \right]}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{b}{a}.\frac{{\left( {0 + 1} \right)}}{{\left( {1 - 0} \right)}}\left[ {\because \sin \frac{\pi }{2} = 1\,\,and\,\,\cos \frac{\pi }{2} = 0} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{b}{a}{/tex} Hence Proved.
solution: {tex}\because x = a\sin 2t\left( {1 + \cos 2t} \right){/tex} and {tex}y = b\cos 2t\left( {1 - \cos 2t} \right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = a\left[ {\sin 2t.\frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {1 + \cos 2t} \right) + \left( {1 + \cos 2t} \right).\frac{d}{{dt}}\sin 2t} \right]{/tex} {tex}= a\left[ {\sin 2t.\left( { - \sin 2t} \right).\frac{d}{{dt}}2t + \left( {1 + \cos 2t} \right).\cos 2t.\frac{d}{{dt}}2t} \right]{/tex} {tex}= - 2a{\sin ^2}2t + 2a\cos 2t\left( {1 + \cos 2t} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = - 2a\left[ {{{\sin }^2}2t - \cos 2t\left( {1 + \cos 2t} \right)} \right]{/tex} ...(i) and {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = b\left[ {\cos 2t.\frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {1 - \cos 2t} \right) + \left( {1 - \cos 2t} \right).\frac{d}{{dt}}\cos 2t} \right]{/tex} {tex}= b\left[ {\cos 2t.\left( {\sin 2t} \right)\frac{d}{{dt}}2t + \left( {1 - \cos 2t} \right)\left( { - \sin 2t} \right).\frac{d}{{dt}}2t} \right]{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex}= b\left[ {2\sin 2t.\cos 2t + 2\left( {1 - \cos 2t} \right)\left( { - \sin 2t} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}= -2b\left[ -{\sin 2t.\cos 2t + \left( {1 - \cos 2t} \right)\sin 2t} \right]{/tex} ..(ii) {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{dy/dt}}{{dx/dt}} = \frac{{ - 2b\left[ { - \sin 2t.\cos 2t + \left( {1 - \cos 2t} \right)\sin 2t} \right]}}{{ - 2a\left[ {{{\sin }^2}2t - \cos 2t\left( {1 + \cos 2t} \right)} \right]}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right)_{t =\pi /4}} = \frac{b}{a}\frac{{\left[ { - \sin \frac{\pi }{2}.\cos \frac{\pi }{2} + \left( {1 - \cos \frac{\pi }{2}} \right)\sin \frac{\pi }{2}} \right]}}{{ \left[ {{{\sin }^2}\frac{\pi }{2} - \cos \frac{\pi }{2}\left( {1 + \cos \frac{\pi }{2}} \right)} \right]}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{b}{a}.\frac{{\left( {0 + 1} \right)}}{{\left( {1 - 0} \right)}}\left[ {\because \sin \frac{\pi }{2} = 1\,\,and\,\,\cos \frac{\pi }{2} = 0} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{b}{a}{/tex} Hence Proved.
solution_html:
{tex}\because x = a\sin 2t\left( {1 + \cos 2t} \right){/tex} and {tex}y = b\cos 2t\left( {1 - \cos 2t} \right){/tex}

{tex}\therefore \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = a\left[ {\sin 2t.\frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {1 + \cos 2t} \right) + \left( {1 + \cos 2t} \right).\frac{d}{{dt}}\sin 2t} \right]{/tex}

{tex}= a\left[ {\sin 2t.\left( { - \sin 2t} \right).\frac{d}{{dt}}2t + \left( {1 + \cos 2t} \right).\cos 2t.\frac{d}{{dt}}2t} \right]{/tex}

{tex}= - 2a{\sin ^2}2t + 2a\cos 2t\left( {1 + \cos 2t} \right){/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = - 2a\left[ {{{\sin }^2}2t - \cos 2t\left( {1 + \cos 2t} \right)} \right]{/tex} ...(i)

and {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = b\left[ {\cos 2t.\frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {1 - \cos 2t} \right) + \left( {1 - \cos 2t} \right).\frac{d}{{dt}}\cos 2t} \right]{/tex}

{tex}= b\left[ {\cos 2t.\left( {\sin 2t} \right)\frac{d}{{dt}}2t + \left( {1 - \cos 2t} \right)\left( { - \sin 2t} \right).\frac{d}{{dt}}2t} \right]{/tex}

{tex}{/tex}

{tex}= b\left[ {2\sin 2t.\cos 2t + 2\left( {1 - \cos 2t} \right)\left( { - \sin 2t} \right)} \right]{/tex}

{tex}= -2b\left[ -{\sin 2t.\cos 2t + \left( {1 - \cos 2t} \right)\sin 2t} \right]{/tex}..(ii)

{tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{dy/dt}}{{dx/dt}} = \frac{{ - 2b\left[ { - \sin 2t.\cos 2t + \left( {1 - \cos 2t} \right)\sin 2t} \right]}}{{ - 2a\left[ {{{\sin }^2}2t - \cos 2t\left( {1 + \cos 2t} \right)} \right]}}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right)_{t =\pi /4}} = \frac{b}{a}\frac{{\left[ { - \sin \frac{\pi }{2}.\cos \frac{\pi }{2} + \left( {1 - \cos \frac{\pi }{2}} \right)\sin \frac{\pi }{2}} \right]}}{{ \left[ {{{\sin }^2}\frac{\pi }{2} - \cos \frac{\pi }{2}\left( {1 + \cos \frac{\pi }{2}} \right)} \right]}}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{b}{a}.\frac{{\left( {0 + 1} \right)}}{{\left( {1 - 0} \right)}}\left[ {\because \sin \frac{\pi }{2} = 1\,\,and\,\,\cos \frac{\pi }{2} = 0} \right]{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{b}{a}{/tex} Hence Proved.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If y = 3 cos (log x) + 4 sin (log x). Show that x2y2 + xy1 + y = 0
question_text: If y = 3 cos (log x) + 4 sin (log x). Show that x 2 y 2 + xy 1 + y = 0
explanation: {tex}y = 3\cos (\log x) + 4\sin (\log x){/tex} Differentiating both side w.r.t. to x {tex}{y_1} = \frac{{ - 3\sin (\log x)}}{x} + \frac{{4\cos (\log x)}}{x}{/tex} {tex}x{y_1} = - 3\sin (\log x) + 4\cos (\log x){/tex} Again differentiating, {tex}x{y_2} + {y_1}.1 = \frac{{ - 3\cos (\log x)}}{x} - \frac{{4\sin (\log x)}}{x}{/tex} {tex}{x^2}{y_2} + x{y_1} = - 3\cos (\log x) - 4\sin (\log x){/tex} {tex}{x^2}{y_2} + x{y_1} = - y{/tex} {tex}{x^2}{y_2} + x{y_1} + y = 0{/tex}
solution: {tex}y = 3\cos (\log x) + 4\sin (\log x){/tex} Differentiating both side w.r.t. to x {tex}{y_1} = \frac{{ - 3\sin (\log x)}}{x} + \frac{{4\cos (\log x)}}{x}{/tex} {tex}x{y_1} = - 3\sin (\log x) + 4\cos (\log x){/tex} Again differentiating, {tex}x{y_2} + {y_1}.1 = \frac{{ - 3\cos (\log x)}}{x} - \frac{{4\sin (\log x)}}{x}{/tex} {tex}{x^2}{y_2} + x{y_1} = - 3\cos (\log x) - 4\sin (\log x){/tex} {tex}{x^2}{y_2} + x{y_1} = - y{/tex} {tex}{x^2}{y_2} + x{y_1} + y = 0{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}y = 3\cos (\log x) + 4\sin (\log x){/tex}

Differentiating both side w.r.t. to x

{tex}{y_1} = \frac{{ - 3\sin (\log x)}}{x} + \frac{{4\cos (\log x)}}{x}{/tex}

{tex}x{y_1} = - 3\sin (\log x) + 4\cos (\log x){/tex}

Again differentiating,

{tex}x{y_2} + {y_1}.1 = \frac{{ - 3\cos (\log x)}}{x} - \frac{{4\sin (\log x)}}{x}{/tex}

{tex}{x^2}{y_2} + x{y_1} = - 3\cos (\log x) - 4\sin (\log x){/tex}

{tex}{x^2}{y_2} + x{y_1} = - y{/tex}

 {tex}{x^2}{y_2} + x{y_1} + y = 0{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Differentiate {tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } - 1 } { x } \right){/tex} with respect to {tex}\sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 2 x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex},if -1 < x < 1, x {tex}\ne{/tex}0.
question_text: Differentiate {tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } - 1 } { x } \right){/tex} with respect to {tex}\sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 2 x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} ,if -1 < x < 1, x {tex}\ne{/tex} 0.
explanation: Let u = {tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } - 1 } { x } \right){/tex} Put x = tan {tex}\theta\Rightarrow \theta = \tan ^ { - 1 } x{/tex} , then {tex}u = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { \sqrt { 1 + \tan ^ { 2 } \theta } - 1 } { \tan \theta } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \tan ^ { -1 } \left[ \frac { \sqrt { \sec ^ { 2 } \theta } - 1 } { \tan \theta } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \tan ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { \sec \theta - 1 } { \tan \theta } \right] = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 1 - \cos \theta } { \sin \theta } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \tan ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta / 2 } { 2 \sin \theta / 2 \cdot \cos \theta / 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \tan ^ { - 1 } [ \tan \theta / 2 ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad u = \frac { \theta } { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } x{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac { d u } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { 2 \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \frac { d } { d x } \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) = \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex} ..........(i) Also, let v = {tex}\sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 2 x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} Put x = tan {tex}\theta \Rightarrow \theta = \tan ^ { - 1 } x{/tex} , then we get, v = {tex}\sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { \text{tan}\theta } { 1 + \tan ^ { 2 } \theta } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad v = \sin ^ { - 1 } [ \sin 2 \theta ] \Rightarrow v = 2 \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} v = 2tan -1 x On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 2 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ............(ii) Now, {tex}\frac { d u } { d v } = \frac { d u } { d x } \times \frac { d x } { d v }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } \times \frac { \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } { 2 }{/tex} [ From Eq(i) and (ii)] {tex}\therefore \frac { d u } { d v } = \frac { 1 } { 4 }{/tex}
solution: Let u = {tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } - 1 } { x } \right){/tex} Put x = tan {tex}\theta\Rightarrow \theta = \tan ^ { - 1 } x{/tex} , then {tex}u = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { \sqrt { 1 + \tan ^ { 2 } \theta } - 1 } { \tan \theta } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \tan ^ { -1 } \left[ \frac { \sqrt { \sec ^ { 2 } \theta } - 1 } { \tan \theta } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \tan ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { \sec \theta - 1 } { \tan \theta } \right] = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 1 - \cos \theta } { \sin \theta } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \tan ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta / 2 } { 2 \sin \theta / 2 \cdot \cos \theta / 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \tan ^ { - 1 } [ \tan \theta / 2 ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad u = \frac { \theta } { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } x{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac { d u } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { 2 \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \frac { d } { d x } \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) = \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex} ..........(i) Also, let v = {tex}\sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 2 x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} Put x = tan {tex}\theta \Rightarrow \theta = \tan ^ { - 1 } x{/tex} , then we get, v = {tex}\sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { \text{tan}\theta } { 1 + \tan ^ { 2 } \theta } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad v = \sin ^ { - 1 } [ \sin 2 \theta ] \Rightarrow v = 2 \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} v = 2tan -1 x On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 2 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ............(ii) Now, {tex}\frac { d u } { d v } = \frac { d u } { d x } \times \frac { d x } { d v }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } \times \frac { \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } { 2 }{/tex} [ From Eq(i) and (ii)] {tex}\therefore \frac { d u } { d v } = \frac { 1 } { 4 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Let u = {tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } - 1 } { x } \right){/tex}

Put x = tan {tex}\theta\Rightarrow \theta = \tan ^ { - 1 } x{/tex}, then
{tex}u = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { \sqrt { 1 + \tan ^ { 2 } \theta } - 1 } { \tan \theta } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \tan ^ { -1 } \left[ \frac { \sqrt { \sec ^ { 2 } \theta } - 1 } { \tan \theta } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \tan ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { \sec \theta - 1 } { \tan \theta } \right] = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 1 - \cos \theta } { \sin \theta } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \tan ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta / 2 } { 2 \sin \theta / 2 \cdot \cos \theta / 2 } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \tan ^ { - 1 } [ \tan \theta / 2 ]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad u = \frac { \theta } { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \tan ^ { - 1 } x{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac { d u } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { 2 \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) }{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because \frac { d } { d x } \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) = \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex}..........(i)
Also, let v ={tex}\sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 2 x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex}
Put x = tan {tex}\theta \Rightarrow \theta = \tan ^ { - 1 } x{/tex}, then we get,
v = {tex}\sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { \text{tan}\theta } { 1 + \tan ^ { 2 } \theta } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad v = \sin ^ { - 1 } [ \sin 2 \theta ] \Rightarrow v = 2 \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} v = 2tan-1 x
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 2 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}............(ii)
Now, {tex}\frac { d u } { d v } = \frac { d u } { d x } \times \frac { d x } { d v }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } \times \frac { \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } { 2 }{/tex}[ From Eq(i) and (ii)]
{tex}\therefore \frac { d u } { d v } = \frac { 1 } { 4 }{/tex}

question_html: y = xx, prove that {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { y } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 } - \frac { y } { x } = 0{/tex}.
question_text: y = x x , prove that {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { y } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 } - \frac { y } { x } = 0{/tex} .
explanation: According to the question, {tex}y = x^x{/tex} Taking log both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow log\ y= log\ x^x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}log\ y = x log\ x{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { y } \frac { d y } { d x } = x \frac { d } { d x } ( \log x ) + \log x \frac { d } { d x } ( x ){/tex} [Using product rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { y } \frac { d y } { d x } = x \times \frac { 1 } { x } + \log x \cdot 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { y } \frac { d y } { d x } = ( 1 + \log x ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = y ( 1 + \log x ){/tex} .......(i) Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = y \frac { d } { d x } ( 1 + \log x ) + ( 1 + \log x ) \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} [Using product rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = y \times \frac { 1 } { x } + ( 1 + \log x ) \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { y } { x } + ( 1 + \log x ) \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { y } { x } + \frac { 1 } { y } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right){/tex} [ Using Equation(i)] {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { y } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 } - \frac { y } { x } = 0{/tex} Hence Proved
solution: According to the question, {tex}y = x^x{/tex} Taking log both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow log\ y= log\ x^x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}log\ y = x log\ x{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { y } \frac { d y } { d x } = x \frac { d } { d x } ( \log x ) + \log x \frac { d } { d x } ( x ){/tex} [Using product rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { y } \frac { d y } { d x } = x \times \frac { 1 } { x } + \log x \cdot 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { y } \frac { d y } { d x } = ( 1 + \log x ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = y ( 1 + \log x ){/tex} .......(i) Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = y \frac { d } { d x } ( 1 + \log x ) + ( 1 + \log x ) \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} [Using product rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = y \times \frac { 1 } { x } + ( 1 + \log x ) \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { y } { x } + ( 1 + \log x ) \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { y } { x } + \frac { 1 } { y } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right){/tex} [ Using Equation(i)] {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { y } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 } - \frac { y } { x } = 0{/tex} Hence Proved
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}y = x^x{/tex}
Taking log both sides, 
{tex}\Rightarrow log\ y= log\ x^x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}log\ y = x log\ x{/tex}
Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { y } \frac { d y } { d x } = x \frac { d } { d x } ( \log x ) + \log x \frac { d } { d x } ( x ){/tex} [Using product rule of derivative]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { y } \frac { d y } { d x } = x \times \frac { 1 } { x } + \log x \cdot 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { y } \frac { d y } { d x } = ( 1 + \log x ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = y ( 1 + \log x ){/tex}.......(i)
Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = y \frac { d } { d x } ( 1 + \log x ) + ( 1 + \log x ) \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} [Using product rule of derivative]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = y \times \frac { 1 } { x } + ( 1 + \log x ) \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { y } { x } + ( 1 + \log x ) \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { y } { x } + \frac { 1 } { y } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right){/tex}[ Using Equation(i)]
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { y } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 } - \frac { y } { x } = 0{/tex}
Hence Proved

question_html: If f(x) ={tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \frac { \sin ( a + 1 ) x + 2 \sin x } { x } , } & { x < 0 } \\ { 2, } & { x = 0 } \\ { \frac { \sqrt { 1 + b x } - 1 } { x } , } & { x > 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0, then find the values of a and b.
question_text: If f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \frac { \sin ( a + 1 ) x + 2 \sin x } { x } , } & { x < 0 } \\ { 2, } & { x = 0 } \\ { \frac { \sqrt { 1 + b x } - 1 } { x } , } & { x > 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0, then find the values of a and b.
explanation: If, {tex}f(x){/tex} = {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \frac { \sin ( a + 1 ) x + 2 \sin x } { x } , } & { x < 0 } \\ { 2, } & { x = 0 } \\ { \frac { \sqrt { 1 + b x } - 1 } { x } , } & { x > 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x =0, We shall use definition of continuity to find the value of constants a and b. Since f(x) is continuous at x=0. {tex}\therefore \quad ( \mathrm { LHL } ) _ { x = 0 } = ( \mathrm { RHL } ) _ { x = 0 } = f ( 0 ){/tex} .......(i) LHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f ( 0 - h ){/tex} [ put x = 0 - h, when x {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0 - , then h {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0] {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin [ ( a + 1 ) ( 0 - h ) ] + 2 \sin ( 0 - h )} { ( 0 - h ) }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { - \sin [ ( a + 1 ) h ] - 2 \sin h } { - h }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \sin ( - \theta ) = - \sin \theta ]{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin ( a + 1 ) h } { h } + \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 2 \sin h } { h }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin ( a + 1 ) h } { ( a + 1 ) h } \times ( a + 1 ) + 2 \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin h } { h }{/tex} {tex}= 1 \times ( a + 1 ) + 2 \times 1{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin x } { x } = 1 \right]{/tex} = {tex}a + 1 + 2 = a + 3{/tex} ...........(ii) RHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f ( 0 + h ){/tex} [ put x = 0 + h, when x {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0 + , then h {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0] {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sqrt { 1 + b ( 0 + h) } - 1 } { 0 + h }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sqrt { 1 + b h } - 1 } { h }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sqrt { 1 + b h } - 1 } { h } \times \frac { \sqrt { 1 + b h } +1} { \sqrt { 1 + b h } + 1 }{/tex} [ multiplying numerator and denominator by {tex}\sqrt { 1 + b h }{/tex} +1] {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { ( 1 + b h ) - 1 } { h ( \sqrt { 1 + b h } + 1 ) } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { b h } { h ( \sqrt { 1 + b h } + 1 ) }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { b } { ( \sqrt { 1 + b h } + 1 ) } = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 1 + 0 } + 1 } = \frac { b } { 2 }{/tex} ............(iii) From Equations (i), (ii) and (iii), {tex}a + 3 {/tex} = {tex}\frac b2{/tex} = 2 [ {tex}\because{/tex} f(0) = 2] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a + 3 = 2 and {tex}\frac b2{/tex} = 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}a = -1\ and\ b = 4{/tex}
solution: If, {tex}f(x){/tex} = {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \frac { \sin ( a + 1 ) x + 2 \sin x } { x } , } & { x < 0 } \\ { 2, } & { x = 0 } \\ { \frac { \sqrt { 1 + b x } - 1 } { x } , } & { x > 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x =0, We shall use definition of continuity to find the value of constants a and b. Since f(x) is continuous at x=0. {tex}\therefore \quad ( \mathrm { LHL } ) _ { x = 0 } = ( \mathrm { RHL } ) _ { x = 0 } = f ( 0 ){/tex} .......(i) LHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f ( 0 - h ){/tex} [ put x = 0 - h, when x {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0 - , then h {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0] {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin [ ( a + 1 ) ( 0 - h ) ] + 2 \sin ( 0 - h )} { ( 0 - h ) }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { - \sin [ ( a + 1 ) h ] - 2 \sin h } { - h }{/tex} {tex}[ \because \sin ( - \theta ) = - \sin \theta ]{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin ( a + 1 ) h } { h } + \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 2 \sin h } { h }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin ( a + 1 ) h } { ( a + 1 ) h } \times ( a + 1 ) + 2 \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin h } { h }{/tex} {tex}= 1 \times ( a + 1 ) + 2 \times 1{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin x } { x } = 1 \right]{/tex} = {tex}a + 1 + 2 = a + 3{/tex} ...........(ii) RHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f ( 0 + h ){/tex} [ put x = 0 + h, when x {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0 + , then h {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0] {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sqrt { 1 + b ( 0 + h) } - 1 } { 0 + h }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sqrt { 1 + b h } - 1 } { h }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sqrt { 1 + b h } - 1 } { h } \times \frac { \sqrt { 1 + b h } +1} { \sqrt { 1 + b h } + 1 }{/tex} [ multiplying numerator and denominator by {tex}\sqrt { 1 + b h }{/tex} +1] {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { ( 1 + b h ) - 1 } { h ( \sqrt { 1 + b h } + 1 ) } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { b h } { h ( \sqrt { 1 + b h } + 1 ) }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { b } { ( \sqrt { 1 + b h } + 1 ) } = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 1 + 0 } + 1 } = \frac { b } { 2 }{/tex} ............(iii) From Equations (i), (ii) and (iii), {tex}a + 3 {/tex} = {tex}\frac b2{/tex} = 2 [ {tex}\because{/tex} f(0) = 2] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a + 3 = 2 and {tex}\frac b2{/tex} = 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}a = -1\ and\ b = 4{/tex}
solution_html:
If, {tex}f(x){/tex} ={tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \frac { \sin ( a + 1 ) x + 2 \sin x } { x } , } & { x < 0 } \\ { 2, } & { x = 0 } \\ { \frac { \sqrt { 1 + b x } - 1 } { x } , } & { x > 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x =0,

We shall use definition of continuity to find the value of constants a and b.

Since f(x) is continuous at x=0.
{tex}\therefore \quad ( \mathrm { LHL } ) _ { x = 0 } = ( \mathrm { RHL } ) _ { x = 0 } = f ( 0 ){/tex}.......(i)
LHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f ( 0 - h ){/tex}[ put x = 0 - h, when x{tex}\rightarrow{/tex}0-, then h{tex}\rightarrow{/tex}0]
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin [ ( a + 1 ) ( 0 - h ) ] + 2 \sin ( 0 - h )} { ( 0 - h ) }{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { - \sin [ ( a + 1 ) h ] - 2 \sin h } { - h }{/tex}{tex}[ \because \sin ( - \theta ) = - \sin \theta ]{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin ( a + 1 ) h } { h } + \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 2 \sin h } { h }{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin ( a + 1 ) h } { ( a + 1 ) h } \times ( a + 1 ) + 2 \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin h } { h }{/tex}
{tex}= 1 \times ( a + 1 ) + 2 \times 1{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin x } { x } = 1 \right]{/tex}
= {tex}a + 1 + 2 = a + 3{/tex}...........(ii)
RHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f ( 0 + h ){/tex}[ put x = 0 + h, when x{tex}\rightarrow{/tex}0+, then h{tex}\rightarrow{/tex}0]
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sqrt { 1 + b ( 0 + h) } - 1 } { 0 + h }{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sqrt { 1 + b h } - 1 } { h }{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sqrt { 1 + b h } - 1 } { h } \times \frac { \sqrt { 1 + b h } +1} { \sqrt { 1 + b h } + 1 }{/tex}[ multiplying numerator and denominator by {tex}\sqrt { 1 + b h }{/tex}+1]
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { ( 1 + b h ) - 1 } { h ( \sqrt { 1 + b h } + 1 ) } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { b h } { h ( \sqrt { 1 + b h } + 1 ) }{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { b } { ( \sqrt { 1 + b h } + 1 ) } = \frac { b } { \sqrt { 1 + 0 } + 1 } = \frac { b } { 2 }{/tex}............(iii)
From Equations (i), (ii) and (iii), 
{tex}a + 3 {/tex}= {tex}\frac b2{/tex}= 2 [{tex}\because{/tex} f(0) = 2]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a + 3 = 2 and {tex}\frac b2{/tex}= 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}a = -1\ and\ b = 4{/tex}

question_html: Find {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}, if y = {tex}\sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 6 x - 4 \sqrt { 1 - 4 x ^ { 2 } } } { 5 } \right]{/tex}.
question_text: Find {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} , if y = {tex}\sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 6 x - 4 \sqrt { 1 - 4 x ^ { 2 } } } { 5 } \right]{/tex} .
explanation: Given y = {tex} \sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 6 x - 4 \sqrt { 1 - 4 x ^ { 2 } } } { 5 } \right]{/tex} Put, x = {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}{/tex} Therefore, y = {tex}\sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 6 \times \frac { \sin \theta } { 2 } - 4 \sqrt { 1 - 4 \times \left( \frac { \sin \theta } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } } { 5 } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 3 \sin \theta - 4 \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } } { 5 } \right){/tex} {tex}= \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 3 \sin \theta - 4 \cos \theta } { 5 } \right){/tex} {tex}= \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 3 } { 5 } \sin \theta - \frac { 4 } { 5 } \cos \theta \right){/tex} ...........(i) Let cos {tex}\phi = \frac { 3 } { 5 }{/tex} , therefore, {tex}sin\phi = \sqrt { 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } \phi } = \sqrt { 1 - \left( \frac { 3 } { 5 } \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 1 - \frac { 9 } { 25 } } = \sqrt { \frac { 16 } { 25 } } = \frac { 4 } { 5 }{/tex} Now, Eq(i) becomes, y = {tex}\sin ^ { - 1 } ( \cos \phi \sin \theta - \sin \phi \cos \theta ){/tex} {tex}= \sin ^ { - 1 } [ \sin ( \theta - \phi ) ] = \theta - \phi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin ^ { - 1 } ( 2 x ) - \cos ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 3 } { 5 } \right){/tex} Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - ( 2 x ) ^ { 2 } } } \frac { d } { d x } ( 2 x ) - 0{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 1 - 4 x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
solution: Given y = {tex} \sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 6 x - 4 \sqrt { 1 - 4 x ^ { 2 } } } { 5 } \right]{/tex} Put, x = {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}{/tex} Therefore, y = {tex}\sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 6 \times \frac { \sin \theta } { 2 } - 4 \sqrt { 1 - 4 \times \left( \frac { \sin \theta } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } } { 5 } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 3 \sin \theta - 4 \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } } { 5 } \right){/tex} {tex}= \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 3 \sin \theta - 4 \cos \theta } { 5 } \right){/tex} {tex}= \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 3 } { 5 } \sin \theta - \frac { 4 } { 5 } \cos \theta \right){/tex} ...........(i) Let cos {tex}\phi = \frac { 3 } { 5 }{/tex} , therefore, {tex}sin\phi = \sqrt { 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } \phi } = \sqrt { 1 - \left( \frac { 3 } { 5 } \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \sqrt { 1 - \frac { 9 } { 25 } } = \sqrt { \frac { 16 } { 25 } } = \frac { 4 } { 5 }{/tex} Now, Eq(i) becomes, y = {tex}\sin ^ { - 1 } ( \cos \phi \sin \theta - \sin \phi \cos \theta ){/tex} {tex}= \sin ^ { - 1 } [ \sin ( \theta - \phi ) ] = \theta - \phi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin ^ { - 1 } ( 2 x ) - \cos ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 3 } { 5 } \right){/tex} Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - ( 2 x ) ^ { 2 } } } \frac { d } { d x } ( 2 x ) - 0{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 1 - 4 x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
solution_html:
Given y = {tex} \sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 6 x - 4 \sqrt { 1 - 4 x ^ { 2 } } } { 5 } \right]{/tex}
Put, x = {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } \sin \theta{/tex}
{tex}{/tex} Therefore, y = {tex}\sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 6 \times \frac { \sin \theta } { 2 } - 4 \sqrt { 1 - 4 \times \left( \frac { \sin \theta } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } } { 5 } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 3 \sin \theta - 4 \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } } { 5 } \right){/tex}
{tex}= \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 3 \sin \theta - 4 \cos \theta } { 5 } \right){/tex}
{tex}= \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 3 } { 5 } \sin \theta - \frac { 4 } { 5 } \cos \theta \right){/tex}...........(i)
Let cos {tex}\phi = \frac { 3 } { 5 }{/tex}, therefore,{tex}sin\phi = \sqrt { 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } \phi } = \sqrt { 1 - \left( \frac { 3 } { 5 } \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \sqrt { 1 - \frac { 9 } { 25 } } = \sqrt { \frac { 16 } { 25 } } = \frac { 4 } { 5 }{/tex}
Now, Eq(i) becomes,
y = {tex}\sin ^ { - 1 } ( \cos \phi \sin \theta - \sin \phi \cos \theta ){/tex}
{tex}= \sin ^ { - 1 } [ \sin ( \theta - \phi ) ] = \theta - \phi{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin ^ { - 1 } ( 2 x ) - \cos ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 3 } { 5 } \right){/tex}
Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get,
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - ( 2 x ) ^ { 2 } } } \frac { d } { d x } ( 2 x ) - 0{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 1 - 4 x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}

question_html: Find the values of p and q for which {tex}f ( x ) = \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \frac { 1 - \sin ^ { 3 } x } { 3 \cos ^ { 2 } x } , } & { \text { if } x < \frac { \pi } { 2 } } \\ { p , } & { \text { if } x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } } \\ { \frac { q ( 1 - \sin x ) } { ( \pi - 2 x ) ^ { 2 } } , } & { \text { if } x > \frac { \pi } { 2 } } \end{array} \right.{/tex}is continuous at x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the values of p and q for which {tex}f ( x ) = \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \frac { 1 - \sin ^ { 3 } x } { 3 \cos ^ { 2 } x } , } & { \text { if } x < \frac { \pi } { 2 } } \\ { p , } & { \text { if } x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } } \\ { \frac { q ( 1 - \sin x ) } { ( \pi - 2 x ) ^ { 2 } } , } & { \text { if } x > \frac { \pi } { 2 } } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} .
explanation: Given, f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{aligned} \frac { 1 - \sin ^ { 3 } x } { 3 \cos ^ { 2 } x } , & \text { if } x < \frac { \pi } { 2 } \\ p , & \text { if } x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } \\ \frac { q ( 1 - \sin x ) } { ( \pi - 2 x ) ^ { 2 } } , & \text { if } x > \frac { \pi } { 2 } \end{aligned} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = {tex}\frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex} We shall use definition of continuity to find the values of constants a and b. Since f(x) is continuous at x= {tex}\frac{ {tex}\pi{/tex} }{2}{/tex} , Therefore, {tex}(LHL)_ { x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } } = ( R H L ) _ { x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } } = f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} ........(i) Now, LHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { \pi } { 2 } ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - h \right){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 1 - \sin ^ { 3 } \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - h \right) } { 3 \cos ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - h \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 1 - \cos ^ { 3 } h } { 3 \sin ^ { 2 } h }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { ( 1 - \cos h ) \left( 1 ^ { 2 } + \cos ^ { 2 } h + 1 \times \cos h \right) } { 3 \left( 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } h \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { ( 1 - \cos h ) \left( 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } h + \cos h \right) } { 3 ( 1 - \cos h ) ( 1 + \cos h ) }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \left( 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } h + \cos h \right) } { 3 ( 1 + \cos h ) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } 0 + \cos 0 } { 3 ( 1 + \cos 0 ) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 + 1 + 1 } { 3 ( 1 + 1 ) } = \frac { 3 } { 3 \times 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} .......(ii) and RHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { \pi } { 2 } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } + h \right){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { q \left[ 1 - \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } + h \right) \right] } { \left[ \pi - 2 \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } + h \right) \right] ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { q ( 1 - \cos h ) } { ( \pi - \pi - 2 h ) ^ { 2 } } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { q ( 1 - \cos h ) } { 4 h ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { q \left( 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { h } { 2 } \right) } { 4 h ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \cos x = 1 - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { q } { 8 } \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \left[ \frac { \sin \left( \frac { h } { 2 } \right) } { \frac { h } { 2 } } \right] ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { q } { 8 } \times 1 = \frac { q } { 8 }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin x } { x } = 1 \right]{/tex} ...........(iii) On substituting the values from Eqs. (ii) and (iii) to Eq.(i), we get {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } = \frac { q } { 8 } = f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } = \frac { q } { 8 } = p{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = p ( \text { given } ) \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } = \frac { q } { 8 }{/tex} and {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } = p{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} q = 4 and p = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}

} } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - h \right){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 1 - \sin ^ { 3 } \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - h \right) } { 3 \cos ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - h \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 1 - \cos ^ { 3 } h } { 3 \sin ^ { 2 } h }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { ( 1 - \cos h ) \left( 1 ^ { 2 } + \cos ^ { 2 } h + 1 \times \cos h \right) } { 3 \left( 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } h \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { ( 1 - \cos h ) \left( 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } h + \cos h \right) } { 3 ( 1 - \cos h ) ( 1 + \cos h ) }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \left( 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } h + \cos h \right) } { 3 ( 1 + \cos h ) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } 0 + \cos 0 } { 3 ( 1 + \cos 0 ) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 + 1 + 1 } { 3 ( 1 + 1 ) } = \frac { 3 } { 3 \times 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} .......(ii) and RHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { \pi } { 2 } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } + h \right){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { q \left[ 1 - \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } + h \right) \right] } { \left[ \pi - 2 \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } + h \right) \right] ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { q ( 1 - \cos h ) } { ( \pi - \pi - 2 h ) ^ { 2 } } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { q ( 1 - \cos h ) } { 4 h ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { q \left( 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { h } { 2 } \right) } { 4 h ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \cos x = 1 - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { q } { 8 } \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \left[ \frac { \sin \left( \frac { h } { 2 } \right) } { \frac { h } { 2 } } \right] ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { q } { 8 } \times 1 = \frac { q } { 8 }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin x } { x } = 1 \right]{/tex} ...........(iii) On substituting the values from Eqs. (ii) and (iii) to Eq.(i), we get {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } = \frac { q } { 8 } = f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } = \frac { q } { 8 } = p{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = p ( \text { given } ) \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } = \frac { q } { 8 }{/tex} and {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } = p{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} q = 4 and p = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{aligned} \frac { 1 - \sin ^ { 3 } x } { 3 \cos ^ { 2 } x } , & \text { if } x < \frac { \pi } { 2 } \\ p , & \text { if } x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } \\ \frac { q ( 1 - \sin x ) } { ( \pi - 2 x ) ^ { 2 } } , & \text { if } x > \frac { \pi } { 2 } \end{aligned} \right.{/tex}is continuous at x = {tex}\frac { \pi } { 2 }{/tex}

 We shall use definition of continuity to find the values of constants a and b.

Since f(x) is continuous at x={tex}\frac{{tex}\pi{/tex}}{2}{/tex},
Therefore,{tex}(LHL)_ { x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } } = ( R H L ) _ { x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } } = f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex}........(i)
Now, LHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { \pi } { 2 } ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - h \right){/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 1 - \sin ^ { 3 } \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - h \right) } { 3 \cos ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - h \right) }{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 1 - \cos ^ { 3 } h } { 3 \sin ^ { 2 } h }{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { ( 1 - \cos h ) \left( 1 ^ { 2 } + \cos ^ { 2 } h + 1 \times \cos h \right) } { 3 \left( 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } h \right) }{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { ( 1 - \cos h ) \left( 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } h + \cos h \right) } { 3 ( 1 - \cos h ) ( 1 + \cos h ) }{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \left( 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } h + \cos h \right) } { 3 ( 1 + \cos h ) }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } 0 + \cos 0 } { 3 ( 1 + \cos 0 ) }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 + 1 + 1 } { 3 ( 1 + 1 ) } = \frac { 3 } { 3 \times 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}.......(ii)
and RHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { \pi } { 2 } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } + h \right){/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { q \left[ 1 - \sin \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } + h \right) \right] } { \left[ \pi - 2 \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } + h \right) \right] ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { q ( 1 - \cos h ) } { ( \pi - \pi - 2 h ) ^ { 2 } } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { q ( 1 - \cos h ) } { 4 h ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { q \left( 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { h } { 2 } \right) } { 4 h ^ { 2 } }{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because \cos x = 1 - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { q } { 8 } \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \left[ \frac { \sin \left( \frac { h } { 2 } \right) } { \frac { h } { 2 } } \right] ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { q } { 8 } \times 1 = \frac { q } { 8 }{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin x } { x } = 1 \right]{/tex}...........(iii)
On substituting the values from Eqs. (ii) and (iii) to Eq.(i), we get
{tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } = \frac { q } { 8 } = f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } = \frac { q } { 8 } = p{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = p ( \text { given } ) \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { 2 } = \frac { q } { 8 }{/tex} and {tex}\frac { 1 } { 2 } = p{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex}q = 4 and p = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}

question_html: If x = a sin 2t (1 + cos 2t) and y = b cos 2t (1 - cos 2t), then find the values of {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} at t = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} and t = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}.
question_text: If x = a sin 2t (1 + cos 2t) and y = b cos 2t (1 - cos 2t), then find the values of {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} at t = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} and t = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} .
explanation: Given, x = a sin 2t (1 + cos 2t) and y = b cos 2t (1 - cos2t) On differentiating x and y separately w.r.t. t, we get, {tex}\frac { d x } { d t } = a [ \sin 2 t \frac { d } { d t } ( 1 + \cos 2 t ) {/tex} {tex}+ ( 1 + \cos 2 t ) \frac { d } { d t } ( \sin 2 t ) ]{/tex} [by using product rule of derivative] = a [sin 2t {tex}\times{/tex} (0- 2sin 2t) + (1 + cos2t) (2cos2t)] = a (-2sin 2 2t + 2 {tex}cos\ 2t{/tex} {tex}{/tex} + 2cos 2 2t) = a [2( cos 2 2t - sin 2 2t) + 2 cos 2t] = a (2cos 4t + 2cos 2t) = 2a(cos 4t + cos 2t) {tex}= 2 a \left[ 2 \cos \left( \frac { 4 t + 2 t } { 2 } \right) \cdot \cos \left( \frac { 4 t - 2 t } { 2 } \right) \right]{/tex} = 4a cos 3t cos t [by using product rule of derivative] Also, {tex}\frac { d y } { d t }{/tex} {tex}= b [ \cos 2 t \frac { d } { d t } ( 1 - \cos 2 t ) {/tex} {tex}+ ( 1 - \cos 2 t ) \frac { d } { d t } ( \cos 2 t ) ]{/tex} = b [cos 2t {tex}\times{/tex} (0 + 2sin 2t) + (1 - cos 2t) (-2sin 2t)] = b (2sin 2t cos 2t - 2sin 2t + 2sin 2t cos 2t) = 2b (2 sin 2t cos 2t - sin 2t) = 2b (sin 4 t - sin 2t) {tex}= 2 b \left[ 2 \cos \left( \frac { 4 t + 2 t } { 2 } \right) \sin \left( \frac { 4 t - 2 t } { 2 } \right) \right]{/tex} = 4b cos 3t sin t Now, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d y / d t } { d x / d t }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 4 b \cos 3 t \sin t } { 4 a \cos 3 t \cdot \cos t }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { b } { a } \tan t{/tex} At t = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} , {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { b } { a } \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } = \frac { b } { a }{/tex} At t = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} , {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { b } { a } \tan \frac { \pi } { 3 } = \frac { \sqrt { 3 } b } { a }{/tex}
solution: Given, x = a sin 2t (1 + cos 2t) and y = b cos 2t (1 - cos2t) On differentiating x and y separately w.r.t. t, we get, {tex}\frac { d x } { d t } = a [ \sin 2 t \frac { d } { d t } ( 1 + \cos 2 t ) {/tex} {tex}+ ( 1 + \cos 2 t ) \frac { d } { d t } ( \sin 2 t ) ]{/tex} [by using product rule of derivative] = a [sin 2t {tex}\times{/tex} (0- 2sin 2t) + (1 + cos2t) (2cos2t)] = a (-2sin 2 2t + 2 {tex}cos\ 2t{/tex} {tex}{/tex} + 2cos 2 2t) = a [2( cos 2 2t - sin 2 2t) + 2 cos 2t] = a (2cos 4t + 2cos 2t) = 2a(cos 4t + cos 2t) {tex}= 2 a \left[ 2 \cos \left( \frac { 4 t + 2 t } { 2 } \right) \cdot \cos \left( \frac { 4 t - 2 t } { 2 } \right) \right]{/tex} = 4a cos 3t cos t [by using product rule of derivative] Also, {tex}\frac { d y } { d t }{/tex} {tex}= b [ \cos 2 t \frac { d } { d t } ( 1 - \cos 2 t ) {/tex} {tex}+ ( 1 - \cos 2 t ) \frac { d } { d t } ( \cos 2 t ) ]{/tex} = b [cos 2t {tex}\times{/tex} (0 + 2sin 2t) + (1 - cos 2t) (-2sin 2t)] = b (2sin 2t cos 2t - 2sin 2t + 2sin 2t cos 2t) = 2b (2 sin 2t cos 2t - sin 2t) = 2b (sin 4 t - sin 2t) {tex}= 2 b \left[ 2 \cos \left( \frac { 4 t + 2 t } { 2 } \right) \sin \left( \frac { 4 t - 2 t } { 2 } \right) \right]{/tex} = 4b cos 3t sin t Now, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d y / d t } { d x / d t }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 4 b \cos 3 t \sin t } { 4 a \cos 3 t \cdot \cos t }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { b } { a } \tan t{/tex} At t = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} , {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { b } { a } \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } = \frac { b } { a }{/tex} At t = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} , {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { b } { a } \tan \frac { \pi } { 3 } = \frac { \sqrt { 3 } b } { a }{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, x = a sin 2t (1 + cos 2t)

and y = b cos 2t (1 - cos2t)

On differentiating x and y separately w.r.t. t, we get,
{tex}\frac { d x } { d t } = a [ \sin 2 t \frac { d } { d t } ( 1 + \cos 2 t ) {/tex}{tex}+ ( 1 + \cos 2 t ) \frac { d } { d t } ( \sin 2 t ) ]{/tex} [by using product rule of derivative]
= a [sin 2t {tex}\times{/tex} (0- 2sin 2t) + (1 + cos2t) (2cos2t)]
= a (-2sin2 2t + 2{tex}cos\ 2t{/tex} {tex}{/tex} + 2cos2 2t)
= a [2( cos2 2t - sin2 2t) + 2 cos 2t]
= a (2cos 4t + 2cos 2t) = 2a(cos 4t + cos 2t)
{tex}= 2 a \left[ 2 \cos \left( \frac { 4 t + 2 t } { 2 } \right) \cdot \cos \left( \frac { 4 t - 2 t } { 2 } \right) \right]{/tex}
= 4a cos 3t cos t [by using product rule of derivative]
Also, {tex}\frac { d y } { d t }{/tex}
{tex}= b [ \cos 2 t \frac { d } { d t } ( 1 - \cos 2 t ) {/tex}{tex}+ ( 1 - \cos 2 t ) \frac { d } { d t } ( \cos 2 t ) ]{/tex}
= b [cos 2t {tex}\times{/tex} (0 + 2sin 2t) + (1 - cos 2t) (-2sin 2t)]
= b (2sin 2t cos 2t - 2sin 2t + 2sin 2t cos 2t)
= 2b (2 sin 2t cos 2t - sin 2t)
= 2b (sin 4 t - sin 2t)
{tex}= 2 b \left[ 2 \cos \left( \frac { 4 t + 2 t } { 2 } \right) \sin \left( \frac { 4 t - 2 t } { 2 } \right) \right]{/tex}
= 4b cos 3t sin t
Now, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d y / d t } { d x / d t }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 4 b \cos 3 t \sin t } { 4 a \cos 3 t \cdot \cos t }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { b } { a } \tan t{/tex}
At t = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { b } { a } \tan \frac { \pi } { 4 } = \frac { b } { a }{/tex}
At t = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex},{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { b } { a } \tan \frac { \pi } { 3 } = \frac { \sqrt { 3 } b } { a }{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Differentiate the function {tex}{x^{\sin x}} + {\left( {\sin x} \right)^{\cos x}}{/tex} w.r.t. x.
question_text: Differentiate the function {tex}{x^{\sin x}} + {\left( {\sin x} \right)^{\cos x}}{/tex} w.r.t. x.
explanation: Let y = u + v When {tex}u = {x^{\sin x}}{/tex} , {tex}v = {\left( {\sin x} \right)^{\cos x}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{du}}{{dx}} + \frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}u = {x^{\sin x}}{/tex} Taking log both side log {tex}u = \log {x^{\sin x}}{/tex} log {tex}u = \sin x.\log x{/tex} diff. both side w.r. to x {tex}\frac{1}{u}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = \sin x.\frac{1}{x} + \log x\cos x{/tex} {tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = u\left[ {\frac{{\sin x}}{x} + \log x.\cos x} \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = {x^{\sin x}}\left[ {\frac{{\sin x + x\log x.\cos x}}{x}} \right]{/tex} {tex}v = {\left( {\sin x} \right)^{\cos x}}{/tex} Taking log both sides log {tex}v = \log {\left( {\sin x} \right)^{\cos x}}{/tex} {tex}\log v = \cos x.\log \left( {\sin x} \right){/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t to x {tex}\frac{1}{v}.\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \cos x.\frac{1}{{\sin x}}\left( {\cos x} \right) + \log \left( {\sin x} \right)\left( { - \sin x} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = v\left[ {\cot x.\cos x - \log \left( {\sin x} \right).\sin x} \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = {\left( {\sin x} \right)^{\cos x}}\left[ {\cot x.\cos x - \log \left( {\sin x} \right).\sin x} \right]{/tex} Hence {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = {x^{\sin x}}\left[ {\frac{{\sin x + x\log x.\cos x}}{x}} \right] + {/tex} {tex}{\left( {\sin x} \right)^{\cos x}}\left[ {\cot x\cos x - \log \left( {\sin x} \right).\sin x} \right]{/tex}
solution: Let y = u + v When {tex}u = {x^{\sin x}}{/tex} , {tex}v = {\left( {\sin x} \right)^{\cos x}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{du}}{{dx}} + \frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}u = {x^{\sin x}}{/tex} Taking log both side log {tex}u = \log {x^{\sin x}}{/tex} log {tex}u = \sin x.\log x{/tex} diff. both side w.r. to x {tex}\frac{1}{u}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = \sin x.\frac{1}{x} + \log x\cos x{/tex} {tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = u\left[ {\frac{{\sin x}}{x} + \log x.\cos x} \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = {x^{\sin x}}\left[ {\frac{{\sin x + x\log x.\cos x}}{x}} \right]{/tex} {tex}v = {\left( {\sin x} \right)^{\cos x}}{/tex} Taking log both sides log {tex}v = \log {\left( {\sin x} \right)^{\cos x}}{/tex} {tex}\log v = \cos x.\log \left( {\sin x} \right){/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t to x {tex}\frac{1}{v}.\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \cos x.\frac{1}{{\sin x}}\left( {\cos x} \right) + \log \left( {\sin x} \right)\left( { - \sin x} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = v\left[ {\cot x.\cos x - \log \left( {\sin x} \right).\sin x} \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = {\left( {\sin x} \right)^{\cos x}}\left[ {\cot x.\cos x - \log \left( {\sin x} \right).\sin x} \right]{/tex} Hence {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = {x^{\sin x}}\left[ {\frac{{\sin x + x\log x.\cos x}}{x}} \right] + {/tex} {tex}{\left( {\sin x} \right)^{\cos x}}\left[ {\cot x\cos x - \log \left( {\sin x} \right).\sin x} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Let y = u + v

When {tex}u = {x^{\sin x}}{/tex}, {tex}v = {\left( {\sin x} \right)^{\cos x}}{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{du}}{{dx}} + \frac{{dv}}{{dx}}{/tex} ...(i)

{tex}u = {x^{\sin x}}{/tex}

Taking log both side

log {tex}u = \log {x^{\sin x}}{/tex}

log {tex}u = \sin x.\log x{/tex}

diff. both side w.r. to x

{tex}\frac{1}{u}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = \sin x.\frac{1}{x} + \log x\cos x{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = u\left[ {\frac{{\sin x}}{x} + \log x.\cos x} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = {x^{\sin x}}\left[ {\frac{{\sin x + x\log x.\cos x}}{x}} \right]{/tex}

{tex}v = {\left( {\sin x} \right)^{\cos x}}{/tex}

Taking log both sides

log {tex}v = \log {\left( {\sin x} \right)^{\cos x}}{/tex}

{tex}\log v = \cos x.\log \left( {\sin x} \right){/tex}

Differentiating both sides  w.r.t to x

{tex}\frac{1}{v}.\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = \cos x.\frac{1}{{\sin x}}\left( {\cos x} \right) + \log \left( {\sin x} \right)\left( { - \sin x} \right){/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = v\left[ {\cot x.\cos x - \log \left( {\sin x} \right).\sin x} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = {\left( {\sin x} \right)^{\cos x}}\left[ {\cot x.\cos x - \log \left( {\sin x} \right).\sin x} \right]{/tex}

Hence

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = {x^{\sin x}}\left[ {\frac{{\sin x + x\log x.\cos x}}{x}} \right] + {/tex}{tex}{\left( {\sin x} \right)^{\cos x}}\left[ {\cot x\cos x - \log \left( {\sin x} \right).\sin x} \right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Differentiate xsinx + (sinx)cosx with respect to x
question_text: Differentiate x sinx + (sinx) cosx with respect to x
explanation: y = x sinx + sin cosx y = u + v {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} u = x sinx ln u = ln x sinx ln u = sinx.ln x differentiating both the sides with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{1}{u} \cdot \frac{d u}{d x}=\sin x \cdot \frac{1}{x}+\cos x \cdot \ln x{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}=x^{\sin x}\left[\frac{\sin x}{x}+\cos x \cdot \ln x\right]{/tex} v = sin cosx ln v = ln sin cosx {tex}=\cos x \cdot \ln sinx{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{V} \cdot \frac{d v}{d x}=\operatorname{cosx} \cdot \frac{1}{\sin x} \cdot \cos x+(-\sin x) \cdot \ln \sin x{/tex} {tex}\frac{dv}{d x}=\sin x^{\cos x}\left[\frac{\cos ^{2} x}{\sin x}-\sin x \cdot \ln \sin x\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=x^{\sin x}\left[\frac{sinx}{x}+\cos x \cdot \ln x\right]{/tex} {tex}+\sin x^{cosx}\left[\frac{\cos ^{2} x}{\sin 1}-\sin a \cdot \ln \sin x\right]{/tex}
solution: y = x sinx + sin cosx y = u + v {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} u = x sinx ln u = ln x sinx ln u = sinx.ln x differentiating both the sides with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{1}{u} \cdot \frac{d u}{d x}=\sin x \cdot \frac{1}{x}+\cos x \cdot \ln x{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}=x^{\sin x}\left[\frac{\sin x}{x}+\cos x \cdot \ln x\right]{/tex} v = sin cosx ln v = ln sin cosx {tex}=\cos x \cdot \ln sinx{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{V} \cdot \frac{d v}{d x}=\operatorname{cosx} \cdot \frac{1}{\sin x} \cdot \cos x+(-\sin x) \cdot \ln \sin x{/tex} {tex}\frac{dv}{d x}=\sin x^{\cos x}\left[\frac{\cos ^{2} x}{\sin x}-\sin x \cdot \ln \sin x\right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=x^{\sin x}\left[\frac{sinx}{x}+\cos x \cdot \ln x\right]{/tex} {tex}+\sin x^{cosx}\left[\frac{\cos ^{2} x}{\sin 1}-\sin a \cdot \ln \sin x\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
y = xsinx + sincosx
y = u + v
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
u = xsinx
ln u = ln xsinx
ln u = sinx.ln x

differentiating both the sides with respect to x, we get
{tex}\frac{1}{u} \cdot \frac{d u}{d x}=\sin x \cdot \frac{1}{x}+\cos x \cdot \ln x{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d u}{d x}=x^{\sin x}\left[\frac{\sin x}{x}+\cos x \cdot \ln x\right]{/tex}
v = sincosx
ln v = ln sincosx {tex}=\cos x \cdot \ln sinx{/tex}
{tex}\frac{1}{V} \cdot \frac{d v}{d x}=\operatorname{cosx} \cdot \frac{1}{\sin x} \cdot \cos x+(-\sin x) \cdot \ln \sin x{/tex}
 {tex}\frac{dv}{d x}=\sin x^{\cos x}\left[\frac{\cos ^{2} x}{\sin x}-\sin x \cdot \ln \sin x\right]{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=x^{\sin x}\left[\frac{sinx}{x}+\cos x \cdot \ln x\right]{/tex}{tex}+\sin x^{cosx}\left[\frac{\cos ^{2} x}{\sin 1}-\sin a \cdot \ln \sin x\right]{/tex}

question_html: If y = 2 cos (log x) + 3 sin (log x) prove that {tex}x^{2} \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} y}{\mathrm{d} x^{2}}+x \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{d} x}+y=0{/tex}
question_text: If y = 2 cos (log x) + 3 sin (log x) prove that {tex}x^{2} \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} y}{\mathrm{d} x^{2}}+x \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{d} x}+y=0{/tex}
explanation: y = 2cos(log x) + 3sin (log x) (given) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = - 2sin (log x) {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex} (log x) + 3cos(log x) {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex} (log x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-2sin(logx)}{x}{/tex} + {tex}​​\frac{3cos(logx)}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{xdy}{dx}{/tex} = -2sin(log x) + 3cos(log x) ...(i) again, differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{xd^2y}{dx^2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = -2cos(log x) {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex} (log x) - 3sin(log x) {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex} (log x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{xd^2y}{dx^2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-2cos(logx)}{x}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{3sin(logx)}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{xd^2y}{dx^2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = - [ {tex}\frac{2cos(logx)}{x}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{3sin(logx)}{x}{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{xd^2y}{dx^2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-y}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x^2d^2y}{dx^2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{xdy}{dx^2}{/tex} = -y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x^2d^2y}{dx^2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{xdy}{dx^2}{/tex} + y = 0 Hence proved.
solution: y = 2cos(log x) + 3sin (log x) (given) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = - 2sin (log x) {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex} (log x) + 3cos(log x) {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex} (log x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-2sin(logx)}{x}{/tex} + {tex}​​\frac{3cos(logx)}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{xdy}{dx}{/tex} = -2sin(log x) + 3cos(log x) ...(i) again, differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{xd^2y}{dx^2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = -2cos(log x) {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex} (log x) - 3sin(log x) {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex} (log x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{xd^2y}{dx^2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-2cos(logx)}{x}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{3sin(logx)}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{xd^2y}{dx^2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = - [ {tex}\frac{2cos(logx)}{x}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{3sin(logx)}{x}{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{xd^2y}{dx^2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-y}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x^2d^2y}{dx^2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{xdy}{dx^2}{/tex} = -y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x^2d^2y}{dx^2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{xdy}{dx^2}{/tex} + y = 0 Hence proved.
solution_html:
y = 2cos(log x) + 3sin (log x) (given)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = - 2sin (log x){tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex}(log x) + 3cos(log x){tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex}(log x)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-2sin(logx)}{x}{/tex} + {tex}​​\frac{3cos(logx)}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{xdy}{dx}{/tex} = -2sin(log x) + 3cos(log x) ...(i)
again, differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{xd^2y}{dx^2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = -2cos(log x){tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex}(log x) - 3sin(log x){tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex}(log x)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{xd^2y}{dx^2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-2cos(logx)}{x}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{3sin(logx)}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{xd^2y}{dx^2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = - [{tex}\frac{2cos(logx)}{x}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{3sin(logx)}{x}{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{xd^2y}{dx^2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-y}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x^2d^2y}{dx^2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{xdy}{dx^2}{/tex} = -y
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x^2d^2y}{dx^2}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{xdy}{dx^2}{/tex} + y = 0
Hence proved.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If x = a (cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex}) and y = a (sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex}), then find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}
question_text: If x = a (cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ) and y = a (sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ), then find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}
explanation: Given: {tex}x=a(\cos \theta+\theta \sin \theta){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d \theta}=a\{(-\sin \theta)+\sin \theta+\theta \cos \theta\}{/tex} {tex}=a \theta \cos \theta{/tex} ..... (i) {tex}y=a(\sin \theta-\theta \cos \theta){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d \theta}=a\{\cos \theta-\cos \theta{/tex} {tex}-\theta(-\sin \theta)\}=a \theta \sin \theta{/tex} ...... (ii) {tex}(2) \div(1) \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\tan \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=\frac{d}{d x}(\tan \theta){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d \theta}(\tan \theta) \cdot \frac{d \theta}{d x}{/tex} {tex}=\sec ^{2} \theta \cdot \frac{1}{a \theta \cos \theta}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{a \theta \cos ^{3} \theta}{/tex}
solution: Given: {tex}x=a(\cos \theta+\theta \sin \theta){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d \theta}=a\{(-\sin \theta)+\sin \theta+\theta \cos \theta\}{/tex} {tex}=a \theta \cos \theta{/tex} ..... (i) {tex}y=a(\sin \theta-\theta \cos \theta){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d \theta}=a\{\cos \theta-\cos \theta{/tex} {tex}-\theta(-\sin \theta)\}=a \theta \sin \theta{/tex} ...... (ii) {tex}(2) \div(1) \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\tan \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=\frac{d}{d x}(\tan \theta){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d \theta}(\tan \theta) \cdot \frac{d \theta}{d x}{/tex} {tex}=\sec ^{2} \theta \cdot \frac{1}{a \theta \cos \theta}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{a \theta \cos ^{3} \theta}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: {tex}x=a(\cos \theta+\theta \sin \theta){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d \theta}=a\{(-\sin \theta)+\sin \theta+\theta \cos \theta\}{/tex} {tex}=a \theta \cos \theta{/tex} ..... (i)
{tex}y=a(\sin \theta-\theta \cos \theta){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d \theta}=a\{\cos \theta-\cos \theta{/tex}{tex}-\theta(-\sin \theta)\}=a \theta \sin \theta{/tex} ...... (ii)
{tex}(2) \div(1) \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\tan \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=\frac{d}{d x}(\tan \theta){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d \theta}(\tan \theta) \cdot \frac{d \theta}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}=\sec ^{2} \theta \cdot \frac{1}{a \theta \cos \theta}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{a \theta \cos ^{3} \theta}{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}x=a \sin 2 t(1+\cos 2 t){/tex} and {tex}y=b \cos 2 t(1-\cos 2 t){/tex}, find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x} \text { at } t=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}x=a \sin 2 t(1+\cos 2 t){/tex} and {tex}y=b \cos 2 t(1-\cos 2 t){/tex} , find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x} \text { at } t=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} .
explanation: x = a sin 2t(1 + cos 2t) and y = b cos 2t(1 - cos2t) x = a sin 2t(1 + cos 2t) y = b cos 2t(1 - cos 2t) {tex}\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} = a[2cos 2t (1 + cos 2t) + sin 2t(-2 sin 2 t)] = 2a [cos 2t + (cos 2 2t - sin 2 2t)] = 2a (cos 2t + cos 4t) {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}={/tex} b[-2 sin 2t(1-cos 2t) + cos 2t(2sin 2t)] = 2b[-sin 2t + 2 sin 2t cos 2t] = 2b(-sin 2t + sin 4t) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d t}}{\frac{d x}{d x}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 b(-\sin 2 t+\sin 4 t)}{2 a(\cos 2 t+\cos 4 t)}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{b\left(-\sin \frac{\pi}{2}+\sin \pi\right)}{a\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{2}+\cos \pi\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{b(-1+0)}{a(0-1)}=\frac{b}{a}{/tex}
solution: x = a sin 2t(1 + cos 2t) and y = b cos 2t(1 - cos2t) x = a sin 2t(1 + cos 2t) y = b cos 2t(1 - cos 2t) {tex}\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} = a[2cos 2t (1 + cos 2t) + sin 2t(-2 sin 2 t)] = 2a [cos 2t + (cos 2 2t - sin 2 2t)] = 2a (cos 2t + cos 4t) {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}={/tex} b[-2 sin 2t(1-cos 2t) + cos 2t(2sin 2t)] = 2b[-sin 2t + 2 sin 2t cos 2t] = 2b(-sin 2t + sin 4t) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d t}}{\frac{d x}{d x}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 b(-\sin 2 t+\sin 4 t)}{2 a(\cos 2 t+\cos 4 t)}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{b\left(-\sin \frac{\pi}{2}+\sin \pi\right)}{a\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{2}+\cos \pi\right)}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{b(-1+0)}{a(0-1)}=\frac{b}{a}{/tex}
solution_html:
x = a sin 2t(1 + cos 2t) and y = b cos 2t(1 - cos2t)
x = a sin 2t(1 + cos 2t)
y = b cos 2t(1 - cos 2t)
{tex}\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} = a[2cos 2t (1 + cos 2t) + sin 2t(-2 sin 2 t)]
= 2a [cos 2t + (cos2 2t - sin2 2t)]
= 2a (cos 2t + cos 4t)
{tex}\frac{d y}{d t}={/tex} b[-2 sin 2t(1-cos 2t) + cos 2t(2sin 2t)]
= 2b[-sin 2t + 2 sin 2t cos 2t]
= 2b(-sin 2t + sin 4t)
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d t}}{\frac{d x}{d x}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 b(-\sin 2 t+\sin 4 t)}{2 a(\cos 2 t+\cos 4 t)}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{b\left(-\sin \frac{\pi}{2}+\sin \pi\right)}{a\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{2}+\cos \pi\right)}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{b(-1+0)}{a(0-1)}=\frac{b}{a}{/tex}

question_html: If (ax + b){tex}\ e^ \frac{y}{x}{/tex} = x or, y = x log{tex}\left(\frac{x}{a+b x}\right){/tex}, prove that x3{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\left(x \frac{d y}{d x}-y\right)^2{/tex}.
question_text: If (ax + b) {tex}\ e^ \frac{y}{x}{/tex} = x or, y = x log {tex}\left(\frac{x}{a+b x}\right){/tex} , prove that x 3 {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\left(x \frac{d y}{d x}-y\right)^2{/tex} .
explanation: We have, {tex}(a x+b) e^\frac{y} { x}=x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}e^ \frac {y} { x}=\frac{x}{a x+b}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y}{x}=\log \left(\frac{x}{a x+b}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}=x \log \left(\frac{x}{a+b x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x{logx - log (a + bx)} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = logx - log (a + bx) ....(i) On differentiating with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{a+b x} \frac{d}{d x}(a+b x){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{x}-\frac{b}{a+b x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - y = {tex}x^{2}\left\{\frac{1}{x}-\frac{b}{a+b x}\right\}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - y = {tex}\frac{a x}{a+b x}{/tex} ...(ii) Again differentiating both sides with respect to x, we get {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(a+b x) a-a x(0+b)}{(a+b x)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{a^{2}}{(a+b x)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x^{3} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{a^{2} x^{2}}{(a+b x)^{2}}{/tex} ......[Multiplying both sides by x 2 ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x^{3} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{a x}{a+b x}\right)^{2}{/tex} ........(iii) From (ii) and (iii), we obtain {tex}x^{3} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\left(x \frac{d y}{d x}-y\right)^{2}{/tex} .
solution: We have, {tex}(a x+b) e^\frac{y} { x}=x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}e^ \frac {y} { x}=\frac{x}{a x+b}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y}{x}=\log \left(\frac{x}{a x+b}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}=x \log \left(\frac{x}{a+b x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x{logx - log (a + bx)} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = logx - log (a + bx) ....(i) On differentiating with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{a+b x} \frac{d}{d x}(a+b x){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{x}-\frac{b}{a+b x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - y = {tex}x^{2}\left\{\frac{1}{x}-\frac{b}{a+b x}\right\}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - y = {tex}\frac{a x}{a+b x}{/tex} ...(ii) Again differentiating both sides with respect to x, we get {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(a+b x) a-a x(0+b)}{(a+b x)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{a^{2}}{(a+b x)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x^{3} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{a^{2} x^{2}}{(a+b x)^{2}}{/tex} ......[Multiplying both sides by x 2 ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x^{3} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{a x}{a+b x}\right)^{2}{/tex} ........(iii) From (ii) and (iii), we obtain {tex}x^{3} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\left(x \frac{d y}{d x}-y\right)^{2}{/tex} .
solution_html:
We have, 
{tex}(a x+b) e^\frac{y} { x}=x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}e^ \frac {y} { x}=\frac{x}{a x+b}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y}{x}=\log \left(\frac{x}{a x+b}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}=x \log \left(\frac{x}{a+b x}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x{logx - log (a + bx)}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{y}{x}{/tex} = logx - log (a + bx) ....(i)
On differentiating with respect to x, we get
{tex}\frac{x \frac{d y}{d x}-y}{x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{x}-\frac{1}{a+b x} \frac{d}{d x}(a+b x){/tex}= {tex}\frac{1}{x}-\frac{b}{a+b x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - y = {tex}x^{2}\left\{\frac{1}{x}-\frac{b}{a+b x}\right\}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} - y = {tex}\frac{a x}{a+b x}{/tex}  ...(ii)
Again differentiating both sides with respect to x, we get
{tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(a+b x) a-a x(0+b)}{(a+b x)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{a^{2}}{(a+b x)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x^{3} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{a^{2} x^{2}}{(a+b x)^{2}}{/tex} ......[Multiplying both sides by x2]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x^{3} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{a x}{a+b x}\right)^{2}{/tex}  ........(iii)
From (ii) and (iii), we obtain
{tex}x^{3} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\left(x \frac{d y}{d x}-y\right)^{2}{/tex}.
