question_html: If y = Aemx + Benx, show that {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} - \left( {m + n} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + mny = 0{/tex}
question_text: If y = Ae mx + Be nx , show that {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} - \left( {m + n} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + mny = 0{/tex}
explanation: Given: y = Ae mx + Be nx ....(i) To prove: {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} - \left( {m + n} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + mny = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} =A{e^{mx}}\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {mx} \right) + B{e^{nx}}\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {nx} \right){/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because \frac{d}{{dx}}{e^{f\left( x \right)}} = {e^{f\left( x \right)}}\frac{d}{{dx}}f\left( x \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = Am{e^{mx}} + Bn{e^{nx}}{/tex} ….(ii) Again {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{{d}x^2}} = Am{e^{mx}}.m + Bn{e^{nx}}.n{/tex} = Am 2 e mx + Bn 2 e nx ...(iii) Now, L.H.S.= {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} - \left( {m + n} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + mny{/tex} = Am 2 e mx + Bn 2 e nx - (m + n)(Ame mx + Bne nx ) + mn(Ae mx + Be nx ) = Am 2 e mx + Bn 2 e nx - Am 2 e mx - Bmne nx - Amne mx - Bn 2 e nx + Amne mx + Bmne nx = 0 = R.H.S. Hence proved.
solution: Given: y = Ae mx + Be nx ....(i) To prove: {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} - \left( {m + n} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + mny = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} =A{e^{mx}}\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {mx} \right) + B{e^{nx}}\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {nx} \right){/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because \frac{d}{{dx}}{e^{f\left( x \right)}} = {e^{f\left( x \right)}}\frac{d}{{dx}}f\left( x \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = Am{e^{mx}} + Bn{e^{nx}}{/tex} ….(ii) Again {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{{d}x^2}} = Am{e^{mx}}.m + Bn{e^{nx}}.n{/tex} = Am 2 e mx + Bn 2 e nx ...(iii) Now, L.H.S.= {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} - \left( {m + n} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + mny{/tex} = Am 2 e mx + Bn 2 e nx - (m + n)(Ame mx + Bne nx ) + mn(Ae mx + Be nx ) = Am 2 e mx + Bn 2 e nx - Am 2 e mx - Bmne nx - Amne mx - Bn 2 e nx + Amne mx + Bmne nx = 0 = R.H.S. Hence proved.
solution_html:
Given: y = Aemx + Benx ....(i)

To prove: {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} - \left( {m + n} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + mny = 0{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} =A{e^{mx}}\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {mx} \right) + B{e^{nx}}\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {nx} \right){/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because \frac{d}{{dx}}{e^{f\left( x \right)}} = {e^{f\left( x \right)}}\frac{d}{{dx}}f\left( x \right)} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = Am{e^{mx}} + Bn{e^{nx}}{/tex}….(ii)

Again {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{{d}x^2}} = Am{e^{mx}}.m + Bn{e^{nx}}.n{/tex}

= Am2emx + Bn2enx ...(iii)

Now, L.H.S.= {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} - \left( {m + n} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + mny{/tex}

= Am2emx + Bn2enx - (m + n)(Amemx + Bnenx) + mn(Aemx + Benx)

= Am2emx + Bn2enx - Am2emx - Bmnenx - Amnemx - Bn2enx + Amnemx + Bmnenx

= 0

= R.H.S. Hence proved.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If x = a cos{tex}\theta{/tex} + b sin{tex}\theta{/tex}, y = a sin{tex}\theta{/tex} - b cos{tex}\theta{/tex},then show that {tex} y ^ { 2 } \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - x \frac { d y } { d x } + y = 0{/tex}.
question_text: If x = a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + b sin {tex}\theta{/tex} , y = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - b cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ,then show that {tex} y ^ { 2 } \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - x \frac { d y } { d x } + y = 0{/tex} .
explanation: According to the question, {tex}x = a cos\theta + b sin\theta{/tex} ......(i) and {tex}y = a sin\theta - bcos\theta{/tex} ........(ii) Differentiating both sides of Equations. (i) and (ii) w.r.t {tex}\theta{/tex} , {tex}\frac { d x } { d \theta } = - a \sin \theta + b \cos \theta{/tex} ......(iii) and {tex}\frac { d y } { d \theta } = a \cos \theta + b \sin \theta{/tex} .....(iv) Now, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d y / d \theta } { d x / d \theta } = \frac { a \cos \theta + b \sin \theta } { b \cos \theta - a \sin \theta }{/tex} [ dividing Eq(iv) by Eq(iii) ] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { x } { - y }{/tex} [ From Equations. (i) and (ii)]........(v) Differentiating both sides of Equation.(v) w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = - \left[ \frac { y \cdot 1 - x \cdot \frac { d y } { d x } } { y ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex} [ Using quotient rule of derivative] {tex}\therefore \quad y ^ { 2 } \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - x \frac { d y } { d x } + y = 0{/tex} Hence Proved
solution: According to the question, {tex}x = a cos\theta + b sin\theta{/tex} ......(i) and {tex}y = a sin\theta - bcos\theta{/tex} ........(ii) Differentiating both sides of Equations. (i) and (ii) w.r.t {tex}\theta{/tex} , {tex}\frac { d x } { d \theta } = - a \sin \theta + b \cos \theta{/tex} ......(iii) and {tex}\frac { d y } { d \theta } = a \cos \theta + b \sin \theta{/tex} .....(iv) Now, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d y / d \theta } { d x / d \theta } = \frac { a \cos \theta + b \sin \theta } { b \cos \theta - a \sin \theta }{/tex} [ dividing Eq(iv) by Eq(iii) ] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { x } { - y }{/tex} [ From Equations. (i) and (ii)]........(v) Differentiating both sides of Equation.(v) w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = - \left[ \frac { y \cdot 1 - x \cdot \frac { d y } { d x } } { y ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex} [ Using quotient rule of derivative] {tex}\therefore \quad y ^ { 2 } \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - x \frac { d y } { d x } + y = 0{/tex} Hence Proved
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}x = a cos\theta + b sin\theta{/tex}......(i)
and {tex}y = a sin\theta - bcos\theta{/tex}........(ii)
Differentiating both sides of Equations. (i) and (ii) w.r.t {tex}\theta{/tex} , 
{tex}\frac { d x } { d \theta } = - a \sin \theta + b \cos \theta{/tex}......(iii)
and {tex}\frac { d y } { d \theta } = a \cos \theta + b \sin \theta{/tex}.....(iv)
Now, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d y / d \theta } { d x / d \theta } = \frac { a \cos \theta + b \sin \theta } { b \cos \theta - a \sin \theta }{/tex}[ dividing Eq(iv) by Eq(iii) ]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { x } { - y }{/tex}[ From Equations. (i) and (ii)]........(v)
Differentiating both sides of Equation.(v) w.r.t x, 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = - \left[ \frac { y \cdot 1 - x \cdot \frac { d y } { d x } } { y ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex}[ Using quotient rule of derivative]
{tex}\therefore \quad y ^ { 2 } \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - x \frac { d y } { d x } + y = 0{/tex}
Hence Proved

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}y=\{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+1}\}^{m}{/tex} show that (x2 + 1) y2 + xy1 - m2y = 0
question_text: If {tex}y=\{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+1}\}^{m}{/tex} show that (x 2 + 1) y 2 + xy 1 - m 2 y = 0
explanation: We have, {tex}y=\{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+1}\}^{m}{/tex} Differentiating with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=m\{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+1}\}^{m-1} \times \frac{d}{d x}\{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+1}\}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=m\left[[x+\sqrt{x^{2}+1}]^{m-1} \times\left.1+\frac{2 x}{2 \sqrt{x^{2}+1}}\right]\right.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{m\{\sqrt{x^{2}+1}+x\}^{m}}{\sqrt{x^{2}+1}}=\frac{m y}{\sqrt{x^{2}+1}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y_{1}=\frac{m y}{\sqrt{x^{2}+1}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y_{1} \sqrt{x^{2}+1}=m y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y_{1}^{2}\left(x^{2}+1\right)=m^{2} y^{2}{/tex} .... [Squaring both sides] Differentiating with respect to x, we get {tex}2 y_{1} y_{2}\left(1+x^{2}\right)+y_{1}^{2}(2 x)=2 m^{2} y y_{1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} y 2 (1 + x 2 ) + xy 1 - m 2 y = 0
solution: We have, {tex}y=\{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+1}\}^{m}{/tex} Differentiating with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=m\{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+1}\}^{m-1} \times \frac{d}{d x}\{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+1}\}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=m\left[[x+\sqrt{x^{2}+1}]^{m-1} \times\left.1+\frac{2 x}{2 \sqrt{x^{2}+1}}\right]\right.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{m\{\sqrt{x^{2}+1}+x\}^{m}}{\sqrt{x^{2}+1}}=\frac{m y}{\sqrt{x^{2}+1}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y_{1}=\frac{m y}{\sqrt{x^{2}+1}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y_{1} \sqrt{x^{2}+1}=m y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y_{1}^{2}\left(x^{2}+1\right)=m^{2} y^{2}{/tex} .... [Squaring both sides] Differentiating with respect to x, we get {tex}2 y_{1} y_{2}\left(1+x^{2}\right)+y_{1}^{2}(2 x)=2 m^{2} y y_{1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} y 2 (1 + x 2 ) + xy 1 - m 2 y = 0
solution_html:
We have,
{tex}y=\{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+1}\}^{m}{/tex}
Differentiating with respect to x, we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=m\{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+1}\}^{m-1} \times \frac{d}{d x}\{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+1}\}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=m\left[[x+\sqrt{x^{2}+1}]^{m-1} \times\left.1+\frac{2 x}{2 \sqrt{x^{2}+1}}\right]\right.{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{m\{\sqrt{x^{2}+1}+x\}^{m}}{\sqrt{x^{2}+1}}=\frac{m y}{\sqrt{x^{2}+1}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y_{1}=\frac{m y}{\sqrt{x^{2}+1}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y_{1} \sqrt{x^{2}+1}=m y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y_{1}^{2}\left(x^{2}+1\right)=m^{2} y^{2}{/tex}     .... [Squaring both sides]
Differentiating with respect to x, we get
{tex}2 y_{1} y_{2}\left(1+x^{2}\right)+y_{1}^{2}(2 x)=2 m^{2} y y_{1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} y2 (1 + x2) + xy1 - m2y = 0

question_html: If y = {tex} e ^ { m \sin ^ { - 1 } x }{/tex}, then show that {tex} \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - x \frac { d y } { d x } - m ^ { 2 } y = 0{/tex}.
question_text: If y = {tex} e ^ { m \sin ^ { - 1 } x }{/tex} , then show that {tex} \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - x \frac { d y } { d x } - m ^ { 2 } y = 0{/tex} .
explanation: According to the question, {tex} y = e ^ { m \sin ^ { - 1 } x }{/tex} ..........(i) Differentiating both sides of Equation(i) w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = e ^ { m \sin ^ { - 1 } x } \frac { d } { d x } \left( m \sin ^ { - 1 } x \right){/tex} [ Using chain rule of derivative] {tex}= e ^ { m \sin ^ { - 1 } x } m \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \frac { d y } { d x } = m y{/tex} [ From Equation(i)] Squaring both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 } = m ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex} .........(ii) Differentiating both sides of Equation (ii) w.r.t x, {tex} \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) 2 \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } {/tex} {tex} + \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 } ( - 2 x ) = 2 m ^ { 2 } y \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - x \frac { d y } { d x } = m ^ { 2 } y{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - x \frac { d y } { d x } - m ^ { 2 } y = 0{/tex} Hence Proved
solution: According to the question, {tex} y = e ^ { m \sin ^ { - 1 } x }{/tex} ..........(i) Differentiating both sides of Equation(i) w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = e ^ { m \sin ^ { - 1 } x } \frac { d } { d x } \left( m \sin ^ { - 1 } x \right){/tex} [ Using chain rule of derivative] {tex}= e ^ { m \sin ^ { - 1 } x } m \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \frac { d y } { d x } = m y{/tex} [ From Equation(i)] Squaring both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 } = m ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex} .........(ii) Differentiating both sides of Equation (ii) w.r.t x, {tex} \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) 2 \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } {/tex} {tex} + \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 } ( - 2 x ) = 2 m ^ { 2 } y \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - x \frac { d y } { d x } = m ^ { 2 } y{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - x \frac { d y } { d x } - m ^ { 2 } y = 0{/tex} Hence Proved
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex} y = e ^ { m \sin ^ { - 1 } x }{/tex}..........(i)
Differentiating both sides of Equation(i) w.r.t x, 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = e ^ { m \sin ^ { - 1 } x } \frac { d } { d x } \left( m \sin ^ { - 1 } x \right){/tex}[ Using chain rule of derivative]
{tex}= e ^ { m \sin ^ { - 1 } x } m \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \frac { d y } { d x } = m y{/tex} [ From Equation(i)]
Squaring both sides, 
{tex}\Rightarrow \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 } = m ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 }{/tex}.........(ii)
Differentiating both sides of Equation (ii) w.r.t x, 
{tex} \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) 2 \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } {/tex}{tex} + \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 } ( - 2 x ) = 2 m ^ { 2 } y \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - x \frac { d y } { d x } = m ^ { 2 } y{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - x \frac { d y } { d x } - m ^ { 2 } y = 0{/tex}
Hence Proved

explanation: According to the question, y = {tex} \left( x + \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { n }{/tex} ..........(i) Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = n \left( x + \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { n - 1 }{/tex} {tex}\left( 1 + \frac { 2 x } { 2 \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } } \right){/tex} [ Using chain rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = n \left( x + \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { n - 1 } \left( \frac { x + \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } } { \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { n \left( x + \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { n } } { \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { n y } { \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} [ From Equation(i)] {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \frac { d y } { d x } = n y{/tex} .........(ii) Differentiating both sides w.r.t x again, {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 2 x } { 2 \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex}.\frac { d y } { d x } = n \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + x \frac { d y } { d x } ={/tex} {tex}n \cdot \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} [ multiplying both sides by {tex}\sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + x \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = {tex}n \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \cdot \frac { n y } { \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} [ From Equation(ii)] {tex}\therefore \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + x \frac { d y } { d x } = n ^ { 2 } y{/tex} Hence Proved

According to the question, y = {tex} \left( x + \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { n }{/tex}..........(i)
Differentiating both sides w.r.t x,

{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \frac { d y } { d x } = n y{/tex}.........(ii)
Differentiating both sides w.r.t x again, 
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 2 x } { 2 \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}{tex}.\frac { d y } { d x } = n \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + x \frac { d y } { d x } = n ^ { 2 } y{/tex}

1 } \left( \cot \frac { \theta } { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}= \tan ^ { - 1 } \left[ \tan \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \frac { \theta } { 2 } \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \frac { \theta } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = \frac { \pi } { 2 } - x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 4 } } }{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, y= {tex} \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } + \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } { \sqrt { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } - \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } \right){/tex}
Put x2 = sin{tex} \theta \Rightarrow \theta = \sin ^ { - 1 } x ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad y = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { \sqrt { 1 + \sin \theta } + \sqrt { 1 - \sin \theta } } { \sqrt { 1 + \sin \theta } - \sqrt { 1 - \sin \theta } } \right){/tex}
= {tex}{\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{\sqrt {{{\cos }^2}\frac{\theta }{2} + {{\sin }^2}\frac{\theta }{2} + 2\sin \frac{\theta }{2}\cos \frac{\theta }{2}} + \sqrt {{{\cos }^2}\frac{\theta }{2} + {{\sin }^2}\frac{\theta }{2} - 2\sin \frac{\theta }{2}\cos \frac{\theta }{2}} }}{{\sqrt {{{\cos }^2}\frac{\theta }{2} + {{\sin }^2}\frac{\theta }{2} + 2\sin \frac{\theta }{2}\cos \frac{\theta }{2}} - \sqrt {{{\cos }^2}\frac{\theta }{2} + {{\sin }^2}\frac{\theta }{2} - 2\sin \frac{\theta }{2}\cos \frac{\theta }{2}} }}} \right){/tex}
 = {tex} {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left[ {\frac{{\sqrt {{{\left( {\cos \frac{\theta }{2} + \sin \frac{\theta }{2}} \right)}^2}} + \sqrt {{{\left( {\cos \frac{\theta }{2} - \sin \frac{\theta }{2}} \right)}^2}} }}{{\sqrt {{{\left( {\cos \frac{\theta }{2} + \sin \frac{\theta }{2}} \right)}^2}} - \sqrt {{{\left( {\cos \frac{\theta }{2} - \sin \frac{\theta }{2}} \right)}^2}} }}} \right]{/tex}
= {tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { \left( \cos \frac { \theta } { 2 } + \sin \frac { \theta } { 2 } \right) + \left( \cos \frac { \theta } { 2 } - \sin \frac { \theta } { 2 } \right) } { \left( \cos \frac { \theta } { 2 } + \sin \frac { \theta } { 2 } \right) - \left( \cos \frac { \theta } { 2 } - \sin \frac { \theta } { 2 } \right) } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 2 \cos \frac { \theta } { 2 } } { 2 \sin \frac { \theta } { 2 } } \right){/tex}
{tex}= \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \cot \frac { \theta } { 2 } \right){/tex}
{tex}= \tan ^ { - 1 } \left[ \tan \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \frac { \theta } { 2 } \right) \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \frac { \theta } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y = \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sin ^ { - 1 } x ^ { 2 }{/tex}
Therefore, on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get,
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - \left( x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } } } ( 2 x ){/tex}
{tex} = \frac { - x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 4 } } }{/tex}

question_text: For what value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} , the function defined by f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { r } { \lambda \left( x ^ { 2 } + 2 \right) , \text { if } x \leq 0 } \\ { 4 x + 6 , \text { if } x > 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0? Hence, check the differentiability of f(x) at x = 0.
explanation: Given f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { r } { \lambda \left( x ^ { 2 } + 2 \right) , \text { if } x \leq 0 } \\ { 4 x + 6 , \text { if } x > 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0 Since f(x) is continuous at x=0.Therefore, we have, LHL = RHL = f(0).......(i) Here, RHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } ( 4 x + 6 ){/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0}[ 4 ( 0 + h ) + 6 ]{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( 4 h + 6 ){/tex} {tex} = 4 \times 0{/tex} + 6 = 6 From Eq(i), RHL = f(0) {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 \lambda = 6 \Rightarrow \lambda = 3{/tex} Now, given function becomes f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { c } { 3 \left( x ^ { 2 } + 2 \right) , \text { if } x \leq 0 } \\ { 4 x + 6 , \text { if } x > 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} Now, let us check the differentiability at x = 0. LHD = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { f ( 0 - h ) - f ( 0 ) } { - h }{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 3 \left[ ( 0 - h ) ^ { 2 } + 2 \right] - 3 ( 0 + 2 ) } { - h }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 3 \left[ h ^ { 2 } + 2 \right] - 6 } { - h } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( - 3 h ){/tex} = 0 and RHD = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { f ( 0 + h ) - f ( 0 ) } { h }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { [ 4 ( 0 + h ) + 6 ] - 3 ( 0 + 2 ) } { h } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 4 h } { h }{/tex} = 4 {tex}\because{/tex} LHD {tex}\ne{/tex} RHD {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) is not differentiable at x = 0.
solution: Given f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { r } { \lambda \left( x ^ { 2 } + 2 \right) , \text { if } x \leq 0 } \\ { 4 x + 6 , \text { if } x > 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0 Since f(x) is continuous at x=0.Therefore, we have, LHL = RHL = f(0).......(i) Here, RHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } ( 4 x + 6 ){/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0}[ 4 ( 0 + h ) + 6 ]{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( 4 h + 6 ){/tex} {tex} = 4 \times 0{/tex} + 6 = 6 From Eq(i), RHL = f(0) {tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 \lambda = 6 \Rightarrow \lambda = 3{/tex} Now, given function becomes f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { c } { 3 \left( x ^ { 2 } + 2 \right) , \text { if } x \leq 0 } \\ { 4 x + 6 , \text { if } x > 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} Now, let us check the differentiability at x = 0. LHD = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { f ( 0 - h ) - f ( 0 ) } { - h }{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 3 \left[ ( 0 - h ) ^ { 2 } + 2 \right] - 3 ( 0 + 2 ) } { - h }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 3 \left[ h ^ { 2 } + 2 \right] - 6 } { - h } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( - 3 h ){/tex} = 0 and RHD = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { f ( 0 + h ) - f ( 0 ) } { h }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { [ 4 ( 0 + h ) + 6 ] - 3 ( 0 + 2 ) } { h } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 4 h } { h }{/tex} = 4 {tex}\because{/tex} LHD {tex}\ne{/tex} RHD {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) is not differentiable at x = 0.
solution_html:
Given f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { r } { \lambda \left( x ^ { 2 } + 2 \right) , \text { if } x \leq 0 } \\ { 4 x + 6 , \text { if } x > 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex}  is continuous at x = 0
Since f(x) is continuous at x=0.Therefore, we have,
 LHL = RHL = f(0).......(i)
Here, RHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } ( 4 x + 6 ){/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0}[ 4 ( 0 + h ) + 6 ]{/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( 4 h + 6 ){/tex}
{tex} = 4 \times 0{/tex} + 6 = 6
From Eq(i), RHL = f(0)
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad 2 \lambda = 6 \Rightarrow \lambda = 3{/tex}
Now, given function becomes
f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { c } { 3 \left( x ^ { 2 } + 2 \right) , \text { if } x \leq 0 } \\ { 4 x + 6 , \text { if } x > 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex}
Now, let us check the differentiability at x = 0.
LHD = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { f ( 0 - h ) - f ( 0 ) } { - h }{/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 3 \left[ ( 0 - h ) ^ { 2 } + 2 \right] - 3 ( 0 + 2 ) } { - h }{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 3 \left[ h ^ { 2 } + 2 \right] - 6 } { - h } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( - 3 h ){/tex} = 0
and RHD = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { f ( 0 + h ) - f ( 0 ) } { h }{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { [ 4 ( 0 + h ) + 6 ] - 3 ( 0 + 2 ) } { h } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 4 h } { h }{/tex} = 4
{tex}\because{/tex} LHD {tex}\ne{/tex} RHD
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) is not differentiable at x = 0.

question_html: If y = {tex} \frac { x \cos ^ { - 1 } x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } - \log \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}, then prove that {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \cos ^ { - 1 } x } { \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } }{/tex}.
question_text: If y = {tex} \frac { x \cos ^ { - 1 } x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } - \log \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} , then prove that {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \cos ^ { - 1 } x } { \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } }{/tex} .
explanation: Now, y = {tex} \frac { x \cos ^ { - 1 } x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } - \log \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} .........(i) Therefore, on differentiating both sides of eq(i) w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} \left[ {x \cdot \frac{{( - 1)}}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} + {{\cos }^{ - 1}}x} \right] - x{{\cos }^{ - 1}}x\frac{1}{{2\ \sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }}( - 2x)}}{{{{\left( {\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}- \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } \cdot \frac { ( - 2 x ) } { 2 \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { - x + \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \cos ^ { - 1 } x + \frac { x ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { - 1 } x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } } { \left( \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} + {tex}\frac { x } { \left( \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} = {tex} \frac { - x + \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \cos ^ { - 1 } x + \frac { x ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { - 1 } x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } + x } { \left( \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \cos ^ { - 1 } x + x ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { - 1 } x } { \left( \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { \cos ^ { - 1 } x } { \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } }{/tex} Hence proved.
solution: Now, y = {tex} \frac { x \cos ^ { - 1 } x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } - \log \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} .........(i) Therefore, on differentiating both sides of eq(i) w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} \left[ {x \cdot \frac{{( - 1)}}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} + {{\cos }^{ - 1}}x} \right] - x{{\cos }^{ - 1}}x\frac{1}{{2\ \sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }}( - 2x)}}{{{{\left( {\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}- \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } \cdot \frac { ( - 2 x ) } { 2 \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { - x + \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \cos ^ { - 1 } x + \frac { x ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { - 1 } x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } } { \left( \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} + {tex}\frac { x } { \left( \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} = {tex} \frac { - x + \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \cos ^ { - 1 } x + \frac { x ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { - 1 } x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } + x } { \left( \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \cos ^ { - 1 } x + x ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { - 1 } x } { \left( \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { \cos ^ { - 1 } x } { \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } }{/tex} Hence proved.
solution_html:
Now, y = {tex} \frac { x \cos ^ { - 1 } x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } - \log \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}.........(i)
Therefore, on differentiating both sides of eq(i) w.r.t x, we get,
{tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} \left[ {x \cdot \frac{{( - 1)}}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} + {{\cos }^{ - 1}}x} \right] - x{{\cos }^{ - 1}}x\frac{1}{{2\ \sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }}( - 2x)}}{{{{\left( {\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} } \right)}^2}}}{/tex}{tex}- \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } \cdot \frac { ( - 2 x ) } { 2 \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
= {tex}\frac { - x + \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \cos ^ { - 1 } x + \frac { x ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { - 1 } x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } } { \left( \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} + {tex}\frac { x } { \left( \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
= {tex} \frac { - x + \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \cos ^ { - 1 } x + \frac { x ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { - 1 } x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } + x } { \left( \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \cos ^ { - 1 } x + x ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { - 1 } x } { \left( \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 3 } }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { \cos ^ { - 1 } x } { \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 / 2 } }{/tex}
Hence proved.

question_html: Differentiate the function (sin x)x + sin–1{tex}\sqrt{x}{/tex} w.r.t. x.
question_text: Differentiate the function (sin x) x + sin –1 {tex}\sqrt{x}{/tex} w.r.t. x.
explanation: Given function is: {tex}(\sin x)^{x}+\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{x}{/tex} Let {tex}y=(\sin x)^{x}+\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{x}{/tex} Let y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = (sin x) x and {tex}\mathrm{v}=\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} For, {tex}\mathrm{u}=(\sin \mathrm{x})^{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} Taking log on both sides, we get log u = log(sin x) x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = x.log sin x Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x {tex}\frac{d}{d x}(\log u)=\frac{d}{d x}[x \cdot \log (\sin x)]{/tex} {tex}\left.\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{u}} \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \log (\sin \mathrm{x})\right)+\log (\sin \mathrm{x}) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\mathrm{x}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{u}\left[\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{1}{\sin \mathrm{x}} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\sin \mathrm{x})+\log (\sin \mathrm{x}) \cdot(1)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=(\sin \mathrm{x})^{\mathrm{x}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sin \mathrm{x}} \cdot \cos \mathrm{x}+\log (\sin \mathrm{x}) \cdot(1)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\sin x)^{x}[x \cdot \cot x+\log \sin x]{/tex} For, v = {tex}\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{x}{/tex} Now, differentiating both sides with respect x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left[\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-(\sqrt{x})^{2}}} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(\sqrt{x}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x}} \cdot \frac{1}{2(\sqrt{x})}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x-x^{2}}}{/tex} Because, y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=(\sin x)^{x}[x \cdot \cot x+\log \sin x]+\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x-x^{2}}}{/tex}
solution: Given function is: {tex}(\sin x)^{x}+\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{x}{/tex} Let {tex}y=(\sin x)^{x}+\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{x}{/tex} Let y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = (sin x) x and {tex}\mathrm{v}=\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} For, {tex}\mathrm{u}=(\sin \mathrm{x})^{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} Taking log on both sides, we get log u = log(sin x) x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = x.log sin x Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x {tex}\frac{d}{d x}(\log u)=\frac{d}{d x}[x \cdot \log (\sin x)]{/tex} {tex}\left.\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{u}} \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \log (\sin \mathrm{x})\right)+\log (\sin \mathrm{x}) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\mathrm{x}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{u}\left[\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{1}{\sin \mathrm{x}} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\sin \mathrm{x})+\log (\sin \mathrm{x}) \cdot(1)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=(\sin \mathrm{x})^{\mathrm{x}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sin \mathrm{x}} \cdot \cos \mathrm{x}+\log (\sin \mathrm{x}) \cdot(1)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\sin x)^{x}[x \cdot \cot x+\log \sin x]{/tex} For, v = {tex}\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{x}{/tex} Now, differentiating both sides with respect x {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left[\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-(\sqrt{x})^{2}}} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(\sqrt{x}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x}} \cdot \frac{1}{2(\sqrt{x})}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x-x^{2}}}{/tex} Because, y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=(\sin x)^{x}[x \cdot \cot x+\log \sin x]+\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x-x^{2}}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given function is: {tex}(\sin x)^{x}+\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{x}{/tex} 
Let {tex}y=(\sin x)^{x}+\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{x}{/tex} 
Let y = u + v
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = (sin x)x and {tex}\mathrm{v}=\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} 
For, {tex}\mathrm{u}=(\sin \mathrm{x})^{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} 
Taking log on both sides, we get
log u = log(sin x)x 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = x.log sin x
Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x
{tex}\frac{d}{d x}(\log u)=\frac{d}{d x}[x \cdot \log (\sin x)]{/tex} 
{tex}\left.\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{u}} \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \log (\sin \mathrm{x})\right)+\log (\sin \mathrm{x}) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\mathrm{x}){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{u}\left[\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{1}{\sin \mathrm{x}} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\sin \mathrm{x})+\log (\sin \mathrm{x}) \cdot(1)\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=(\sin \mathrm{x})^{\mathrm{x}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sin \mathrm{x}} \cdot \cos \mathrm{x}+\log (\sin \mathrm{x}) \cdot(1)\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\sin x)^{x}[x \cdot \cot x+\log \sin x]{/tex} 
For, v = {tex}\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{x}{/tex} 
Now, differentiating both sides with respect x
{tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left[\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{x}\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-(\sqrt{x})^{2}}} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(\sqrt{x}){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x}} \cdot \frac{1}{2(\sqrt{x})}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x-x^{2}}}{/tex} 
Because, y = u + v
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=(\sin x)^{x}[x \cdot \cot x+\log \sin x]+\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x-x^{2}}}{/tex}

question_html: Find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} if yx + xy + xx = ab
question_text: Find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} if y x + x y + x x = a b
explanation: Let {tex}u = {y^x},v = {x^y},w = {x^x}{/tex} , then {tex}u + v + w = {a^b}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} + \frac{{dw}}{{dx}} + \frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} ...(1) {tex}u = {y^x}{/tex} Taking log both sides, we get {tex}\log u = \log {y^x}{/tex} {tex}\log u = x.\log y{/tex} Differentiate both sides w.r.t. to x {tex}\frac{1}{u}.\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = x.\frac{1}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log y.1{/tex} {tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = u\left[ {\frac{x}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log y} \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = {y^x}\left( {\frac{x}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log y} \right){/tex} ...(2) {tex}v = {x^y}{/tex} Taking log both sides, we get {tex}\log v = \log {x^y}{/tex} {tex}\log v = y.\log x{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}.\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = y.\frac{1}{x} + \log x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = v\left[ {\frac{y}{x} + \log x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = {x^y}\left[ {\frac{y}{x} + \log x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right]{/tex} ...(3) {tex}w = {x^x}{/tex} Taking log both sides, we get {tex}\log w = \log {x^x}{/tex} {tex}\log w = x\log x{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{w}.\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = x.\frac{1}{x} + \log x.1{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{w}.\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = 1 + \log x{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = w\left( {1 + \log x} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = {x^x}\left( {1 + \log x} \right){/tex} ... (4) {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - {x^x}\left( {1 + \log x} \right) - y.{x^{y - 1}} - {y^x}\log y}}{{x.{y^{x - 1}} + {x^y}\log x}}{/tex} (by putting(2), (3) and (4) in (1))
solution: Let {tex}u = {y^x},v = {x^y},w = {x^x}{/tex} , then {tex}u + v + w = {a^b}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} + \frac{{dw}}{{dx}} + \frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} ...(1) {tex}u = {y^x}{/tex} Taking log both sides, we get {tex}\log u = \log {y^x}{/tex} {tex}\log u = x.\log y{/tex} Differentiate both sides w.r.t. to x {tex}\frac{1}{u}.\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = x.\frac{1}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log y.1{/tex} {tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = u\left[ {\frac{x}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log y} \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = {y^x}\left( {\frac{x}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log y} \right){/tex} ...(2) {tex}v = {x^y}{/tex} Taking log both sides, we get {tex}\log v = \log {x^y}{/tex} {tex}\log v = y.\log x{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{v}.\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = y.\frac{1}{x} + \log x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = v\left[ {\frac{y}{x} + \log x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = {x^y}\left[ {\frac{y}{x} + \log x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right]{/tex} ...(3) {tex}w = {x^x}{/tex} Taking log both sides, we get {tex}\log w = \log {x^x}{/tex} {tex}\log w = x\log x{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{w}.\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = x.\frac{1}{x} + \log x.1{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{w}.\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = 1 + \log x{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = w\left( {1 + \log x} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = {x^x}\left( {1 + \log x} \right){/tex} ... (4) {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - {x^x}\left( {1 + \log x} \right) - y.{x^{y - 1}} - {y^x}\log y}}{{x.{y^{x - 1}} + {x^y}\log x}}{/tex} (by putting(2), (3) and (4) in (1))
solution_html:
Let {tex}u = {y^x},v = {x^y},w = {x^x}{/tex}, then

{tex}u + v + w = {a^b}{/tex}

Therefore, {tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} + \frac{{dw}}{{dx}} + \frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} ...(1)

{tex}u = {y^x}{/tex}

Taking log both sides, we get

{tex}\log u = \log {y^x}{/tex}

{tex}\log u = x.\log y{/tex}

Differentiate both sides w.r.t. to x

{tex}\frac{1}{u}.\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = x.\frac{1}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log y.1{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = u\left[ {\frac{x}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log y} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{du}}{{dx}} = {y^x}\left( {\frac{x}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log y} \right){/tex}...(2)

{tex}v = {x^y}{/tex}

Taking log both sides, we get

{tex}\log v = \log {x^y}{/tex}

{tex}\log v = y.\log x{/tex}

{tex}\frac{1}{v}.\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = y.\frac{1}{x} + \log x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = v\left[ {\frac{y}{x} + \log x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dx}} = {x^y}\left[ {\frac{y}{x} + \log x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right]{/tex}...(3)

{tex}w = {x^x}{/tex}

Taking log both sides, we get

{tex}\log w = \log {x^x}{/tex}

{tex}\log w = x\log x{/tex}

{tex}\frac{1}{w}.\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = x.\frac{1}{x} + \log x.1{/tex}

{tex}\frac{1}{w}.\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = 1 + \log x{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = w\left( {1 + \log x} \right){/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dw}}{{dx}} = {x^x}\left( {1 + \log x} \right){/tex}... (4)

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - {x^x}\left( {1 + \log x} \right) - y.{x^{y - 1}} - {y^x}\log y}}{{x.{y^{x - 1}} + {x^y}\log x}}{/tex} (by putting(2), (3) and (4) in (1))

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Discuss the continuity and differentiability of f(x) = |x| + |x - 1| in the interval ( - 1, 2).
question_text: Discuss the continuity and differentiability of f(x) = |x| + |x - 1| in the interval ( - 1, 2).
explanation: f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{aligned} x+x+1,-1<x<0 \\ 1,0 \leq x \leq 1 \\ -x-x+1,1<x<2 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex} f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{c} {2 x+1,-1<x<0} \\ {1,0 \leq x \leq 1} \\ {-2 x+1,1<x<2} \end{array}\right.{/tex} we know that a polynomial and a constant function is continuous and differentiable every where. So, f(x) is continuous and differentiable for {tex}x \in(-1,0){/tex} and {tex}x \in(0,1){/tex} and (1,2). We need to check continuity and differentiability at x = 0 and x = 1. Continuity at x = 0 {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} 2 x+1=1{/tex} {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0}+f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0}+1=1{/tex} f(0) = 1 {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x)=f(0){/tex} Since, f(x) is continuous at x = 0 Continuity at x = 1 {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} 1=1{/tex} {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{+}}+f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} 1=1{/tex} f(1) = 1 {tex}\log \limits_{x \rightarrow 0}+f(x)=\log \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=1{/tex} Since, f(x) is continuous at x = 1 For differentiability, LHD(at x = 0) = RHD (at x = 0) Differentiability at x = 0 (LHD at x = 0) = {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{f(x)-f(0)}{x-0}{/tex} = {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{2 x+1-1}{x-0}{/tex} = {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 x}{x}{/tex} = 2 (RHD at x = 0) = {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} \frac{f(x)-f(0)}{x-0}{/tex} = {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} \frac{1-1}{x}{/tex} = {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} \frac{0}{x}{/tex} = 0 Since,(LHD at x = 0) {tex}\neq{/tex} (RHD at x = 0) So, f(x) is differentiable at x = 0. For differentiability, LHD(at x = 1) = RHD (at x = 1) Differentiability at x = 1 (LHD at x = 1) = {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} \frac{f(x)-f(1)}{x-1}{/tex} = {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow-1^{-}} \frac{1-1}{x-1}{/tex} = 0 (RHD at x = 1) = {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} \frac{f(x)-f(1)}{x-1}{/tex} = {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} \frac{-2 x+1-1}{x-1}{/tex} = {tex}\infty{/tex} Since, f(x) is not differentiable at x = 1. So, f(x) is continuous on ( - 1,2) but not differentiable at x = 0, 1
solution: f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{aligned} x+x+1,-1<x<0 \\ 1,0 \leq x \leq 1 \\ -x-x+1,1<x<2 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex} f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{c} {2 x+1,-1<x<0} \\ {1,0 \leq x \leq 1} \\ {-2 x+1,1<x<2} \end{array}\right.{/tex} we know that a polynomial and a constant function is continuous and differentiable every where. So, f(x) is continuous and differentiable for {tex}x \in(-1,0){/tex} and {tex}x \in(0,1){/tex} and (1,2). We need to check continuity and differentiability at x = 0 and x = 1. Continuity at x = 0 {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} 2 x+1=1{/tex} {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0}+f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0}+1=1{/tex} f(0) = 1 {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x)=f(0){/tex} Since, f(x) is continuous at x = 0 Continuity at x = 1 {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} 1=1{/tex} {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{+}}+f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} 1=1{/tex} f(1) = 1 {tex}\log \limits_{x \rightarrow 0}+f(x)=\log \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=1{/tex} Since, f(x) is continuous at x = 1 For differentiability, LHD(at x = 0) = RHD (at x = 0) Differentiability at x = 0 (LHD at x = 0) = {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{f(x)-f(0)}{x-0}{/tex} = {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{2 x+1-1}{x-0}{/tex} = {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 x}{x}{/tex} = 2 (RHD at x = 0) = {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} \frac{f(x)-f(0)}{x-0}{/tex} = {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} \frac{1-1}{x}{/tex} = {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} \frac{0}{x}{/tex} = 0 Since,(LHD at x = 0) {tex}\neq{/tex} (RHD at x = 0) So, f(x) is differentiable at x = 0. For differentiability, LHD(at x = 1) = RHD (at x = 1) Differentiability at x = 1 (LHD at x = 1) = {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} \frac{f(x)-f(1)}{x-1}{/tex} = {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow-1^{-}} \frac{1-1}{x-1}{/tex} = 0 (RHD at x = 1) = {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} \frac{f(x)-f(1)}{x-1}{/tex} = {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} \frac{-2 x+1-1}{x-1}{/tex} = {tex}\infty{/tex} Since, f(x) is not differentiable at x = 1. So, f(x) is continuous on ( - 1,2) but not differentiable at x = 0, 1
solution_html:
f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{aligned} x+x+1,-1<x<0 \\ 1,0 \leq x \leq 1 \\ -x-x+1,1<x<2 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex}

f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{c} {2 x+1,-1<x<0} \\ {1,0 \leq x \leq 1} \\ {-2 x+1,1<x<2} \end{array}\right.{/tex}

we know that a polynomial and a constant function is continuous and differentiable every where. So, f(x) is continuous and differentiable for {tex}x \in(-1,0){/tex}and {tex}x \in(0,1){/tex} and (1,2).
We need to check continuity and differentiability at x = 0 and x = 1.
Continuity at x = 0
{tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} 2 x+1=1{/tex}
{tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0}+f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0}+1=1{/tex}
f(0) = 1
{tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x)=f(0){/tex}
Since, f(x) is continuous at x = 0
Continuity at x = 1
{tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} 1=1{/tex}
{tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{+}}+f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} 1=1{/tex}
f(1) = 1
{tex}\log \limits_{x \rightarrow 0}+f(x)=\log \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=1{/tex}
Since, f(x) is continuous at x = 1
For differentiability,
LHD(at x = 0) = RHD (at x = 0)
Differentiability at x = 0
(LHD at x = 0) = {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{f(x)-f(0)}{x-0}{/tex}
= {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{2 x+1-1}{x-0}{/tex}
= {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 x}{x}{/tex}
= 2
(RHD at x = 0) = {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} \frac{f(x)-f(0)}{x-0}{/tex}
= {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} \frac{1-1}{x}{/tex}
= {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} \frac{0}{x}{/tex}
= 0
Since,(LHD at x = 0){tex}\neq{/tex}(RHD at x = 0)
So, f(x) is differentiable at x = 0.
For differentiability,
LHD(at x = 1) = RHD (at x = 1)
Differentiability at x = 1
(LHD at x = 1) = {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} \frac{f(x)-f(1)}{x-1}{/tex}
= {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow-1^{-}} \frac{1-1}{x-1}{/tex}
= 0
(RHD at x = 1) = {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} \frac{f(x)-f(1)}{x-1}{/tex}
= {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} \frac{-2 x+1-1}{x-1}{/tex}
= {tex}\infty{/tex}
Since, f(x) is not differentiable at x = 1.
So, f(x) is continuous on ( - 1,2) but not differentiable at x = 0, 1

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If x = a(cos 2t + 2t sin 2t) and y = a (sin 2t - 2t cos 2t), then find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}
question_text: If x = a(cos 2t + 2t sin 2t) and y = a (sin 2t - 2t cos 2t), then find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}
explanation: Given, x = a (cos 2t + 2t sin 2t) {tex}\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} = -2 a sin 2t + 2 a sin 2t + 4 a t cos 2 t = 4 a t cos 2t ....(i) Also, given that, y = a(sin 2t - 2t cos 2t) {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}{/tex} = 2 a cos 2t - 2 a cos 2t + 4 a t sin 2t = 4 a t sin 2t......(ii) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\tan 2 t{/tex} (divide (ii) by (i)) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}(\tan 2 t){/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\sec ^{2} 2 t \frac{d}{d x}(2 t){/tex} (using chain rule) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=2 \sec ^{2} 2 t \frac{d}{d x}(t){/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=2 \sec ^{2} 2 t \times \frac{1}{4 a t \cos 2 t}{/tex} (from (i)) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{1}{2 a} \sec ^{3} 2 t{/tex}
solution: Given, x = a (cos 2t + 2t sin 2t) {tex}\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} = -2 a sin 2t + 2 a sin 2t + 4 a t cos 2 t = 4 a t cos 2t ....(i) Also, given that, y = a(sin 2t - 2t cos 2t) {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}{/tex} = 2 a cos 2t - 2 a cos 2t + 4 a t sin 2t = 4 a t sin 2t......(ii) {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\tan 2 t{/tex} (divide (ii) by (i)) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}(\tan 2 t){/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\sec ^{2} 2 t \frac{d}{d x}(2 t){/tex} (using chain rule) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=2 \sec ^{2} 2 t \frac{d}{d x}(t){/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=2 \sec ^{2} 2 t \times \frac{1}{4 a t \cos 2 t}{/tex} (from (i)) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{1}{2 a} \sec ^{3} 2 t{/tex}
solution_html:
Given,
x = a (cos 2t + 2t sin 2t)
{tex}\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} = -2 a sin 2t + 2 a sin 2t + 4 a t cos 2 t = 4 a t cos 2t ....(i)
Also, given that,
y = a(sin 2t - 2t cos 2t)
{tex}\frac{d y}{d t}{/tex} = 2 a cos 2t - 2 a cos 2t + 4 a t sin 2t = 4 a t sin 2t......(ii)
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\tan 2 t{/tex}         (divide (ii) by (i))
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}(\tan 2 t){/tex}
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\sec ^{2} 2 t \frac{d}{d x}(2 t){/tex}   (using chain rule)
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=2 \sec ^{2} 2 t \frac{d}{d x}(t){/tex}
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=2 \sec ^{2} 2 t \times \frac{1}{4 a t \cos 2 t}{/tex}    (from (i))
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{1}{2 a} \sec ^{3} 2 t{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}\frac{x}{x-y}=\log \frac{a}{x-y}{/tex}, then prove that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=2-\frac{x}{y}{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}\frac{x}{x-y}=\log \frac{a}{x-y}{/tex} , then prove that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=2-\frac{x}{y}{/tex} .
explanation: We have {tex}\frac{x}{(x-y)}=\log \left(\frac{a}{x-y}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{(x-y)}{/tex} = log a - log (x - y) [As, log {tex}(\frac{m}{n}){/tex} = log m - log n] Differentiating both sides with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{({x}-{y}) \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}({x})-{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} {x}}({x}-{y})}{({x}-{y})^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{(x-y)} \frac{d}{d x}(x-y){/tex} [using division rule and {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex} {log(a)} = 0] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{(x-y)-x\left(1-\frac{d y}{d x}\right)}{(x-y)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{(x-y)}\left(1-\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - y) - {tex}\mathrm{x}\left(1-\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right){/tex} = -(x - y) {tex}\left(1-\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - y) = {-(x - y) + x} {tex}\left(1-\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - y) = y {tex}\left(1-\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(1-\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{x-y}{y}=\frac{x}{y}{/tex} - 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{y}}-2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=2-\frac{x}{y}{/tex}
solution: We have {tex}\frac{x}{(x-y)}=\log \left(\frac{a}{x-y}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{(x-y)}{/tex} = log a - log (x - y) [As, log {tex}(\frac{m}{n}){/tex} = log m - log n] Differentiating both sides with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{({x}-{y}) \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}({x})-{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} {x}}({x}-{y})}{({x}-{y})^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{(x-y)} \frac{d}{d x}(x-y){/tex} [using division rule and {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex} {log(a)} = 0] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{(x-y)-x\left(1-\frac{d y}{d x}\right)}{(x-y)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{(x-y)}\left(1-\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - y) - {tex}\mathrm{x}\left(1-\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right){/tex} = -(x - y) {tex}\left(1-\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - y) = {-(x - y) + x} {tex}\left(1-\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - y) = y {tex}\left(1-\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(1-\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{x-y}{y}=\frac{x}{y}{/tex} - 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{y}}-2{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=2-\frac{x}{y}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have
{tex}\frac{x}{(x-y)}=\log \left(\frac{a}{x-y}\right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{x}{(x-y)}{/tex} = log a - log (x - y)  [As, log{tex}(\frac{m}{n}){/tex} = log m - log n]
Differentiating both sides with respect to x, we get
{tex}\frac{({x}-{y}) \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}({x})-{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d} {x}}({x}-{y})}{({x}-{y})^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{(x-y)} \frac{d}{d x}(x-y){/tex} [using division rule and {tex}\frac{d}{dx}{/tex} {log(a)} = 0]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{(x-y)-x\left(1-\frac{d y}{d x}\right)}{(x-y)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{(x-y)}\left(1-\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - y) - {tex}\mathrm{x}\left(1-\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right){/tex} = -(x - y) {tex}\left(1-\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - y) = {-(x - y) + x} {tex}\left(1-\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - y) = y{tex}\left(1-\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(1-\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{x-y}{y}=\frac{x}{y}{/tex} - 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}-\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{y}}-2{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=2-\frac{x}{y}{/tex}

question_html: If y = eax.cos bx, then prove that: {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-2 a \frac{d y}{d x}+\left(a^{2}+b^{2}\right) y=0{/tex}
question_text: If y = e ax .cos bx, then prove that: {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-2 a \frac{d y}{d x}+\left(a^{2}+b^{2}\right) y=0{/tex}
explanation: Given y = e ax .cos bx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = ae ax cos bx - be ax sin bx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = ay - be ax sin bx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = a {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} - b[ae ax sin bx + be ax cos bx] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = a {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} - {tex}a\left(a y-\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} - b 2 y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 2a {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} - a 2 y - b 2 y {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} - 2a {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} + (a 2 + b 2 )y = 0, Hence proved.
solution: Given y = e ax .cos bx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = ae ax cos bx - be ax sin bx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = ay - be ax sin bx {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = a {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} - b[ae ax sin bx + be ax cos bx] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = a {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} - {tex}a\left(a y-\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} - b 2 y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 2a {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} - a 2 y - b 2 y {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} - 2a {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} + (a 2 + b 2 )y = 0, Hence proved.
solution_html:
Given y = eax.cos bx
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}= aeaxcos bx - beaxsin bx
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}= ay - beax sin bx
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}= a{tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} - b[aeaxsin bx + beaxcos bx]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = a{tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} - {tex}a\left(a y-\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex}- b2y
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}= 2a {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} - a2y - b2y
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} - 2a {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} + (a2 + b2)y = 0, Hence proved.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If x = a sin 2t (1 + cos 2t) and y = b cos 2t (1 – cos 2t), then find {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} at t = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}.
question_text: If x = a sin 2t (1 + cos 2t) and y = b cos 2t (1 – cos 2t), then find {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} at t = {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} .
explanation: We know that, x = a sin 2t (1 + cos 2t) and y = b cos 2t (1 – cos 2t) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dt}{/tex} = a[-2sin 2t sin 2t + 2cos 2t (1 + cos 2t)]....(I) and {tex}\frac{dx}{dt}{/tex} = b[2sin 2t cos 2t - 2sin 2t (1 - cos 2t)]...(ii) {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{dy}{dt}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{dt}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{b[2 \sin 2 t \cos 2 t-2 \sin 2 t(1-\cos 2 t)]}{a\left[-2 \sin ^{2} 2 t+2 \cos 2 t(1+\cos 2 t)\right]}{/tex} . [Using (i) and (ii)] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left.\frac{d y}{d x}\right]_{at\; t=\pi/4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{b\left[2 \sin \frac{\pi}{2} \cos \frac{\pi}{2}-2 \sin \frac{\pi}{2}\left(1-\cos \frac{\pi}{2}\right)\right]}{a\left[-2 \sin ^{2}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)+2 \cos \frac{\pi}{2}\left(1+\cos \frac{\pi}{2}\right)\right]}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{b}{a} \cdot \frac{(0-1)}{(-1-0)}=\frac{b}{a}{/tex}
solution: We know that, x = a sin 2t (1 + cos 2t) and y = b cos 2t (1 – cos 2t) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dt}{/tex} = a[-2sin 2t sin 2t + 2cos 2t (1 + cos 2t)]....(I) and {tex}\frac{dx}{dt}{/tex} = b[2sin 2t cos 2t - 2sin 2t (1 - cos 2t)]...(ii) {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{dy}{dt}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{dt}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{b[2 \sin 2 t \cos 2 t-2 \sin 2 t(1-\cos 2 t)]}{a\left[-2 \sin ^{2} 2 t+2 \cos 2 t(1+\cos 2 t)\right]}{/tex} . [Using (i) and (ii)] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left.\frac{d y}{d x}\right]_{at\; t=\pi/4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{b\left[2 \sin \frac{\pi}{2} \cos \frac{\pi}{2}-2 \sin \frac{\pi}{2}\left(1-\cos \frac{\pi}{2}\right)\right]}{a\left[-2 \sin ^{2}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)+2 \cos \frac{\pi}{2}\left(1+\cos \frac{\pi}{2}\right)\right]}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{b}{a} \cdot \frac{(0-1)}{(-1-0)}=\frac{b}{a}{/tex}
solution_html:
We know that, x = a sin 2t (1 + cos 2t) and y = b cos 2t (1 – cos 2t)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dt}{/tex} = a[-2sin 2t sin 2t + 2cos 2t (1 + cos 2t)]....(I)
and {tex}\frac{dx}{dt}{/tex} = b[2sin 2t cos 2t - 2sin 2t (1 - cos 2t)]...(ii)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}= {tex}\frac{dy}{dt}{/tex}{tex}\times{/tex}{tex}\frac{dt}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{b[2 \sin 2 t \cos 2 t-2 \sin 2 t(1-\cos 2 t)]}{a\left[-2 \sin ^{2} 2 t+2 \cos 2 t(1+\cos 2 t)\right]}{/tex}.  [Using (i) and (ii)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left.\frac{d y}{d x}\right]_{at\; t=\pi/4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{b\left[2 \sin \frac{\pi}{2} \cos \frac{\pi}{2}-2 \sin \frac{\pi}{2}\left(1-\cos \frac{\pi}{2}\right)\right]}{a\left[-2 \sin ^{2}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)+2 \cos \frac{\pi}{2}\left(1+\cos \frac{\pi}{2}\right)\right]}{/tex}  {tex}=\frac{b}{a} \cdot \frac{(0-1)}{(-1-0)}=\frac{b}{a}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Let f(x) = x - {tex}\left|\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}^{2}\right|, \mathbf{x} \in{/tex} [-1, 1]. Find the point of discontinuity, (if any), of this function on [-1 , 1].
question_text: Let f(x) = x - {tex}\left|\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}^{2}\right|, \mathbf{x} \in{/tex} [-1, 1]. Find the point of discontinuity, (if any), of this function on [-1 , 1].
explanation: Here {tex}f(x)=x-\left|x-x^{2}\right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{x}-\left|-\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}-\mathrm{x}\right)\right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{x}-\left|\mathrm{x}^{2}-\mathrm{x}\right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{x}-|\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-1)|{/tex} Now {tex}|\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-1)|=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} -\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-1) & 0 \leq \mathrm{x} \leq 1 \\ \mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-1) & -1 \leq \mathrm{x} \leq 0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-1) & 0 \leq \mathrm{x} \leq 1 \\ \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-1) & -1 \leq \mathrm{x} \leq 0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} x-x^{2}+x 0 \leq x \leq 1 \\ x+x^{2}-x-1 \leq x \leq 0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} 2 \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}^{2} & 0 \leq \mathrm{x}<1 \\ \mathrm{x}^{2} & -1 \leq \mathrm{x}<0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} Now our point of concern is x = 0 R.H.L. {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x){/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} 2 x-x^{2}{/tex} = 0 - 0 = 0 L.H.L. {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}){/tex} {tex}= \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow0^-}x^2{/tex} = 0 - 0 = 0 And, f(0) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=f(0){/tex} Therefore, Function is continuous at x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})=\mathbf{x}-\left|\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}^{2}\right|{/tex} is continuous for all {tex}\mathbf{x} \in[-\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{1}]{/tex}
solution: Here {tex}f(x)=x-\left|x-x^{2}\right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{x}-\left|-\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}-\mathrm{x}\right)\right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{x}-\left|\mathrm{x}^{2}-\mathrm{x}\right|{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{x}-|\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-1)|{/tex} Now {tex}|\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-1)|=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} -\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-1) & 0 \leq \mathrm{x} \leq 1 \\ \mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-1) & -1 \leq \mathrm{x} \leq 0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-1) & 0 \leq \mathrm{x} \leq 1 \\ \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-1) & -1 \leq \mathrm{x} \leq 0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} x-x^{2}+x 0 \leq x \leq 1 \\ x+x^{2}-x-1 \leq x \leq 0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} 2 \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}^{2} & 0 \leq \mathrm{x}<1 \\ \mathrm{x}^{2} & -1 \leq \mathrm{x}<0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} Now our point of concern is x = 0 R.H.L. {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x){/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} 2 x-x^{2}{/tex} = 0 - 0 = 0 L.H.L. {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}){/tex} {tex}= \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow0^-}x^2{/tex} = 0 - 0 = 0 And, f(0) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=f(0){/tex} Therefore, Function is continuous at x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})=\mathbf{x}-\left|\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}^{2}\right|{/tex} is continuous for all {tex}\mathbf{x} \in[-\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{1}]{/tex}
solution_html:
Here
{tex}f(x)=x-\left|x-x^{2}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{x}-\left|-\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}-\mathrm{x}\right)\right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{x}-\left|\mathrm{x}^{2}-\mathrm{x}\right|{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\mathrm{x}-|\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-1)|{/tex}
Now
{tex}|\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-1)|=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} -\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-1) & 0 \leq \mathrm{x} \leq 1 \\ \mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-1) & -1 \leq \mathrm{x} \leq 0 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-1) & 0 \leq \mathrm{x} \leq 1 \\ \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x}(\mathrm{x}-1) & -1 \leq \mathrm{x} \leq 0 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} x-x^{2}+x 0 \leq x \leq 1 \\ x+x^{2}-x-1 \leq x \leq 0 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} 2 \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}^{2} & 0 \leq \mathrm{x}<1 \\ \mathrm{x}^{2} & -1 \leq \mathrm{x}<0 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
Now our point of concern is x = 0
R.H.L. {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x){/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} 2 x-x^{2}{/tex}= 0 - 0 = 0
L.H.L. {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x}){/tex} {tex}= \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow0^-}x^2{/tex} = 0 - 0 = 0
And, f(0) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=f(0){/tex}
Therefore, Function is continuous at x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x})=\mathbf{x}-\left|\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}^{2}\right|{/tex} is continuous for all {tex}\mathbf{x} \in[-\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{1}]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the derivative of sec-1{tex}\left(\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{x}^{2}-1}\right){/tex} w.r.t {tex} \frac{1}{1-x^{2}}\\ {/tex} at x = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
question_text: Find the derivative of sec -1 {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{x}^{2}-1}\right){/tex} w.r.t {tex} \frac{1}{1-x^{2}}\\ {/tex} at x = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}u=s e c^{-1}\left[\frac{1}{2 x^{2}-1}\right]{/tex} Put x = cos {tex}\theta{/tex} So, {tex}u=\sec ^{-1}\left[\frac{1}{2 \cos ^{2} \theta-1}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow u=\sec ^{-1}\left[\frac{1}{\cos 2 \theta}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow u=\sec ^{-1}(\sec 2 \theta){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow u=2 \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow u=2 \cos ^{-1} x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=-\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} Let v = {tex} \frac{1}{1-x^{2}}\\ {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2 x}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{d u}{d x} \times \frac{d x}{d v}=-\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} \times \frac{\left(1-x^{2}\right)^{2}}{2 x}=-\frac{\left(1-x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{x}{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{d u}{d v}\right]_{x=1 \over 2}=-\frac{(3 / 4)^{3 \over 2}}{1 \over 2}=-\frac{3 \sqrt{3} \over 8}{1 \over 2}=-\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{4}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}u=s e c^{-1}\left[\frac{1}{2 x^{2}-1}\right]{/tex} Put x = cos {tex}\theta{/tex} So, {tex}u=\sec ^{-1}\left[\frac{1}{2 \cos ^{2} \theta-1}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow u=\sec ^{-1}\left[\frac{1}{\cos 2 \theta}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow u=\sec ^{-1}(\sec 2 \theta){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow u=2 \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow u=2 \cos ^{-1} x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=-\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} Let v = {tex} \frac{1}{1-x^{2}}\\ {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2 x}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{d u}{d x} \times \frac{d x}{d v}=-\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} \times \frac{\left(1-x^{2}\right)^{2}}{2 x}=-\frac{\left(1-x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{x}{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{d u}{d v}\right]_{x=1 \over 2}=-\frac{(3 / 4)^{3 \over 2}}{1 \over 2}=-\frac{3 \sqrt{3} \over 8}{1 \over 2}=-\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}u=s e c^{-1}\left[\frac{1}{2 x^{2}-1}\right]{/tex}
Put x = cos{tex}\theta{/tex}
So, {tex}u=\sec ^{-1}\left[\frac{1}{2 \cos ^{2} \theta-1}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow u=\sec ^{-1}\left[\frac{1}{\cos 2 \theta}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow u=\sec ^{-1}(\sec 2 \theta){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow u=2 \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow u=2 \cos ^{-1} x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=-\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex}
Let v = {tex} \frac{1}{1-x^{2}}\\ {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2 x}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{d u}{d x} \times \frac{d x}{d v}=-\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} \times \frac{\left(1-x^{2}\right)^{2}}{2 x}=-\frac{\left(1-x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\left[\frac{d u}{d v}\right]_{x=1 \over 2}=-\frac{(3 / 4)^{3 \over 2}}{1 \over 2}=-\frac{3 \sqrt{3} \over 8}{1 \over 2}=-\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{4}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If y = {tex}\sqrt{x+1}-\sqrt{x-1}{/tex}, prove that {tex}\left(x^{2}-1\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{1}{4} y=0{/tex}
question_text: If y = {tex}\sqrt{x+1}-\sqrt{x-1}{/tex} , prove that {tex}\left(x^{2}-1\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{1}{4} y=0{/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}y=\sqrt{x+1}-\sqrt{x-1}{/tex} ........(1) So {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x+1}}-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x-1}}{/tex} ...........(2) {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{x-1}-\sqrt{x+1}}{4 \sqrt{x^{2}-1}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sqrt{x-1}-\sqrt{x+1}}{4 \sqrt{x^{2}-1}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\sqrt{x^{2}-1}\left(\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x-1}}-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x+1}}\right)-(\sqrt{x-1}-\sqrt{x+1}) \times \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^{2}-1}}}{4\left(x^{2}-1\right)}{/tex} {tex}4\left(x^{2}-1\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\sqrt{x-1} \times \sqrt{x+1}\left(\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x-1}},-, \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x+1}}\right)-(\sqrt{x-1}-\sqrt{x+1}) \times \frac{x}{\sqrt{x-1} \times \sqrt{x+1}}{/tex} {tex}4\left(x^{2}-1\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{1}{2}[\sqrt{x+1}-\sqrt{x-1}]-x\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{x+1}},-, \frac{1}{\sqrt{x-1}}\right){/tex} {tex}4\left(x^{2}-1\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{y}{2}-2 x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\left(x^{2}-1\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{x}{2} \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{8}=0{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}y=\sqrt{x+1}-\sqrt{x-1}{/tex} ........(1) So {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x+1}}-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x-1}}{/tex} ...........(2) {tex}=\frac{\sqrt{x-1}-\sqrt{x+1}}{4 \sqrt{x^{2}-1}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sqrt{x-1}-\sqrt{x+1}}{4 \sqrt{x^{2}-1}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\sqrt{x^{2}-1}\left(\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x-1}}-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x+1}}\right)-(\sqrt{x-1}-\sqrt{x+1}) \times \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^{2}-1}}}{4\left(x^{2}-1\right)}{/tex} {tex}4\left(x^{2}-1\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\sqrt{x-1} \times \sqrt{x+1}\left(\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x-1}},-, \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x+1}}\right)-(\sqrt{x-1}-\sqrt{x+1}) \times \frac{x}{\sqrt{x-1} \times \sqrt{x+1}}{/tex} {tex}4\left(x^{2}-1\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{1}{2}[\sqrt{x+1}-\sqrt{x-1}]-x\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{x+1}},-, \frac{1}{\sqrt{x-1}}\right){/tex} {tex}4\left(x^{2}-1\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{y}{2}-2 x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\left(x^{2}-1\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{x}{2} \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{8}=0{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}y=\sqrt{x+1}-\sqrt{x-1}{/tex}  ........(1)
So {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x+1}}-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x-1}}{/tex} ...........(2)
{tex}=\frac{\sqrt{x-1}-\sqrt{x+1}}{4 \sqrt{x^{2}-1}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sqrt{x-1}-\sqrt{x+1}}{4 \sqrt{x^{2}-1}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\sqrt{x^{2}-1}\left(\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x-1}}-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x+1}}\right)-(\sqrt{x-1}-\sqrt{x+1}) \times \frac{x}{\sqrt{x^{2}-1}}}{4\left(x^{2}-1\right)}{/tex}
{tex}4\left(x^{2}-1\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\sqrt{x-1} \times \sqrt{x+1}\left(\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x-1}},-, \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x+1}}\right)-(\sqrt{x-1}-\sqrt{x+1}) \times \frac{x}{\sqrt{x-1} \times \sqrt{x+1}}{/tex}
{tex}4\left(x^{2}-1\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{1}{2}[\sqrt{x+1}-\sqrt{x-1}]-x\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{x+1}},-, \frac{1}{\sqrt{x-1}}\right){/tex}
{tex}4\left(x^{2}-1\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{y}{2}-2 x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\left(x^{2}-1\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{x}{2} \frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{8}=0{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: if {tex}\mathbf{x}=\alpha \sin 2 t(1+\cos 2 t) \text { and } y=\beta \cos 2 t(1-\cos 2 t){/tex} , show that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\beta}{\alpha} {/tex} at t = {tex}\frac\pi4{/tex}
question_text: if {tex}\mathbf{x}=\alpha \sin 2 t(1+\cos 2 t) \text { and } y=\beta \cos 2 t(1-\cos 2 t){/tex} , show that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\beta}{\alpha} {/tex} at t = {tex}\frac\pi4{/tex}
explanation: Here we have have, {tex}x=\alpha \sin 2 t(1+\cos 2 t){/tex} and {tex}y=\beta\cos 2 t(1-\cos 2 t){/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{d x}{d t}=\alpha\left[\sin 2 t \cdot \frac{d}{d t}(1+\cos 2 t)+(1+\cos 2 t) \frac{d}{d t} \sin 2 t\right]{/tex} = {tex}\alpha{/tex} [sin 2t .(-2 sin 2 t) + (1+cos 2 t) . 2 cos 2t] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}=-2 \alpha\left[\sin ^{2} 2 t-\cos 2 t(1+\cos 2 t)\right]{/tex} ........(i) and {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=\beta\left[\cos 2 t \cdot \frac{d}{d t}(1-\cos 2 t)+(1-\cos 2 t) \cdot \frac{d}{d t} \cos 2 t\right]{/tex} = {tex}\beta{/tex} [cos 2 t(2 sin 2 t)+(1-cos 2 t)(-2 sin 2 t)] = 2 {tex}\beta{/tex} [sin 2 t cos 2 t-(1-cos 2 t) sin 2 t] {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y / d t}{d x / d t}=\frac{2 \beta[\sin 2 t \cdot \cos 2 t-(1-\cos 2 t) \sin 2 t]}{-2 \alpha\left[\sin ^{2} 2 t-\cos 2 t(1+\cos 2 t)\right]}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{t=\pi / 4}=-\frac{\beta}{\alpha} \frac{\left[\sin \frac{\pi}{2} \cos \frac{\pi}{2}-\left(1-\cos \frac{\pi}{2}\right) \sin \frac{\pi}{2}\right]}{\left[\sin ^{2} \frac{\pi}{2}-\cos \frac{\pi}{2}\left(1+\cos \frac{\pi}{2}\right)\right]}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{\beta}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{(0-1)}{(1-0)}=\frac{\beta}{\alpha} {/tex}
solution: Here we have have, {tex}x=\alpha \sin 2 t(1+\cos 2 t){/tex} and {tex}y=\beta\cos 2 t(1-\cos 2 t){/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{d x}{d t}=\alpha\left[\sin 2 t \cdot \frac{d}{d t}(1+\cos 2 t)+(1+\cos 2 t) \frac{d}{d t} \sin 2 t\right]{/tex} = {tex}\alpha{/tex} [sin 2t .(-2 sin 2 t) + (1+cos 2 t) . 2 cos 2t] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}=-2 \alpha\left[\sin ^{2} 2 t-\cos 2 t(1+\cos 2 t)\right]{/tex} ........(i) and {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=\beta\left[\cos 2 t \cdot \frac{d}{d t}(1-\cos 2 t)+(1-\cos 2 t) \cdot \frac{d}{d t} \cos 2 t\right]{/tex} = {tex}\beta{/tex} [cos 2 t(2 sin 2 t)+(1-cos 2 t)(-2 sin 2 t)] = 2 {tex}\beta{/tex} [sin 2 t cos 2 t-(1-cos 2 t) sin 2 t] {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y / d t}{d x / d t}=\frac{2 \beta[\sin 2 t \cdot \cos 2 t-(1-\cos 2 t) \sin 2 t]}{-2 \alpha\left[\sin ^{2} 2 t-\cos 2 t(1+\cos 2 t)\right]}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{t=\pi / 4}=-\frac{\beta}{\alpha} \frac{\left[\sin \frac{\pi}{2} \cos \frac{\pi}{2}-\left(1-\cos \frac{\pi}{2}\right) \sin \frac{\pi}{2}\right]}{\left[\sin ^{2} \frac{\pi}{2}-\cos \frac{\pi}{2}\left(1+\cos \frac{\pi}{2}\right)\right]}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{\beta}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{(0-1)}{(1-0)}=\frac{\beta}{\alpha} {/tex}
solution_html:
Here we have have, {tex}x=\alpha \sin 2 t(1+\cos 2 t){/tex} and  {tex}y=\beta\cos 2 t(1-\cos 2 t){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{d x}{d t}=\alpha\left[\sin 2 t \cdot \frac{d}{d t}(1+\cos 2 t)+(1+\cos 2 t) \frac{d}{d t} \sin 2 t\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\alpha{/tex}[sin 2t .(-2 sin 2 t) + (1+cos 2 t) . 2 cos 2t]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}=-2 \alpha\left[\sin ^{2} 2 t-\cos 2 t(1+\cos 2 t)\right]{/tex}........(i)
and {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=\beta\left[\cos 2 t \cdot \frac{d}{d t}(1-\cos 2 t)+(1-\cos 2 t) \cdot \frac{d}{d t} \cos 2 t\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\beta{/tex}[cos 2 t(2 sin 2 t)+(1-cos 2 t)(-2 sin 2 t)]
= 2 {tex}\beta{/tex}[sin 2 t cos 2 t-(1-cos 2 t) sin 2 t]
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y / d t}{d x / d t}=\frac{2 \beta[\sin 2 t \cdot \cos 2 t-(1-\cos 2 t) \sin 2 t]}{-2 \alpha\left[\sin ^{2} 2 t-\cos 2 t(1+\cos 2 t)\right]}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{t=\pi / 4}=-\frac{\beta}{\alpha} \frac{\left[\sin \frac{\pi}{2} \cos \frac{\pi}{2}-\left(1-\cos \frac{\pi}{2}\right) \sin \frac{\pi}{2}\right]}{\left[\sin ^{2} \frac{\pi}{2}-\cos \frac{\pi}{2}\left(1+\cos \frac{\pi}{2}\right)\right]}{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{\beta}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{(0-1)}{(1-0)}=\frac{\beta}{\alpha} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Find:
{tex}\frac{d}{d x} \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{x-x^{-1}}{x+x^{-1}}\right){/tex}
question_text: Find: {tex}\frac{d}{d x} \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{x-x^{-1}}{x+x^{-1}}\right){/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}\mathrm{y}=\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}^{-1}}{\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x}^{-1}}\right]{/tex} , then we have {tex} \mathrm{y}=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}}{\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}-1}{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}\right]{/tex} Put x = tan {tex}\theta{/tex} Now, y = {tex}\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{\tan ^{2} \theta-1}{\tan ^{2} \theta+1}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\cos ^{-1}\left[-\left(\frac{1-\tan ^{2} \theta}{1+\tan ^{2} \theta}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\cos ^{-1}[-\cos 2 \theta]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\cos ^{-1}[\cos (\pi+2 \theta)]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\cos ^{-1}[\cos (\pi+2 \theta)]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=0+\frac{2}{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}=\frac{2}{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}\mathrm{y}=\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}^{-1}}{\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x}^{-1}}\right]{/tex} , then we have {tex} \mathrm{y}=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}}{\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}-1}{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}\right]{/tex} Put x = tan {tex}\theta{/tex} Now, y = {tex}\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{\tan ^{2} \theta-1}{\tan ^{2} \theta+1}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\cos ^{-1}\left[-\left(\frac{1-\tan ^{2} \theta}{1+\tan ^{2} \theta}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\cos ^{-1}[-\cos 2 \theta]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\cos ^{-1}[\cos (\pi+2 \theta)]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\cos ^{-1}[\cos (\pi+2 \theta)]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=0+\frac{2}{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}=\frac{2}{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\mathrm{y}=\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{x}^{-1}}{\mathrm{x}+\mathrm{x}^{-1}}\right]{/tex}, then we have
{tex} \mathrm{y}=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}}{\mathrm{x}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}^{2}-1}{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}\right]{/tex}
Put x = tan{tex}\theta{/tex}
Now, y ={tex}\cos ^{-1}\left[\frac{\tan ^{2} \theta-1}{\tan ^{2} \theta+1}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\cos ^{-1}\left[-\left(\frac{1-\tan ^{2} \theta}{1+\tan ^{2} \theta}\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\cos ^{-1}[-\cos 2 \theta]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\cos ^{-1}[\cos (\pi+2 \theta)]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\cos ^{-1}[\cos (\pi+2 \theta)]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=0+\frac{2}{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}=\frac{2}{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Find the derivative of the following function f(x) w.r.t. x, at x = 1:
{tex}\cos ^{-1}[\sin \sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}}]+x^{x}{/tex}
question_text: Find the derivative of the following function f(x) w.r.t. x, at x = 1: {tex}\cos ^{-1}[\sin \sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}}]+x^{x}{/tex}
explanation: Let f(x) = {tex}\cos ^{-1}[\sin \sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}}]+x^{x}{/tex} {tex}\sin \theta=\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right){/tex} {tex}\cos ^{-1}\left(\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}}\right)\right)+x^{x}{/tex} {tex}f(x)=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}}+x^{x}{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}}} \times \frac{1}{2}+x^{x}(1+\log x){/tex} Put x = 1, we get {tex}f^{\prime}(1)=\frac{-1}{4 \sqrt{1}}+1(\log (1+1){/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{4}+1{/tex} = {tex}3 \over 4{/tex}
solution: Let f(x) = {tex}\cos ^{-1}[\sin \sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}}]+x^{x}{/tex} {tex}\sin \theta=\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right){/tex} {tex}\cos ^{-1}\left(\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}}\right)\right)+x^{x}{/tex} {tex}f(x)=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}}+x^{x}{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}}} \times \frac{1}{2}+x^{x}(1+\log x){/tex} Put x = 1, we get {tex}f^{\prime}(1)=\frac{-1}{4 \sqrt{1}}+1(\log (1+1){/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{4}+1{/tex} = {tex}3 \over 4{/tex}
solution_html:
Let f(x) = {tex}\cos ^{-1}[\sin \sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}}]+x^{x}{/tex}
{tex}\sin \theta=\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right){/tex}
{tex}\cos ^{-1}\left(\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}}\right)\right)+x^{x}{/tex}
{tex}f(x)=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}}+x^{x}{/tex}
{tex}f^{\prime}(x)=-\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{\frac{1+x}{2}}} \times \frac{1}{2}+x^{x}(1+\log x){/tex}
Put x = 1, we get
{tex}f^{\prime}(1)=\frac{-1}{4 \sqrt{1}}+1(\log (1+1){/tex}
= {tex}\frac{-1}{4}+1{/tex} = {tex}3 \over 4{/tex}
