question_html:
Examine the following function f(x) for continuity at x = 1 and differentiability at x = 2.
f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{ccc} 5 x-4 & , & 0<x<1 \\ 4 x^{2}-3 x & , & 1<x<2 \\ 3 x+4 & , & x \geq 2 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
question_text: Examine the following function f(x) for continuity at x = 1 and differentiability at x = 2. f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{ccc} 5 x-4 & , & 0<x<1 \\ 4 x^{2}-3 x & , & 1<x<2 \\ 3 x+4 & , & x \geq 2 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
explanation: Here, f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{ccc} 5 x-4 & , & 0<x<1 \\ 4 x^{2}-3 x & , & 1<x<2 \\ 3 x+4 & , & x \geq 2 \end{array}\right.{/tex} Now, f(1) = 5(1) - 4 = 1 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} f(x){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }}5x - 4 {/tex} = 5(1)-4=1 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} f(x){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} 4x^2 - 3x{/tex} =4(1) -3(1)=1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} f is continuous at x = 1 Next, we check its continuity at x = 2 We have, f(2) = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + 4 = 10 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} 4x^2 - 3x{/tex} = 16 - 6 = 10 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ = }} 3x + 4{/tex} = 6 = 4 = 10 {tex}\therefore{/tex} f is continuous at x = 2 Differentiating at x = 2 L.H.D = {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 2^{-}} \frac{f(x)-f(2)}{x-2}{/tex} = {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 2} \frac{4 x^{2}-3 x-10}{x-2}{/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} \frac{{(4x + 5)(x - 2)}}{{(x - 2)}}{/tex} = 13 R.H.D = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} \frac{{f(x) - f(2)}}{{x - 2}}{/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} \frac{{3x + 4 - 10}}{{x - 2}}{/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} \frac{{3x - 6}}{{x - 2}}{/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} \frac{3({x - 2})}{{(x - 2)}}{/tex} = 3 Therefore, f is not differentiable at x = 2 As L.H.D. {tex}\ne{/tex} R.H.D
solution: Here, f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{ccc} 5 x-4 & , & 0<x<1 \\ 4 x^{2}-3 x & , & 1<x<2 \\ 3 x+4 & , & x \geq 2 \end{array}\right.{/tex} Now, f(1) = 5(1) - 4 = 1 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} f(x){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }}5x - 4 {/tex} = 5(1)-4=1 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} f(x){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} 4x^2 - 3x{/tex} =4(1) -3(1)=1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} f is continuous at x = 1 Next, we check its continuity at x = 2 We have, f(2) = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + 4 = 10 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} 4x^2 - 3x{/tex} = 16 - 6 = 10 {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ = }} 3x + 4{/tex} = 6 = 4 = 10 {tex}\therefore{/tex} f is continuous at x = 2 Differentiating at x = 2 L.H.D = {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 2^{-}} \frac{f(x)-f(2)}{x-2}{/tex} = {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 2} \frac{4 x^{2}-3 x-10}{x-2}{/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} \frac{{(4x + 5)(x - 2)}}{{(x - 2)}}{/tex} = 13 R.H.D = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} \frac{{f(x) - f(2)}}{{x - 2}}{/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} \frac{{3x + 4 - 10}}{{x - 2}}{/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} \frac{{3x - 6}}{{x - 2}}{/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} \frac{3({x - 2})}{{(x - 2)}}{/tex} = 3 Therefore, f is not differentiable at x = 2 As L.H.D. {tex}\ne{/tex} R.H.D
solution_html:
Here, f(x) = {tex}\left\{\begin{array}{ccc} 5 x-4 & , & 0<x<1 \\ 4 x^{2}-3 x & , & 1<x<2 \\ 3 x+4 & , & x \geq 2 \end{array}\right.{/tex} 
Now, f(1) = 5(1) - 4 = 1
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }} f(x){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ - }}5x - 4 {/tex} = 5(1)-4=1
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} f(x){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {1^ + }} 4x^2 - 3x{/tex} =4(1) -3(1)=1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f is continuous at x = 1
Next, we check its continuity at x = 2
We have, f(2) = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + 4 = 10
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} 4x^2 - 3x{/tex} = 16 - 6 = 10
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ = }} 3x + 4{/tex} = 6 = 4 = 10
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f is continuous at x = 2
Differentiating at x = 2
L.H.D = {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 2^{-}} \frac{f(x)-f(2)}{x-2}{/tex} = {tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 2} \frac{4 x^{2}-3 x-10}{x-2}{/tex} 
= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} \frac{{(4x + 5)(x - 2)}}{{(x - 2)}}{/tex} = 13
R.H.D = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} \frac{{f(x) - f(2)}}{{x - 2}}{/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} \frac{{3x + 4 - 10}}{{x - 2}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} \frac{{3x - 6}}{{x - 2}}{/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 2} \frac{3({x - 2})}{{(x - 2)}}{/tex} = 3
Therefore, f is not differentiable at x = 2 
As L.H.D. {tex}\ne{/tex} R.H.D

question_html: If y = Peax + Qebx, then show that {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - ( a + b ) \frac { d y } { d x } + a b y = 0{/tex}.
question_text: If y = Pe ax + Qe bx , then show that {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - ( a + b ) \frac { d y } { d x } + a b y = 0{/tex} .
explanation: Given, y = Pe ax + Qe bx .........(i) Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = P \frac { d } { d x } \left( e ^ { a x } \right) + Q \frac { d } { d x } \left( e ^ { b x } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = Pae ax + Qbe bx ........(ii) Again, differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = P a \frac { d } { d x } \left( e ^ { a x } \right) + Q b \frac { d } { d x } \left( e ^ { b x } \right){/tex} = Pa(ae ax ) + Qb(be bx ) = a 2 Pe ax + b 2 Qe bx ...........(iii) Now, LHS = {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - ( a + b ) \frac { d y } { d x } + a b y{/tex} Therefore,on putting the values from Eqs.(i), (ii) and (iii), we get, LHS = a 2 Pe ax + b 2 Qe bx - (a + b)(aPe ax + bQe bx ) + ab(Pe ax + Qe bx ) = a 2 Pe ax + b 2 Qe bx - a 2 Pe ax - abQe bx - abP e ax - b 2 Qe bx + ab P e ax + ab Q e bx = 0 = RHS Hence Proved.
solution: Given, y = Pe ax + Qe bx .........(i) Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = P \frac { d } { d x } \left( e ^ { a x } \right) + Q \frac { d } { d x } \left( e ^ { b x } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = Pae ax + Qbe bx ........(ii) Again, differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = P a \frac { d } { d x } \left( e ^ { a x } \right) + Q b \frac { d } { d x } \left( e ^ { b x } \right){/tex} = Pa(ae ax ) + Qb(be bx ) = a 2 Pe ax + b 2 Qe bx ...........(iii) Now, LHS = {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - ( a + b ) \frac { d y } { d x } + a b y{/tex} Therefore,on putting the values from Eqs.(i), (ii) and (iii), we get, LHS = a 2 Pe ax + b 2 Qe bx - (a + b)(aPe ax + bQe bx ) + ab(Pe ax + Qe bx ) = a 2 Pe ax + b 2 Qe bx - a 2 Pe ax - abQe bx - abP e ax - b 2 Qe bx + ab P e ax + ab Q e bx = 0 = RHS Hence Proved.
solution_html:
Given, y = Peax + Qebx .........(i)

Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get,
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = P \frac { d } { d x } \left( e ^ { a x } \right) + Q \frac { d } { d x } \left( e ^ { b x } \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = Paeax + Qbebx ........(ii)
Again, differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get,
{tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = P a \frac { d } { d x } \left( e ^ { a x } \right) + Q b \frac { d } { d x } \left( e ^ { b x } \right){/tex}
= Pa(aeax) + Qb(bebx)
= a2Peax + b2Qebx ...........(iii)
Now, LHS = {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - ( a + b ) \frac { d y } { d x } + a b y{/tex}
Therefore,on putting the values from Eqs.(i), (ii) and (iii), we get,
LHS = a2Peax + b2Qebx - (a + b)(aPeax + bQebx) + ab(Peax + Qebx)
= a2Peax + b2Qebx - a2Peax - abQebx - abP eax - b2Qebx + ab P eax + ab Q ebx
= 0 = RHS
Hence Proved.

question_html: If y = {tex}{\sin ^{ - 1}}\left. {\left\{ {x\sqrt {1 - x} - \sqrt x \sqrt {1 - {x^2}} } \right.} \right\}{/tex}and 0< x < 1, then find {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}.
question_text: If y = {tex}{\sin ^{ - 1}}\left. {\left\{ {x\sqrt {1 - x} - \sqrt x \sqrt {1 - {x^2}} } \right.} \right\}{/tex} and 0< x < 1, then find {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} .
explanation: According to the question, if, {tex}y=\sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ x \sqrt { 1 - x } - \sqrt { x } \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex} . We have to find the derivative of y w.r.t x.We shall make use of the substitution to find the derivative. Now,y can be rewritten as {tex}y=\sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ x \sqrt { 1 - ( \sqrt { x } ) ^ { 2 } } - \sqrt { x } \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex} Now, put {tex}\sqrt { x } = \sin \theta{/tex} and x = {tex}\sin \phi{/tex} , so that y = {tex}\sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ \sin \phi \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } - \sin \theta \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin ^ { - 1 } [ \sin \phi \cos \theta - \sin \theta \cos \phi ]{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } x } = \cos x \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin ^ { - 1 } \sin ( \phi - \theta ){/tex} {tex}[ \because \sin \phi \cos \theta - \cos \phi \sin \theta = \sin ( \phi - \theta ) ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \phi - \theta{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \sin ^ { - 1 } \sin \theta = \theta \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin ^ { - 1 } x - \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \phi = \sin ^ { - 1 } x \text { and } \theta = \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x } \right]{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - ( \sqrt { x } ) ^ { 2 } } } \times \frac { d } { d x } ( \sqrt { x } ){/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( \sin ^ { - 1 } \theta \right) = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - \theta ^ { 2 } } } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x } } \cdot \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { x } }{/tex} Hence, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } - \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { x - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
solution: According to the question, if, {tex}y=\sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ x \sqrt { 1 - x } - \sqrt { x } \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex} . We have to find the derivative of y w.r.t x.We shall make use of the substitution to find the derivative. Now,y can be rewritten as {tex}y=\sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ x \sqrt { 1 - ( \sqrt { x } ) ^ { 2 } } - \sqrt { x } \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex} Now, put {tex}\sqrt { x } = \sin \theta{/tex} and x = {tex}\sin \phi{/tex} , so that y = {tex}\sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ \sin \phi \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } - \sin \theta \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin ^ { - 1 } [ \sin \phi \cos \theta - \sin \theta \cos \phi ]{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } x } = \cos x \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin ^ { - 1 } \sin ( \phi - \theta ){/tex} {tex}[ \because \sin \phi \cos \theta - \cos \phi \sin \theta = \sin ( \phi - \theta ) ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \phi - \theta{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \sin ^ { - 1 } \sin \theta = \theta \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin ^ { - 1 } x - \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \phi = \sin ^ { - 1 } x \text { and } \theta = \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x } \right]{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - ( \sqrt { x } ) ^ { 2 } } } \times \frac { d } { d x } ( \sqrt { x } ){/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( \sin ^ { - 1 } \theta \right) = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - \theta ^ { 2 } } } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x } } \cdot \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { x } }{/tex} Hence, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } - \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { x - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, if, {tex}y=\sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ x \sqrt { 1 - x } - \sqrt { x } \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex}.

We have to find the derivative of y w.r.t x.We shall make use of the substitution to find the derivative.
Now,y  can be  rewritten as {tex}y=\sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ x \sqrt { 1 - ( \sqrt { x } ) ^ { 2 } } - \sqrt { x } \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex}
Now, put {tex}\sqrt { x } = \sin \theta{/tex} and x ={tex}\sin \phi{/tex}, so that
y = {tex}\sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ \sin \phi \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } - \sin \theta \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin ^ { - 1 } [ \sin \phi \cos \theta - \sin \theta \cos \phi ]{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } x } = \cos x \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin ^ { - 1 } \sin ( \phi - \theta ){/tex}{tex}[ \because \sin \phi \cos \theta - \cos \phi \sin \theta = \sin ( \phi - \theta ) ]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \phi - \theta{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because \sin ^ { - 1 } \sin \theta = \theta \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \sin ^ { - 1 } x - \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x }{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because \phi = \sin ^ { - 1 } x \text { and } \theta = \sin ^ { - 1 } \sqrt { x } \right]{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - ( \sqrt { x } ) ^ { 2 } } } \times \frac { d } { d x } ( \sqrt { x } ){/tex}{tex}\left[ \because \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( \sin ^ { - 1 } \theta \right) = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - \theta ^ { 2 } } } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x } } \cdot \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { x } }{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } - \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { x - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If (x - y){tex} e ^ { \frac { x } { x - y } }{/tex} = a, prove that {tex} y \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}+ x = 2y.
question_text: If (x - y) {tex} e ^ { \frac { x } { x - y } }{/tex} = a, prove that {tex} y \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} + x = 2y.
explanation: Given, {tex}(x-y) e ^ { \frac { x } { x - y } }{/tex} = a Taking log both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left[ ( x - y ) \cdot e ^ { \frac { x } { x - y } } \right]{/tex} = log a {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \log ( x - y ) + \log e ^ { \frac { x } { x - y } }= log\ a \ \ [ log(mn) = log\ m + log\ n]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \log ( x - y ) + \frac { x } { x - y } log_ee = log\ a\ \ [ log_ee = 1]{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d } { d x } [ \log ( x - y ) ] + \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { x } { x - y } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } ( \log a ){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { x - y } \frac { d } { d x } ( x - y ){/tex} {tex} + \frac { ( x - y ) \frac { d } { d x } ( x ) - x \frac { d } { d x } ( x - y ) } { ( x - y ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} = 0 [Using quotient rule of derivative] where, {tex} y' = \frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(x - y)( 1- y') + x - y - x(1 - y') = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}yy' + x - 2y = 0{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad y \frac { d y } { d x } + x = 2 y{/tex} Hence Proved.
solution: Given, {tex}(x-y) e ^ { \frac { x } { x - y } }{/tex} = a Taking log both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow \log \left[ ( x - y ) \cdot e ^ { \frac { x } { x - y } } \right]{/tex} = log a {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \log ( x - y ) + \log e ^ { \frac { x } { x - y } }= log\ a \ \ [ log(mn) = log\ m + log\ n]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \log ( x - y ) + \frac { x } { x - y } log_ee = log\ a\ \ [ log_ee = 1]{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d } { d x } [ \log ( x - y ) ] + \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { x } { x - y } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } ( \log a ){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { x - y } \frac { d } { d x } ( x - y ){/tex} {tex} + \frac { ( x - y ) \frac { d } { d x } ( x ) - x \frac { d } { d x } ( x - y ) } { ( x - y ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} = 0 [Using quotient rule of derivative] where, {tex} y' = \frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(x - y)( 1- y') + x - y - x(1 - y') = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}yy' + x - 2y = 0{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad y \frac { d y } { d x } + x = 2 y{/tex} Hence Proved.
solution_html:
Given,{tex}(x-y) e ^ { \frac { x } { x - y } }{/tex} = a
Taking log both sides,
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \left[ ( x - y ) \cdot e ^ { \frac { x } { x - y } } \right]{/tex} = log a
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \log ( x - y ) + \log e ^ { \frac { x } { x - y } }= log\ a \ \ [ log(mn) = log\ m + log\ n]{/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \log ( x - y ) + \frac { x } { x - y } log_ee = log\ a\ \ [ log_ee = 1]{/tex}
Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d } { d x } [ \log ( x - y ) ] + \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { x } { x - y } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } ( \log a ){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { x - y } \frac { d } { d x } ( x - y ){/tex}{tex} + \frac { ( x - y ) \frac { d } { d x } ( x ) - x \frac { d } { d x } ( x - y ) } { ( x - y ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}= 0 [Using quotient rule of derivative]
where,{tex} y' = \frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}(x - y)( 1- y') + x - y - x(1 - y') = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}yy' + x - 2y = 0{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad y \frac { d y } { d x } + x = 2 y{/tex}
Hence Proved.

question_html: Find the value of {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} at {tex}\theta = \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex}, if x = {tex}a e ^ { \theta } ( \sin \theta - \cos \theta ){/tex} and y = {tex}a e ^ { \theta } ( \sin \theta + \cos \theta ){/tex}.
question_text: Find the value of {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} at {tex}\theta = \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex} , if x = {tex}a e ^ { \theta } ( \sin \theta - \cos \theta ){/tex} and y = {tex}a e ^ { \theta } ( \sin \theta + \cos \theta ){/tex} .
explanation: According to the question, {tex}x=a e ^ { \theta } ( \sin \theta - \cos \theta ){/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t {tex}\theta{/tex} , {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d x } { d \theta } = a \frac { d } { d \theta } \left[ e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta - e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta \right]{/tex} {tex}= a \left[ \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta \right) - \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}= a \left[ e ^ { \theta } \frac { d } { d \theta } ( \sin \theta ) + \sin \theta \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( e ^ { \theta } \right)\right.{/tex} {tex}- e ^ { \theta } \frac { d } { d \theta } ( \cos \theta ) - \cos \theta \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( e ^ { \theta } \right) ]{/tex} [using product rule of derivative] {tex}= a \left[ e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta + e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta - e ^ { \theta } ( - \sin \theta ) - e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta \right]{/tex} {tex}= a \left[ e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta + e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta + e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta - e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d x } { d \theta } = a \left[ 2 e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta \right] = 2 a e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta{/tex} .....(i) We have {tex}y=a e ^ { \theta } ( \sin \theta + \cos \theta ){/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex} , {tex}\Rightarrow\frac { d y } { d \theta } = a \left[ \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta \right) + \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}= a \left[ e ^ { \theta } \frac { d } { d \theta } ( \sin \theta ) + \sin \theta \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( e ^ { \theta } \right) + e ^ { \theta } \frac { d } { d \theta } ( \cos \theta )\right.{/tex} {tex}+ \cos \theta \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( e ^ { \theta } \right) ]{/tex} [ using product rule of derivative] {tex}= a \left[ e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta + e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta - e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta + e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta \right]{/tex} {tex}= a \left[ 2 e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d\theta } = 2 a e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta{/tex} ......(ii) Now, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d y } { d \theta } \times \frac { d \theta } { d x } = \frac { 2 a e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta } { 2 a e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta }{/tex} [ From Equations(i) and (ii)] = cot {tex}\theta{/tex} At, {tex}\theta = \frac { \pi } { 4 } , \frac { d y } { d x } = \cot \frac { \pi } { 4 } = 1{/tex} ​
solution: According to the question, {tex}x=a e ^ { \theta } ( \sin \theta - \cos \theta ){/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t {tex}\theta{/tex} , {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d x } { d \theta } = a \frac { d } { d \theta } \left[ e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta - e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta \right]{/tex} {tex}= a \left[ \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta \right) - \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}= a \left[ e ^ { \theta } \frac { d } { d \theta } ( \sin \theta ) + \sin \theta \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( e ^ { \theta } \right)\right.{/tex} {tex}- e ^ { \theta } \frac { d } { d \theta } ( \cos \theta ) - \cos \theta \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( e ^ { \theta } \right) ]{/tex} [using product rule of derivative] {tex}= a \left[ e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta + e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta - e ^ { \theta } ( - \sin \theta ) - e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta \right]{/tex} {tex}= a \left[ e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta + e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta + e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta - e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d x } { d \theta } = a \left[ 2 e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta \right] = 2 a e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta{/tex} .....(i) We have {tex}y=a e ^ { \theta } ( \sin \theta + \cos \theta ){/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex} , {tex}\Rightarrow\frac { d y } { d \theta } = a \left[ \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta \right) + \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}= a \left[ e ^ { \theta } \frac { d } { d \theta } ( \sin \theta ) + \sin \theta \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( e ^ { \theta } \right) + e ^ { \theta } \frac { d } { d \theta } ( \cos \theta )\right.{/tex} {tex}+ \cos \theta \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( e ^ { \theta } \right) ]{/tex} [ using product rule of derivative] {tex}= a \left[ e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta + e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta - e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta + e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta \right]{/tex} {tex}= a \left[ 2 e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d\theta } = 2 a e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta{/tex} ......(ii) Now, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d y } { d \theta } \times \frac { d \theta } { d x } = \frac { 2 a e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta } { 2 a e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta }{/tex} [ From Equations(i) and (ii)] = cot {tex}\theta{/tex} At, {tex}\theta = \frac { \pi } { 4 } , \frac { d y } { d x } = \cot \frac { \pi } { 4 } = 1{/tex} ​
solution_html:
According to the question,  {tex}x=a e ^ { \theta } ( \sin \theta - \cos \theta ){/tex} 
Differentiating both sides w.r.t {tex}\theta{/tex}, 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d x } { d \theta } = a \frac { d } { d \theta } \left[ e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta - e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta \right]{/tex}
{tex}= a \left[ \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta \right) - \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta \right) \right]{/tex}
{tex}= a \left[ e ^ { \theta } \frac { d } { d \theta } ( \sin \theta ) + \sin \theta \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( e ^ { \theta } \right)\right.{/tex}{tex}- e ^ { \theta } \frac { d } { d \theta } ( \cos \theta ) - \cos \theta \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( e ^ { \theta } \right) ]{/tex}[using product rule of derivative]
{tex}= a \left[ e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta + e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta - e ^ { \theta } ( - \sin \theta ) - e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta \right]{/tex}
{tex}= a \left[ e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta + e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta + e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta - e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d x } { d \theta } = a \left[ 2 e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta \right] = 2 a e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta{/tex}.....(i)
We have {tex}y=a e ^ { \theta } ( \sin \theta + \cos \theta ){/tex}
Differentiating both sides w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex}, 
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac { d y } { d \theta } = a \left[ \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta \right) + \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta \right) \right]{/tex}
{tex}= a \left[ e ^ { \theta } \frac { d } { d \theta } ( \sin \theta ) + \sin \theta \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( e ^ { \theta } \right) + e ^ { \theta } \frac { d } { d \theta } ( \cos \theta )\right.{/tex}{tex}+ \cos \theta \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( e ^ { \theta } \right) ]{/tex} [ using product rule of derivative]
{tex}= a \left[ e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta + e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta - e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta + e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta \right]{/tex}
{tex}= a \left[ 2 e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d\theta } = 2 a e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta{/tex}......(ii)
Now, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d y } { d \theta } \times \frac { d \theta } { d x } = \frac { 2 a e ^ { \theta } \cos \theta } { 2 a e ^ { \theta } \sin \theta }{/tex}[ From Equations(i) and (ii)]
= cot {tex}\theta{/tex}
At, {tex}\theta = \frac { \pi } { 4 } , \frac { d y } { d x } = \cot \frac { \pi } { 4 } = 1{/tex}​

question_html: If y = tan-1{tex} \left( \frac { a } { x } \right) + \log \sqrt { \frac { x - a } { x + a } }{/tex}, prove that {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 2 a ^ { 3 } } { x ^ { 4 } - a ^ { 4 } }{/tex}.
question_text: If y = tan -1 {tex} \left( \frac { a } { x } \right) + \log \sqrt { \frac { x - a } { x + a } }{/tex} , prove that {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 2 a ^ { 3 } } { x ^ { 4 } - a ^ { 4 } }{/tex} .
explanation: Given, y = {tex} \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { a } { x } \right) + \log \sqrt { \frac { x - a } { x + a } }{/tex} {tex} = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { a } { x } \right) + \log \left( \frac { x - a } { x + a } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { a } { x } \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} [log (x - a) - log(x + a)] {tex} \left[ \because \log \frac { m } { n } = \log m - \log n \right]{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { 1 + \frac { a ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } } \cdot \left( \frac { - a } { x ^ { 2 } } \right) +{/tex} {tex} \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \frac { 1 } { x - a } - \frac { 1 } { x + a } \right]{/tex} {tex} [ \because \frac { d } { d x } \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) = \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } {/tex} {tex} \text { and } \frac { d } { d x } ( \log x ) = \frac { 1 } { x }]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { - a } { x ^ { 2 } + a ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \frac { x + a - x + a } { ( x - a ) ( x + a ) } \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { - a } { x ^ { 2 } + a ^ { 2 } } + \frac { a } { x ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { - x ^ { 2 } a + a ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 2 } a + a ^ { 3 } } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + a ^ { 2 } \right) \left( x ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } \right) }{/tex} {tex} \therefore \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 2 a ^ { 3 } } { x ^ { 4 } - a ^ { 4 } }{/tex} Hence Proved.
solution: Given, y = {tex} \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { a } { x } \right) + \log \sqrt { \frac { x - a } { x + a } }{/tex} {tex} = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { a } { x } \right) + \log \left( \frac { x - a } { x + a } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { a } { x } \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} [log (x - a) - log(x + a)] {tex} \left[ \because \log \frac { m } { n } = \log m - \log n \right]{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { 1 + \frac { a ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } } \cdot \left( \frac { - a } { x ^ { 2 } } \right) +{/tex} {tex} \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \frac { 1 } { x - a } - \frac { 1 } { x + a } \right]{/tex} {tex} [ \because \frac { d } { d x } \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) = \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } {/tex} {tex} \text { and } \frac { d } { d x } ( \log x ) = \frac { 1 } { x }]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { - a } { x ^ { 2 } + a ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \frac { x + a - x + a } { ( x - a ) ( x + a ) } \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { - a } { x ^ { 2 } + a ^ { 2 } } + \frac { a } { x ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { - x ^ { 2 } a + a ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 2 } a + a ^ { 3 } } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + a ^ { 2 } \right) \left( x ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } \right) }{/tex} {tex} \therefore \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 2 a ^ { 3 } } { x ^ { 4 } - a ^ { 4 } }{/tex} Hence Proved.
solution_html:
Given, y ={tex} \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { a } { x } \right) + \log \sqrt { \frac { x - a } { x + a } }{/tex}
{tex} = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { a } { x } \right) + \log \left( \frac { x - a } { x + a } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad y = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { a } { x } \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}[log (x - a) - log(x + a)]{tex} \left[ \because \log \frac { m } { n } = \log m - \log n \right]{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get
{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { 1 + \frac { a ^ { 2 } } { x ^ { 2 } } } \cdot \left( \frac { - a } { x ^ { 2 } } \right) +{/tex}{tex} \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \frac { 1 } { x - a } - \frac { 1 } { x + a } \right]{/tex}{tex} [ \because \frac { d } { d x } \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) = \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } {/tex}{tex} \text { and } \frac { d } { d x } ( \log x ) = \frac { 1 } { x }]{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { - a } { x ^ { 2 } + a ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \frac { x + a - x + a } { ( x - a ) ( x + a ) } \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { - a } { x ^ { 2 } + a ^ { 2 } } + \frac { a } { x ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { - x ^ { 2 } a + a ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 2 } a + a ^ { 3 } } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + a ^ { 2 } \right) \left( x ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } \right) }{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 2 a ^ { 3 } } { x ^ { 4 } - a ^ { 4 } }{/tex}
Hence Proved.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Write the derivative of sin x with respect to cos x.
question_text: Write the derivative of sin x with respect to cos x.
explanation: Let u = sinx On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex}\frac { d u } { d x } = \cos x{/tex} ........(i) Also, let v = cosx On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex}\frac { d v } { d x } = - \sin x{/tex} .......(ii) Now, {tex}\frac { d u } { d v } = \frac { d u } { d x } \times \frac { d x } { d v }{/tex} = {tex}- \frac { \cos x } { \sin x }{/tex} [ from eq(i) and (ii)] {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { d u } { d v } = - \cot x{/tex}
solution: Let u = sinx On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex}\frac { d u } { d x } = \cos x{/tex} ........(i) Also, let v = cosx On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex}\frac { d v } { d x } = - \sin x{/tex} .......(ii) Now, {tex}\frac { d u } { d v } = \frac { d u } { d x } \times \frac { d x } { d v }{/tex} = {tex}- \frac { \cos x } { \sin x }{/tex} [ from eq(i) and (ii)] {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { d u } { d v } = - \cot x{/tex}
solution_html:
Let u = sinx
On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get
{tex}\frac { d u } { d x } = \cos x{/tex}........(i)
Also, let v = cosx
On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get
{tex}\frac { d v } { d x } = - \sin x{/tex}.......(ii)
Now,{tex}\frac { d u } { d v } = \frac { d u } { d x } \times \frac { d x } { d v }{/tex}= {tex}- \frac { \cos x } { \sin x }{/tex} [ from eq(i) and (ii)]
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac { d u } { d v } = - \cot x{/tex}

question_html: Differentiate tan-1{tex} \left( \frac { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } { x } \right){/tex} w.r.t cos-1{tex} \left( 2 x \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex}, when x {tex} \ne{/tex} 0.
question_text: Differentiate tan -1 {tex} \left( \frac { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } { x } \right){/tex} w.r.t cos -1 {tex} \left( 2 x \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} , when x {tex} \ne{/tex} 0.
explanation: Let u = {tex} \tan ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } { x } \right]{/tex} Put x = {tex}\cos \theta \Rightarrow \theta = \cos ^ { - 1 } x{/tex} Then, u = tan -1 {tex}\left[ \frac { \sqrt { 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } } { \cos \theta } \right]{/tex} = tan -1 {tex} \left[ \frac { \sqrt { \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } } { \cos \theta } \right]{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \cos ^ { 2 } \theta + \sin ^ { 2 } \theta = 1 \Rightarrow \sin ^ { 2 } \theta = 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \right]{/tex} = tan -1 {tex} \left[ \frac { \sin \theta } { \cos \theta } \right]{/tex} = tan -1 {tex} [ \tan \theta ] = \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad u = \cos ^ { - 1 } x{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex}\frac { d u } { d x } = - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} Again , let v = {tex}\cos ^ { - 1 } \left( 2 x \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} Put x = {tex}\cos \theta \Rightarrow \theta = \cos ^ { - 1 } x{/tex} Then, v = {tex}\cos ^ { - 1 } \left[ 2 \cos \theta \sqrt { 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \cos ^ { - 1 } [ 2 \cos \theta \sin \theta ]{/tex} {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { \because \sin \theta = \sqrt { 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } } \\ { \Rightarrow \sin ^ { 2 } \theta = 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \cos ^ { - 1 } [ \sin 2 \theta ]{/tex} {tex}= \cos ^ { - 1 } \left[ \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - 2 \theta \right) \right] = \frac { \pi } { 2 } - 2 \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v = \frac { \pi } { 2 } - 2 \cos ^ { - 1 } x{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \theta = \cos ^ { - 1 } x \right]{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex}\frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} Now, {tex} \frac { d u } { d v } = \frac { d u } { d x } \times \frac { d x } { d v }{/tex} = {tex} - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } \times \frac { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex} = - \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
solution: Let u = {tex} \tan ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } { x } \right]{/tex} Put x = {tex}\cos \theta \Rightarrow \theta = \cos ^ { - 1 } x{/tex} Then, u = tan -1 {tex}\left[ \frac { \sqrt { 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } } { \cos \theta } \right]{/tex} = tan -1 {tex} \left[ \frac { \sqrt { \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } } { \cos \theta } \right]{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \cos ^ { 2 } \theta + \sin ^ { 2 } \theta = 1 \Rightarrow \sin ^ { 2 } \theta = 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \right]{/tex} = tan -1 {tex} \left[ \frac { \sin \theta } { \cos \theta } \right]{/tex} = tan -1 {tex} [ \tan \theta ] = \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad u = \cos ^ { - 1 } x{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex}\frac { d u } { d x } = - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} Again , let v = {tex}\cos ^ { - 1 } \left( 2 x \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} Put x = {tex}\cos \theta \Rightarrow \theta = \cos ^ { - 1 } x{/tex} Then, v = {tex}\cos ^ { - 1 } \left[ 2 \cos \theta \sqrt { 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \cos ^ { - 1 } [ 2 \cos \theta \sin \theta ]{/tex} {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { \because \sin \theta = \sqrt { 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } } \\ { \Rightarrow \sin ^ { 2 } \theta = 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \cos ^ { - 1 } [ \sin 2 \theta ]{/tex} {tex}= \cos ^ { - 1 } \left[ \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - 2 \theta \right) \right] = \frac { \pi } { 2 } - 2 \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v = \frac { \pi } { 2 } - 2 \cos ^ { - 1 } x{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \theta = \cos ^ { - 1 } x \right]{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex}\frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} Now, {tex} \frac { d u } { d v } = \frac { d u } { d x } \times \frac { d x } { d v }{/tex} = {tex} - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } \times \frac { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } { 2 }{/tex} {tex} = - \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Let u ={tex} \tan ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } { x } \right]{/tex}

Put x ={tex}\cos \theta \Rightarrow \theta = \cos ^ { - 1 } x{/tex}
Then, u = tan-1{tex}\left[ \frac { \sqrt { 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } } { \cos \theta } \right]{/tex}
= tan-1{tex} \left[ \frac { \sqrt { \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } } { \cos \theta } \right]{/tex}{tex} \left[ \because \cos ^ { 2 } \theta + \sin ^ { 2 } \theta = 1 \Rightarrow \sin ^ { 2 } \theta = 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \right]{/tex}
= tan-1{tex} \left[ \frac { \sin \theta } { \cos \theta } \right]{/tex}
= tan-1{tex} [ \tan \theta ] = \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad u = \cos ^ { - 1 } x{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get
{tex}\frac { d u } { d x } = - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
Again , let v ={tex}\cos ^ { - 1 } \left( 2 x \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex}
Put x ={tex}\cos \theta \Rightarrow \theta = \cos ^ { - 1 } x{/tex}
Then, v = {tex}\cos ^ { - 1 } \left[ 2 \cos \theta \sqrt { 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \cos ^ { - 1 } [ 2 \cos \theta \sin \theta ]{/tex}{tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { \because \sin \theta = \sqrt { 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } } \\ { \Rightarrow \sin ^ { 2 } \theta = 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \cos ^ { - 1 } [ \sin 2 \theta ]{/tex}
{tex}= \cos ^ { - 1 } \left[ \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - 2 \theta \right) \right] = \frac { \pi } { 2 } - 2 \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow v = \frac { \pi } { 2 } - 2 \cos ^ { - 1 } x{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because \theta = \cos ^ { - 1 } x \right]{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get
{tex}\frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
Now, {tex} \frac { d u } { d v } = \frac { d u } { d x } \times \frac { d x } { d v }{/tex}= {tex} - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } \times \frac { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex} = - \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}

question_html: If x = cos t(3 - 2 cos2 t) and y = sin t(3 - 2 sin2 t), then find the value of {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } \text { at } t = \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex}.
question_text: If x = cos t(3 - 2 cos 2 t) and y = sin t(3 - 2 sin 2 t), then find the value of {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } \text { at } t = \frac { \pi } { 4 }{/tex} .
explanation: Given, {tex}x = cos\ t(3 - 2 cos^2 t){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x = 3cos\ t - 2 cos^3 t{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t t, {tex}\frac { d x } { d t }{/tex} = {tex} 3(-sin\ t) - 2(3)cos^2t(-sin t){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac { d x } { d t }{/tex} = {tex}-3sin\ t + 6 cos^2t sin\ t {/tex} ..........(i) Also, {tex}y = sin\ t(3 - 2 sin^2t){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}y = 3 sin\ t - 2 sin^3t{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t t, {tex}\Rightarrow\frac { d y } { d t }{/tex} = 3 cos t - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} sin 2 t cos t {tex}\Rightarrow\frac { d y } { d t }{/tex} = 3 cos t - 6 sin 2 t cos t ..........(ii) Now, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d y } { d t } \times \frac { d t } { d x }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 3 \cos t - 6 \cos t \sin ^ { 2 } t } { - 3 \sin t + 6 \cos ^ { 2 } t \sin t }{/tex} [From Eqs.(i) and (ii)] {tex}= \frac { \cos t - 2 \cos t \sin ^ { 2 } t } { - \sin t + 2 \cos ^ { 2 } t \sin t }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \cos t \left( 1 - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } t \right) } { \sin t \left( 2 \cos ^ { 2 } t - 1 \right) } = \frac { \cos t \cdot \cos 2 t } { \sin t \cdot \cos 2 t }{/tex} = cot(t) {tex}\therefore \quad \left. \frac { d y } { d x } \right| _ { \text { at } t = \frac { \pi } { 4 } } = \cot \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) = 1{/tex}
solution: Given, {tex}x = cos\ t(3 - 2 cos^2 t){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x = 3cos\ t - 2 cos^3 t{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t t, {tex}\frac { d x } { d t }{/tex} = {tex} 3(-sin\ t) - 2(3)cos^2t(-sin t){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac { d x } { d t }{/tex} = {tex}-3sin\ t + 6 cos^2t sin\ t {/tex} ..........(i) Also, {tex}y = sin\ t(3 - 2 sin^2t){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} {tex}y = 3 sin\ t - 2 sin^3t{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t t, {tex}\Rightarrow\frac { d y } { d t }{/tex} = 3 cos t - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} sin 2 t cos t {tex}\Rightarrow\frac { d y } { d t }{/tex} = 3 cos t - 6 sin 2 t cos t ..........(ii) Now, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d y } { d t } \times \frac { d t } { d x }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 3 \cos t - 6 \cos t \sin ^ { 2 } t } { - 3 \sin t + 6 \cos ^ { 2 } t \sin t }{/tex} [From Eqs.(i) and (ii)] {tex}= \frac { \cos t - 2 \cos t \sin ^ { 2 } t } { - \sin t + 2 \cos ^ { 2 } t \sin t }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \cos t \left( 1 - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } t \right) } { \sin t \left( 2 \cos ^ { 2 } t - 1 \right) } = \frac { \cos t \cdot \cos 2 t } { \sin t \cdot \cos 2 t }{/tex} = cot(t) {tex}\therefore \quad \left. \frac { d y } { d x } \right| _ { \text { at } t = \frac { \pi } { 4 } } = \cot \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) = 1{/tex}
solution_html:
Given,{tex}x = cos\ t(3 - 2 cos^2 t){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x = 3cos\ t - 2 cos^3 t{/tex} 
Differentiating both sides w.r.t t,
{tex}\frac { d x } { d t }{/tex} ={tex} 3(-sin\ t) - 2(3)cos^2t(-sin t){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac { d x } { d t }{/tex}= {tex}-3sin\ t + 6 cos^2t sin\ t {/tex}..........(i)
Also, {tex}y = sin\ t(3 - 2 sin^2t){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex}{tex}y = 3 sin\ t - 2 sin^3t{/tex}
Differentiating both sides w.r.t t,
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac { d y } { d t }{/tex} = 3 cos t - 2 {tex}\times{/tex}3{tex}\times{/tex}sin2t cos t
 {tex}\Rightarrow\frac { d y } { d t }{/tex}= 3 cos t - 6 sin2t cos t ..........(ii)
Now, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d y } { d t } \times \frac { d t } { d x }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 3 \cos t - 6 \cos t \sin ^ { 2 } t } { - 3 \sin t + 6 \cos ^ { 2 } t \sin t }{/tex} [From Eqs.(i) and (ii)]
{tex}= \frac { \cos t - 2 \cos t \sin ^ { 2 } t } { - \sin t + 2 \cos ^ { 2 } t \sin t }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \cos t \left( 1 - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } t \right) } { \sin t \left( 2 \cos ^ { 2 } t - 1 \right) } = \frac { \cos t \cdot \cos 2 t } { \sin t \cdot \cos 2 t }{/tex}
= cot(t)
{tex}\therefore \quad \left. \frac { d y } { d x } \right| _ { \text { at } t = \frac { \pi } { 4 } } = \cot \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) = 1{/tex}

question_html: If (tan-1 x)y + ycotx = 1, then find dy/dx.
question_text: If (tan -1 x) y + y cotx = 1, then find dy/dx.
explanation: Given (tan -1 x) y + y cotx = 1 Let {tex}u = (tan^{-1} x)^y\ \ and\ \ v = y^{cotx}{/tex} Then, given equation becomes u + v = 1 Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d u } { d x } + \frac { d v } { d x } = 0{/tex} ........(i) {tex}Now,\ u = (tan^{-1} x)^y\ \ {/tex} Taking log both sides, we get, {tex}\Rightarrow log\ u = y\ log(tan^{-1}x){/tex} {tex}{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = \frac { d } { d x } ( y ) \cdot \log \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) + y \frac { d } { d x } \left( \log \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right){/tex} [ Using product rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = \frac { d } { d x } ( y ) log(tan^{-1}x)+ \frac { y } { \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d u } { d x } = \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) ^ { y } \left[ \frac { d y } { d x } \log \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right)\right.{/tex} {tex} + \frac { y } { \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } ]{/tex} .......(ii) Also, {tex}v= y^{cotx}{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { v } \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { d } { d x } ( \cot x ) \cdot \log y + \cot x \frac { d } { d x } ( \log y ){/tex} [Using product rule of derivative] {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { v } \frac { d v } { d x } = - \text{cosec} ^ { 2 } x \log y + \frac { \cot x } { y } \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d v } { d x } = y ^ { \cot x } \left[ - \text{cosec} ^ { 2 } x \log y + \frac { \cot x } { y } \frac { d y } { d x } \right]{/tex} .............(iii) Putting values from Equations (ii), (iii) in Equation (i), {tex}\Rightarrow \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) ^ { y } \left[ \frac { d y } { d x } \log \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) + \frac { y } { \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } \right]{/tex} {tex} + y ^ { \cot x } \left[ - \text{cosec} ^ { 2 } x \log y + \frac { \cot x } { y } \frac { d y } { d x } \right]{/tex} = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } \left[ \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) ^ { y } \log \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) + \cot x \cdot y ^ { \cot x - 1 } \right.]{/tex} {tex}= - \left[ \frac { y } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) ^ { y - 1 } - y ^ { \cot x } \text{cosec} ^ { 2 } x \log y \right]{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = {tex} \frac{{ - \left[ {\frac{y}{{1 + {x^2}}}{{\left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x} \right)}^{y - 1}} - {y^{\cot x}}\text{cosec}{^2}x\log y} \right]}}{{\left[ {{{\left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x} \right)}^y}\log \left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x} \right) + \cot x \cdot {y^{\cot x - 1}}} \right]}}{/tex}
solution: Given (tan -1 x) y + y cotx = 1 Let {tex}u = (tan^{-1} x)^y\ \ and\ \ v = y^{cotx}{/tex} Then, given equation becomes u + v = 1 Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d u } { d x } + \frac { d v } { d x } = 0{/tex} ........(i) {tex}Now,\ u = (tan^{-1} x)^y\ \ {/tex} Taking log both sides, we get, {tex}\Rightarrow log\ u = y\ log(tan^{-1}x){/tex} {tex}{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = \frac { d } { d x } ( y ) \cdot \log \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) + y \frac { d } { d x } \left( \log \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right){/tex} [ Using product rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = \frac { d } { d x } ( y ) log(tan^{-1}x)+ \frac { y } { \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d u } { d x } = \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) ^ { y } \left[ \frac { d y } { d x } \log \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right)\right.{/tex} {tex} + \frac { y } { \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } ]{/tex} .......(ii) Also, {tex}v= y^{cotx}{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { v } \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { d } { d x } ( \cot x ) \cdot \log y + \cot x \frac { d } { d x } ( \log y ){/tex} [Using product rule of derivative] {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { v } \frac { d v } { d x } = - \text{cosec} ^ { 2 } x \log y + \frac { \cot x } { y } \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d v } { d x } = y ^ { \cot x } \left[ - \text{cosec} ^ { 2 } x \log y + \frac { \cot x } { y } \frac { d y } { d x } \right]{/tex} .............(iii) Putting values from Equations (ii), (iii) in Equation (i), {tex}\Rightarrow \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) ^ { y } \left[ \frac { d y } { d x } \log \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) + \frac { y } { \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } \right]{/tex} {tex} + y ^ { \cot x } \left[ - \text{cosec} ^ { 2 } x \log y + \frac { \cot x } { y } \frac { d y } { d x } \right]{/tex} = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } \left[ \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) ^ { y } \log \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) + \cot x \cdot y ^ { \cot x - 1 } \right.]{/tex} {tex}= - \left[ \frac { y } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) ^ { y - 1 } - y ^ { \cot x } \text{cosec} ^ { 2 } x \log y \right]{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = {tex} \frac{{ - \left[ {\frac{y}{{1 + {x^2}}}{{\left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x} \right)}^{y - 1}} - {y^{\cot x}}\text{cosec}{^2}x\log y} \right]}}{{\left[ {{{\left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x} \right)}^y}\log \left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x} \right) + \cot x \cdot {y^{\cot x - 1}}} \right]}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given (tan-1 x)y + ycotx = 1

Let {tex}u = (tan^{-1} x)^y\ \  and\ \ v = y^{cotx}{/tex}
Then, given equation becomes u + v = 1
Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, 
 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d u } { d x } + \frac { d v } { d x } = 0{/tex} ........(i)

{tex}Now,\ u = (tan^{-1} x)^y\ \  {/tex}
Taking log both sides, we get,

{tex}\Rightarrow log\ u = y\ log(tan^{-1}x){/tex}
{tex}{/tex}Differentiating both sides w.r.t x,
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = \frac { d } { d x } ( y ) \cdot \log \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) + y \frac { d } { d x } \left( \log \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right){/tex}[ Using product rule of derivative] 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = \frac { d } { d x } ( y ) log(tan^{-1}x)+ \frac { y } { \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) }{/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d u } { d x } = \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) ^ { y } \left[ \frac { d y } { d x } \log \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right)\right.{/tex}{tex} + \frac { y } { \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } ]{/tex}.......(ii)
Also,{tex}v= y^{cotx}{/tex}
Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { v } \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { d } { d x } ( \cot x ) \cdot \log y + \cot x \frac { d } { d x } ( \log y ){/tex} [Using product rule of derivative]
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { v } \frac { d v } { d x } = - \text{cosec} ^ { 2 } x \log y + \frac { \cot x } { y } \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d v } { d x } = y ^ { \cot x } \left[ - \text{cosec} ^ { 2 } x \log y + \frac { \cot x } { y } \frac { d y } { d x } \right]{/tex}.............(iii)
Putting values from Equations (ii), (iii) in Equation (i), 
{tex}\Rightarrow \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) ^ { y } \left[ \frac { d y } { d x } \log \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) + \frac { y } { \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) } \right]{/tex}{tex} + y ^ { \cot x } \left[ - \text{cosec} ^ { 2 } x \log y + \frac { \cot x } { y } \frac { d y } { d x } \right]{/tex}= 0
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } \left[ \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) ^ { y } \log \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) + \cot x \cdot y ^ { \cot x - 1 } \right.]{/tex}
{tex}= - \left[ \frac { y } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \left( \tan ^ { - 1 } x \right) ^ { y - 1 } - y ^ { \cot x } \text{cosec} ^ { 2 } x \log y \right]{/tex}{tex}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}= {tex} \frac{{ - \left[ {\frac{y}{{1 + {x^2}}}{{\left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x} \right)}^{y - 1}} - {y^{\cot x}}\text{cosec}{^2}x\log y} \right]}}{{\left[ {{{\left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x} \right)}^y}\log \left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x} \right) + \cot x \cdot {y^{\cot x - 1}}} \right]}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If ex + ey = ex + y, prove that {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}+ ey-x = 0.
question_text: If e x + e y = e x + y , prove that {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} + e y-x = 0.
explanation: Given e x + e y = e x + y ...(i) On dividing Eq(i) by e x+y , we get, e -y + e -x = 1 ...(ii) Therefore,on differentiating both sides of Eq(ii) w.r.t x, we get, {tex}e ^ { - y } \cdot \left( \frac { - d y } { d x } \right){/tex} + e -x (-1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow - e ^ { - y } \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} + e -x (-1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow - e ^ { - y } \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = e -x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = - \frac { e ^ { - x } } { e ^ { - y } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = - e (y- x) {tex}\therefore \frac { d y } { d x } {/tex} + e (y- x) = 0 Hence Proved.
solution: Given e x + e y = e x + y ...(i) On dividing Eq(i) by e x+y , we get, e -y + e -x = 1 ...(ii) Therefore,on differentiating both sides of Eq(ii) w.r.t x, we get, {tex}e ^ { - y } \cdot \left( \frac { - d y } { d x } \right){/tex} + e -x (-1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow - e ^ { - y } \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} + e -x (-1) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow - e ^ { - y } \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = e -x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = - \frac { e ^ { - x } } { e ^ { - y } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = - e (y- x) {tex}\therefore \frac { d y } { d x } {/tex} + e (y- x) = 0 Hence Proved.
solution_html:
Given  ex + ey = ex + y  ...(i)
On dividing Eq(i) by ex+y, we get,
e-y + e-x = 1 ...(ii)
Therefore,on differentiating both sides of Eq(ii) w.r.t x, we get,
{tex}e ^ { - y } \cdot \left( \frac { - d y } { d x } \right){/tex} +  e-x(-1) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow - e ^ { - y } \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}+ e-x(-1) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow - e ^ { - y } \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = e-x
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = - \frac { e ^ { - x } } { e ^ { - y } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = - e (y- x)
{tex}\therefore \frac { d y } { d x } {/tex} + e (y- x) = 0
Hence Proved.
