question_html: If x = a{tex}\left( \cos t + \log \tan \frac { t } { 2 } \right){/tex}, y = a sin t, then evaluate {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } \text { at } t = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex}.
question_text: If x = a {tex}\left( \cos t + \log \tan \frac { t } { 2 } \right){/tex} , y = a sin t, then evaluate {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } \text { at } t = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex} .
explanation: Given, x = a {tex}\left( \cos t + \log \tan \frac { t } { 2 } \right){/tex} .........(i) and y = a sin t..........(ii) Therefore, on differentiating both sides w.r.t t, we get, {tex}\frac { d x } { d t } = a \left[ \frac { d } { d t } ( \cos t ) + \frac { d } { d t } \log \tan \frac { t } { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}= a \left[ - \sin t + \frac { 1 } { \tan \frac { t } { 2 } } \frac { d } { d t } \left( \tan \frac { t } { 2 } \right) \right]{/tex} [by using chain rule of derivative] {tex}= a \left[ - \sin t + \frac { 1 } { \tan \frac { t } { 2 } } \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } \frac { t } { 2 } \frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { t } { 2 } \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}= a \left[ - \sin t + \frac { 1 } { \tan \frac { t } { 2 } } \times \sec ^ { 2 } \frac { t } { 2 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}= a \left[ - \sin t + \frac { 1 } { \frac { \sin t / 2 } { \cos t / 2 } } \times \frac { 1 } { \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { t } { 2 } } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}= a \left[ - \sin t + \frac { 1 } { 2 \sin \frac { t } { 2 } \cdot \cos \frac { t } { 2 } } \right]{/tex} {tex}= a \left[ - \sin t + \frac { 1 } { \sin t } \right]{/tex} {tex}[ \because \sin 2 \theta = 2 \sin \theta \cos \theta ]{/tex} {tex}= a \left( \frac { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } t } { \sin t } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d x } { d t } = a \left( \frac { \cos ^ { 2 } t } { \sin t } \right){/tex} {tex}\left[ \because 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \theta = \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \right]{/tex} ..........(iii) Again,on differentiating both sides of (ii) w.r.t t, we get, {tex}\frac { d y } { d t }{/tex} = a cos t...............(iv) Therefore, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \frac { d y } { d t } } { \frac { d x } { d t } } = \frac { a \cos t } { a \left( \frac { \cos ^ { 2 } t } { \sin t } \right) }{/tex} [ from Eqs(iii) and (iv)] {tex}= \frac { a \cos t } { a \cos ^ { 2 } t } \times \sin t{/tex} = tan t Therefore,on differentiating both sides of above equation w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } ( \tan t ){/tex} {tex}= \frac { d } { d t } ( \tan t ) \frac { d t } { d x }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \frac { d } { d x } f ( t ) = \frac { d } { d t } f ( t ) \cdot \frac { d t } { d x } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \sec ^ { 2 } t \times \frac { \sin t } { a \cos ^ { 2 } t }{/tex} [ From Eq.(iii)] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \sin t \sec ^ { 4 } t } { a }{/tex} Therefore, on putting t = {tex}\frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex} ,we get, {tex}\left[ \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } \right] _ { t = \frac { \pi } { 3 } }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { \sin \frac { \pi } { 3 } \times \sec ^ { 4 } \frac { \pi } { 3 } } { a }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } \times ( 2 ) ^ { 4 } } { a }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { 8 \sqrt { 3 } } { a }{/tex}
solution: Given, x = a {tex}\left( \cos t + \log \tan \frac { t } { 2 } \right){/tex} .........(i) and y = a sin t..........(ii) Therefore, on differentiating both sides w.r.t t, we get, {tex}\frac { d x } { d t } = a \left[ \frac { d } { d t } ( \cos t ) + \frac { d } { d t } \log \tan \frac { t } { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}= a \left[ - \sin t + \frac { 1 } { \tan \frac { t } { 2 } } \frac { d } { d t } \left( \tan \frac { t } { 2 } \right) \right]{/tex} [by using chain rule of derivative] {tex}= a \left[ - \sin t + \frac { 1 } { \tan \frac { t } { 2 } } \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } \frac { t } { 2 } \frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { t } { 2 } \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}= a \left[ - \sin t + \frac { 1 } { \tan \frac { t } { 2 } } \times \sec ^ { 2 } \frac { t } { 2 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}= a \left[ - \sin t + \frac { 1 } { \frac { \sin t / 2 } { \cos t / 2 } } \times \frac { 1 } { \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { t } { 2 } } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex}= a \left[ - \sin t + \frac { 1 } { 2 \sin \frac { t } { 2 } \cdot \cos \frac { t } { 2 } } \right]{/tex} {tex}= a \left[ - \sin t + \frac { 1 } { \sin t } \right]{/tex} {tex}[ \because \sin 2 \theta = 2 \sin \theta \cos \theta ]{/tex} {tex}= a \left( \frac { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } t } { \sin t } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d x } { d t } = a \left( \frac { \cos ^ { 2 } t } { \sin t } \right){/tex} {tex}\left[ \because 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \theta = \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \right]{/tex} ..........(iii) Again,on differentiating both sides of (ii) w.r.t t, we get, {tex}\frac { d y } { d t }{/tex} = a cos t...............(iv) Therefore, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \frac { d y } { d t } } { \frac { d x } { d t } } = \frac { a \cos t } { a \left( \frac { \cos ^ { 2 } t } { \sin t } \right) }{/tex} [ from Eqs(iii) and (iv)] {tex}= \frac { a \cos t } { a \cos ^ { 2 } t } \times \sin t{/tex} = tan t Therefore,on differentiating both sides of above equation w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } ( \tan t ){/tex} {tex}= \frac { d } { d t } ( \tan t ) \frac { d t } { d x }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \frac { d } { d x } f ( t ) = \frac { d } { d t } f ( t ) \cdot \frac { d t } { d x } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \sec ^ { 2 } t \times \frac { \sin t } { a \cos ^ { 2 } t }{/tex} [ From Eq.(iii)] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \sin t \sec ^ { 4 } t } { a }{/tex} Therefore, on putting t = {tex}\frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex} ,we get, {tex}\left[ \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } \right] _ { t = \frac { \pi } { 3 } }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { \sin \frac { \pi } { 3 } \times \sec ^ { 4 } \frac { \pi } { 3 } } { a }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } \times ( 2 ) ^ { 4 } } { a }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { 8 \sqrt { 3 } } { a }{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, x = a{tex}\left( \cos t + \log \tan \frac { t } { 2 } \right){/tex}.........(i)
and y = a sin t..........(ii)
Therefore, on differentiating both sides w.r.t t, we get,
{tex}\frac { d x } { d t } = a \left[ \frac { d } { d t } ( \cos t ) + \frac { d } { d t } \log \tan \frac { t } { 2 } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= a \left[ - \sin t + \frac { 1 } { \tan \frac { t } { 2 } } \frac { d } { d t } \left( \tan \frac { t } { 2 } \right) \right]{/tex}[by using chain rule of derivative]
{tex}= a \left[ - \sin t + \frac { 1 } { \tan \frac { t } { 2 } } \cdot \sec ^ { 2 } \frac { t } { 2 } \frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { t } { 2 } \right) \right]{/tex}
{tex}= a \left[ - \sin t + \frac { 1 } { \tan \frac { t } { 2 } } \times \sec ^ { 2 } \frac { t } { 2 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= a \left[ - \sin t + \frac { 1 } { \frac { \sin t / 2 } { \cos t / 2 } } \times \frac { 1 } { \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { t } { 2 } } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= a \left[ - \sin t + \frac { 1 } { 2 \sin \frac { t } { 2 } \cdot \cos \frac { t } { 2 } } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= a \left[ - \sin t + \frac { 1 } { \sin t } \right]{/tex} {tex}[ \because \sin 2 \theta = 2 \sin \theta \cos \theta ]{/tex}
{tex}= a \left( \frac { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } t } { \sin t } \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d x } { d t } = a \left( \frac { \cos ^ { 2 } t } { \sin t } \right){/tex}{tex}\left[ \because 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \theta = \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \right]{/tex}..........(iii)
Again,on differentiating both sides of (ii) w.r.t t, we get,
{tex}\frac { d y } { d t }{/tex} = a cos t...............(iv)
Therefore, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \frac { d y } { d t } } { \frac { d x } { d t } } = \frac { a \cos t } { a \left( \frac { \cos ^ { 2 } t } { \sin t } \right) }{/tex} [ from Eqs(iii) and (iv)]
{tex}= \frac { a \cos t } { a \cos ^ { 2 } t } \times \sin t{/tex} =  tan t
Therefore,on differentiating both sides of above equation w.r.t x, we get,
{tex}\frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } ( \tan t ){/tex}
{tex}= \frac { d } { d t } ( \tan t ) \frac { d t } { d x }{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because \frac { d } { d x } f ( t ) = \frac { d } { d t } f ( t ) \cdot \frac { d t } { d x } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \sec ^ { 2 } t \times \frac { \sin t } { a \cos ^ { 2 } t }{/tex} [ From Eq.(iii)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \sin t \sec ^ { 4 } t } { a }{/tex}
Therefore, on putting t = {tex}\frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex},we get,
{tex}\left[ \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } \right] _ { t = \frac { \pi } { 3 } }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { \sin \frac { \pi } { 3 } \times \sec ^ { 4 } \frac { \pi } { 3 } } { a }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } \times ( 2 ) ^ { 4 } } { a }{/tex}
= {tex}\frac { 8 \sqrt { 3 } } { a }{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}\cos y = x\cos \left( {a + y} \right){/tex} with {tex}\cos a \ne \pm 1{/tex} prove that {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{{\sin a}}{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}\cos y = x\cos \left( {a + y} \right){/tex} with {tex}\cos a \ne \pm 1{/tex} prove that {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{{\sin a}}{/tex}
explanation: Given: {tex}\cos y = x\cos \left( {a + y} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x = \frac{{\cos y}}{{\cos \left( {a + y} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{d}{{dy}}\left( {\frac{{\cos y}}{{\cos \left( {a + y} \right)}}} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{{\cos \left( {a + y} \right)\frac{d}{{dy}}\cos y - \cos y\frac{d}{{dy}}\cos \left( {a + y} \right)}}{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{{\cos \left( {a + y} \right)\left( { - \sin y} \right) - \cos y\left\{ { - \sin \left( {a + y} \right)} \right\}}}{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{ - \cos \left( {a + y} \right)\sin y + sin\left( {a + y} \right)\cos y}}{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{{\sin \left( {a + y - y} \right)}}{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\sin a}}{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{{\sin a}}{/tex} [ reciprocal]
solution: Given: {tex}\cos y = x\cos \left( {a + y} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x = \frac{{\cos y}}{{\cos \left( {a + y} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{d}{{dy}}\left( {\frac{{\cos y}}{{\cos \left( {a + y} \right)}}} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{{\cos \left( {a + y} \right)\frac{d}{{dy}}\cos y - \cos y\frac{d}{{dy}}\cos \left( {a + y} \right)}}{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{{\cos \left( {a + y} \right)\left( { - \sin y} \right) - \cos y\left\{ { - \sin \left( {a + y} \right)} \right\}}}{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{ - \cos \left( {a + y} \right)\sin y + sin\left( {a + y} \right)\cos y}}{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{{\sin \left( {a + y - y} \right)}}{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\sin a}}{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{{\sin a}}{/tex} [ reciprocal]
solution_html:
Given: {tex}\cos y = x\cos \left( {a + y} \right){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow x = \frac{{\cos y}}{{\cos \left( {a + y} \right)}}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{d}{{dy}}\left( {\frac{{\cos y}}{{\cos \left( {a + y} \right)}}} \right){/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{{\cos \left( {a + y} \right)\frac{d}{{dy}}\cos y - \cos y\frac{d}{{dy}}\cos \left( {a + y} \right)}}{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{{\cos \left( {a + y} \right)\left( { - \sin y} \right) - \cos y\left\{ { - \sin \left( {a + y} \right)} \right\}}}{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{ - \cos \left( {a + y} \right)\sin y + sin\left( {a + y} \right)\cos y}}{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{{\sin \left( {a + y - y} \right)}}{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{\sin a}}{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{{\cos }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{{\sin a}}{/tex} [ reciprocal]

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Differentiate {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{x}\right){/tex} with respect to {tex}\cos ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex}, where {tex}x \in\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, 1\right){/tex}
question_text: Differentiate {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{x}\right){/tex} with respect to {tex}\cos ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex} , where {tex}x \in\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, 1\right){/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}u=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{x}\right){/tex} and {tex}v=\cos ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex} . We want to find {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{\frac{d u}{d x}}{\frac{d v}{d x}}{/tex} Now {tex}u=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{x}\right){/tex} . Put x = {tex}\sin \theta \cdot\left(\frac{\pi}{4}<\theta<\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} Then u {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}}{\sin \theta}\right)=\tan ^{-1}(\cot\theta){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left\{\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right)\right\}=\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{-1} x{/tex} Hence {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} Now {tex}v=\cos ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{-1}(2 \sin \theta \sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta})=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{-1}(\sin 2 \theta){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{-1}\{\sin (\pi-2 \theta)\} \quad\left[\text { since } \frac{\pi}{2}<2 \theta<\pi\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}-(\pi-2 \theta)=\frac{-\pi}{2}+2 \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad v=\frac{-\pi}{2}+2 \sin ^{-1} x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} Hence {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{\frac{d u}{d x}}{\frac{d v}{d x}}=\frac{\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}}{\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}}=\frac{-1}{2}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}u=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{x}\right){/tex} and {tex}v=\cos ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex} . We want to find {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{\frac{d u}{d x}}{\frac{d v}{d x}}{/tex} Now {tex}u=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{x}\right){/tex} . Put x = {tex}\sin \theta \cdot\left(\frac{\pi}{4}<\theta<\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} Then u {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}}{\sin \theta}\right)=\tan ^{-1}(\cot\theta){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left\{\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right)\right\}=\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{-1} x{/tex} Hence {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} Now {tex}v=\cos ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{-1}(2 \sin \theta \sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta})=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{-1}(\sin 2 \theta){/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{-1}\{\sin (\pi-2 \theta)\} \quad\left[\text { since } \frac{\pi}{2}<2 \theta<\pi\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}-(\pi-2 \theta)=\frac{-\pi}{2}+2 \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad v=\frac{-\pi}{2}+2 \sin ^{-1} x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} Hence {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{\frac{d u}{d x}}{\frac{d v}{d x}}=\frac{\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}}{\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}}=\frac{-1}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}u=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{x}\right){/tex} and  {tex}v=\cos ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex}.
We want to find {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{\frac{d u}{d x}}{\frac{d v}{d x}}{/tex}
Now {tex}u=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{x}\right){/tex}. Put x = {tex}\sin \theta \cdot\left(\frac{\pi}{4}<\theta<\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}
Then u {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}}{\sin \theta}\right)=\tan ^{-1}(\cot\theta){/tex}
{tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left\{\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right)\right\}=\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{-1} x{/tex}
Hence {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}=\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex}
Now {tex}v=\cos ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{-1}(2 \sin \theta \sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta})=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{-1}(\sin 2 \theta){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{-1}\{\sin (\pi-2 \theta)\} \quad\left[\text { since } \frac{\pi}{2}<2 \theta<\pi\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\pi}{2}-(\pi-2 \theta)=\frac{-\pi}{2}+2 \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad v=\frac{-\pi}{2}+2 \sin ^{-1} x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex}
Hence {tex}\frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{\frac{d u}{d x}}{\frac{d v}{d x}}=\frac{\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}}{\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}}=\frac{-1}{2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If x = a (cos t + t sin t) and y = a (sin t - t cos t), then find the value of {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} at {tex}t=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
question_text: If x = a (cos t + t sin t) and y = a (sin t - t cos t), then find the value of {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} at {tex}t=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
explanation: According to the question, x = a (cos t + t sin t) {tex}\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} = -a sin t + a t cos t + a sin t = a t cos t {tex}\frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}{/tex} = -a t sin t + a cos t y = a(sin t - t cos t) {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}{/tex} = a cos t - a cos t + a t sin t = a t sin t {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d t^{2}}{/tex} = a t cos t + a sin t {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\frac{d x}{d t} \frac{d^{2} y}{d t^{2}}-\frac{d y}{d t} \frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}}{\left(\frac{d x}{d t}\right)^{3}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{a t \cos t(a t \cos t+a \sin t)-a t \sin t(-a t \sin t+a \cos t)}{(a t \cos t)^{3}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{a^{2} t^{2} \cos ^{2} t+a^{2} t \cos t \sin t+a^{2} t^{2} \sin ^{2} t-a^{2} t \sin t \cos t}{(a t \cos t)^{3}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{a^{2} t^{2}}{a^{3} t^{3} \cos ^{3} t}=\frac{1}{a t \cos ^{3} t}{/tex} Put the value of t in above equation, {tex}\left.\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right|_{t=\frac{x}{4}}=\frac{1}{a \times \frac{\pi}{4} \cos ^{3} \frac{\pi}{4}}=\frac{8 \sqrt{2}}{\pi a}{/tex}
solution: According to the question, x = a (cos t + t sin t) {tex}\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} = -a sin t + a t cos t + a sin t = a t cos t {tex}\frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}{/tex} = -a t sin t + a cos t y = a(sin t - t cos t) {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}{/tex} = a cos t - a cos t + a t sin t = a t sin t {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d t^{2}}{/tex} = a t cos t + a sin t {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\frac{d x}{d t} \frac{d^{2} y}{d t^{2}}-\frac{d y}{d t} \frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}}{\left(\frac{d x}{d t}\right)^{3}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{a t \cos t(a t \cos t+a \sin t)-a t \sin t(-a t \sin t+a \cos t)}{(a t \cos t)^{3}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{a^{2} t^{2} \cos ^{2} t+a^{2} t \cos t \sin t+a^{2} t^{2} \sin ^{2} t-a^{2} t \sin t \cos t}{(a t \cos t)^{3}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{a^{2} t^{2}}{a^{3} t^{3} \cos ^{3} t}=\frac{1}{a t \cos ^{3} t}{/tex} Put the value of t in above equation, {tex}\left.\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right|_{t=\frac{x}{4}}=\frac{1}{a \times \frac{\pi}{4} \cos ^{3} \frac{\pi}{4}}=\frac{8 \sqrt{2}}{\pi a}{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question,
x = a (cos t + t sin t)
{tex}\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} = -a sin t + a t cos t + a sin t
= a t cos t
{tex}\frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}{/tex} = -a t sin t + a cos t
y = a(sin t - t cos t)
{tex}\frac{d y}{d t}{/tex} = a cos t - a cos t + a t sin t
= a t sin t
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d t^{2}}{/tex} = a t cos t + a sin t
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{\frac{d x}{d t} \frac{d^{2} y}{d t^{2}}-\frac{d y}{d t} \frac{d^{2} x}{d t^{2}}}{\left(\frac{d x}{d t}\right)^{3}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{a t \cos t(a t \cos t+a \sin t)-a t \sin t(-a t \sin t+a \cos t)}{(a t \cos t)^{3}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{a^{2} t^{2} \cos ^{2} t+a^{2} t \cos t \sin t+a^{2} t^{2} \sin ^{2} t-a^{2} t \sin t \cos t}{(a t \cos t)^{3}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{a^{2} t^{2}}{a^{3} t^{3} \cos ^{3} t}=\frac{1}{a t \cos ^{3} t}{/tex}

Put the value of t in above equation, 
{tex}\left.\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right|_{t=\frac{x}{4}}=\frac{1}{a \times \frac{\pi}{4} \cos ^{3} \frac{\pi}{4}}=\frac{8 \sqrt{2}}{\pi a}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}, when x = {tex}a e^{\theta}{/tex}(sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta{/tex}), y = {tex}a e^{\theta}{/tex}(sin {tex}\theta{/tex} + cos {tex}\theta{/tex})
question_text: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} , when x = {tex}a e^{\theta}{/tex} (sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ), y = {tex}a e^{\theta}{/tex} (sin {tex}\theta{/tex} + cos {tex}\theta{/tex} )
explanation: ATQ, x = {tex}a e^{\theta}{/tex} (sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ), y = {tex}a e^{\theta}{/tex} (sin {tex}\theta{/tex} + cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d \theta}=a\left[e^{\theta} \frac{d}{d \theta}(\sin \theta-\cos \theta)+(\sin \theta-\cos \theta) \frac{d}{d \theta}\left(e^{\theta}\right)\right]{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}=a\left[e^{\theta} \frac{d}{d \theta}(\sin \theta+\cos \theta)+(\sin \theta+\cos \theta) \frac{d}{d \theta}\left(e^{\theta}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d \theta}=a\left[e^{\theta}(\cos \theta+\sin \theta)+(\sin \theta-\cos \theta) e^{\theta}\right]{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}=a\left[e^{\theta}(\cos \theta-\sin \theta)+(\sin \theta+\cos \theta) e^{\theta}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d \theta}=a\left[2 e^{\theta} \sin \theta\right]{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}=a\left[2 e^{\theta} \cos \theta\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\frac{d y}{d \theta}}{\frac{d x}{d \theta}}=\frac{a\left(2 e^{\theta} \cos \theta\right)}{a\left(2 e^{\theta} \sin \theta\right)}=\cot \theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}=Cot \theta {/tex} The differentiation of the given function y wrt x is as above.
solution: ATQ, x = {tex}a e^{\theta}{/tex} (sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ), y = {tex}a e^{\theta}{/tex} (sin {tex}\theta{/tex} + cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d \theta}=a\left[e^{\theta} \frac{d}{d \theta}(\sin \theta-\cos \theta)+(\sin \theta-\cos \theta) \frac{d}{d \theta}\left(e^{\theta}\right)\right]{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}=a\left[e^{\theta} \frac{d}{d \theta}(\sin \theta+\cos \theta)+(\sin \theta+\cos \theta) \frac{d}{d \theta}\left(e^{\theta}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d \theta}=a\left[e^{\theta}(\cos \theta+\sin \theta)+(\sin \theta-\cos \theta) e^{\theta}\right]{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}=a\left[e^{\theta}(\cos \theta-\sin \theta)+(\sin \theta+\cos \theta) e^{\theta}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d \theta}=a\left[2 e^{\theta} \sin \theta\right]{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}=a\left[2 e^{\theta} \cos \theta\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\frac{d y}{d \theta}}{\frac{d x}{d \theta}}=\frac{a\left(2 e^{\theta} \cos \theta\right)}{a\left(2 e^{\theta} \sin \theta\right)}=\cot \theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}=Cot \theta {/tex} The differentiation of the given function y wrt x is as above.
solution_html:
ATQ,
x = {tex}a e^{\theta}{/tex}(sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta{/tex}), y = {tex}a e^{\theta}{/tex}(sin {tex}\theta{/tex} + cos {tex}\theta{/tex})
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d \theta}=a\left[e^{\theta} \frac{d}{d \theta}(\sin \theta-\cos \theta)+(\sin \theta-\cos \theta) \frac{d}{d \theta}\left(e^{\theta}\right)\right]{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}=a\left[e^{\theta} \frac{d}{d \theta}(\sin \theta+\cos \theta)+(\sin \theta+\cos \theta) \frac{d}{d \theta}\left(e^{\theta}\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d \theta}=a\left[e^{\theta}(\cos \theta+\sin \theta)+(\sin \theta-\cos \theta) e^{\theta}\right]{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}=a\left[e^{\theta}(\cos \theta-\sin \theta)+(\sin \theta+\cos \theta) e^{\theta}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d \theta}=a\left[2 e^{\theta} \sin \theta\right]{/tex}and {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}=a\left[2 e^{\theta} \cos \theta\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{\frac{d y}{d \theta}}{\frac{d x}{d \theta}}=\frac{a\left(2 e^{\theta} \cos \theta\right)}{a\left(2 e^{\theta} \sin \theta\right)}=\cot \theta{/tex}
{tex}\frac{dy}{dx}=Cot \theta {/tex}
The differentiation of the given function  y wrt x   is as above.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Differentiate {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\right){/tex} with respect to {tex}\sin ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex}, if {tex}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}<x<\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}.
question_text: Differentiate {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\right){/tex} with respect to {tex}\sin ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex} , if {tex}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}<x<\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} .
explanation: Let {tex}\mathbf{u}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\right){/tex} Put x = sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = sin -1 x {tex}\Rightarrow u=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin \theta}{\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow u=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = tan -1 (tan {tex}\theta{/tex} ) ...(i) And Let {tex}{v}=\sin ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex} {tex}v=\sin ^{-1}(2 \sin \theta \sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}){/tex} v = sin -1 (2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ) v = sin -1 (sin 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} ) ...(ii) Here, {tex}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}<x<\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}<\sin \theta<\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{\pi}{4}<\theta<\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} So, from equation (i) {tex}u=\theta\left[\text { since, } \tan ^{-1}(\tan \theta)=\theta, \text { if } \theta \in\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = sin -1 x Differentiating it with respect to x, {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} ....(iii) from equation (ii), {tex}v=2 \theta\left[\text { since, } \sin ^{-1}(\sin \theta)=\theta, \text { if } \theta \in\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v = 2sin -1 x Differentiating it with respect to x, {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} ...(iv) Dividing equation (iii) by (iv), {tex}\frac{\frac{d u}{d x}}{\frac{d v}{d x}}=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\right)\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} .
solution: Let {tex}\mathbf{u}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\right){/tex} Put x = sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = sin -1 x {tex}\Rightarrow u=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin \theta}{\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow u=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = tan -1 (tan {tex}\theta{/tex} ) ...(i) And Let {tex}{v}=\sin ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex} {tex}v=\sin ^{-1}(2 \sin \theta \sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}){/tex} v = sin -1 (2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ) v = sin -1 (sin 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} ) ...(ii) Here, {tex}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}<x<\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}<\sin \theta<\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{\pi}{4}<\theta<\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} So, from equation (i) {tex}u=\theta\left[\text { since, } \tan ^{-1}(\tan \theta)=\theta, \text { if } \theta \in\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = sin -1 x Differentiating it with respect to x, {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} ....(iii) from equation (ii), {tex}v=2 \theta\left[\text { since, } \sin ^{-1}(\sin \theta)=\theta, \text { if } \theta \in\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v = 2sin -1 x Differentiating it with respect to x, {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} ...(iv) Dividing equation (iii) by (iv), {tex}\frac{\frac{d u}{d x}}{\frac{d v}{d x}}=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\right)\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} .
solution_html:
Let {tex}\mathbf{u}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\right){/tex}
Put x = sin{tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\theta{/tex} = sin-1x
{tex}\Rightarrow u=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin \theta}{\sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow u=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = tan-1(tan {tex}\theta{/tex}) ...(i)
And
Let {tex}{v}=\sin ^{-1}(2 x \sqrt{1-x^{2}}){/tex}
{tex}v=\sin ^{-1}(2 \sin \theta \sqrt{1-\sin ^{2} \theta}){/tex}
v = sin-1(2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex})
v = sin-1(sin 2{tex}\theta{/tex}) ...(ii)
Here,
{tex}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}<x<\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}<\sin \theta<\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow-\frac{\pi}{4}<\theta<\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
So, from equation (i)
{tex}u=\theta\left[\text { since, } \tan ^{-1}(\tan \theta)=\theta, \text { if } \theta \in\left(-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = sin-1x
Differentiating it with respect to x,
{tex}\frac{d u}{d x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} ....(iii)
from equation (ii),
{tex}v=2 \theta\left[\text { since, } \sin ^{-1}(\sin \theta)=\theta, \text { if } \theta \in\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v = 2sin-1x
Differentiating it with respect to x,
{tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} ...(iv)
Dividing equation (iii) by (iv),
{tex}\frac{\frac{d u}{d x}}{\frac{d v}{d x}}=\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\right)\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{d u}{d v}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If ex + ey = ex+y, prove that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{e^{x}\left(e^{y}-1\right)}{e^{y}\left(e^{x}-1\right)}{/tex} or, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+e^{y-x}=0{/tex}
question_text: If e x + e y = e x+y , prove that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{e^{x}\left(e^{y}-1\right)}{e^{y}\left(e^{x}-1\right)}{/tex} or, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}+e^{y-x}=0{/tex}
explanation: Given that, e x + e y = e x+y Differentiating both sides with respect to x, {tex}\Rightarrow e^{x}+e^{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{x+y}\left(1+\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{x}+e^{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{x+y}+e^{x+y} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{y} \frac{d y}{d x}-e^{x+y} \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{x+y}-e^{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} (e y - e x+y ) = e x+y - e x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{e^{x+y}-e^{x}}{e^{y}-e^{x+y}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{e^{x}\left(e^{y}-1\right)}{e^{y}\left(1-e^{x}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{e^{x}\left(e^{y}-1\right)}{e^{y}\left(e^{x}-1\right)} {/tex}
solution: Given that, e x + e y = e x+y Differentiating both sides with respect to x, {tex}\Rightarrow e^{x}+e^{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{x+y}\left(1+\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{x}+e^{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{x+y}+e^{x+y} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow e^{y} \frac{d y}{d x}-e^{x+y} \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{x+y}-e^{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} (e y - e x+y ) = e x+y - e x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{e^{x+y}-e^{x}}{e^{y}-e^{x+y}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{e^{x}\left(e^{y}-1\right)}{e^{y}\left(1-e^{x}\right)}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{e^{x}\left(e^{y}-1\right)}{e^{y}\left(e^{x}-1\right)} {/tex}
solution_html:
Given that, ex + ey = ex+y
Differentiating both sides with respect to x,
{tex}\Rightarrow e^{x}+e^{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{x+y}\left(1+\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex}     
{tex}\Rightarrow e^{x}+e^{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{x+y}+e^{x+y} \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow e^{y} \frac{d y}{d x}-e^{x+y} \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{x+y}-e^{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} (ey - ex+y) = ex+y - ex
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{e^{x+y}-e^{x}}{e^{y}-e^{x+y}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{e^{x}\left(e^{y}-1\right)}{e^{y}\left(1-e^{x}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{e^{x}\left(e^{y}-1\right)}{e^{y}\left(e^{x}-1\right)} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If y = {tex} \frac { \sin ^ { - 1 } x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}, then show that {tex} \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - 3 x \frac { d y } { d x } - y = 0{/tex}.
question_text: If y = {tex} \frac { \sin ^ { - 1 } x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} , then show that {tex} \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - 3 x \frac { d y } { d x } - y = 0{/tex} .
explanation: According to the question, if y = {tex} \frac { \sin ^ { - 1 } x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} , then we have to show that {tex} \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - 3 x \frac { d y } { d x } - y = 0{/tex} . Now,we have, y = {tex} \frac { \sin ^ { - 1 } x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \times \frac { d } { d x } \left( \sin ^ { - 1 } x \right) - \left( \sin ^ { - 1 } x \right) \times \frac { d } { d x } \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } { \left( \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} = {tex} \frac{{\left[ {\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} \times \frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} - {{\sin }^{ - 1}}x.\frac{1}{{2\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} \cdot \frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {1 - {x^2}} \right)} \right]}}{{{{\left( {\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} = {tex} \frac{{\left[ {\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} \cdot \frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} - \left( {{{\sin }^{ - 1}}x} \right) \cdot \frac{{ - 2x}}{{2\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }}} \right]}}{{1 - {x^2}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 + \frac { x \sin ^ { - 1 } x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } } { \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 + x y } { 1 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = 1 + xy Therefore,on differentiating both sides of above equation w.r.t x, we get, {tex} \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) {/tex} {tex} + \frac { d y } { d x } \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } ( 1 + x y ){/tex} [By using product rule of derivative] {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { d y } { d x } ( - 2 x ) = 0 + x \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} + y.1 {tex} \Rightarrow \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - 2 x \frac { d y } { d x } - x \frac { d y } { d x } - y = 0{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - 3 x \frac { d y } { d x } - y = 0{/tex}
solution: According to the question, if y = {tex} \frac { \sin ^ { - 1 } x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} , then we have to show that {tex} \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - 3 x \frac { d y } { d x } - y = 0{/tex} . Now,we have, y = {tex} \frac { \sin ^ { - 1 } x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \times \frac { d } { d x } \left( \sin ^ { - 1 } x \right) - \left( \sin ^ { - 1 } x \right) \times \frac { d } { d x } \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } { \left( \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} = {tex} \frac{{\left[ {\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} \times \frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} - {{\sin }^{ - 1}}x.\frac{1}{{2\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} \cdot \frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {1 - {x^2}} \right)} \right]}}{{{{\left( {\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} = {tex} \frac{{\left[ {\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} \cdot \frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} - \left( {{{\sin }^{ - 1}}x} \right) \cdot \frac{{ - 2x}}{{2\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }}} \right]}}{{1 - {x^2}}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 + \frac { x \sin ^ { - 1 } x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } } { \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 + x y } { 1 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = 1 + xy Therefore,on differentiating both sides of above equation w.r.t x, we get, {tex} \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) {/tex} {tex} + \frac { d y } { d x } \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } ( 1 + x y ){/tex} [By using product rule of derivative] {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { d y } { d x } ( - 2 x ) = 0 + x \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} + y.1 {tex} \Rightarrow \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - 2 x \frac { d y } { d x } - x \frac { d y } { d x } - y = 0{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - 3 x \frac { d y } { d x } - y = 0{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, if y = {tex} \frac { \sin ^ { - 1 } x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}, then we have to show that {tex} \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - 3 x \frac { d y } { d x } - y = 0{/tex}.

Now,we have, y = {tex} \frac { \sin ^ { - 1 } x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get,
{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \times \frac { d } { d x } \left( \sin ^ { - 1 } x \right) - \left( \sin ^ { - 1 } x \right) \times \frac { d } { d x } \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } { \left( \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
={tex} \frac{{\left[ {\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} \times \frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} - {{\sin }^{ - 1}}x.\frac{1}{{2\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} \cdot \frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {1 - {x^2}} \right)} \right]}}{{{{\left( {\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} } \right)}^2}}}{/tex}
= {tex} \frac{{\left[ {\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} \cdot \frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} - \left( {{{\sin }^{ - 1}}x} \right) \cdot \frac{{ - 2x}}{{2\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }}} \right]}}{{1 - {x^2}}}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 1 + \frac { x \sin ^ { - 1 } x } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } } { \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 + x y } { 1 - x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = 1 + xy
Therefore,on differentiating both sides of above equation w.r.t x, we get,
{tex} \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) {/tex}{tex} + \frac { d y } { d x } \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } ( 1 + x y ){/tex} [By using product rule of derivative]
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { d y } { d x } ( - 2 x ) = 0 + x \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}+ y.1
{tex} \Rightarrow \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - 2 x \frac { d y } { d x } - x \frac { d y } { d x } - y = 0{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad \left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - 3 x \frac { d y } { d x } - y = 0{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If x = {tex}a\left(\cos \theta+\log \tan \frac{\theta}{2}\right){/tex} and y = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex}, show that the value of {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} at {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} is {tex}\frac{4}{a}{/tex}.
question_text: If x = {tex}a\left(\cos \theta+\log \tan \frac{\theta}{2}\right){/tex} and y = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} , show that the value of {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} at {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} is {tex}\frac{4}{a}{/tex} .
explanation: Given, x = {tex}a\left(\cos \theta+\log \tan \frac{\theta}{2}\right){/tex} , y = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{dx}{d\theta}{/tex} = {tex}a\left(-\sin \theta+\frac{\sec ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}}{2 \tan \frac{\theta}{2}}\right) \frac{d y}{d \theta}{/tex} = a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}a\left(-\sin \theta+\frac{1}{\sin \theta}\right){/tex} = {tex}a\left(\frac{-\sin ^{2} \theta+1}{\sin \theta}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\operatorname{acos}^{2} \theta}{\sin \theta}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d \theta} \div \frac{d x}{d \theta}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\operatorname{acos} \theta \times \frac{\sin \theta}{\operatorname{acos}^{2} \theta}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = tan {tex}\theta{/tex} Differentiating with respect to x,we get {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sec ^{2} \theta \frac{d \theta}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=(\sqrt{2})^{2} \times \frac{\sin \theta}{a \cos ^{2} \theta}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}2 \times \frac{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}{a\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{a}{/tex}
solution: Given, x = {tex}a\left(\cos \theta+\log \tan \frac{\theta}{2}\right){/tex} , y = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{dx}{d\theta}{/tex} = {tex}a\left(-\sin \theta+\frac{\sec ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}}{2 \tan \frac{\theta}{2}}\right) \frac{d y}{d \theta}{/tex} = a cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = {tex}a\left(-\sin \theta+\frac{1}{\sin \theta}\right){/tex} = {tex}a\left(\frac{-\sin ^{2} \theta+1}{\sin \theta}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{\operatorname{acos}^{2} \theta}{\sin \theta}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d \theta} \div \frac{d x}{d \theta}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\operatorname{acos} \theta \times \frac{\sin \theta}{\operatorname{acos}^{2} \theta}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = tan {tex}\theta{/tex} Differentiating with respect to x,we get {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sec ^{2} \theta \frac{d \theta}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=(\sqrt{2})^{2} \times \frac{\sin \theta}{a \cos ^{2} \theta}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}2 \times \frac{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}{a\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{a}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, x = {tex}a\left(\cos \theta+\log \tan \frac{\theta}{2}\right){/tex}, y = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\frac{dx}{d\theta}{/tex} = {tex}a\left(-\sin \theta+\frac{\sec ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}}{2 \tan \frac{\theta}{2}}\right) \frac{d y}{d \theta}{/tex} = a cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
= {tex}a\left(-\sin \theta+\frac{1}{\sin \theta}\right){/tex} 
= {tex}a\left(\frac{-\sin ^{2} \theta+1}{\sin \theta}\right){/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{\operatorname{acos}^{2} \theta}{\sin \theta}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d \theta} \div \frac{d x}{d \theta}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\operatorname{acos} \theta \times \frac{\sin \theta}{\operatorname{acos}^{2} \theta}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = tan {tex}\theta{/tex} 
Differentiating with respect to x,we get
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\sec ^{2} \theta \frac{d \theta}{d x}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=(\sqrt{2})^{2} \times \frac{\sin \theta}{a \cos ^{2} \theta}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}2 \times \frac{\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}}{a\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{a}{/tex}

question_html: Let  {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{1 - \cos 4x}}{{{x^2}}},\,\,if\,\,x < 0 \hfill \\ = a,\,\,if\,\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \frac{{\sqrt x }}{{\sqrt {16+ \sqrt x} - 4}},\,\,if\,x > 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}. For what value of a, f is continuous at x = 0?
question_text: Let {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{1 - \cos 4x}}{{{x^2}}},\,\,if\,\,x < 0 \hfill \\ = a,\,\,if\,\,x = 0 \hfill \\ \frac{{\sqrt x }}{{\sqrt {16+ \sqrt x} - 4}},\,\,if\,x > 0 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} . For what value of a, f is continuous at x = 0?
explanation: Here f(0) = a . Left-hand limit of f at 0 is {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} \frac{{1 - \cos 4x}}{{{x^2}}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} \frac{{2{{\sin }^2}2x}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{2x \to {0^ - }} 8{\left( {\frac{{\sin 2x}}{{2x}}} \right)^2} = 8{\left( 1 \right)^2} = 8{/tex} and right hand limit of f at 0 is {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \frac{{\sqrt x }}{{\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x} - 4 }}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ +}} \frac{{\sqrt x \left( {\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x } + 4} \right)}}{{\left( {\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x} + 4} \right)\left( {\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x } - 4} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \frac{{\sqrt x \left( {\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x } + 4} \right)}}{{{16} + \sqrt x - 16}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \left( {\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x } + 4} \right) = 8{/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f(x) = 8.{/tex} Hence f is continuous at x = 0 only if a = 8.
solution: Here f(0) = a . Left-hand limit of f at 0 is {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} \frac{{1 - \cos 4x}}{{{x^2}}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} \frac{{2{{\sin }^2}2x}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{2x \to {0^ - }} 8{\left( {\frac{{\sin 2x}}{{2x}}} \right)^2} = 8{\left( 1 \right)^2} = 8{/tex} and right hand limit of f at 0 is {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \frac{{\sqrt x }}{{\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x} - 4 }}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ +}} \frac{{\sqrt x \left( {\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x } + 4} \right)}}{{\left( {\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x} + 4} \right)\left( {\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x } - 4} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \frac{{\sqrt x \left( {\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x } + 4} \right)}}{{{16} + \sqrt x - 16}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \left( {\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x } + 4} \right) = 8{/tex} Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f(x) = 8.{/tex} Hence f is continuous at x = 0 only if a = 8.
solution_html:
Here f(0) = a .

Left-hand limit of f at 0 is

{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} \frac{{1 - \cos 4x}}{{{x^2}}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} \frac{{2{{\sin }^2}2x}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{2x \to {0^ - }} 8{\left( {\frac{{\sin 2x}}{{2x}}} \right)^2} = 8{\left( 1 \right)^2} = 8{/tex}

and right hand limit of f at 0 is

{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \frac{{\sqrt x }}{{\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x} - 4 }}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ +}} \frac{{\sqrt x \left( {\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x } + 4} \right)}}{{\left( {\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x} + 4} \right)\left( {\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x } - 4} \right)}}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \frac{{\sqrt x \left( {\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x } + 4} \right)}}{{{16} + \sqrt x - 16}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} \left( {\sqrt {16 + \sqrt x } + 4} \right) = 8{/tex}

Thus, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {0^ + }} f(x) = 8.{/tex} 

Hence f is continuous at x = 0 only if a = 8.

question_html: If x = tan{tex} \left( \frac { 1 } { a } \log y \right){/tex}, then show that {tex} \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + ( 2 x - a ) \frac { d y } { d x } = 0 {/tex}.
question_text: If x = tan {tex} \left( \frac { 1 } { a } \log y \right){/tex} , then show that {tex} \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + ( 2 x - a ) \frac { d y } { d x } = 0 {/tex} .
explanation: According to the question,if x = tan {tex} \left( \frac { 1 } { a } \log y \right){/tex} , then we have to show that {tex} \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + ( 2 x - a ) \frac { d y } { d x } = 0 {/tex} . We shall use product rule of differentiation to prove the above result. Now, x = tan {tex} \left( \frac { 1 } { a } \log y \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \tan ^ { - 1 } x = \frac { 1 } { a } \log y{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} a tan {tex}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}x{/tex} = log y On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex} a \times \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { y } \cdot \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = a y{/tex} Again, differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex} \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) +{/tex} {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } ( a y ){/tex} [By using product rule of derivative] {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { d y } { d x } \cdot ( 2 x ) = a \cdot \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + 2 x \frac { d y } { d x } - a \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + ( 2 x - a ) \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex} Hence Proved.
solution: According to the question,if x = tan {tex} \left( \frac { 1 } { a } \log y \right){/tex} , then we have to show that {tex} \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + ( 2 x - a ) \frac { d y } { d x } = 0 {/tex} . We shall use product rule of differentiation to prove the above result. Now, x = tan {tex} \left( \frac { 1 } { a } \log y \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \tan ^ { - 1 } x = \frac { 1 } { a } \log y{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} a tan {tex}^{-1}{/tex} {tex}x{/tex} = log y On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex} a \times \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { y } \cdot \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = a y{/tex} Again, differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex} \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) +{/tex} {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } ( a y ){/tex} [By using product rule of derivative] {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { d y } { d x } \cdot ( 2 x ) = a \cdot \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + 2 x \frac { d y } { d x } - a \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + ( 2 x - a ) \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex} Hence Proved.
solution_html:
According to the question,if x = tan{tex} \left( \frac { 1 } { a } \log y \right){/tex}, then  we have to  show that {tex} \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + ( 2 x - a ) \frac { d y } { d x } = 0 {/tex}.

We shall use product rule of differentiation to prove the above result.

Now, x = tan{tex} \left( \frac { 1 } { a } \log y \right){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \tan ^ { - 1 } x = \frac { 1 } { a } \log y{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} a tan {tex}^{-1}{/tex}{tex}x{/tex} = log y
On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get,
{tex} a \times \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { y } \cdot \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = a y{/tex}
Again, differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get,
{tex} \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) +{/tex}{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } ( a y ){/tex}[By using product rule of derivative]
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { d y } { d x } \cdot ( 2 x ) = a \cdot \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + 2 x \frac { d y } { d x } - a \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad \left( 1 + x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + ( 2 x - a ) \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex}
Hence Proved.

question_html: If y = log{tex}\left\{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}\right\}{/tex}, prove that: (x2 + a2){tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} + {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 0.
question_text: If y = log {tex}\left\{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}\right\}{/tex} , prove that: (x 2 + a 2 ) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} + {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 0.
explanation: We have, y = log {tex}\left\{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}\right\}{/tex} On differentiating with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}} \times \frac{d}{d x}\left\{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}\right\}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}}\left\{1+\frac{2 x}{2 \sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}}\right\}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}} \times\left\{\frac{\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}+x}{\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}}\right\}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y 1 = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}}{/tex} , where y 1 = {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y_{1}^{2}\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right){/tex} = 1 Differentiating with respect to x, we get {tex}y_{1}^{2} \frac{d}{d x}\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right){/tex} + (x 2 + a 2 ) {tex}\frac{d }{d x}{/tex} ( {tex}y_{1}^{2}{/tex} ) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y_{1}^{2}(2 x)+\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right) \times 2 y_{1} y_{2}{/tex} = 0 .......[ {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(y_{1}^{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}2\left(y_{1}\right)^{2-1} \frac{d}{d x}\left(y_{1}\right){/tex} = 2 {tex}y_{1} \frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} = 2y 1 y 2 ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y 1 {tex}\left\{y_{2}\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right)+x y_{1}\right\}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y_{2}\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right)+x y_{1}{/tex} = 0 .....[ {tex}\because {y_{1} \neq 0}{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x 2 + a 2 ) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} + {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 0
solution: We have, y = log {tex}\left\{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}\right\}{/tex} On differentiating with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}} \times \frac{d}{d x}\left\{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}\right\}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}}\left\{1+\frac{2 x}{2 \sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}}\right\}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}} \times\left\{\frac{\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}+x}{\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}}\right\}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y 1 = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}}{/tex} , where y 1 = {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y_{1}^{2}\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right){/tex} = 1 Differentiating with respect to x, we get {tex}y_{1}^{2} \frac{d}{d x}\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right){/tex} + (x 2 + a 2 ) {tex}\frac{d }{d x}{/tex} ( {tex}y_{1}^{2}{/tex} ) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y_{1}^{2}(2 x)+\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right) \times 2 y_{1} y_{2}{/tex} = 0 .......[ {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(y_{1}^{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}2\left(y_{1}\right)^{2-1} \frac{d}{d x}\left(y_{1}\right){/tex} = 2 {tex}y_{1} \frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} = 2y 1 y 2 ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y 1 {tex}\left\{y_{2}\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right)+x y_{1}\right\}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y_{2}\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right)+x y_{1}{/tex} = 0 .....[ {tex}\because {y_{1} \neq 0}{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x 2 + a 2 ) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} + {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 0
solution_html:
We have,
y = log{tex}\left\{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}\right\}{/tex}
On differentiating with respect to x, we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}} \times \frac{d}{d x}\left\{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}\right\}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}}\left\{1+\frac{2 x}{2 \sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}}\right\}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}} \times\left\{\frac{\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}+x}{\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}}\right\}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y1 = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{x^{2}+a^{2}}}{/tex}, where y1 = {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y_{1}^{2}\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right){/tex} = 1
Differentiating with respect to x, we get
{tex}y_{1}^{2} \frac{d}{d x}\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right){/tex} + (x2 + a2){tex}\frac{d }{d x}{/tex}({tex}y_{1}^{2}{/tex}) = 0 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y_{1}^{2}(2 x)+\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right) \times 2 y_{1} y_{2}{/tex}= 0 .......[{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(y_{1}^{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}2\left(y_{1}\right)^{2-1} \frac{d}{d x}\left(y_{1}\right){/tex} = 2{tex}y_{1} \frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} = 2y1y2] 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y1{tex}\left\{y_{2}\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right)+x y_{1}\right\}{/tex}= 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}y_{2}\left(x^{2}+a^{2}\right)+x y_{1}{/tex} = 0 .....[{tex}\because {y_{1} \neq 0}{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x2 + a2){tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} + {tex}x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 0

question_html: If x = cos{tex}\theta{/tex} and y = sin3 {tex}\theta{/tex} , then prove that {tex}y \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 } = 3 \sin^ 2 \theta ( 5 \cos ^2 \theta - 1 ){/tex}
question_text: If x = cos {tex}\theta{/tex} and y = sin 3 {tex}\theta{/tex} , then prove that {tex}y \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 } = 3 \sin^ 2 \theta ( 5 \cos ^2 \theta - 1 ){/tex}
explanation: According to the question, {tex}x = cos\theta{/tex} ..........(i) and {tex}y = sin^3\theta{/tex} ............(ii) Differentiating both sides of Equations.(i) and (ii) w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d x } { d \theta } = - \sin \theta \text { and } \frac { d y } { d \theta } = 3 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d y / d \theta } { d x / d \theta } = \frac { 3 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cos \theta } { - \sin \theta }{/tex} {tex}= - 3 \sin \theta \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}= \frac { - 3 } { 2 } ( 2 \sin \theta \cos \theta ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { - 3 } { 2 } \sin 2 \theta{/tex} ..........(iii) Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { - 3 } { 2 } \cdot 2 \cos 2 \theta \cdot \frac { d \theta } { d x }{/tex} {tex}= - 3 \cos 2 \theta \cdot \left( \frac { - 1 } { \sin \theta } \right){/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \frac { d \theta } { d x } = - \frac { 1 } { \sin \theta } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 3 \cos 2 \theta } { \sin \theta }{/tex} ..........(iv) Consider LHS = {tex}y \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \sin ^ { 3 } \theta \left( \frac { 3 \cos 2 \theta } { \sin \theta } \right) + \left( \frac { - 3 } { 2 } \sin 2 \theta \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot 3 \cdot \cos 2 \theta + \frac { 9 } { 4 } \sin ^ { 2 } 2 \theta{/tex} {tex}= 3 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \left( 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta - 1 \right) + \frac { 9 } { 4 } \left( 4 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \right){/tex} {tex}= 6 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cos ^ { 2 } \theta - 3 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + 9 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cos ^ { 2 } \theta{/tex} {tex}= 15 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cos ^ { 2 } \theta - 3 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta{/tex} {tex}= 3 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \left( 5 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta - 1 \right){/tex} Hence proved
solution: According to the question, {tex}x = cos\theta{/tex} ..........(i) and {tex}y = sin^3\theta{/tex} ............(ii) Differentiating both sides of Equations.(i) and (ii) w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d x } { d \theta } = - \sin \theta \text { and } \frac { d y } { d \theta } = 3 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d y / d \theta } { d x / d \theta } = \frac { 3 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cos \theta } { - \sin \theta }{/tex} {tex}= - 3 \sin \theta \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}= \frac { - 3 } { 2 } ( 2 \sin \theta \cos \theta ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { - 3 } { 2 } \sin 2 \theta{/tex} ..........(iii) Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { - 3 } { 2 } \cdot 2 \cos 2 \theta \cdot \frac { d \theta } { d x }{/tex} {tex}= - 3 \cos 2 \theta \cdot \left( \frac { - 1 } { \sin \theta } \right){/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \frac { d \theta } { d x } = - \frac { 1 } { \sin \theta } \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 3 \cos 2 \theta } { \sin \theta }{/tex} ..........(iv) Consider LHS = {tex}y \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \sin ^ { 3 } \theta \left( \frac { 3 \cos 2 \theta } { \sin \theta } \right) + \left( \frac { - 3 } { 2 } \sin 2 \theta \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot 3 \cdot \cos 2 \theta + \frac { 9 } { 4 } \sin ^ { 2 } 2 \theta{/tex} {tex}= 3 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \left( 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta - 1 \right) + \frac { 9 } { 4 } \left( 4 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \right){/tex} {tex}= 6 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cos ^ { 2 } \theta - 3 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + 9 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cos ^ { 2 } \theta{/tex} {tex}= 15 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cos ^ { 2 } \theta - 3 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta{/tex} {tex}= 3 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \left( 5 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta - 1 \right){/tex} Hence proved
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}x = cos\theta{/tex}..........(i)
and  {tex}y = sin^3\theta{/tex} ............(ii)
Differentiating both sides of Equations.(i) and (ii) w.r.t x, 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d x } { d \theta } = - \sin \theta \text { and } \frac { d y } { d \theta } = 3 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot \cos \theta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d y / d \theta } { d x / d \theta } = \frac { 3 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cos \theta } { - \sin \theta }{/tex}
{tex}= - 3 \sin \theta \cos \theta{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { - 3 } { 2 } ( 2 \sin \theta \cos \theta ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { - 3 } { 2 } \sin 2 \theta{/tex} ..........(iii)
Differentiating both sides w.r.t x,
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { - 3 } { 2 } \cdot 2 \cos 2 \theta \cdot \frac { d \theta } { d x }{/tex}{tex}= - 3 \cos 2 \theta \cdot \left( \frac { - 1 } { \sin \theta } \right){/tex}{tex}\left[ \because \frac { d \theta } { d x } = - \frac { 1 } { \sin \theta } \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 3 \cos 2 \theta } { \sin \theta }{/tex} ..........(iv)
Consider LHS = {tex}y \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= \sin ^ { 3 } \theta \left( \frac { 3 \cos 2 \theta } { \sin \theta } \right) + \left( \frac { - 3 } { 2 } \sin 2 \theta \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot 3 \cdot \cos 2 \theta + \frac { 9 } { 4 } \sin ^ { 2 } 2 \theta{/tex}
{tex}= 3 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \left( 2 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta - 1 \right) + \frac { 9 } { 4 } \left( 4 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \right){/tex}
{tex}= 6 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cos ^ { 2 } \theta - 3 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + 9 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cos ^ { 2 } \theta{/tex}
{tex}= 15 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cos ^ { 2 } \theta - 3 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta{/tex}
{tex}= 3 \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \left( 5 \cos ^ { 2 } \theta - 1 \right){/tex}
Hence proved

question_html: Show that the function {tex}f(x)=|x - 3|{/tex}, x {tex} \in{/tex} R, is continuous but not differentiable at x = 3.
question_text: Show that the function {tex}f(x)=|x - 3|{/tex} , x {tex} \in{/tex} R, is continuous but not differentiable at x = 3.
explanation: According to the question, {tex}f(x)=|x - 3|{/tex} To Check the continuity of f(x) at x = 3. Here, LHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { x \rightarrow 3 ^ { - } } | x - 3 | = \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } | 3 - h - 3 |{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } | - h | = 0{/tex} RHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { x \rightarrow 3 ^ { + } } | x - 3 | = \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } | 3 + h - 3 |{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } | h | = 0{/tex} {tex}and\ \ f(3) = |3-3| = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} , LHL = RHL = f(3) Hence, f is continuous at x = 3. To check the differentiability of f(x) at x = 3. LHD = {tex} f ^ { \prime } \left( 3 ^ { - } \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { f ( 3 - h ) - f ( 3 ) } { - h }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { | 3 - h - 3 | - | 3 - 3 | } { - h }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { | - h | } { - h } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { h } { - h } = - 1{/tex} RHD = f'(3 + ) = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { f ( 3 + h ) - f ( 3 ) } { h }{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { | 3 + h - 3 | - | 3 - 3 | } { h }{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { | h | } { h } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { h } { h } = 1{/tex} Since, LHD {tex} \ne{/tex} RHD at x = 3. {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) is not differentiable at x=3 Hence proved.
solution: According to the question, {tex}f(x)=|x - 3|{/tex} To Check the continuity of f(x) at x = 3. Here, LHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { x \rightarrow 3 ^ { - } } | x - 3 | = \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } | 3 - h - 3 |{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } | - h | = 0{/tex} RHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { x \rightarrow 3 ^ { + } } | x - 3 | = \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } | 3 + h - 3 |{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } | h | = 0{/tex} {tex}and\ \ f(3) = |3-3| = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} , LHL = RHL = f(3) Hence, f is continuous at x = 3. To check the differentiability of f(x) at x = 3. LHD = {tex} f ^ { \prime } \left( 3 ^ { - } \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { f ( 3 - h ) - f ( 3 ) } { - h }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { | 3 - h - 3 | - | 3 - 3 | } { - h }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { | - h | } { - h } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { h } { - h } = - 1{/tex} RHD = f'(3 + ) = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { f ( 3 + h ) - f ( 3 ) } { h }{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { | 3 + h - 3 | - | 3 - 3 | } { h }{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { | h | } { h } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { h } { h } = 1{/tex} Since, LHD {tex} \ne{/tex} RHD at x = 3. {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) is not differentiable at x=3 Hence proved.
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}f(x)=|x - 3|{/tex}
To Check the continuity of f(x) at x = 3.
Here, LHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { x \rightarrow 3 ^ { - } } | x - 3 | = \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } | 3 - h - 3 |{/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } | - h | = 0{/tex}
RHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { x \rightarrow 3 ^ { + } } | x - 3 | = \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } | 3 + h - 3 |{/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } | h | = 0{/tex}
{tex}and\ \ f(3) = |3-3| = 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex}, LHL = RHL = f(3)
Hence, f is continuous at x = 3.
To check the differentiability of f(x) at x = 3.
LHD = {tex} f ^ { \prime } \left( 3 ^ { - } \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { f ( 3 - h ) - f ( 3 ) } { - h }{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { | 3 - h - 3 | - | 3 - 3 | } { - h }{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { | - h | } { - h } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { h } { - h } = - 1{/tex}
RHD = f'(3+) = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { f ( 3 + h ) - f ( 3 ) } { h }{/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { | 3 + h - 3 | - | 3 - 3 | } { h }{/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { | h | } { h } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { h } { h } = 1{/tex}
Since, LHD {tex} \ne{/tex}RHD at x = 3.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) is not differentiable at x=3
Hence proved.

question_html: Find the value of k, for which f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l } { \frac { \sqrt { 1 + k x } - \sqrt { 1 - k x } } { x } , \text { if } - 1 \leq x < 0 } \\ { \frac { 2 x + 1 } { x - 1 } , \quad \text { if } 0 \leq x < 1 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0.
question_text: Find the value of k, for which f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l } { \frac { \sqrt { 1 + k x } - \sqrt { 1 - k x } } { x } , \text { if } - 1 \leq x < 0 } \\ { \frac { 2 x + 1 } { x - 1 } , \quad \text { if } 0 \leq x < 1 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0.
explanation: According to the question,we are given that, f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { l } { \frac { \sqrt { 1 + k x } - \sqrt { 1 - k x } } { x } , \text { if } - 1 \leq x < 0 } \\ { \frac { 2 x + 1 } { x - 1 } , \quad \text { if } 0 \leq x < 1 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0 and we have to find the value of k. Now, f(0) = {tex}\frac { 2(0) + 1 } { 0 - 1 } = \frac { 1 } { - 1 }{/tex} =-1 and LHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f ( 0 - h ){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sqrt { 1 - k h } - \sqrt { 1 + k h } } { - h }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sqrt { 1 - k h } - \sqrt { 1 + k h } } { - h } \times \frac { ( \sqrt { 1 - k h } + \sqrt { 1 + k h } ) } { ( \sqrt { 1 - k h } + \sqrt { 1 + k h } ) }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { ( 1 - k h ) - ( 1 + k h ) } { - h ( \sqrt { 1 - k h } + \sqrt { 1 + k h } ) }{/tex} [(a + b)(a - b) = a 2 - b 2 ] {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { - 2 k h } { - h ( \sqrt { 1 - k h } + \sqrt { 1 + k h } ) }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 2 k } { \sqrt { 1 - k h } + \sqrt { 1 + k h } } = \frac { 2 k } { 1 + 1 } = \frac { 2 k } { 2 }{/tex} = 2 Since, f(x) is continuous at x = 0. {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(0) = LHL {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - 1 = k {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} k = -1
solution: According to the question,we are given that, f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { l } { \frac { \sqrt { 1 + k x } - \sqrt { 1 - k x } } { x } , \text { if } - 1 \leq x < 0 } \\ { \frac { 2 x + 1 } { x - 1 } , \quad \text { if } 0 \leq x < 1 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0 and we have to find the value of k. Now, f(0) = {tex}\frac { 2(0) + 1 } { 0 - 1 } = \frac { 1 } { - 1 }{/tex} =-1 and LHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f ( 0 - h ){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sqrt { 1 - k h } - \sqrt { 1 + k h } } { - h }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sqrt { 1 - k h } - \sqrt { 1 + k h } } { - h } \times \frac { ( \sqrt { 1 - k h } + \sqrt { 1 + k h } ) } { ( \sqrt { 1 - k h } + \sqrt { 1 + k h } ) }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { ( 1 - k h ) - ( 1 + k h ) } { - h ( \sqrt { 1 - k h } + \sqrt { 1 + k h } ) }{/tex} [(a + b)(a - b) = a 2 - b 2 ] {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { - 2 k h } { - h ( \sqrt { 1 - k h } + \sqrt { 1 + k h } ) }{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 2 k } { \sqrt { 1 - k h } + \sqrt { 1 + k h } } = \frac { 2 k } { 1 + 1 } = \frac { 2 k } { 2 }{/tex} = 2 Since, f(x) is continuous at x = 0. {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(0) = LHL {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - 1 = k {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} k = -1
solution_html:
According to the question,we are given that, f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { l } { \frac { \sqrt { 1 + k x } - \sqrt { 1 - k x } } { x } , \text { if } - 1 \leq x < 0 } \\ { \frac { 2 x + 1 } { x - 1 } , \quad \text { if } 0 \leq x < 1 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0

and we have to find the value of k.
Now, f(0) = {tex}\frac { 2(0) + 1 } { 0 - 1 } = \frac { 1 } { - 1 }{/tex} =-1
and LHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f ( 0 - h ){/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sqrt { 1 - k h } - \sqrt { 1 + k h } } { - h }{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sqrt { 1 - k h } - \sqrt { 1 + k h } } { - h } \times \frac { ( \sqrt { 1 - k h } + \sqrt { 1 + k h } ) } { ( \sqrt { 1 - k h } + \sqrt { 1 + k h } ) }{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { ( 1 - k h ) - ( 1 + k h ) } { - h ( \sqrt { 1 - k h } + \sqrt { 1 + k h } ) }{/tex} [(a + b)(a - b) = a2 - b2]
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { - 2 k h } { - h ( \sqrt { 1 - k h } + \sqrt { 1 + k h } ) }{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 2 k } { \sqrt { 1 - k h } + \sqrt { 1 + k h } } = \frac { 2 k } { 1 + 1 } = \frac { 2 k } { 2 }{/tex}= 2
Since, f(x) is continuous at x = 0.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f(0) = LHL {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - 1 = k
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} k = -1

question_html: If x = 2 cos{tex} \theta{/tex} - cos 2{tex} \theta{/tex} and y = 2 sin {tex} \theta{/tex} - sin 2{tex} \theta{/tex}, then prove that {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \tan \left( \frac { 3 \theta } { 2 } \right) {/tex}.
question_text: If x = 2 cos {tex} \theta{/tex} - cos 2 {tex} \theta{/tex} and y = 2 sin {tex} \theta{/tex} - sin 2 {tex} \theta{/tex} , then prove that {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \tan \left( \frac { 3 \theta } { 2 } \right) {/tex} .
explanation: According to the question,we are given that x = 2 cos {tex} \theta{/tex} - cos 2 {tex} \theta{/tex} and y = 2 sin {tex} \theta{/tex} - sin 2 {tex} \theta{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex}{/tex} then we have to prove that {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \tan \left( \frac { 3 \theta } { 2 } \right) {/tex} . Therefore, differentiating both sides w.r.t {tex} \theta{/tex} , we get, {tex} \frac { d x } { d \theta } = - 2 \sin \theta + 2 \sin 2 \theta{/tex} and {tex} \frac { d y } { d \theta } = 2 \cos \theta - 2 \cos 2 \theta{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d y / d \theta } { d x / d \theta } = \frac { 2 ( \cos \theta - \cos 2 \theta ) } { 2 ( - \sin \theta + \sin 2 \theta ) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{2\sin \left( {\frac{{\theta + 2\theta }}{2}} \right)\sin \left( {\frac{{2\theta - \theta }}{2}} \right)}}{{2\left[ {\cos \left( {\frac{{2\theta + \theta }}{2}} \right)\sin \left( {\frac{{2\theta - \theta }}{2}} \right)} \right]}}{/tex} {tex} \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \because \cos C - \cos D = 2 \sin \left( \frac { C + D } { 2 } \right) \sin \left( \frac { D - C } { 2 } \right) } \\ { \text { and } \sin C - \sin D = 2 \cos \left( \frac { C + D } { 2 } \right) \sin \left( \frac { C - D } { 2 } \right) } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { \sin \left( \frac { 3 \theta } { 2 } \right) \sin \left( \frac { \theta } { 2 } \right) } { \cos \left( \frac { 3 \theta } { 2 } \right) \sin \left( \begin{array} { l } \frac { \theta } { 2 } \end{array} \right) }{/tex} {tex} = \tan \left( \frac { 3 \theta } { 2 } \right){/tex}
solution: According to the question,we are given that x = 2 cos {tex} \theta{/tex} - cos 2 {tex} \theta{/tex} and y = 2 sin {tex} \theta{/tex} - sin 2 {tex} \theta{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex}{/tex} {tex}{/tex} then we have to prove that {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \tan \left( \frac { 3 \theta } { 2 } \right) {/tex} . Therefore, differentiating both sides w.r.t {tex} \theta{/tex} , we get, {tex} \frac { d x } { d \theta } = - 2 \sin \theta + 2 \sin 2 \theta{/tex} and {tex} \frac { d y } { d \theta } = 2 \cos \theta - 2 \cos 2 \theta{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d y / d \theta } { d x / d \theta } = \frac { 2 ( \cos \theta - \cos 2 \theta ) } { 2 ( - \sin \theta + \sin 2 \theta ) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{2\sin \left( {\frac{{\theta + 2\theta }}{2}} \right)\sin \left( {\frac{{2\theta - \theta }}{2}} \right)}}{{2\left[ {\cos \left( {\frac{{2\theta + \theta }}{2}} \right)\sin \left( {\frac{{2\theta - \theta }}{2}} \right)} \right]}}{/tex} {tex} \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \because \cos C - \cos D = 2 \sin \left( \frac { C + D } { 2 } \right) \sin \left( \frac { D - C } { 2 } \right) } \\ { \text { and } \sin C - \sin D = 2 \cos \left( \frac { C + D } { 2 } \right) \sin \left( \frac { C - D } { 2 } \right) } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \frac { \sin \left( \frac { 3 \theta } { 2 } \right) \sin \left( \frac { \theta } { 2 } \right) } { \cos \left( \frac { 3 \theta } { 2 } \right) \sin \left( \begin{array} { l } \frac { \theta } { 2 } \end{array} \right) }{/tex} {tex} = \tan \left( \frac { 3 \theta } { 2 } \right){/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question,we are given that x = 2 cos{tex} \theta{/tex} - cos 2{tex} \theta{/tex}
and y = 2 sin {tex} \theta{/tex} - sin 2{tex} \theta{/tex}

{tex}{/tex}{tex}{/tex} {tex}{/tex}{tex}{/tex} then we have to prove that {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \tan \left( \frac { 3 \theta } { 2 } \right) {/tex}.

Therefore, differentiating both sides w.r.t {tex} \theta{/tex}, we get,
{tex} \frac { d x } { d \theta } = - 2 \sin \theta + 2 \sin 2 \theta{/tex}
and {tex} \frac { d y } { d \theta } = 2 \cos \theta - 2 \cos 2 \theta{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d y / d \theta } { d x / d \theta } = \frac { 2 ( \cos \theta - \cos 2 \theta ) } { 2 ( - \sin \theta + \sin 2 \theta ) }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{{2\sin \left( {\frac{{\theta + 2\theta }}{2}} \right)\sin \left( {\frac{{2\theta - \theta }}{2}} \right)}}{{2\left[ {\cos \left( {\frac{{2\theta + \theta }}{2}} \right)\sin \left( {\frac{{2\theta - \theta }}{2}} \right)} \right]}}{/tex}{tex} \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \because \cos C - \cos D = 2 \sin \left( \frac { C + D } { 2 } \right) \sin \left( \frac { D - C } { 2 } \right) } \\ { \text { and } \sin C - \sin D = 2 \cos \left( \frac { C + D } { 2 } \right) \sin \left( \frac { C - D } { 2 } \right) } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { \sin \left( \frac { 3 \theta } { 2 } \right) \sin \left( \frac { \theta } { 2 } \right) } { \cos \left( \frac { 3 \theta } { 2 } \right) \sin \left( \begin{array} { l } \frac { \theta } { 2 } \end{array} \right) }{/tex}
{tex} = \tan \left( \frac { 3 \theta } { 2 } \right){/tex}
