question_html: If x sin(a + y) + sin a cos(a + y) = 0, then prove that {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \sin ^ { 2 } ( a + y ) } { \sin a }{/tex}.
question_text: If x sin(a + y) + sin a cos(a + y) = 0, then prove that {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \sin ^ { 2 } ( a + y ) } { \sin a }{/tex} .
explanation: Given, x sin(a + y) + sin a cos(a + y) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x = \frac { - \sin a \cos ( a + y ) } { \sin ( a + y ) }{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t y,we get {tex}\frac { d x } { d y }{/tex} = {tex} - \frac{{\left[ {\sin (a + y)\frac{d}{{dy}}\{ \sin a\cos (a + y)\} - \sin a\cos (a + y)\frac{d}{{dy}}\{ \sin (a + y)\} } \right]}}{{{{\sin }^2}(a + y)}}{/tex} ...[ By using quotient rule of derivative] {tex}= \left\{ \frac { \sin ( a + y ) \cdot \sin a \sin ( a + y )+sina\;cos(a+y)\;cos(a+y) } { \sin ^ { 2 } ( a + y ) } \right\}{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \sin a } { \sin ^ { 2 } ( a + y ) }{/tex} {sin 2 (a + y) + cos 2 (a + y)} {tex} = \frac { \sin a } { \sin ^ { 2 } ( a + y ) } \cdot 1{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + \cos ^ { 2 } \theta = 1 \right]{/tex} {tex} \therefore \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \sin ^ { 2 } ( a + y ) } { \sin a }{/tex} Hence Proved.
solution: Given, x sin(a + y) + sin a cos(a + y) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x = \frac { - \sin a \cos ( a + y ) } { \sin ( a + y ) }{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t y,we get {tex}\frac { d x } { d y }{/tex} = {tex} - \frac{{\left[ {\sin (a + y)\frac{d}{{dy}}\{ \sin a\cos (a + y)\} - \sin a\cos (a + y)\frac{d}{{dy}}\{ \sin (a + y)\} } \right]}}{{{{\sin }^2}(a + y)}}{/tex} ...[ By using quotient rule of derivative] {tex}= \left\{ \frac { \sin ( a + y ) \cdot \sin a \sin ( a + y )+sina\;cos(a+y)\;cos(a+y) } { \sin ^ { 2 } ( a + y ) } \right\}{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \sin a } { \sin ^ { 2 } ( a + y ) }{/tex} {sin 2 (a + y) + cos 2 (a + y)} {tex} = \frac { \sin a } { \sin ^ { 2 } ( a + y ) } \cdot 1{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + \cos ^ { 2 } \theta = 1 \right]{/tex} {tex} \therefore \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \sin ^ { 2 } ( a + y ) } { \sin a }{/tex} Hence Proved.
solution_html:
Given,  x sin(a + y) + sin a cos(a + y) = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad x = \frac { - \sin a \cos ( a + y ) } { \sin ( a + y ) }{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t y,we get
{tex}\frac { d x } { d y }{/tex}= {tex} - \frac{{\left[ {\sin (a + y)\frac{d}{{dy}}\{ \sin a\cos (a + y)\} - \sin a\cos (a + y)\frac{d}{{dy}}\{ \sin (a + y)\} } \right]}}{{{{\sin }^2}(a + y)}}{/tex}   ...[ By using quotient rule of derivative]
{tex}= \left\{ \frac { \sin ( a + y ) \cdot \sin a \sin ( a + y )+sina\;cos(a+y)\;cos(a+y) } { \sin ^ { 2 } ( a + y ) } \right\}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \sin a } { \sin ^ { 2 } ( a + y ) }{/tex}{sin2(a + y) + cos2(a + y)}
{tex} = \frac { \sin a } { \sin ^ { 2 } ( a + y ) } \cdot 1{/tex}{tex} \left[ \because \sin ^ { 2 } \theta + \cos ^ { 2 } \theta = 1 \right]{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \sin ^ { 2 } ( a + y ) } { \sin a }{/tex}
Hence Proved.

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Diﬀerentiate the following function with respect to x

f(x)=(log x)x + xlog x.
question_text: Diﬀerentiate the following function with respect to x f(x)=(log x) x + x log x .
explanation: Let y = (log x) x + x log x Also, let u = (log x) x and v = x log x , then y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d u } { d x } + \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} ..........(i) Now, consider u = (log x) x Therefore,on taking log both sides, we get log u = log(log x) x = x log(log x) Therefore, differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \log ( \log x ) + \log ( \log x ) \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( x ){/tex} {tex}= \frac { x } { \log x } \cdot \frac { 1 } { x } + \log ( \log x ){/tex} {tex}\frac { d u } { d x } = u \left[ \frac { 1 } { \log x } + \log ( \log x ) \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d u } { d x } = ( \log x ) ^ { x } \left[ \frac { 1 } { \log x } + \log ( \log x ) \right]{/tex} ...........(ii) Since, v = x log x Therefore,on taking log both sides, we get, log v = log(x log x ) = (log x)(log x) = (log x) {tex}^2{/tex} Therefore,On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac{1}{v} \frac { dv } { d x } = 2 \log x \cdot \frac { 1 } { x } \Rightarrow \frac { d v } { d x } = v \left[ \frac { 2 \log x } { x } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d v } { d x } = x ^ { \log x } \left[ \frac { 2 \log x } { x } \right]{/tex} .............(iii) From Eqs(i), (ii) and (iii), we get {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = ( \log x ) ^ { x } \left\{ \frac { 1 } { \log x } + \log ( \log x ) \right\}{/tex} {tex}+ 2 \left( \frac { \log x } { x } \right) x ^ { \log x } {/tex}
solution: Let y = (log x) x + x log x Also, let u = (log x) x and v = x log x , then y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d u } { d x } + \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} ..........(i) Now, consider u = (log x) x Therefore,on taking log both sides, we get log u = log(log x) x = x log(log x) Therefore, differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \log ( \log x ) + \log ( \log x ) \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( x ){/tex} {tex}= \frac { x } { \log x } \cdot \frac { 1 } { x } + \log ( \log x ){/tex} {tex}\frac { d u } { d x } = u \left[ \frac { 1 } { \log x } + \log ( \log x ) \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d u } { d x } = ( \log x ) ^ { x } \left[ \frac { 1 } { \log x } + \log ( \log x ) \right]{/tex} ...........(ii) Since, v = x log x Therefore,on taking log both sides, we get, log v = log(x log x ) = (log x)(log x) = (log x) {tex}^2{/tex} Therefore,On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac{1}{v} \frac { dv } { d x } = 2 \log x \cdot \frac { 1 } { x } \Rightarrow \frac { d v } { d x } = v \left[ \frac { 2 \log x } { x } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d v } { d x } = x ^ { \log x } \left[ \frac { 2 \log x } { x } \right]{/tex} .............(iii) From Eqs(i), (ii) and (iii), we get {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = ( \log x ) ^ { x } \left\{ \frac { 1 } { \log x } + \log ( \log x ) \right\}{/tex} {tex}+ 2 \left( \frac { \log x } { x } \right) x ^ { \log x } {/tex}
solution_html:
Let y = (log x)x + xlog x

Also, let u = (log x)x and v = xlog x, then y = u + v
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d u } { d x } + \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex}..........(i)
Now, consider u = (log x)x
Therefore,on taking log both sides, we get
log u = log(log x)x = x log(log x)
Therefore, differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get,
{tex}\frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \log ( \log x ) + \log ( \log x ) \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( x ){/tex}
{tex}= \frac { x } { \log x } \cdot \frac { 1 } { x } + \log ( \log x ){/tex}
{tex}\frac { d u } { d x } = u \left[ \frac { 1 } { \log x } + \log ( \log x ) \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d u } { d x } = ( \log x ) ^ { x } \left[ \frac { 1 } { \log x } + \log ( \log x ) \right]{/tex}...........(ii)
Since, v =  xlog x
Therefore,on taking log both sides, we get,
log v = log(xlog x) = (log x)(log x) = (log x){tex}^2{/tex}
Therefore,On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get,
{tex}\frac{1}{v} \frac { dv } { d x } = 2 \log x \cdot \frac { 1 } { x } \Rightarrow \frac { d v } { d x } = v \left[ \frac { 2 \log x } { x } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d v } { d x } = x ^ { \log x } \left[ \frac { 2 \log x } { x } \right]{/tex}.............(iii)
From Eqs(i), (ii) and (iii), we get
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = ( \log x ) ^ { x } \left\{ \frac { 1 } { \log x } + \log ( \log x ) \right\}{/tex}{tex}+ 2 \left( \frac { \log x } { x } \right) x ^ { \log x } {/tex}

question_html: If x = a cos3 {tex} \theta{/tex} and y = a sin3 {tex} \theta{/tex}, find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}. Also, find its value at {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}
question_text: If x = a cos 3 {tex} \theta{/tex} and y = a sin 3 {tex} \theta{/tex} , find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} . Also, find its value at {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{6}{/tex}
explanation: According to the question, x = a cos 3 {tex} \theta{/tex} and y = a sin 3 {tex} \theta{/tex} We are required to find the value of {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } \text { at } \theta = \frac { \pi } { 6 }{/tex} Therefore,on differentiating both sides of x and y w.r.t {tex}\theta{/tex} ,we get, {tex} \frac { d x } { d \theta } = 3 a \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \frac { d } { d \theta } ( \cos \theta ){/tex} {tex} = 3 a \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot ( - \sin \theta ){/tex} {tex} = - 3 a \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot \sin \theta{/tex} and {tex} \frac { d y } { d \theta } = 3 a \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \frac { d } { d \theta } ( \sin \theta ){/tex} {tex} = 3 a \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot ( \cos \theta ) = 3 a \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot \cos \theta{/tex} Now, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \left( \frac { d y / d \theta } { d x / d \theta } \right){/tex} {tex} = \frac { 3 a \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot \cos \theta } { - 3 a \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot \sin \theta } = - \tan \theta{/tex} Again, On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } ( - \tan \theta ) = - \frac { d } { d \theta } ( \tan \theta ) \frac { d \theta } { d x }{/tex} {tex} = - \sec ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot \frac { d \theta } { d x }{/tex} {tex} = - \sec ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot \left( \frac { - 1 } { 3 a \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot \sin \theta } \right)= \ \left( \frac { 1 } { 3 a \cos ^ { 4 } \theta \cdot \sin \theta } \right){/tex} {tex} \therefore \text { At } \theta = \frac { \pi } { 6 }{/tex} {tex} \left( \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } \right) _ { a t \theta = \frac { \pi } { 6 } } = \frac { 1 } { 3 a \left( \cos \frac { \pi } { 6 } \right) ^ { 4 } \left( \sin \frac { \pi } { 6 } \right) }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 3 a \left( \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 4 } \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 3 a \left( \frac { 9 } { 16 } \right) \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) } = \frac { 32 } { 27 a }{/tex}
solution: According to the question, x = a cos 3 {tex} \theta{/tex} and y = a sin 3 {tex} \theta{/tex} We are required to find the value of {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } \text { at } \theta = \frac { \pi } { 6 }{/tex} Therefore,on differentiating both sides of x and y w.r.t {tex}\theta{/tex} ,we get, {tex} \frac { d x } { d \theta } = 3 a \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \frac { d } { d \theta } ( \cos \theta ){/tex} {tex} = 3 a \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot ( - \sin \theta ){/tex} {tex} = - 3 a \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot \sin \theta{/tex} and {tex} \frac { d y } { d \theta } = 3 a \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \frac { d } { d \theta } ( \sin \theta ){/tex} {tex} = 3 a \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot ( \cos \theta ) = 3 a \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot \cos \theta{/tex} Now, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \left( \frac { d y / d \theta } { d x / d \theta } \right){/tex} {tex} = \frac { 3 a \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot \cos \theta } { - 3 a \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot \sin \theta } = - \tan \theta{/tex} Again, On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } ( - \tan \theta ) = - \frac { d } { d \theta } ( \tan \theta ) \frac { d \theta } { d x }{/tex} {tex} = - \sec ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot \frac { d \theta } { d x }{/tex} {tex} = - \sec ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot \left( \frac { - 1 } { 3 a \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot \sin \theta } \right)= \ \left( \frac { 1 } { 3 a \cos ^ { 4 } \theta \cdot \sin \theta } \right){/tex} {tex} \therefore \text { At } \theta = \frac { \pi } { 6 }{/tex} {tex} \left( \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } \right) _ { a t \theta = \frac { \pi } { 6 } } = \frac { 1 } { 3 a \left( \cos \frac { \pi } { 6 } \right) ^ { 4 } \left( \sin \frac { \pi } { 6 } \right) }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 3 a \left( \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 4 } \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 3 a \left( \frac { 9 } { 16 } \right) \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) } = \frac { 32 } { 27 a }{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, x = a cos3 {tex} \theta{/tex} and y = a sin3 {tex} \theta{/tex}

We are required to find the value of  {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } \text { at } \theta = \frac { \pi } { 6 }{/tex}
Therefore,on differentiating both sides of x and y w.r.t {tex}\theta{/tex}  ,we get,
{tex} \frac { d x } { d \theta } = 3 a \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \frac { d } { d \theta } ( \cos \theta ){/tex}
{tex} = 3 a \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot ( - \sin \theta ){/tex}
{tex} = - 3 a \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot \sin \theta{/tex}
and {tex} \frac { d y } { d \theta } = 3 a \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \frac { d } { d \theta } ( \sin \theta ){/tex}
{tex} = 3 a \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot ( \cos \theta ) = 3 a \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot \cos \theta{/tex}
Now,{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \left( \frac { d y / d \theta } { d x / d \theta } \right){/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 3 a \sin ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot \cos \theta } { - 3 a \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot \sin \theta } = - \tan \theta{/tex}
Again, On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get,
{tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } ( - \tan \theta ) = - \frac { d } { d \theta } ( \tan \theta ) \frac { d \theta } { d x }{/tex}
{tex} = - \sec ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot \frac { d \theta } { d x }{/tex}
{tex} = - \sec ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot \left( \frac { - 1 } { 3 a \cos ^ { 2 } \theta \cdot \sin \theta } \right)= \ \left( \frac { 1 } { 3 a \cos ^ { 4 } \theta \cdot \sin \theta } \right){/tex}
{tex} \therefore \text { At } \theta = \frac { \pi } { 6 }{/tex}
{tex} \left( \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } \right) _ { a t \theta = \frac { \pi } { 6 } } = \frac { 1 } { 3 a \left( \cos \frac { \pi } { 6 } \right) ^ { 4 } \left( \sin \frac { \pi } { 6 } \right) }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 1 } { 3 a \left( \frac { \sqrt { 3 } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 4 } \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 1 } { 3 a \left( \frac { 9 } { 16 } \right) \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) } = \frac { 32 } { 27 a }{/tex}

question_html: Differentiate {tex}\sin ^{-1}\left\{\frac{2^{x+1} \cdot 3^{x}}{1+(36)^{x}}\right\}{/tex} with respect to x.
question_text: Differentiate {tex}\sin ^{-1}\left\{\frac{2^{x+1} \cdot 3^{x}}{1+(36)^{x}}\right\}{/tex} with respect to x.
explanation: Let y = {tex} \sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 2 ^ { x + 1 } \cdot 3 ^ { x } } { 1 + ( 36 ) ^ { x } } \right]{/tex} {tex} = \sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 2 \cdot 2 ^ { x } \cdot 3 ^ { x } } { 1 + \left( 6 ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { x } } \right]{/tex} {tex} = \sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 2 \cdot 6 ^ { x } } { 1 + \left( 6 ^ { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex} Put 6 x = tan {tex} \theta {/tex} ,then {tex} \theta { }= \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( 6 ^ { x } \right){/tex} Therefore, y = {tex} \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 2 \cdot \tan \theta } { 1 + \tan ^ { 2 } \theta } \right){/tex} {tex} = \sin ^ { - 1 } ( \sin 2 \theta ){/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \sin 2 \theta = \frac { 2 \tan \theta } { 1 + \tan ^ { 2 } \theta } \right]{/tex} {tex} = 2 \theta{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} y = 2 tan -1 (6 x ) {tex} \left[ \because \theta = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( 6 ^ { x } \right) \right]{/tex} Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 2 } { 1 + \left( 6 ^ { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } \frac { d } { d x } \left( 6 ^ { x } \right){/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \frac { d } { d x } \tan ^ { - 1 } x = \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \right] {/tex} {tex} \therefore \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 2 } { 1 + \left( 6 ^ { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } \cdot 6 ^ { x } \cdot \log 6{/tex} {tex} = \left[ \frac { 2 ^ { x + 1 } \cdot 3 ^ { x } } { 1 + ( 36 ) ^ { x } } \right] \log 6{/tex}
solution: Let y = {tex} \sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 2 ^ { x + 1 } \cdot 3 ^ { x } } { 1 + ( 36 ) ^ { x } } \right]{/tex} {tex} = \sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 2 \cdot 2 ^ { x } \cdot 3 ^ { x } } { 1 + \left( 6 ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { x } } \right]{/tex} {tex} = \sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 2 \cdot 6 ^ { x } } { 1 + \left( 6 ^ { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex} Put 6 x = tan {tex} \theta {/tex} ,then {tex} \theta { }= \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( 6 ^ { x } \right){/tex} Therefore, y = {tex} \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 2 \cdot \tan \theta } { 1 + \tan ^ { 2 } \theta } \right){/tex} {tex} = \sin ^ { - 1 } ( \sin 2 \theta ){/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \sin 2 \theta = \frac { 2 \tan \theta } { 1 + \tan ^ { 2 } \theta } \right]{/tex} {tex} = 2 \theta{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} y = 2 tan -1 (6 x ) {tex} \left[ \because \theta = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( 6 ^ { x } \right) \right]{/tex} Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 2 } { 1 + \left( 6 ^ { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } \frac { d } { d x } \left( 6 ^ { x } \right){/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \frac { d } { d x } \tan ^ { - 1 } x = \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \right] {/tex} {tex} \therefore \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 2 } { 1 + \left( 6 ^ { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } \cdot 6 ^ { x } \cdot \log 6{/tex} {tex} = \left[ \frac { 2 ^ { x + 1 } \cdot 3 ^ { x } } { 1 + ( 36 ) ^ { x } } \right] \log 6{/tex}
solution_html:
Let y ={tex} \sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 2 ^ { x + 1 } \cdot 3 ^ { x } } { 1 + ( 36 ) ^ { x } } \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 2 \cdot 2 ^ { x } \cdot 3 ^ { x } } { 1 + \left( 6 ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { x } } \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 2 \cdot 6 ^ { x } } { 1 + \left( 6 ^ { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex}
Put 6x = tan {tex} \theta {/tex},then {tex} \theta { }= \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( 6 ^ { x } \right){/tex}
Therefore, y = {tex} \sin ^ { - 1 } \left( \frac { 2 \cdot \tan \theta } { 1 + \tan ^ { 2 } \theta } \right){/tex}
{tex} = \sin ^ { - 1 } ( \sin 2 \theta ){/tex}{tex} \left[ \because \sin 2 \theta = \frac { 2 \tan \theta } { 1 + \tan ^ { 2 } \theta } \right]{/tex}
{tex} = 2 \theta{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} y = 2 tan-1(6x){tex} \left[ \because \theta = \tan ^ { - 1 } \left( 6 ^ { x } \right) \right]{/tex}
Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get,
{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 2 } { 1 + \left( 6 ^ { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } \frac { d } { d x } \left( 6 ^ { x } \right){/tex}{tex} \left[ \because \frac { d } { d x } \tan ^ { - 1 } x = \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } \right] {/tex}
{tex} \therefore \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 2 } { 1 + \left( 6 ^ { x } \right) ^ { 2 } } \cdot 6 ^ { x } \cdot \log 6{/tex}{tex} = \left[ \frac { 2 ^ { x + 1 } \cdot 3 ^ { x } } { 1 + ( 36 ) ^ { x } } \right] \log 6{/tex}

question_html: Differentiate  {tex}{\sin ^{ - 1}}\left[ {\frac{{{2^{x + 1}}}}{{1 + {4^x}}}} \right]{/tex}.
question_text: Differentiate {tex}{\sin ^{ - 1}}\left[ {\frac{{{2^{x + 1}}}}{{1 + {4^x}}}} \right]{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}y = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{{2^{x + 1}}}}{{1 + {4^x}}}} \right){/tex} {tex} = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left[ {\frac{{{2^x}.2}}{{1 + {{\left( {{2^x}} \right)}^2}}}} \right]{/tex} Put {tex}{2^x} = \tan \theta {/tex} {tex} = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{2\tan \theta }}{{1 + {{\tan }^2}\theta }}} \right){/tex} {tex} = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\sin 2\theta } \right){/tex} {tex} = 2\theta {/tex} {tex}y = 2.{\tan ^{ - 1}}{2^x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 2.\frac{1}{{1 + {{\left( {{2^x}} \right)}^2}}}.\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {{2^x}} \right){/tex} {tex} = \frac{2}{{1 + {4^x}}}{.2^x}.\log 2{/tex}
solution: {tex}y = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{{2^{x + 1}}}}{{1 + {4^x}}}} \right){/tex} {tex} = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left[ {\frac{{{2^x}.2}}{{1 + {{\left( {{2^x}} \right)}^2}}}} \right]{/tex} Put {tex}{2^x} = \tan \theta {/tex} {tex} = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{2\tan \theta }}{{1 + {{\tan }^2}\theta }}} \right){/tex} {tex} = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\sin 2\theta } \right){/tex} {tex} = 2\theta {/tex} {tex}y = 2.{\tan ^{ - 1}}{2^x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 2.\frac{1}{{1 + {{\left( {{2^x}} \right)}^2}}}.\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {{2^x}} \right){/tex} {tex} = \frac{2}{{1 + {4^x}}}{.2^x}.\log 2{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}y = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{{2^{x + 1}}}}{{1 + {4^x}}}} \right){/tex}

{tex} = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left[ {\frac{{{2^x}.2}}{{1 + {{\left( {{2^x}} \right)}^2}}}} \right]{/tex}

Put {tex}{2^x} = \tan \theta {/tex}

{tex} = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{2\tan \theta }}{{1 + {{\tan }^2}\theta }}} \right){/tex}

{tex} = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left( {\sin 2\theta } \right){/tex}

{tex} = 2\theta {/tex}

{tex}y = 2.{\tan ^{ - 1}}{2^x}{/tex}

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 2.\frac{1}{{1 + {{\left( {{2^x}} \right)}^2}}}.\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {{2^x}} \right){/tex}

{tex} = \frac{2}{{1 + {4^x}}}{.2^x}.\log 2{/tex}

question_html: If x = a sin t and y = a {tex}\left(\cos t+\log \tan \frac{t}{2}\right),{/tex} find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}
question_text: If x = a sin t and y = a {tex}\left(\cos t+\log \tan \frac{t}{2}\right),{/tex} find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}
explanation: We have, x = a sin t and y = a {tex}\left(\cos t+\log \tan \frac{t}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{d x}{d t}=a \cos t{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=a\left(-\sin t+\frac{1}{\tan \frac t2} \cdot \sec ^{2} \frac{t}{2} \times \frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{d x}{d t}=a \cos t{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=a\left(-\sin t+\frac{1}{\sin t}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}=a \cos t{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=a\left(-\sin t+\frac{1}{\sin t}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}=a \cos t{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=\frac{a\left(1-\sin ^{2} t\right)}{\sin t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}=a \cos t{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=\frac{a \cos ^{2} t}{\sin t}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d t}}{\frac{d x}{d t}}=\frac{\frac{a \cos ^{2} t}{\sin t}}{a \cos t}=\frac{\cos t}{\sin t}=\cot t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=\frac{d}{d x}(\cot t)=-\ c o s e c ^{2} t \frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=-\csc ^{2} t \times \frac{1}{a \cos t}=-\frac{1}{a \sin ^{2} t \cos t}{/tex}
solution: We have, x = a sin t and y = a {tex}\left(\cos t+\log \tan \frac{t}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{d x}{d t}=a \cos t{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=a\left(-\sin t+\frac{1}{\tan \frac t2} \cdot \sec ^{2} \frac{t}{2} \times \frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{d x}{d t}=a \cos t{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=a\left(-\sin t+\frac{1}{\sin t}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}=a \cos t{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=a\left(-\sin t+\frac{1}{\sin t}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}=a \cos t{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=\frac{a\left(1-\sin ^{2} t\right)}{\sin t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}=a \cos t{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=\frac{a \cos ^{2} t}{\sin t}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d t}}{\frac{d x}{d t}}=\frac{\frac{a \cos ^{2} t}{\sin t}}{a \cos t}=\frac{\cos t}{\sin t}=\cot t{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=\frac{d}{d x}(\cot t)=-\ c o s e c ^{2} t \frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=-\csc ^{2} t \times \frac{1}{a \cos t}=-\frac{1}{a \sin ^{2} t \cos t}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have,
x = a sin t and y = a {tex}\left(\cos t+\log \tan \frac{t}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac{d x}{d t}=a \cos t{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=a\left(-\sin t+\frac{1}{\tan \frac t2} \cdot \sec ^{2} \frac{t}{2} \times \frac{1}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac{d x}{d t}=a \cos t{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=a\left(-\sin t+\frac{1}{\sin t}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}=a \cos t{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=a\left(-\sin t+\frac{1}{\sin t}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}=a \cos t{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=\frac{a\left(1-\sin ^{2} t\right)}{\sin t}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d x}{d t}=a \cos t{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d t}=\frac{a \cos ^{2} t}{\sin t}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d t}}{\frac{d x}{d t}}=\frac{\frac{a \cos ^{2} t}{\sin t}}{a \cos t}=\frac{\cos t}{\sin t}=\cot t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)=\frac{d}{d x}(\cot t)=-\ c o s e c ^{2} t \frac{d t}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=-\csc ^{2} t \times \frac{1}{a \cos t}=-\frac{1}{a \sin ^{2} t \cos t}{/tex}

question_html: Find the values of a and b so that the function f given by {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} 1, & \text { if } x \leq 3 \\ a x+b, & \text { if } 3<x<5 \\ 7, & \text { if } x \geq 5 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 3 and x = 5
question_text: Find the values of a and b so that the function f given by {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} 1, & \text { if } x \leq 3 \\ a x+b, & \text { if } 3<x<5 \\ 7, & \text { if } x \geq 5 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 3 and x = 5
explanation: Given function is: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} 1, \text { if } x \leq 3 \\ a x+b, \text { if } 3<x<5 \\ 7, \text { if } x \geq 5 \end{array}\right.{/tex} We have, (LHL at x = 3) = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(3-h){/tex} {tex}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}(1)=1{/tex} (RHL at x = 3) = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3^{+}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(3+h){/tex} {tex}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} a(3+h)+b=3 a+b{/tex} (LHL at x = 5) = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 5^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(5-h){/tex} {tex}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}(a(5-h)+b)=5 a+b{/tex} (RHL at x = 5) = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 5^{+}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(5+h){/tex} {tex}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}{/tex} 7 = 7 If f(x) is continuous at x = 3 and 5, then {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3^{+}} f(x) \text { and } \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 5^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 5^{+}} f(x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = 3a + b ...(i) and 5a + b = 7 ...(ii) On solving eqs. (i) and (ii), we get, a = 3 and b = -8
solution: Given function is: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} 1, \text { if } x \leq 3 \\ a x+b, \text { if } 3<x<5 \\ 7, \text { if } x \geq 5 \end{array}\right.{/tex} We have, (LHL at x = 3) = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(3-h){/tex} {tex}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}(1)=1{/tex} (RHL at x = 3) = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3^{+}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(3+h){/tex} {tex}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} a(3+h)+b=3 a+b{/tex} (LHL at x = 5) = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 5^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(5-h){/tex} {tex}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}(a(5-h)+b)=5 a+b{/tex} (RHL at x = 5) = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 5^{+}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(5+h){/tex} {tex}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}{/tex} 7 = 7 If f(x) is continuous at x = 3 and 5, then {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3^{+}} f(x) \text { and } \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 5^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 5^{+}} f(x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = 3a + b ...(i) and 5a + b = 7 ...(ii) On solving eqs. (i) and (ii), we get, a = 3 and b = -8
solution_html:
Given function is: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} 1, \text { if } x \leq 3 \\ a x+b, \text { if } 3<x<5 \\ 7, \text { if } x \geq 5 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
We have,
(LHL at x = 3) = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(3-h){/tex}
{tex}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}(1)=1{/tex}
(RHL at x = 3) = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3^{+}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(3+h){/tex}
{tex}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} a(3+h)+b=3 a+b{/tex}
(LHL at x = 5) = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 5^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(5-h){/tex}
{tex}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}(a(5-h)+b)=5 a+b{/tex}
(RHL at x = 5) = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 5^{+}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(5+h){/tex}
{tex}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}{/tex} 7 = 7
If f(x) is continuous at x = 3 and 5, then
{tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 3^{+}} f(x) \text { and } \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 5^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 5^{+}} f(x){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = 3a + b ...(i)
and 5a + b = 7 ...(ii)
On solving eqs. (i) and (ii), we get,
a = 3 and b = -8

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Differentiate the function (log x)x + xlog x w.r.t. x.
question_text: Differentiate the function (log x) x + x log x w.r.t. x.
explanation: Given: (log x) x + x log x Let y = (log x) x + x log x Let y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = (log x) x and v = x log x For, u = (log x) x Taking log on both sides, we get log u = log (log x) x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = x.log (log (x)) Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{u})=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}[\mathrm{x} \cdot \log (\log \mathrm{x})]{/tex} {tex}\left.\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{u}} \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \log (\log \mathrm{x})\right)+\log (\log \mathrm{x}) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\mathrm{x}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{u}\left[\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{1}{\log \mathrm{x}} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{x})+\log (\log \mathrm{x}) \cdot(1)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{u}}{\mathrm{dx}}=(\log \mathrm{x})^{\mathrm{x}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\log \mathrm{x}} \cdot \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}+\log (\log \mathrm{x}) \cdot(1)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\log x)^{x}\left[\frac{1+\log (\log x) \cdot(\log x)}{\log x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\log x)^{x-1}[1+\log x \cdot \log (\log x)]{/tex} For, v = x log x Taking log on both sides, we get log v = log (x log x ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log v = log x. log x Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x {tex}\frac{d}{d x}(\log v)=\frac{d}{d x}\left[(\log x)^{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=2 \cdot \log \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{x}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=v\left[2 \cdot \frac{\log x}{x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=x^{\log x}\left[2 \cdot \frac{\log x}{x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=2 \cdot x^{\log x-1} \cdot \log x{/tex} Because, y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=(\log x)^{x-1}[1+\log x \cdot \log (\log x)]+2 \cdot x^{\log x-1} \cdot \log x{/tex}
solution: Given: (log x) x + x log x Let y = (log x) x + x log x Let y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = (log x) x and v = x log x For, u = (log x) x Taking log on both sides, we get log u = log (log x) x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = x.log (log (x)) Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{u})=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}[\mathrm{x} \cdot \log (\log \mathrm{x})]{/tex} {tex}\left.\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{u}} \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \log (\log \mathrm{x})\right)+\log (\log \mathrm{x}) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\mathrm{x}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{u}\left[\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{1}{\log \mathrm{x}} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{x})+\log (\log \mathrm{x}) \cdot(1)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{u}}{\mathrm{dx}}=(\log \mathrm{x})^{\mathrm{x}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\log \mathrm{x}} \cdot \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}+\log (\log \mathrm{x}) \cdot(1)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\log x)^{x}\left[\frac{1+\log (\log x) \cdot(\log x)}{\log x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\log x)^{x-1}[1+\log x \cdot \log (\log x)]{/tex} For, v = x log x Taking log on both sides, we get log v = log (x log x ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log v = log x. log x Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x {tex}\frac{d}{d x}(\log v)=\frac{d}{d x}\left[(\log x)^{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=2 \cdot \log \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{x}){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=v\left[2 \cdot \frac{\log x}{x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=x^{\log x}\left[2 \cdot \frac{\log x}{x}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=2 \cdot x^{\log x-1} \cdot \log x{/tex} Because, y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=(\log x)^{x-1}[1+\log x \cdot \log (\log x)]+2 \cdot x^{\log x-1} \cdot \log x{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: (log x)x + xlog x 
Let y = (log x)x + xlog x 
Let y = u + v
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} u = (log x)x and v = xlog x 
For, u = (log x)x 
Taking log on both sides, we get
log u = log (log x)x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = x.log (log (x))
Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{u})=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}[\mathrm{x} \cdot \log (\log \mathrm{x})]{/tex} 
{tex}\left.\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{u}} \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \log (\log \mathrm{x})\right)+\log (\log \mathrm{x}) \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\mathrm{x}){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{du}}{\mathrm{dx}}=\mathrm{u}\left[\mathrm{x} \cdot \frac{1}{\log \mathrm{x}} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{x})+\log (\log \mathrm{x}) \cdot(1)\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{u}}{\mathrm{dx}}=(\log \mathrm{x})^{\mathrm{x}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\log \mathrm{x}} \cdot \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}+\log (\log \mathrm{x}) \cdot(1)\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\log x)^{x}\left[\frac{1+\log (\log x) \cdot(\log x)}{\log x}\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=(\log x)^{x-1}[1+\log x \cdot \log (\log x)]{/tex} 
For, v = xlog x  
Taking log on both sides, we get
log v = log (xlog x)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log v = log x. log x
Now, differentiate both sides with respect to x
{tex}\frac{d}{d x}(\log v)=\frac{d}{d x}\left[(\log x)^{2}\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{v}} \frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dx}}=2 \cdot \log \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\log \mathrm{x}){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=v\left[2 \cdot \frac{\log x}{x}\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=x^{\log x}\left[2 \cdot \frac{\log x}{x}\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=2 \cdot x^{\log x-1} \cdot \log x{/tex} 
Because, y = u + v
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=(\log x)^{x-1}[1+\log x \cdot \log (\log x)]+2 \cdot x^{\log x-1} \cdot \log x{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If y = (sin x)x + sin-1 {tex}\sqrt{x}{/tex}, find {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}.
question_text: If y = (sin x) x + sin -1 {tex}\sqrt{x}{/tex} , find {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} .
explanation: Let (sin x) x = u and sin -1 {tex}\sqrt{x}{/tex} = v. Then, y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} .....(i) Now, u = (sin x) x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = x log (sin x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{u} \cdot \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}x \cdot \frac{1}{\sin x} \cdot \cos x{/tex} + log (sin x) {tex}\cdot{/tex} 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = u {x cot x + log (sin x)} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = (sin x) x {tex}\cdot{/tex} {x cot x + log (sin x)} ......(ii) v = sin -1 {tex}\sqrt{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-x)}} \cdot \frac{1}{2} x^{-1 / 2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x} \sqrt{(1-x)}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x-x^{2}}}{/tex} ......(iii) Using (ii) and (iii) in (i), we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = (sin x) x {x cot x + log (sin x)} + {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x-x^{2}}}{/tex}
solution: Let (sin x) x = u and sin -1 {tex}\sqrt{x}{/tex} = v. Then, y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} .....(i) Now, u = (sin x) x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = x log (sin x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{u} \cdot \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}x \cdot \frac{1}{\sin x} \cdot \cos x{/tex} + log (sin x) {tex}\cdot{/tex} 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = u {x cot x + log (sin x)} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = (sin x) x {tex}\cdot{/tex} {x cot x + log (sin x)} ......(ii) v = sin -1 {tex}\sqrt{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-x)}} \cdot \frac{1}{2} x^{-1 / 2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x} \sqrt{(1-x)}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x-x^{2}}}{/tex} ......(iii) Using (ii) and (iii) in (i), we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = (sin x) x {x cot x + log (sin x)} + {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x-x^{2}}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let (sin x)x = u and sin-1 {tex}\sqrt{x}{/tex} = v. Then,
y = u + v 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} .....(i)
Now, u = (sin x)x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = x log (sin x)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{u} \cdot \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}x \cdot \frac{1}{\sin x} \cdot \cos x{/tex} + log (sin x) {tex}\cdot{/tex} 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = u {x cot x + log (sin x)}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = (sin x)x {tex}\cdot{/tex} {x cot x + log (sin x)}  ......(ii)
v = sin-1 {tex}\sqrt{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-x)}} \cdot \frac{1}{2} x^{-1 / 2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x} \sqrt{(1-x)}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x-x^{2}}}{/tex} ......(iii)
Using (ii) and (iii) in (i), we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = (sin x)x {x cot x + log (sin x)} + {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x-x^{2}}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If y = {tex}\{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+1}\}^{m}{/tex}, then show that {tex}\left(x^{2}+1\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}-m^{2} y{/tex} = 0.
question_text: If y = {tex}\{x+\sqrt{x^{2}+1}\}^{m}{/tex} , then show that {tex}\left(x^{2}+1\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}-m^{2} y{/tex} = 0.
explanation: According to the question, y = {tex} \left( x + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 }+1 } \right) ^ { m}{/tex} ..........(i) Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = m \left( x + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } +1} \right) ^ { m - 1 }{/tex} {tex}\left( 1 + \frac { 2 x } { 2 \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } +1} } \right){/tex} [ Using chain rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex} m \left( x + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } +1} \right) ^ { m - 1 } \left( \frac { x + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } +1} } { \sqrt { x ^ { 2 }+1 } } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { m \left( x + \sqrt {x ^ { 2 } +1} \right) ^ { m } } { \sqrt {x ^ { 2 }+1 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { m y } { \sqrt { x ^ { 2 }+1 } }{/tex} [ From Equation(i)] {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { x ^ { 2 }+1 } \frac { d y } { d x } = m y{/tex} .........(ii) Differentiating both sides w.r.t x again, we get {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } +1} \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 2 x } { 2 \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } +1} }{/tex} {tex}.\frac { d y } { d x } = m \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( x ^ { 2 }+1 \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + x \frac { d y } { d x } ={/tex} {tex}m \cdot \sqrt { x ^ { 2 }+1 } \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} [ multiplying both sides by {tex}\sqrt { x ^ { 2 }+1 }{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow \left( x ^ { 2 }+1 \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + x \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = {tex}m \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } +1} \cdot \frac { m y } { \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } +1} }{/tex} [ From Equation(ii)] we get {tex}\therefore \left( x ^ { 2 } +1\right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + x \frac { d y } { d x } = m ^ { 2 } y{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex} \left( x ^ { 2 } +1\right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + x \frac { d y } { d x } - m ^ { 2 } y{/tex} = 0 Hence Proved
solution: According to the question, y = {tex} \left( x + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 }+1 } \right) ^ { m}{/tex} ..........(i) Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = m \left( x + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } +1} \right) ^ { m - 1 }{/tex} {tex}\left( 1 + \frac { 2 x } { 2 \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } +1} } \right){/tex} [ Using chain rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex} m \left( x + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } +1} \right) ^ { m - 1 } \left( \frac { x + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } +1} } { \sqrt { x ^ { 2 }+1 } } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { m \left( x + \sqrt {x ^ { 2 } +1} \right) ^ { m } } { \sqrt {x ^ { 2 }+1 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { m y } { \sqrt { x ^ { 2 }+1 } }{/tex} [ From Equation(i)] {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { x ^ { 2 }+1 } \frac { d y } { d x } = m y{/tex} .........(ii) Differentiating both sides w.r.t x again, we get {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } +1} \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 2 x } { 2 \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } +1} }{/tex} {tex}.\frac { d y } { d x } = m \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( x ^ { 2 }+1 \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + x \frac { d y } { d x } ={/tex} {tex}m \cdot \sqrt { x ^ { 2 }+1 } \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} [ multiplying both sides by {tex}\sqrt { x ^ { 2 }+1 }{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow \left( x ^ { 2 }+1 \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + x \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = {tex}m \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } +1} \cdot \frac { m y } { \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } +1} }{/tex} [ From Equation(ii)] we get {tex}\therefore \left( x ^ { 2 } +1\right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + x \frac { d y } { d x } = m ^ { 2 } y{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex} \left( x ^ { 2 } +1\right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + x \frac { d y } { d x } - m ^ { 2 } y{/tex} = 0 Hence Proved
solution_html:
According to the question, y = {tex} \left( x + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 }+1 } \right) ^ { m}{/tex}..........(i)
Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = m \left( x + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } +1} \right) ^ { m - 1 }{/tex}{tex}\left( 1 + \frac { 2 x } { 2 \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } +1} } \right){/tex}[ Using chain rule of derivative]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex} m \left( x + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } +1} \right) ^ { m - 1 } \left( \frac { x + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } +1} } { \sqrt { x ^ { 2 }+1 } } \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { m \left( x + \sqrt {x ^ { 2 } +1} \right) ^ { m } } { \sqrt {x ^ { 2 }+1 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { m y } { \sqrt { x ^ { 2 }+1 } }{/tex}[ From Equation(i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { x ^ { 2 }+1 } \frac { d y } { d x } = m y{/tex}.........(ii)
Differentiating both sides w.r.t x again, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } +1} \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 2 x } { 2 \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } +1} }{/tex}{tex}.\frac { d y } { d x } = m \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left( x ^ { 2 }+1 \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + x \frac { d y } { d x } ={/tex}{tex}m \cdot \sqrt { x ^ { 2 }+1 } \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}[ multiplying both sides by {tex}\sqrt { x ^ { 2 }+1 }{/tex}]
{tex}\Rightarrow \left( x ^ { 2 }+1 \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + x \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}= {tex}m \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } +1} \cdot \frac { m y } { \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } +1} }{/tex}[ From Equation(ii)] we get
{tex}\therefore \left( x ^ { 2 } +1\right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + x \frac { d y } { d x } = m ^ { 2 } y{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex} \left( x ^ { 2 } +1\right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + x \frac { d y } { d x } - m ^ { 2 } y{/tex} = 0
Hence Proved

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the values of k so that the function f is continuous at the indicated point: {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{k\cos x}}{{\pi - 2x}},\,\,if\,x \ne \frac{\pi }{2} \hfill \\ 3,\,\,if\,\,x = \frac{\pi }{2} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} at {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex}
question_text: Find the values of k so that the function f is continuous at the indicated point: {tex}f\left( x \right) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} \frac{{k\cos x}}{{\pi - 2x}},\,\,if\,x \ne \frac{\pi }{2} \hfill \\ 3,\,\,if\,\,x = \frac{\pi }{2} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} at {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex}
explanation: Here, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{2}} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{2}} \frac{{k\cos x}}{{\pi - 2x}}{/tex} {tex}\because x \to \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x \ne \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} Put {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{2} + h{/tex} where {tex}h \to 0{/tex} , we get {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{2}} f\left( x \right) {/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{k\cos \left( {\frac{\pi }{2} + h} \right)}}{{\pi - 2\left( {\frac{\pi }{2} + h} \right)}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{ - k\sinh }}{{\pi - \pi - 2h}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{k\sin \,h}}{{ 2h}} = \frac{k}{2} \times \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{\sin h}}{h}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{k}{2}{/tex} …….(i) And {tex}f\left( {\frac{\pi }{2}} \right) = 3{/tex} ……….(ii) {tex}\because f\left( x \right) = 3{/tex} when {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} [Given] Since f(x) is continuous at {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{2}} f\left( x \right) = f\left( {\frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} From eq. (i) and (ii), {tex}\frac{k}{2} = 3{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow k = 6{/tex}
solution: Here, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{2}} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{2}} \frac{{k\cos x}}{{\pi - 2x}}{/tex} {tex}\because x \to \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x \ne \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} Put {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{2} + h{/tex} where {tex}h \to 0{/tex} , we get {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{2}} f\left( x \right) {/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{k\cos \left( {\frac{\pi }{2} + h} \right)}}{{\pi - 2\left( {\frac{\pi }{2} + h} \right)}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{ - k\sinh }}{{\pi - \pi - 2h}}{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{k\sin \,h}}{{ 2h}} = \frac{k}{2} \times \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{\sin h}}{h}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{k}{2}{/tex} …….(i) And {tex}f\left( {\frac{\pi }{2}} \right) = 3{/tex} ……….(ii) {tex}\because f\left( x \right) = 3{/tex} when {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} [Given] Since f(x) is continuous at {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{2}} f\left( x \right) = f\left( {\frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} From eq. (i) and (ii), {tex}\frac{k}{2} = 3{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow k = 6{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{2}} f\left( x \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{2}} \frac{{k\cos x}}{{\pi - 2x}}{/tex}

{tex}\because x \to \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow x \ne \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex}

Put {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{2} + h{/tex} where {tex}h \to 0{/tex} , we get

{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{2}} f\left( x \right) {/tex}{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{k\cos \left( {\frac{\pi }{2} + h} \right)}}{{\pi - 2\left( {\frac{\pi }{2} + h} \right)}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{ - k\sinh }}{{\pi - \pi - 2h}}{/tex}

{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{k\sin \,h}}{{ 2h}} = \frac{k}{2} \times \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \frac{{\sin h}}{h}{/tex}

{tex} = \frac{k}{2}{/tex}…….(i)

And {tex}f\left( {\frac{\pi }{2}} \right) = 3{/tex} ……….(ii)

{tex}\because f\left( x \right) = 3{/tex} when {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} [Given]

Since  f(x) is continuous at {tex}x = \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to \frac{\pi }{2}} f\left( x \right) = f\left( {\frac{\pi }{2}} \right){/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex} From eq. (i) and (ii),

{tex}\frac{k}{2} = 3{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow k = 6{/tex}

question_html: Find the value of k, so that the function f defined by f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { r } { \frac { k \cos x } { \pi - 2 x } , \text { if } x \neq \frac { \pi } { 2 } } \\ { 3 , \text { if } x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x ={tex} \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}.
question_text: Find the value of k, so that the function f defined by f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { r } { \frac { k \cos x } { \pi - 2 x } , \text { if } x \neq \frac { \pi } { 2 } } \\ { 3 , \text { if } x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = {tex} \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} .
explanation: Let f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{aligned} \frac { k \cos x } { \pi - 2 x } , & \text { if } x \neq \frac { \pi } { 2 } \\ 3 , & \text { if } x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } \end{aligned} \right. {/tex} is continuous at x = {tex} \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} Therefore, at x = {tex} \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} , LHL = RHL = {tex} f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} ........(i) Now, LHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { \pi { } } { 2 }^{-} } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { \pi } { 2 } ^ { - } } \frac { k \cos x } { \pi - 2 x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \ { LHL } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { k \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - h \right) } { \pi - 2 \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - h \right) }{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { k \sin h } { \pi - \pi + 2 h }{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \theta \right) = \sin \theta \right]{/tex} = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { k \sin h } { 2 h } = \frac { k } { 2 } \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin h } { h }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { LHL } = \frac { k } { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin h } { h } = 1 \right]{/tex} Also, from the given function, we get {tex}f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = 3{/tex} Now, from Eq. (i), we have LHL = {tex}f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) \Rightarrow \frac { k } { 2 }{/tex} = 3 {tex}\therefore{/tex} k = 6
solution: Let f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{aligned} \frac { k \cos x } { \pi - 2 x } , & \text { if } x \neq \frac { \pi } { 2 } \\ 3 , & \text { if } x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } \end{aligned} \right. {/tex} is continuous at x = {tex} \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} Therefore, at x = {tex} \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} , LHL = RHL = {tex} f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} ........(i) Now, LHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { \pi { } } { 2 }^{-} } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { \pi } { 2 } ^ { - } } \frac { k \cos x } { \pi - 2 x }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \ { LHL } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { k \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - h \right) } { \pi - 2 \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - h \right) }{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { k \sin h } { \pi - \pi + 2 h }{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \theta \right) = \sin \theta \right]{/tex} = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { k \sin h } { 2 h } = \frac { k } { 2 } \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin h } { h }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { LHL } = \frac { k } { 2 }{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin h } { h } = 1 \right]{/tex} Also, from the given function, we get {tex}f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = 3{/tex} Now, from Eq. (i), we have LHL = {tex}f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) \Rightarrow \frac { k } { 2 }{/tex} = 3 {tex}\therefore{/tex} k = 6
solution_html:
Let f(x) ={tex} \left\{ \begin{aligned} \frac { k \cos x } { \pi - 2 x } , & \text { if } x \neq \frac { \pi } { 2 } \\ 3 , & \text { if } x = \frac { \pi } { 2 } \end{aligned} \right. {/tex} is continuous at x ={tex} \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
Therefore, at x ={tex} \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}, LHL = RHL ={tex} f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right){/tex} ........(i)
Now, LHL ={tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { \pi { } } { 2 }^{-} } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { \pi } { 2 } ^ { - } } \frac { k \cos x } { \pi - 2 x }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \ { LHL } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { k \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - h \right) } { \pi - 2 \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - h \right) }{/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { k \sin h } { \pi - \pi + 2 h }{/tex}{tex} \left[ \because \cos \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \theta \right) = \sin \theta \right]{/tex}
={tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { k \sin h } { 2 h } = \frac { k } { 2 } \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin h } { h }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { LHL } = \frac { k } { 2 }{/tex}{tex} \left[ \because \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin h } { h } = 1 \right]{/tex}
Also, from the given function, we get
{tex}f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = 3{/tex}
Now, from Eq. (i), we have
LHL = {tex}f \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) \Rightarrow \frac { k } { 2 }{/tex}= 3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} k = 6

question_html: If x = {tex}a(\cos \theta+\theta \sin \theta){/tex} and y = {tex}a(\sin \theta-\theta \cos \theta){/tex}, show that {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{1}{a}\left(\frac{\sec ^{3} \theta}{\theta}\right){/tex}.
question_text: If x = {tex}a(\cos \theta+\theta \sin \theta){/tex} and y = {tex}a(\sin \theta-\theta \cos \theta){/tex} , show that {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{1}{a}\left(\frac{\sec ^{3} \theta}{\theta}\right){/tex} .
explanation: Given that, x = a (cosθ + θsinθ) and y = a(sinθ − θcosθ) Differentiate the function x w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}{/tex} = {tex}a[-a \sin \theta+\theta \cos \theta+\sin \theta]{/tex} = {tex}(a \theta \cos \theta){/tex} Again differentiate the function y w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}{/tex} = a[cos {tex}\theta{/tex} - ( {tex}\theta{/tex} (-sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ) + cos {tex}\theta{/tex} )] {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}{/tex} = a[cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ] {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}{/tex} = a {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{a \theta \sin \theta}{a \theta \cos \theta}{/tex} = tan {tex}\theta{/tex} Again differentiate the function y w.r.t. x, {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\sec ^{2} \theta \frac{d \theta}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\sec ^{2} \theta \times \frac{1}{a \theta \cos \theta}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{\sec ^{3} \theta}{a \theta}\right){/tex}
solution: Given that, x = a (cosθ + θsinθ) and y = a(sinθ − θcosθ) Differentiate the function x w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}{/tex} = {tex}a[-a \sin \theta+\theta \cos \theta+\sin \theta]{/tex} = {tex}(a \theta \cos \theta){/tex} Again differentiate the function y w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}{/tex} = a[cos {tex}\theta{/tex} - ( {tex}\theta{/tex} (-sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ) + cos {tex}\theta{/tex} )] {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}{/tex} = a[cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ] {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}{/tex} = a {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{a \theta \sin \theta}{a \theta \cos \theta}{/tex} = tan {tex}\theta{/tex} Again differentiate the function y w.r.t. x, {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\sec ^{2} \theta \frac{d \theta}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\sec ^{2} \theta \times \frac{1}{a \theta \cos \theta}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{\sec ^{3} \theta}{a \theta}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
Given that,  x = a (cosθ + θsinθ) and y = a(sinθ − θcosθ)
Differentiate the function x w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}{/tex} = {tex}a[-a \sin \theta+\theta \cos \theta+\sin \theta]{/tex} = {tex}(a \theta \cos \theta){/tex} 
Again differentiate the function y w.r.t. {tex}\theta{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}{/tex} = a[cos {tex}\theta{/tex} - ({tex}\theta{/tex}(-sin {tex}\theta{/tex}) + cos {tex}\theta{/tex})]
{tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}{/tex} = a[cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + {tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta{/tex}]
{tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}{/tex} = a{tex}\theta{/tex} sin {tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{a \theta \sin \theta}{a \theta \cos \theta}{/tex} = tan {tex}\theta{/tex} 

Again differentiate the function y w.r.t. x,
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\sec ^{2} \theta \frac{d \theta}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\sec ^{2} \theta \times \frac{1}{a \theta \cos \theta}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{\sec ^{3} \theta}{a \theta}\right){/tex}

question_html: If siny = xsin(a + y), prove that {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{{\sin }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{{\sin a}}{/tex}
question_text: If siny = xsin(a + y), prove that {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{{\sin }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{{\sin a}}{/tex}
explanation: siny = x sin(a + y) {tex}x = \frac{{\sin y}}{{\sin \left( {a + y} \right)}}{/tex} Differentiate both sides w.r.t. to x {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{{\sin \left( {a + y} \right)\cos y - \sin y\cos \left( {a + y} \right)}}{{{{\sin }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{{\sin \left( {a + y - y} \right)}}{{{{\sin }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{{{{\sin }}{a} }}{{\sin ^2(a+y)}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{{\sin^2 }}{(a+y)} }}{{\sin a}}{/tex}
solution: siny = x sin(a + y) {tex}x = \frac{{\sin y}}{{\sin \left( {a + y} \right)}}{/tex} Differentiate both sides w.r.t. to x {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{{\sin \left( {a + y} \right)\cos y - \sin y\cos \left( {a + y} \right)}}{{{{\sin }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{{\sin \left( {a + y - y} \right)}}{{{{\sin }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{{{{\sin }}{a} }}{{\sin ^2(a+y)}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{{\sin^2 }}{(a+y)} }}{{\sin a}}{/tex}
solution_html:
siny = x sin(a + y)
{tex}x = \frac{{\sin y}}{{\sin \left( {a + y} \right)}}{/tex}
Differentiate both sides w.r.t. to x
{tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{{\sin \left( {a + y} \right)\cos y - \sin y\cos \left( {a + y} \right)}}{{{{\sin }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{{\sin \left( {a + y - y} \right)}}{{{{\sin }^2}\left( {a + y} \right)}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dy}} = \frac{{{{\sin }}{a} }}{{\sin ^2(a+y)}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{{\sin^2 }}{(a+y)} }}{{\sin a}}{/tex}

question_text: If y = (tan -1 x) 2 show that (x 2 + 1) 2 y 2 + 2x(x 2 + 1)y 1 = 2
explanation: y = (tan -1 x) 2 (given) Differentiating both sides w.r.t to x {tex}{y_1} = 2{\tan ^{ - 1}}x.\frac{1}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex} (1 + x 2 )y 1 = 2tan -1 x Again differentiating both sides w.r.t to x {tex}\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right){y_2} + {y_1}\left( {2x} \right) = 2.\frac{1}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex} (1 + x 2 ) 2 y 2 + 2x(x 2 + 1)y 1 = 2
solution: y = (tan -1 x) 2 (given) Differentiating both sides w.r.t to x {tex}{y_1} = 2{\tan ^{ - 1}}x.\frac{1}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex} (1 + x 2 )y 1 = 2tan -1 x Again differentiating both sides w.r.t to x {tex}\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right){y_2} + {y_1}\left( {2x} \right) = 2.\frac{1}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex} (1 + x 2 ) 2 y 2 + 2x(x 2 + 1)y 1 = 2
solution_html:
y = (tan-1x)2 (given)

Differentiating both sides w.r.t to x

{tex}{y_1} = 2{\tan ^{ - 1}}x.\frac{1}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex}

(1 + x2)y1 = 2tan-1x

Again differentiating both sides w.r.t to x

{tex}\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right){y_2} + {y_1}\left( {2x} \right) = 2.\frac{1}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex}

(1 + x2)2 y2 + 2x(x2 + 1)y1 = 2

1 } t } \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } \frac { d } { d t } \left( a ^ { \sin ^ { - 1 } t } \right){/tex} [ Using chain rule of derivative] {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( a ^ { \mathrm { sin } ^ { - 1 } t } \right) ^ { - \frac { \sqrt { a ^ { \cos ^ { - 1 } t } } } { \sqrt { a ^ { \sin ^ { - 1 } t } } } = - \frac { y } { x }{/tex} Hence proved
solution: According to the question, x = {tex} \sqrt { a ^ { \sin ^ { - 1 } t} }{/tex} and y = {tex} \sqrt { a ^ { \cos ^ { - 1 } t}}{/tex} Consider, {tex}x= \left( a ^ { \sin ^ { - 1 } t } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 }{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d x } { d t } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( a ^ { \sin ^ { - 1 } t } \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } \frac { d } { d t } \left( a ^ { \sin ^ { - 1 } t } \right){/tex} [ Using chain rule of derivative] {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( a ^ { \mathrm { sin } ^ { - 1 } t } \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } a ^ { \sin ^ { - 1 } t } \log a \frac { d } { d t } \left( \sin ^ { - 1 } t \right){/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( a ^ { \sin ^ { - 1 } t } \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } a ^ { \sin ^ { - 1 } t } \log a \cdot \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - t ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( a ^ { \sin ^ { - 1 } t } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } \log a \cdot \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - t ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d x } { d t } = \frac { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sqrt { a ^ { \sin ^ { - 1 } t } } \cdot \log a } { \sqrt { 1 - t ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} ..........(i) Consider , {tex}y= \left( a ^ { \cos ^ { - 1 } t } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 }{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex} \frac { d y } { d t } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( a ^ { \cos ^ { - 1 } t } \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } \frac { d } { d t } \left( a ^ { \cos ^ { - 1 } t } \right){/tex} [Using chain rule of derivative] {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( a ^ { \cos ^ { - 1 } t } \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } a ^ { \cos ^ { - 1 } t } \log a \frac { d } { d t } \left( \cos ^ { - 1 } t \right){/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( a ^ { \cos ^ { - 1 } t } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } \log a \cdot \frac { ( - 1 ) } { \sqrt { 1 - t ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d t } = \frac { - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sqrt { a ^ { \cos ^ { - 1 } t } } \cdot \log a } { \sqrt { 1 - t ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} ..........(ii) Dividing Eq.(ii) by Eq.(i), {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{\left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}} \right)}}{{\left( {\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}} \right)}} = \frac{{\left( {\frac{{ - \frac{1}{2}\sqrt {{a^{{{\cos }^{ - 1}}t}}} \log a}}{{\sqrt {1 - {t^2}} }}} \right)}}{{\left( {\frac{{\frac{1}{2}\sqrt {{a^{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}t}}\log a} }}{{\sqrt {1 - {t^2}} }}} \right)}}{/tex} {tex} = - \frac { \sqrt { a ^ { \cos ^ { - 1 } t } } } { \sqrt { a ^ { \sin ^ { - 1 } t } } } = - \frac { y } { x }{/tex} Hence proved
solution_html:
According to the question,  x = {tex} \sqrt { a ^ { \sin ^ { - 1 } t} }{/tex} and y = {tex} \sqrt { a ^ { \cos ^ { - 1 } t}}{/tex}
Consider,  {tex}x= \left( a ^ { \sin ^ { - 1 } t } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 }{/tex}
Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d x } { d t } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( a ^ { \sin ^ { - 1 } t } \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } \frac { d } { d t } \left( a ^ { \sin ^ { - 1 } t } \right){/tex}[ Using chain rule of derivative]
{tex} = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( a ^ { \mathrm { sin } ^ { - 1 } t } \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } a ^ { \sin ^ { - 1 } t } \log a \frac { d } { d t } \left( \sin ^ { - 1 } t \right){/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( a ^ { \sin ^ { - 1 } t } \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } a ^ { \sin ^ { - 1 } t } \log a \cdot \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - t ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( a ^ { \sin ^ { - 1 } t } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } \log a \cdot \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - t ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d x } { d t } = \frac { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sqrt { a ^ { \sin ^ { - 1 } t } } \cdot \log a } { \sqrt { 1 - t ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}..........(i)
Consider , {tex}y= \left( a ^ { \cos ^ { - 1 } t } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 }{/tex}
Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, 
{tex} \frac { d y } { d t } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( a ^ { \cos ^ { - 1 } t } \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } \frac { d } { d t } \left( a ^ { \cos ^ { - 1 } t } \right){/tex} [Using chain rule of derivative]
{tex} = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( a ^ { \cos ^ { - 1 } t } \right) ^ { - 1 / 2 } a ^ { \cos ^ { - 1 } t } \log a \frac { d } { d t } \left( \cos ^ { - 1 } t \right){/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( a ^ { \cos ^ { - 1 } t } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } \log a \cdot \frac { ( - 1 ) } { \sqrt { 1 - t ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d t } = \frac { - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sqrt { a ^ { \cos ^ { - 1 } t } } \cdot \log a } { \sqrt { 1 - t ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}..........(ii)
Dividing Eq.(ii) by Eq.(i), 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{\left( {\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}} \right)}}{{\left( {\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}} \right)}} = \frac{{\left( {\frac{{ - \frac{1}{2}\sqrt {{a^{{{\cos }^{ - 1}}t}}} \log a}}{{\sqrt {1 - {t^2}} }}} \right)}}{{\left( {\frac{{\frac{1}{2}\sqrt {{a^{{{\sin }^{ - 1}}t}}\log a} }}{{\sqrt {1 - {t^2}} }}} \right)}}{/tex}
{tex} = - \frac { \sqrt { a ^ { \cos ^ { - 1 } t } } } { \sqrt { a ^ { \sin ^ { - 1 } t } } } = - \frac { y } { x }{/tex}
Hence proved

question_html:
Find the value of k so that the following function is continuous at x = 2.
f(x) = {tex} \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{{{x^3} + {x^2} - 16x + 20}}{{{{(x - 2)}^2}}},}&{x \ne 2} \\ {k,}&{x = 2} \end{array}} \right\}{/tex}
question_text: Find the value of k so that the following function is continuous at x = 2. f(x) = {tex} \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{{{x^3} + {x^2} - 16x + 20}}{{{{(x - 2)}^2}}},}&{x \ne 2} \\ {k,}&{x = 2} \end{array}} \right\}{/tex}
explanation: According to the question, f(x) = {tex} \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{{{x^3} + {x^2} - 16x + 20}}{{{{(x - 2)}^2}}},}&{x \ne 2} \\ {k,}&{x = 2} \end{array}} \right\}{/tex} is continuous at {tex}x = 2.{/tex} Now, we have f(2) = k {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 2 } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 2 } \frac { x ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 2 } - 16 x + 20 } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 2 } \frac { ( x - 2 ) \left( x ^ { 2 } + 3 x - 10 \right) } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 2 } \frac { ( x - 2 ) ( x + 5 ) ( x - 2 ) } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 2 } ( x + 5 ){/tex} = 2+ 5 = 7 f(x) is continuous at x = 2. {tex} \therefore \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 2 } f ( x ){/tex} = f(2) {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 7 = k {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} k = 7
solution: According to the question, f(x) = {tex} \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{{{x^3} + {x^2} - 16x + 20}}{{{{(x - 2)}^2}}},}&{x \ne 2} \\ {k,}&{x = 2} \end{array}} \right\}{/tex} is continuous at {tex}x = 2.{/tex} Now, we have f(2) = k {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 2 } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 2 } \frac { x ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 2 } - 16 x + 20 } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 2 } \frac { ( x - 2 ) \left( x ^ { 2 } + 3 x - 10 \right) } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 2 } \frac { ( x - 2 ) ( x + 5 ) ( x - 2 ) } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 2 } ( x + 5 ){/tex} = 2+ 5 = 7 f(x) is continuous at x = 2. {tex} \therefore \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 2 } f ( x ){/tex} = f(2) {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 7 = k {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} k = 7
solution_html:
According to the question,  f(x) = {tex} \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\frac{{{x^3} + {x^2} - 16x + 20}}{{{{(x - 2)}^2}}},}&{x \ne 2} \\ {k,}&{x = 2} \end{array}} \right\}{/tex} is continuous at {tex}x = 2.{/tex}
Now, we have f(2) = k
{tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 2 } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 2 } \frac { x ^ { 3 } + x ^ { 2 } - 16 x + 20 } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 2 } \frac { ( x - 2 ) \left( x ^ { 2 } + 3 x - 10 \right) } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 2 } \frac { ( x - 2 ) ( x + 5 ) ( x - 2 ) } { ( x - 2 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 2 } ( x + 5 ){/tex} = 2+ 5 = 7 
f(x) is continuous at x = 2.
{tex} \therefore \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 2 } f ( x ){/tex}= f(2) {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 7 = k{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex}k = 7

question_html: Find {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} of the function (cos x)y = (cos y)x.
question_text: Find {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} of the function (cos x) y = (cos y) x .
explanation: We have, (cos x) y = (cos y) x On taking log both sides, we get log(cos x) y = log(cos y) x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y log(cos x) = x log(cos y) On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex}y \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \log ( \cos x ) + \log \cos x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( y ){/tex} {tex}= x \frac { d } { d x } \log \left( \cos y) + \log ( \cos y ) \frac { d } { d x } ( x )\right.{/tex} [by using product rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \cdot \frac { 1 } { \cos x } \frac { d } { d x } ( \cos x ) + \log ( \cos x ) \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} {tex}= x \cdot \frac { 1 } { \cos y } \frac { d } { d x }{/tex} (cos y) + log cos y.1 {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \frac { 1 } { \cos x } ( - \sin x ) + \log ( \cos x ) \cdot \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} {tex}= x \cdot \frac { 1 } { \cos y } {/tex} (-sin y) {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} + log cos y.1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - y tanx + log(cos x) {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} =-x tan y {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} + log(cos y) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [ x tan y + log (cos x)] {tex}​​\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = log(cos y) + y tan x {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \log ( \cos y ) + y \tan x } { x \tan y + \log ( \cos x ) }{/tex}
solution: We have, (cos x) y = (cos y) x On taking log both sides, we get log(cos x) y = log(cos y) x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y log(cos x) = x log(cos y) On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex}y \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \log ( \cos x ) + \log \cos x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( y ){/tex} {tex}= x \frac { d } { d x } \log \left( \cos y) + \log ( \cos y ) \frac { d } { d x } ( x )\right.{/tex} [by using product rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \cdot \frac { 1 } { \cos x } \frac { d } { d x } ( \cos x ) + \log ( \cos x ) \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} {tex}= x \cdot \frac { 1 } { \cos y } \frac { d } { d x }{/tex} (cos y) + log cos y.1 {tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \frac { 1 } { \cos x } ( - \sin x ) + \log ( \cos x ) \cdot \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} {tex}= x \cdot \frac { 1 } { \cos y } {/tex} (-sin y) {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} + log cos y.1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - y tanx + log(cos x) {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} =-x tan y {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} + log(cos y) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} [ x tan y + log (cos x)] {tex}​​\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = log(cos y) + y tan x {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \log ( \cos y ) + y \tan x } { x \tan y + \log ( \cos x ) }{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, (cos x)y = (cos y)x

On taking log both sides, we get
log(cos x)y = log(cos y)x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y log(cos x) = x log(cos y)
On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get
{tex}y \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \log ( \cos x ) + \log \cos x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( y ){/tex}
{tex}= x \frac { d } { d x } \log \left( \cos y) + \log ( \cos y ) \frac { d } { d x } ( x )\right.{/tex} [by using product rule of derivative]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y \cdot \frac { 1 } { \cos x } \frac { d } { d x } ( \cos x ) + \log ( \cos x ) \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}{tex}= x \cdot \frac { 1 } { \cos y } \frac { d } { d x }{/tex}(cos y) + log cos y.1
{tex}\Rightarrow y \cdot \frac { 1 } { \cos x } ( - \sin x ) + \log ( \cos x ) \cdot \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}{tex}= x \cdot \frac { 1 } { \cos y } {/tex}(-sin y){tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} + log cos y.1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} - y tanx + log(cos x){tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} =-x tan y{tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}+ log(cos y)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}[ x tan y + log (cos x)]{tex}​​\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}= log(cos y) + y tan x
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \log ( \cos y ) + y \tan x } { x \tan y + \log ( \cos x ) }{/tex}

question_html: If x= a(cos t + t sin t) and y = a (sin t - t cos t), then find {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } }, \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d t ^ { 2 } }{/tex} and {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}.
question_text: If x= a(cos t + t sin t) and y = a (sin t - t cos t), then find {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } }, \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d t ^ { 2 } }{/tex} and {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} .
explanation: Given, x = a(cos t + t sin t) On differentiating both sides w.r.t t, we get {tex} \frac { d x } { d t } = a \left[ - \sin t + \frac { d } { d t } ( t ) \cdot \sin t + t \frac { d } { d t } ( \sin t ) \right]{/tex} [ by using product rule of derivative] {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d x } { d t }{/tex} = a(-sin t + 1. sin t + t cos t) = a t cos t.......(i) Also, given, y = a(sin t - t cos t) On differentiating both sides w.r.t t, we get {tex} \frac { d y } { d t } = a \left[ \cos t - \frac { d } { d t } ( t ) \cos t - t \frac { d } { d t } ( \cos t ) \right]{/tex} [ by using product rule of derivative] {tex} \frac { d y } { d t }{/tex} = a(cos t - cos t.1 + t sin t) = a t sin t..............(ii) Now, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \frac { d y } { d t } } { \frac { d x } { d t } } = \frac { a t \sin t } { a t \cos t }{/tex} = tan t [ From Eqs.(i) and (ii)] Again,differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) = \frac { d } { d t } ( \tan t ) \frac { d t } { d x }{/tex} {tex} = \sec ^ { 2 } t \frac { 1 } { d x / d t }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } t } { a t \cos t } = \frac { \sec ^ { 3 } t } { a t }{/tex} [ From Eq(i)] Also, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d t } ( a t \cos t ){/tex} {tex} = a \frac { d } { d t } ( t \cos t ){/tex} {tex} = a \left[ \frac { d } { d t } ( t ) \cdot \cos t + t \frac { d } { d t } ( \cos t ) \right]{/tex} [ by using product rule of derivative] = a[cos t - sin t] and {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d t ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { d y } { d t } \right) = \frac { d } { d t } ( a t \sin t ){/tex} = a(sin t + t cos t)
solution: Given, x = a(cos t + t sin t) On differentiating both sides w.r.t t, we get {tex} \frac { d x } { d t } = a \left[ - \sin t + \frac { d } { d t } ( t ) \cdot \sin t + t \frac { d } { d t } ( \sin t ) \right]{/tex} [ by using product rule of derivative] {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d x } { d t }{/tex} = a(-sin t + 1. sin t + t cos t) = a t cos t.......(i) Also, given, y = a(sin t - t cos t) On differentiating both sides w.r.t t, we get {tex} \frac { d y } { d t } = a \left[ \cos t - \frac { d } { d t } ( t ) \cos t - t \frac { d } { d t } ( \cos t ) \right]{/tex} [ by using product rule of derivative] {tex} \frac { d y } { d t }{/tex} = a(cos t - cos t.1 + t sin t) = a t sin t..............(ii) Now, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \frac { d y } { d t } } { \frac { d x } { d t } } = \frac { a t \sin t } { a t \cos t }{/tex} = tan t [ From Eqs.(i) and (ii)] Again,differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) = \frac { d } { d t } ( \tan t ) \frac { d t } { d x }{/tex} {tex} = \sec ^ { 2 } t \frac { 1 } { d x / d t }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } t } { a t \cos t } = \frac { \sec ^ { 3 } t } { a t }{/tex} [ From Eq(i)] Also, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d t } ( a t \cos t ){/tex} {tex} = a \frac { d } { d t } ( t \cos t ){/tex} {tex} = a \left[ \frac { d } { d t } ( t ) \cdot \cos t + t \frac { d } { d t } ( \cos t ) \right]{/tex} [ by using product rule of derivative] = a[cos t - sin t] and {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d t ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { d y } { d t } \right) = \frac { d } { d t } ( a t \sin t ){/tex} = a(sin t + t cos t)
solution_html:
Given, x = a(cos t + t sin t)

On differentiating both sides w.r.t t, we get
{tex} \frac { d x } { d t } = a \left[ - \sin t + \frac { d } { d t } ( t ) \cdot \sin t + t \frac { d } { d t } ( \sin t ) \right]{/tex}[ by using product rule of derivative]
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d x } { d t }{/tex} = a(-sin t + 1. sin t + t cos t) = a t cos t.......(i) 
Also, given, y = a(sin t - t cos t)
On differentiating both sides w.r.t t, we get
{tex} \frac { d y } { d t } = a \left[ \cos t - \frac { d } { d t } ( t ) \cos t - t \frac { d } { d t } ( \cos t ) \right]{/tex}[ by using product rule of derivative]
{tex} \frac { d y } { d t }{/tex} = a(cos t - cos t.1 + t sin t)
= a t sin t..............(ii)
Now, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \frac { d y } { d t } } { \frac { d x } { d t } } = \frac { a t \sin t } { a t \cos t }{/tex} = tan t [ From Eqs.(i) and (ii)]
Again,differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get
{tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) = \frac { d } { d t } ( \tan t ) \frac { d t } { d x }{/tex}{tex} = \sec ^ { 2 } t \frac { 1 } { d x / d t }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { \sec ^ { 2 } t } { a t \cos t } = \frac { \sec ^ { 3 } t } { a t }{/tex}[ From Eq(i)]
Also, {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d t } ( a t \cos t ){/tex}
{tex} = a \frac { d } { d t } ( t \cos t ){/tex}
{tex} = a \left[ \frac { d } { d t } ( t ) \cdot \cos t + t \frac { d } { d t } ( \cos t ) \right]{/tex}[ by using product rule of derivative]
= a[cos t - sin t]
and {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d t ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { d y } { d t } \right) = \frac { d } { d t } ( a t \sin t ){/tex}
= a(sin t + t cos t)
