question_html: If y=xsinx - cosx {tex}+\frac { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 }{/tex}, then find {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}.
question_text: If y=x sinx - cosx {tex}+\frac { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 }{/tex} , then find {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} .
explanation: Given y=x sinx - cosx {tex}+\frac { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 }{/tex} Let u = x sinx - cosx and v = {tex}\frac { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 }{/tex} Therefore, the given equations become, y = u + v Differentiating both sides w.r.t x,we get, {tex} \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d u } { d x } + \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} ..........(i) Consider, u = x sinx - cosx Therefore,on taking log both sides, we get, log u = (sin x - cos x). log x Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = ( \sin x - \cos x ) \cdot \frac { 1 } { x }{/tex} + log x.(cos x + sin x) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d u } { d x } = x ^ { \sin x - \cos x }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \frac { \sin x - \cos x } { x } + \log x \cdot ( \cos x + \sin x ) \right]{/tex} .......(ii) Now, consider v = {tex}\frac { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 } = 1 - \frac { 2 } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 }{/tex} Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { d v } { d x } = 0 - \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) \frac { d } { d x } ( 2 ) - 2 \frac { d } { d x } \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} [By using quotient rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d v } { d x } = - \left[ \frac { 0 - 2 \cdot 2 x } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) ^ { 2 } } \right] = \frac { 4 x } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ................(iii) Therefore,on substituting the values from Eqs.(ii) and (iii) to Eq.(i), we get, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = x ^ { \sin x - \cos x } \left[ \frac { \sin x - \cos x } { x } + \log x ( \cos x + \sin x ) \right]{/tex} {tex}+ \frac { 4 x } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
solution: Given y=x sinx - cosx {tex}+\frac { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 }{/tex} Let u = x sinx - cosx and v = {tex}\frac { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 }{/tex} Therefore, the given equations become, y = u + v Differentiating both sides w.r.t x,we get, {tex} \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d u } { d x } + \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} ..........(i) Consider, u = x sinx - cosx Therefore,on taking log both sides, we get, log u = (sin x - cos x). log x Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = ( \sin x - \cos x ) \cdot \frac { 1 } { x }{/tex} + log x.(cos x + sin x) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d u } { d x } = x ^ { \sin x - \cos x }{/tex} {tex}\left[ \frac { \sin x - \cos x } { x } + \log x \cdot ( \cos x + \sin x ) \right]{/tex} .......(ii) Now, consider v = {tex}\frac { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 } = 1 - \frac { 2 } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 }{/tex} Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { d v } { d x } = 0 - \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) \frac { d } { d x } ( 2 ) - 2 \frac { d } { d x } \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} [By using quotient rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d v } { d x } = - \left[ \frac { 0 - 2 \cdot 2 x } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) ^ { 2 } } \right] = \frac { 4 x } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ................(iii) Therefore,on substituting the values from Eqs.(ii) and (iii) to Eq.(i), we get, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = x ^ { \sin x - \cos x } \left[ \frac { \sin x - \cos x } { x } + \log x ( \cos x + \sin x ) \right]{/tex} {tex}+ \frac { 4 x } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
solution_html:
Given  y=xsinx - cosx {tex}+\frac { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 }{/tex}

Let u = xsinx - cosx and v = {tex}\frac { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 }{/tex}
Therefore, the given equations become,
y = u + v

Differentiating both sides w.r.t x,we get,
{tex} \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d u } { d x } + \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex}..........(i) 
Consider, u = xsinx - cosx
Therefore,on taking log both sides, we get,
log u = (sin x - cos x). log x
Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get,
{tex}\frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = ( \sin x - \cos x ) \cdot \frac { 1 } { x }{/tex}+ log x.(cos x + sin x)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d u } { d x } = x ^ { \sin x - \cos x }{/tex}{tex}\left[ \frac { \sin x - \cos x } { x } + \log x \cdot ( \cos x + \sin x ) \right]{/tex}.......(ii)
Now, consider v = {tex}\frac { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 } = 1 - \frac { 2 } { x ^ { 2 } + 1 }{/tex}
Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get,
{tex}\frac { d v } { d x } = 0 - \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) \frac { d } { d x } ( 2 ) - 2 \frac { d } { d x } \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}[By using quotient rule of derivative]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d v } { d x } = - \left[ \frac { 0 - 2 \cdot 2 x } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) ^ { 2 } } \right] = \frac { 4 x } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}................(iii)
Therefore,on substituting the values from Eqs.(ii) and (iii) to Eq.(i), we get,
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = x ^ { \sin x - \cos x } \left[ \frac { \sin x - \cos x } { x } + \log x ( \cos x + \sin x ) \right]{/tex} {tex}+ \frac { 4 x } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}

question_html: Find {tex} \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}, when y = {tex} x ^ { \cot x } + \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } - 3 } { x ^ { 2 } + x + 2 }{/tex}.
question_text: Find {tex} \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} , when y = {tex} x ^ { \cot x } + \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } - 3 } { x ^ { 2 } + x + 2 }{/tex} .
explanation: Given that, y = x cot x + {tex} \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } - 3 } { x ^ { 2 } + x + 2 }{/tex} . Let u = x cot x and v = {tex} \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } - 3 } { x ^ { 2 } + x + 2 }{/tex} Then, given equation becomes y = u + v Differentiating both sides w.r.t x,we get, {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d u } { d x } + \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} .........(i) Consider u = x cot x Therefore,on taking log both sides, we get, log u= cot x log x Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x,we get, {tex} \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = \cot x \cdot \frac { 1 } { x } - \text{cosec} ^ { 2 } x \cdot \log x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d u } { d x } = u \left( \frac { \cot x } { x } - \text{cosec} ^ { 2 } x \cdot \log x \right){/tex} {tex} = x ^ { \cot x } \left( \frac { \cot x } { x } - \text{cosec} ^ { 2 } x \cdot \log x \right){/tex} .........(ii) Now, consider v = {tex} \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } - 3 } { x ^ { 2 } + x + 2 }{/tex} Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x,we get, {tex} \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + x + 2 \right) ( 4 x ) - \left( 2 x ^ { 2 } - 3 \right) ( 2 x + 1 ) } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + x + 2 \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} [ By using quotient rule of derivative] {tex} = \frac { 4 x ^ { 3 } + 4 x ^ { 2 } + 8 x - 4 x ^ { 3 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } + 6 x + 3 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + x + 2 \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + 14 x + 3 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + x + 2 \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ....................(iii) Therefore,on substituting the values of Eqs.(ii) and (iii) to Eq.(i), we get, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = x ^ { \cot x } \left( \frac { \cot x } { x } - \text{cosec} ^ { 2 } x \cdot \log x \right){/tex} {tex} + \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + 14 x + 3 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + x + 2 \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
solution: Given that, y = x cot x + {tex} \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } - 3 } { x ^ { 2 } + x + 2 }{/tex} . Let u = x cot x and v = {tex} \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } - 3 } { x ^ { 2 } + x + 2 }{/tex} Then, given equation becomes y = u + v Differentiating both sides w.r.t x,we get, {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d u } { d x } + \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} .........(i) Consider u = x cot x Therefore,on taking log both sides, we get, log u= cot x log x Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x,we get, {tex} \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = \cot x \cdot \frac { 1 } { x } - \text{cosec} ^ { 2 } x \cdot \log x{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d u } { d x } = u \left( \frac { \cot x } { x } - \text{cosec} ^ { 2 } x \cdot \log x \right){/tex} {tex} = x ^ { \cot x } \left( \frac { \cot x } { x } - \text{cosec} ^ { 2 } x \cdot \log x \right){/tex} .........(ii) Now, consider v = {tex} \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } - 3 } { x ^ { 2 } + x + 2 }{/tex} Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x,we get, {tex} \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + x + 2 \right) ( 4 x ) - \left( 2 x ^ { 2 } - 3 \right) ( 2 x + 1 ) } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + x + 2 \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} [ By using quotient rule of derivative] {tex} = \frac { 4 x ^ { 3 } + 4 x ^ { 2 } + 8 x - 4 x ^ { 3 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } + 6 x + 3 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + x + 2 \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + 14 x + 3 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + x + 2 \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} ....................(iii) Therefore,on substituting the values of Eqs.(ii) and (iii) to Eq.(i), we get, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = x ^ { \cot x } \left( \frac { \cot x } { x } - \text{cosec} ^ { 2 } x \cdot \log x \right){/tex} {tex} + \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + 14 x + 3 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + x + 2 \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
solution_html:
Given that, y = xcot x + {tex} \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } - 3 } { x ^ { 2 } + x + 2 }{/tex}.
Let u = xcot x and v = {tex} \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } - 3 } { x ^ { 2 } + x + 2 }{/tex}
Then, given equation becomes
y = u + v

Differentiating both sides w.r.t x,we get,
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d u } { d x } + \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} .........(i)
Consider u = x cot x
Therefore,on taking log both sides, we get,
log u= cot x log x
Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t  x,we get,
{tex} \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = \cot x \cdot \frac { 1 } { x } - \text{cosec} ^ { 2 } x \cdot \log x{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d u } { d x } = u \left( \frac { \cot x } { x } - \text{cosec} ^ { 2 } x \cdot \log x \right){/tex}
{tex} = x ^ { \cot x } \left( \frac { \cot x } { x } - \text{cosec} ^ { 2 } x \cdot \log x \right){/tex}.........(ii)
Now, consider v = {tex} \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } - 3 } { x ^ { 2 } + x + 2 }{/tex}
Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t  x,we get,
{tex} \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + x + 2 \right) ( 4 x ) - \left( 2 x ^ { 2 } - 3 \right) ( 2 x + 1 ) } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + x + 2 \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}[ By using quotient rule of derivative]
{tex} = \frac { 4 x ^ { 3 } + 4 x ^ { 2 } + 8 x - 4 x ^ { 3 } - 2 x ^ { 2 } + 6 x + 3 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + x + 2 \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + 14 x + 3 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + x + 2 \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}....................(iii)
Therefore,on substituting the values of Eqs.(ii) and (iii) to Eq.(i), we get,
{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = x ^ { \cot x } \left( \frac { \cot x } { x } - \text{cosec} ^ { 2 } x \cdot \log x \right){/tex}{tex} + \frac { 2 x ^ { 2 } + 14 x + 3 } { \left( x ^ { 2 } + x + 2 \right) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}

question_html: If y = sin–1 x, show that (1 – x2) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex}.
question_text: If y = sin –1 x, show that (1 – x 2 ) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex} .
explanation: We have y = sin –1 x. Then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}{/tex} or {tex}\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)} \frac{d y}{d x}=1{/tex} So, {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}\right)=0{/tex} or {tex}\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)} \cdot \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{d y}{d x} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)})=0{/tex} or {tex}\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)} \cdot \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\frac{d y}{d x} \cdot \frac{2 x}{2 \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}=0{/tex} Hence {tex}\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex}
solution: We have y = sin –1 x. Then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}{/tex} or {tex}\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)} \frac{d y}{d x}=1{/tex} So, {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}\right)=0{/tex} or {tex}\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)} \cdot \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{d y}{d x} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)})=0{/tex} or {tex}\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)} \cdot \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\frac{d y}{d x} \cdot \frac{2 x}{2 \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}=0{/tex} Hence {tex}\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex}
solution_html:
We have y = sin–1x. Then 
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}{/tex}
or  {tex}\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)} \frac{d y}{d x}=1{/tex}
So,  {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}\right)=0{/tex}
or  {tex}\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)} \cdot \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{d y}{d x} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)})=0{/tex}
or  {tex}\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)} \cdot \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\frac{d y}{d x} \cdot \frac{2 x}{2 \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}=0{/tex}
Hence {tex}\left(1-x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-x \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex}

question_html: Find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex}, if {tex}{\left( {\cos x} \right)^y} = {\left( {\cos y} \right)^x}{/tex}
question_text: Find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} , if {tex}{\left( {\cos x} \right)^y} = {\left( {\cos y} \right)^x}{/tex}
explanation: Given: (cosx) y = (cosy) x {tex}\Rightarrow \log {\left( {\cos x} \right)^y} = \log {\left( {\cos y} \right)^x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y\log \cos x = x\log \cos y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {y\log \cos x} \right) = \frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {x\log \cos y} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y\frac{d}{{dx}}\log \cos x + \log \cos x\frac{dy}{{dx}} = x\frac{d}{{dx}}\log \cos y + \log \cos y\frac{d}{{dx}}x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y\frac{1}{{\cos x}}\frac{d}{{dx}}\cos x + \log \cos x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = x\frac{1}{{\cos y}}\frac{d}{{dx}}\cos y + \log \cos y.1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y\frac{1}{{\cos x}}\left( { - \sin x} \right) + \log \cos x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = x\frac{1}{{\cos y}}\left( { - \sin y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right) + \log \cos y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - y\tan x + \log \cos x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - x\tan y.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log \cos y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x\tan y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log \cos x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} {/tex} = y tan x + log cos y {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}\left( {x\tan y + \log \cos x} \right) {/tex} = y tan x + log cos y {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{y\tan x + \log \cos y}}{{x\tan y + \log \cos x}}{/tex}
solution: Given: (cosx) y = (cosy) x {tex}\Rightarrow \log {\left( {\cos x} \right)^y} = \log {\left( {\cos y} \right)^x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y\log \cos x = x\log \cos y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {y\log \cos x} \right) = \frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {x\log \cos y} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y\frac{d}{{dx}}\log \cos x + \log \cos x\frac{dy}{{dx}} = x\frac{d}{{dx}}\log \cos y + \log \cos y\frac{d}{{dx}}x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y\frac{1}{{\cos x}}\frac{d}{{dx}}\cos x + \log \cos x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = x\frac{1}{{\cos y}}\frac{d}{{dx}}\cos y + \log \cos y.1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y\frac{1}{{\cos x}}\left( { - \sin x} \right) + \log \cos x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = x\frac{1}{{\cos y}}\left( { - \sin y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right) + \log \cos y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow - y\tan x + \log \cos x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - x\tan y.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log \cos y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x\tan y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log \cos x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} {/tex} = y tan x + log cos y {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}\left( {x\tan y + \log \cos x} \right) {/tex} = y tan x + log cos y {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{y\tan x + \log \cos y}}{{x\tan y + \log \cos x}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: (cosx)y = (cosy)x
{tex}\Rightarrow \log {\left( {\cos x} \right)^y} = \log {\left( {\cos y} \right)^x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y\log \cos x = x\log \cos y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {y\log \cos x} \right) = \frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {x\log \cos y} \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y\frac{d}{{dx}}\log \cos x + \log \cos x\frac{dy}{{dx}} = x\frac{d}{{dx}}\log \cos y + \log \cos y\frac{d}{{dx}}x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y\frac{1}{{\cos x}}\frac{d}{{dx}}\cos x + \log \cos x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = x\frac{1}{{\cos y}}\frac{d}{{dx}}\cos y + \log \cos y.1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y\frac{1}{{\cos x}}\left( { - \sin x} \right) + \log \cos x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = x\frac{1}{{\cos y}}\left( { - \sin y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right) + \log \cos y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow - y\tan x + \log \cos x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - x\tan y.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log \cos y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x\tan y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + \log \cos x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} {/tex} = y tan x + log cos y
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}\left( {x\tan y + \log \cos x} \right) {/tex} = y tan x + log cos y
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{y\tan x + \log \cos y}}{{x\tan y + \log \cos x}}{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}y = {\left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x} \right)^2}{/tex} show that {tex}{\left( {{x^2} + 1} \right)^2}{y_2} + 2x\left( {{x^2} + 1} \right){y_1} = 2{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}y = {\left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x} \right)^2}{/tex} show that {tex}{\left( {{x^2} + 1} \right)^2}{y_2} + 2x\left( {{x^2} + 1} \right){y_1} = 2{/tex}
explanation: {tex}y = {\left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x} \right)^2}{/tex} (given) Differentiating both sides w.r.t to x, {tex}{y_1} = 2{\tan ^{ - 1}}x.\frac{1}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex} {tex}\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right){y_1} = 2{\tan ^{ - 1}}x{/tex} Again differentiating both sides w.r.t to x, {tex}\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right){y_2} + {y_1}.\left( {2x} \right) = 2.\frac{1}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex} {tex}{\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)^2}{y_2} + 2x\left( {{x^2} + 1} \right){y_1} = 2{/tex}
solution: {tex}y = {\left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x} \right)^2}{/tex} (given) Differentiating both sides w.r.t to x, {tex}{y_1} = 2{\tan ^{ - 1}}x.\frac{1}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex} {tex}\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right){y_1} = 2{\tan ^{ - 1}}x{/tex} Again differentiating both sides w.r.t to x, {tex}\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right){y_2} + {y_1}.\left( {2x} \right) = 2.\frac{1}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex} {tex}{\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)^2}{y_2} + 2x\left( {{x^2} + 1} \right){y_1} = 2{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}y = {\left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x} \right)^2}{/tex} (given)

Differentiating both sides w.r.t to x,

{tex}{y_1} = 2{\tan ^{ - 1}}x.\frac{1}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex}

{tex}\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right){y_1} = 2{\tan ^{ - 1}}x{/tex}

Again differentiating both sides w.r.t to x,

{tex}\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right){y_2} + {y_1}.\left( {2x} \right) = 2.\frac{1}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex}

{tex}{\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)^2}{y_2} + 2x\left( {{x^2} + 1} \right){y_1} = 2{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If y = ex cosx, prove that {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=2 e^{x} \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}
question_text: If y = e x cosx, prove that {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=2 e^{x} \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}
explanation: Given, y = e x cosx To prove: {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=2 e^{x} \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} We have, {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x} \cos x\right){/tex} Using product rule, we get; {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=e^{x} \frac{d}{d x}(\cos x)+\cos x \frac{d y}{d x} e^{x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-e^{x} \sin x+e^{x} \cos x{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \frac{d}{d x}(\cos x)=-\sin x \& \frac{d}{d x} e^{x}=e^{x}\right]{/tex} Again differentiating w.r.t x: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \sin \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \cos \mathrm{x}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(-e^{x} \sin x\right)+\frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x} \cos x\right){/tex} Again using the product rule: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\sin \mathrm{x})-\sin \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\cos \mathrm{x})+\cos \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}^{2}}{/tex} = -e x cosx - e x sinx - e x sinx + e x cosx {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -2e x sinx [ {tex}\because{/tex} -sinx = cos (x + {tex}\pi / 2{/tex} )] {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=-2 e^{x} \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} Hence proved
solution: Given, y = e x cosx To prove: {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=2 e^{x} \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} We have, {tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x} \cos x\right){/tex} Using product rule, we get; {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=e^{x} \frac{d}{d x}(\cos x)+\cos x \frac{d y}{d x} e^{x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-e^{x} \sin x+e^{x} \cos x{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \frac{d}{d x}(\cos x)=-\sin x \& \frac{d}{d x} e^{x}=e^{x}\right]{/tex} Again differentiating w.r.t x: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \sin \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \cos \mathrm{x}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(-e^{x} \sin x\right)+\frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x} \cos x\right){/tex} Again using the product rule: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\sin \mathrm{x})-\sin \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\cos \mathrm{x})+\cos \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}^{2}}{/tex} = -e x cosx - e x sinx - e x sinx + e x cosx {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -2e x sinx [ {tex}\because{/tex} -sinx = cos (x + {tex}\pi / 2{/tex} )] {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=-2 e^{x} \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} Hence proved
solution_html:
Given,
y = ex cosx
To prove:
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=2 e^{x} \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}
We have,
{tex} \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x} \cos x\right){/tex}
Using product rule, we get;
 {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=e^{x} \frac{d}{d x}(\cos x)+\cos x \frac{d y}{d x} e^{x}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-e^{x} \sin x+e^{x} \cos x{/tex} {tex}\left[\because \frac{d}{d x}(\cos x)=-\sin x \& \frac{d}{d x} e^{x}=e^{x}\right]{/tex}
Again differentiating w.r.t x:
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \sin \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \cos \mathrm{x}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(-e^{x} \sin x\right)+\frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{x} \cos x\right){/tex}
Again using the product rule:
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=-\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\sin \mathrm{x})-\sin \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\cos \mathrm{x})+\cos \mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}^{2}}{/tex} = -ex cosx - ex sinx - ex sinx + ex cosx
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -2ex sinx [{tex}\because{/tex} -sinx = cos (x + {tex}\pi / 2{/tex})]
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=-2 e^{x} \cos \left(x+\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}
Hence proved

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Differentiate the function with respect to x: {tex}x^{\cot x}+\frac{2 x^{2}-3}{x^{2}+x+2}{/tex}
question_text: Differentiate the function with respect to x: {tex}x^{\cot x}+\frac{2 x^{2}-3}{x^{2}+x+2}{/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}y=x^{\cot x}+\frac{2 x^{2}-3}{x^{2}+x+2}{/tex} {tex}y=e^{\cot x \log x}+\frac{2 x^{2}-3}{x^{2}+x+2} {/tex} {tex}\left[\because x^{\cot x}=e^{\log x^{\cot x}}=e^{\cot x \log x}\right]{/tex} On differentiating both sides with respect to x, we get {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{\cot x \log x}\right)+\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{2 x^{2}-3}{x^{2}+x+2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{\cot x \log x} \frac{d}{d x}(\cot x \cdot \log x)+\frac{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right) \frac{d}{d x}\left(2 x^{2}-3\right)-\left(2 x^{2}-3\right) \frac{d}{d x}\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)}{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=x^{\cot x}\left\{(\log x) \frac{d}{d x}(\cot x)+(\cot x) \frac{d}{d x}(\log x)\right\}+\frac{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)(4 x)-\left(2 x^{2}-3\right)(2 x+1)}{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=x^{\cot x}\left\{-\csc ^{2} x \cdot \log x+\frac{\cot x}{x}\right\}+\frac{2 x^{2}+14 x+3}{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)^{2}}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}y=x^{\cot x}+\frac{2 x^{2}-3}{x^{2}+x+2}{/tex} {tex}y=e^{\cot x \log x}+\frac{2 x^{2}-3}{x^{2}+x+2} {/tex} {tex}\left[\because x^{\cot x}=e^{\log x^{\cot x}}=e^{\cot x \log x}\right]{/tex} On differentiating both sides with respect to x, we get {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{\cot x \log x}\right)+\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{2 x^{2}-3}{x^{2}+x+2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{\cot x \log x} \frac{d}{d x}(\cot x \cdot \log x)+\frac{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right) \frac{d}{d x}\left(2 x^{2}-3\right)-\left(2 x^{2}-3\right) \frac{d}{d x}\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)}{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=x^{\cot x}\left\{(\log x) \frac{d}{d x}(\cot x)+(\cot x) \frac{d}{d x}(\log x)\right\}+\frac{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)(4 x)-\left(2 x^{2}-3\right)(2 x+1)}{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=x^{\cot x}\left\{-\csc ^{2} x \cdot \log x+\frac{\cot x}{x}\right\}+\frac{2 x^{2}+14 x+3}{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)^{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}y=x^{\cot x}+\frac{2 x^{2}-3}{x^{2}+x+2}{/tex}
{tex}y=e^{\cot x \log x}+\frac{2 x^{2}-3}{x^{2}+x+2} {/tex} {tex}\left[\because x^{\cot x}=e^{\log x^{\cot x}}=e^{\cot x \log x}\right]{/tex}
On differentiating both sides with respect to x, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{\cot x \log x}\right)+\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{2 x^{2}-3}{x^{2}+x+2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=e^{\cot x \log x} \frac{d}{d x}(\cot x \cdot \log x)+\frac{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right) \frac{d}{d x}\left(2 x^{2}-3\right)-\left(2 x^{2}-3\right) \frac{d}{d x}\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)}{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=x^{\cot x}\left\{(\log x) \frac{d}{d x}(\cot x)+(\cot x) \frac{d}{d x}(\log x)\right\}+\frac{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)(4 x)-\left(2 x^{2}-3\right)(2 x+1)}{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=x^{\cot x}\left\{-\csc ^{2} x \cdot \log x+\frac{\cot x}{x}\right\}+\frac{2 x^{2}+14 x+3}{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)^{2}}{/tex}

question_html: If y = sin-1 x, show that {tex}\left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - x \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex}.
question_text: If y = sin -1 x, show that {tex}\left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - x \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex} .
explanation: According to the question, {tex}y = sin^{-1}x{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } \Rightarrow \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \frac { d y } { d x } = 1{/tex} Squaring both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow (1 - x^2)-\left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 }=1{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow\left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) 2 \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) \left( \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } \right) + \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 } ( - 2 x ) = 0{/tex} Dividing both sides by 2 {tex}\left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right){/tex} , {tex}\Rightarrow\left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - x \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex} Hence Proved.
solution: According to the question, {tex}y = sin^{-1}x{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } \Rightarrow \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \frac { d y } { d x } = 1{/tex} Squaring both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow (1 - x^2)-\left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 }=1{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow\left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) 2 \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) \left( \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } \right) + \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 } ( - 2 x ) = 0{/tex} Dividing both sides by 2 {tex}\left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right){/tex} , {tex}\Rightarrow\left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - x \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex} Hence Proved.
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}y = sin^{-1}x{/tex}
Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, 
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } \Rightarrow \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } \frac { d y } { d x } = 1{/tex}
Squaring both sides, 
{tex}\Rightarrow (1 - x^2)-\left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 }=1{/tex} 
Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, 
{tex}\Rightarrow\left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) 2 \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) \left( \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } \right) + \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 } ( - 2 x ) = 0{/tex}
Dividing both sides by 2{tex}\left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right){/tex}, 
{tex}\Rightarrow\left( 1 - x ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - x \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex}
Hence Proved.

question_html: If y = (tan-1x)2 show that (x2 + 1)2y2 + 2x(x2 + 1)y1 = 2
question_text: If y = (tan -1 x) 2 show that (x 2 + 1) 2 y2 + 2x(x 2 + 1)y 1 = 2
explanation: Given: y = (tan -1 x) 2 ....(i) {tex}\therefore {y_1} = 2\left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x} \right)\frac{d}{{dx}}{\tan ^{ - 1}}x{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because \frac{d}{{dx}}{{\left\{ {f\left( x \right)} \right\}}^n} = n{{\left\{ {f\left( x \right)} \right\}}^{n - 1}}\frac{d}{{dx}}f\left( x \right)} \right]{/tex} And {tex}{y_1} = 2\left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x} \right)\frac{1}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{2{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x}}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( {1 + {x^2}} \right){y_1} = 2{\tan ^{ - 1}}x{/tex} Again differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, {tex}\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)\frac{d}{{dx}}{y_1} + {y_1}\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right) = 2.\frac{1}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( {1 + {x^2}} \right){y_2} + {y_1}.2x = \frac{2}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)^2\ {y_2} + 2x\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right) y_1= 2{/tex} .Hence proved.
solution: Given: y = (tan -1 x) 2 ....(i) {tex}\therefore {y_1} = 2\left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x} \right)\frac{d}{{dx}}{\tan ^{ - 1}}x{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because \frac{d}{{dx}}{{\left\{ {f\left( x \right)} \right\}}^n} = n{{\left\{ {f\left( x \right)} \right\}}^{n - 1}}\frac{d}{{dx}}f\left( x \right)} \right]{/tex} And {tex}{y_1} = 2\left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x} \right)\frac{1}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{2{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x}}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( {1 + {x^2}} \right){y_1} = 2{\tan ^{ - 1}}x{/tex} Again differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, {tex}\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)\frac{d}{{dx}}{y_1} + {y_1}\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right) = 2.\frac{1}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left( {1 + {x^2}} \right){y_2} + {y_1}.2x = \frac{2}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)^2\ {y_2} + 2x\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right) y_1= 2{/tex} .Hence proved.
solution_html:
Given: y = (tan-1x)2 ....(i)

{tex}\therefore {y_1} = 2\left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x} \right)\frac{d}{{dx}}{\tan ^{ - 1}}x{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because \frac{d}{{dx}}{{\left\{ {f\left( x \right)} \right\}}^n} = n{{\left\{ {f\left( x \right)} \right\}}^{n - 1}}\frac{d}{{dx}}f\left( x \right)} \right]{/tex}

And {tex}{y_1} = 2\left( {{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x} \right)\frac{1}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{2{{\tan }^{ - 1}}x}}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left( {1 + {x^2}} \right){y_1} = 2{\tan ^{ - 1}}x{/tex}

Again differentiating both sides w.r.t. x,

{tex}\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)\frac{d}{{dx}}{y_1} + {y_1}\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right) = 2.\frac{1}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left( {1 + {x^2}} \right){y_2} + {y_1}.2x = \frac{2}{{1 + {x^2}}}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \left( {1 + {x^2}} \right)^2\ {y_2} + 2x\left( {1 + {x^2}} \right) y_1= 2{/tex} .Hence proved.

question_html: If y = xcot x + {tex}\frac{2 x^{2}-3}{x^{2}+x+2}{/tex}, find {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}.
question_text: If y = x cot x + {tex}\frac{2 x^{2}-3}{x^{2}+x+2}{/tex} , find {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} .
explanation: Here, it is given that y = x cot x + {tex}\frac{2 x^{2}-3}{x^{2}+x+2}{/tex} y = u + v Let u = x cot x and let v = {tex}\frac{2 x^{2}-3}{x^{2}+x+2}{/tex} log u = cot x log x {tex}\frac{1}{u} \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\cot x \times \frac{1}{x}{/tex} + log x {tex}\times{/tex} (-cosec 2 x) {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}x^{\cot x}\left(\cot x \times \frac{1}{x}\right.{/tex} - log x {tex}\times{/tex} cosec 2 x) v = {tex}\frac{2 x^{2}-3}{x^{2}+x+2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right) \times 4 x-\left(2 x^{2}-3\right)(2 x+1)}{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4 x^{3}+4 x^{2}+8 x-\left(4 x^{3}+2 x^{2}-6 x-3\right)}{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4 x^{2}-2 x^{2}+8 x+6 x+3}{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 x^{2}+14 x+3}{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}x^{\cot x}\left(\cot x \times \frac{1}{x}\right.{/tex} - log x {tex}\times{/tex} cosec 2 x) + {tex}\frac{2 x^{2}+14 x+3}{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)^{2}}{/tex}
solution: Here, it is given that y = x cot x + {tex}\frac{2 x^{2}-3}{x^{2}+x+2}{/tex} y = u + v Let u = x cot x and let v = {tex}\frac{2 x^{2}-3}{x^{2}+x+2}{/tex} log u = cot x log x {tex}\frac{1}{u} \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\cot x \times \frac{1}{x}{/tex} + log x {tex}\times{/tex} (-cosec 2 x) {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}x^{\cot x}\left(\cot x \times \frac{1}{x}\right.{/tex} - log x {tex}\times{/tex} cosec 2 x) v = {tex}\frac{2 x^{2}-3}{x^{2}+x+2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right) \times 4 x-\left(2 x^{2}-3\right)(2 x+1)}{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4 x^{3}+4 x^{2}+8 x-\left(4 x^{3}+2 x^{2}-6 x-3\right)}{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4 x^{2}-2 x^{2}+8 x+6 x+3}{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 x^{2}+14 x+3}{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}x^{\cot x}\left(\cot x \times \frac{1}{x}\right.{/tex} - log x {tex}\times{/tex} cosec 2 x) + {tex}\frac{2 x^{2}+14 x+3}{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)^{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here, it is given that y = xcot x + {tex}\frac{2 x^{2}-3}{x^{2}+x+2}{/tex} 
y = u + v
Let u = xcot x and  let v = {tex}\frac{2 x^{2}-3}{x^{2}+x+2}{/tex} 
log u = cot x log x
{tex}\frac{1}{u} \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\cot x \times \frac{1}{x}{/tex} + log x {tex}\times{/tex} (-cosec2 x)
{tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}x^{\cot x}\left(\cot x \times \frac{1}{x}\right.{/tex} - log x {tex}\times{/tex} cosec2 x)
v = {tex}\frac{2 x^{2}-3}{x^{2}+x+2}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right) \times 4 x-\left(2 x^{2}-3\right)(2 x+1)}{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)^{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4 x^{3}+4 x^{2}+8 x-\left(4 x^{3}+2 x^{2}-6 x-3\right)}{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)^{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4 x^{2}-2 x^{2}+8 x+6 x+3}{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)^{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 x^{2}+14 x+3}{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)^{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}x^{\cot x}\left(\cot x \times \frac{1}{x}\right.{/tex}- log x {tex}\times{/tex} cosec2 x) + {tex}\frac{2 x^{2}+14 x+3}{\left(x^{2}+x+2\right)^{2}}{/tex}

question_html: Differentiate {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}-1}{\mathrm{x}}\right]{/tex}with respect to x.
question_text: Differentiate {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}-1}{\mathrm{x}}\right]{/tex} with respect to x.
explanation: Let, y = tan -1 {tex}\left[\frac{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}-1}{x}\right]{/tex} Put x = tan {tex}\beta{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = tan -1 {tex}\left[\frac{\sqrt{1+\tan ^{2} \beta}-1}{\tan \beta}\right]{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} 1 + tan 2 {tex}\beta{/tex} = sec 2 {tex}\beta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = tan -1 {tex}\left[\frac{\sec \beta-1}{\tan \beta}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = tan -1 {tex}\left[\frac{1-\cos \beta}{\sin \beta}\right]{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}1-\cos \beta=2 \sin ^{2} \frac{\beta}{2} \sin \beta=2 \frac{\sin \beta}{2} \frac{\cos \beta}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = tan -1 {tex}\left[\frac{2 \sin ^{2} \frac{\beta}{2}}{2 \sin \frac{\beta}{2} \cos \frac{\beta}{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = tan -1 (tan {tex}\frac{\beta}{2}{/tex} ) { {tex}\because{/tex} tan -1 (tan x) = x } {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac{\beta}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} tan -1 x Differentiating both sides - {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{1+x^{2}}=\frac{1}{2\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex} { {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)=\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} } {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex}
solution: Let, y = tan -1 {tex}\left[\frac{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}-1}{x}\right]{/tex} Put x = tan {tex}\beta{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = tan -1 {tex}\left[\frac{\sqrt{1+\tan ^{2} \beta}-1}{\tan \beta}\right]{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} 1 + tan 2 {tex}\beta{/tex} = sec 2 {tex}\beta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = tan -1 {tex}\left[\frac{\sec \beta-1}{\tan \beta}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = tan -1 {tex}\left[\frac{1-\cos \beta}{\sin \beta}\right]{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}1-\cos \beta=2 \sin ^{2} \frac{\beta}{2} \sin \beta=2 \frac{\sin \beta}{2} \frac{\cos \beta}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = tan -1 {tex}\left[\frac{2 \sin ^{2} \frac{\beta}{2}}{2 \sin \frac{\beta}{2} \cos \frac{\beta}{2}}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = tan -1 (tan {tex}\frac{\beta}{2}{/tex} ) { {tex}\because{/tex} tan -1 (tan x) = x } {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac{\beta}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} tan -1 x Differentiating both sides - {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{1+x^{2}}=\frac{1}{2\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex} { {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)=\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} } {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let, y = tan-1 {tex}\left[\frac{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}-1}{x}\right]{/tex}
Put x = tan {tex}\beta{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y = tan-1 {tex}\left[\frac{\sqrt{1+\tan ^{2} \beta}-1}{\tan \beta}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} 1 + tan2 {tex}\beta{/tex} = sec2 {tex}\beta{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = tan-1 {tex}\left[\frac{\sec \beta-1}{\tan \beta}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = tan-1 {tex}\left[\frac{1-\cos \beta}{\sin \beta}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex}{tex}1-\cos \beta=2 \sin ^{2} \frac{\beta}{2} \sin \beta=2 \frac{\sin \beta}{2} \frac{\cos \beta}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}y = tan-1 {tex}\left[\frac{2 \sin ^{2} \frac{\beta}{2}}{2 \sin \frac{\beta}{2} \cos \frac{\beta}{2}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y = tan-1 (tan {tex}\frac{\beta}{2}{/tex}) {{tex}\because{/tex} tan-1(tan x) = x }
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac{\beta}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} tan-1x
Differentiating both sides -
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{1+x^{2}}=\frac{1}{2\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex} {{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)=\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}{/tex}}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2\left(1+x^{2}\right)}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}y\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} = \log \left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x} \right){/tex}. Prove that {tex}\left( {{x^2} + 1} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + xy + 1 = 0{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}y\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} = \log \left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x} \right){/tex} . Prove that {tex}\left( {{x^2} + 1} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + xy + 1 = 0{/tex}
explanation: We have {tex}y\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} = \log \left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x} \right){/tex} Differentiating both sides, we get {tex}y.\frac{1}{{2\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}\left( {2x} \right) + \sqrt {{x^2} + 1} .\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} {/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x}}\left[ {\frac{{1\left( {2x} \right)}}{{2\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }} - 1} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{xy}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }} + \sqrt {{x^2} + 1} .\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x}}\left[ {\frac{{x - \sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow~\frac{{xy + \left( {{x^2} + 1} \right)}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} =\frac{{ - \left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x} \right)}}{{ \left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x} \right)\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow xy + \left( {{x^2} + 1} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left( {{x^2} + 1} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + xy + 1 = 0{/tex}
solution: We have {tex}y\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} = \log \left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x} \right){/tex} Differentiating both sides, we get {tex}y.\frac{1}{{2\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}\left( {2x} \right) + \sqrt {{x^2} + 1} .\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} {/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x}}\left[ {\frac{{1\left( {2x} \right)}}{{2\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }} - 1} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{xy}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }} + \sqrt {{x^2} + 1} .\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x}}\left[ {\frac{{x - \sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow~\frac{{xy + \left( {{x^2} + 1} \right)}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} =\frac{{ - \left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x} \right)}}{{ \left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x} \right)\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow xy + \left( {{x^2} + 1} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left( {{x^2} + 1} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + xy + 1 = 0{/tex}
solution_html:
We have
{tex}y\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} = \log \left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x} \right){/tex}

Differentiating both sides, we get {tex}y.\frac{1}{{2\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}\left( {2x} \right) + \sqrt {{x^2} + 1} .\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} {/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x}}\left[ {\frac{{1\left( {2x} \right)}}{{2\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }} - 1} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{xy}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }} + \sqrt {{x^2} + 1} .\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x}}\left[ {\frac{{x - \sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow~\frac{{xy + \left( {{x^2} + 1} \right)}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} =\frac{{ - \left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x} \right)}}{{ \left( {\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} - x} \right)\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow xy + \left( {{x^2} + 1} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - 1{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow\left( {{x^2} + 1} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} + xy + 1 = 0{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the value of a, if the function f(x) defined by f(x) ={tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { 2 x - 1 , } & { x < 2 } \\ { a , } & { x = 2 } \\ { x + 1 , } & { x > 2 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 2. Also, discuss the continuity of f(x) at x = 3.
question_text: Find the value of a, if the function f(x) defined by f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { 2 x - 1 , } & { x < 2 } \\ { a , } & { x = 2 } \\ { x + 1 , } & { x > 2 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 2. Also, discuss the continuity of f(x) at x = 3.
explanation: Given that f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { c l } { 2 x - 1 , } & { x < 2 } \\ { a , } & { x = 2 } \\ { x + 1 , } & { x > 2 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} (LHL) {tex}_{x=2}{/tex} = (RHL) {tex}_{x=2}{/tex} = f(2)..........(i) Now, f(2) = a and LHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 2 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 2 ^ { - } } ( 2 x - 1 ){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ 2 ( 2 - h ) - 1 ]{/tex} = 3 From Eq(i), we have LHL = f(2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 3 Now, let us check the continuity at x = 3. Consider, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 } ( x + 1 ){/tex} = 4 = f(3) {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) is continuous at x = 3.
solution: Given that f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { c l } { 2 x - 1 , } & { x < 2 } \\ { a , } & { x = 2 } \\ { x + 1 , } & { x > 2 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} (LHL) {tex}_{x=2}{/tex} = (RHL) {tex}_{x=2}{/tex} = f(2)..........(i) Now, f(2) = a and LHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 2 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 2 ^ { - } } ( 2 x - 1 ){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ 2 ( 2 - h ) - 1 ]{/tex} = 3 From Eq(i), we have LHL = f(2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 3 Now, let us check the continuity at x = 3. Consider, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 } ( x + 1 ){/tex} = 4 = f(3) {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) is continuous at x = 3.
solution_html:
Given that f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { c l } { 2 x - 1 , } & { x < 2 } \\ { a , } & { x = 2 } \\ { x + 1 , } & { x > 2 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex}(LHL){tex}_{x=2}{/tex} = (RHL){tex}_{x=2}{/tex} = f(2)..........(i)
Now, f(2) = a
and LHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 2 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 2 ^ { - } } ( 2 x - 1 ){/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ 2 ( 2 - h ) - 1 ]{/tex} = 3
From Eq(i), we have
LHL = f(2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 3
Now, let us check the continuity at x = 3.
Consider, {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 } ( x + 1 ){/tex}
= 4 = f(3) 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) is continuous at x = 3.

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Find the value of k so that the function f is continuous at the indicated point:

{tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} Kx + 1\,\,if\,x \leq \pi \, \hfill \\ \cos x\,\,if\,\,x > \pi \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}at {tex}x = \pi {/tex}
question_text: Find the value of k so that the function f is continuous at the indicated point: {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} Kx + 1\,\,if\,x \leq \pi \, \hfill \\ \cos x\,\,if\,\,x > \pi \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex} at {tex}x = \pi {/tex}
explanation: Left hand limit= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\pi ^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\pi ^ - }} \left( {Kx + 1} \right){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left[ {K\left( {\pi - h} \right) + 1} \right]{/tex} {tex}= K\pi + 1{/tex} Right hand limit = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\pi ^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\pi ^ + }} \cos x{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \cos \left( {\pi + h} \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} - \cos \,h{/tex} = -cos 0 = - 1 Therefore, {tex}K\pi + 1 = - 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow K = \frac{{ - 2}}{\pi }{/tex}
solution: Left hand limit= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\pi ^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\pi ^ - }} \left( {Kx + 1} \right){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left[ {K\left( {\pi - h} \right) + 1} \right]{/tex} {tex}= K\pi + 1{/tex} Right hand limit = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\pi ^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\pi ^ + }} \cos x{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \cos \left( {\pi + h} \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} - \cos \,h{/tex} = -cos 0 = - 1 Therefore, {tex}K\pi + 1 = - 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow K = \frac{{ - 2}}{\pi }{/tex}
solution_html:
Left hand limit= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\pi ^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\pi ^ - }} \left( {Kx + 1} \right){/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left[ {K\left( {\pi - h} \right) + 1} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= K\pi + 1{/tex}
Right hand limit = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\pi ^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {\pi ^ + }} \cos x{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \cos \left( {\pi + h} \right) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} - \cos \,h{/tex}
= -cos 0 = - 1
Therefore,
{tex}K\pi + 1 = - 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow K = \frac{{ - 2}}{\pi }{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Find the values of a and b such that the function defined by
f(x) = {tex}\begin{cases}5, & \text { if } x \leq 2 \\ a x+b, & \text { if } 2<x<10 \\ 21, & \text { if } x \geq 10\end{cases}{/tex} is a continuous function.
question_text: Find the values of a and b such that the function defined by f(x) = {tex}\begin{cases}5, & \text { if } x \leq 2 \\ a x+b, & \text { if } 2<x<10 \\ 21, & \text { if } x \geq 10\end{cases}{/tex} is a continuous function.
explanation: According to the question, f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { c c } { 5 , } & { x \leq 2 } \\ { a x + b , } & { 2 < x < 10 } \\ { 21 , } & { x \geq 10 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is a continuous function. So, it is continuous at x = 2 and at x = 10. {tex}\therefore{/tex} LHL x = 2 = RHL x = 2 = f(2) .....(i) and LHL x = 10 = RHL x = 10 = f(10) .....(ii) Now, let us calculate LHL and RHL at x = 2. LHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { - } } 5 = 5{/tex} and RHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { + } } ( a x + b ){/tex} {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \{ a ( 2 + h ) + b \} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } ( 2 a + a h + b ){/tex} = 2a + b {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 2a + b = 5.........(iii) Now, we have to find LHL and RHL at x = 10. LHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 10 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 10 ^ { - } } ( a x + b ){/tex} {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } [ a ( 10 - h ) + b ]{/tex} {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } ( 10 a - a h + b ){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} LHL = 10a + b and RHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 10 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 10 ^ { + } } 21{/tex} = 21 Now, from Eq. (ii), we have LHL= RHL {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}10 a + b = 21{/tex} ......(iv) Subtracting Equation (iv) from Equation (iii), {tex}\Rightarrow - 8a = -16{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}a = 2{/tex} Putting a = 2 in Equation (iv), 10 (2) + b = 21 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}b = 1{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}a = 2 \ and\ b = 1{/tex}
solution: According to the question, f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { c c } { 5 , } & { x \leq 2 } \\ { a x + b , } & { 2 < x < 10 } \\ { 21 , } & { x \geq 10 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is a continuous function. So, it is continuous at x = 2 and at x = 10. {tex}\therefore{/tex} LHL x = 2 = RHL x = 2 = f(2) .....(i) and LHL x = 10 = RHL x = 10 = f(10) .....(ii) Now, let us calculate LHL and RHL at x = 2. LHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { - } } 5 = 5{/tex} and RHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { + } } ( a x + b ){/tex} {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \{ a ( 2 + h ) + b \} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } ( 2 a + a h + b ){/tex} = 2a + b {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 2a + b = 5.........(iii) Now, we have to find LHL and RHL at x = 10. LHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 10 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 10 ^ { - } } ( a x + b ){/tex} {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } [ a ( 10 - h ) + b ]{/tex} {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } ( 10 a - a h + b ){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} LHL = 10a + b and RHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 10 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 10 ^ { + } } 21{/tex} = 21 Now, from Eq. (ii), we have LHL= RHL {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}10 a + b = 21{/tex} ......(iv) Subtracting Equation (iv) from Equation (iii), {tex}\Rightarrow - 8a = -16{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}a = 2{/tex} Putting a = 2 in Equation (iv), 10 (2) + b = 21 {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}b = 1{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}a = 2 \ and\ b = 1{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { c c } { 5 , } & { x \leq 2 } \\ { a x + b , } & { 2 < x < 10 } \\ { 21 , } & { x \geq 10 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is a continuous function. So, it is continuous at x = 2 and at x = 10.
{tex}\therefore{/tex}LHLx = 2 = RHLx = 2 = f(2)    .....(i)
and LHLx = 10 = RHLx = 10 = f(10)   .....(ii)
Now, let us calculate LHL and RHL at x = 2.
LHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { - } } 5 = 5{/tex}
and RHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { + } } ( a x + b ){/tex}
{tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \{ a ( 2 + h ) + b \} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } ( 2 a + a h + b ){/tex}
= 2a + b
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 2a + b = 5.........(iii)
Now, we have to find LHL and RHL at x = 10.
LHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 10 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 10 ^ { - } } ( a x + b ){/tex}
{tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } [ a ( 10 - h ) + b ]{/tex}
{tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } ( 10 a - a h + b ){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex}LHL = 10a + b
and RHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 10 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 10 ^ { + } } 21{/tex} = 21
Now, from Eq. (ii), we have
LHL= RHL
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}10 a + b = 21{/tex}   ......(iv)
Subtracting Equation (iv) from Equation (iii), 
{tex}\Rightarrow - 8a = -16{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}a = 2{/tex}
Putting a = 2 in Equation (iv), 
10 (2) + b = 21
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}b = 1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}a = 2 \ and\ b = 1{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If x = a ({tex}\theta{/tex} - sin {tex}\theta{/tex}), y = a (1 + cos {tex}\theta{/tex}), then find {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}.
question_text: If x = a ( {tex}\theta{/tex} - sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ), y = a (1 + cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ), then find {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} .
explanation: According to the question, x = a ( {tex}\theta{/tex} - sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ), y = a (1 + cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ) Differentiating both sides of x and y w.r.t {tex}\theta{/tex} , {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d x } { d \theta } = a ( 1 - \cos \theta ){/tex} and {tex}\frac { d y } { d \theta } = - a \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \left( \frac { d y } { d \theta } \right) } { \left( \frac { d x } { d \theta } \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { - a \sin \theta } { a ( 1 - \cos \theta ) } = \frac { - \sin \theta } { 1 - \cos \theta }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { - 2 \sin \frac { \theta } { 2 } \cos \frac { \theta } { 2 } } { 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { \theta } { 2 } } ={/tex} {tex}- \cot \frac { \theta } { 2 }{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } \left( - \cot \frac { \theta } { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( - \cot \frac { \theta } { 2 } \right) \times \frac { d \theta } { d x }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \text{cosec} ^ { 2 } \frac { \theta } { 2 } \times \frac { 1 } { a ( 1 - \cos \theta ) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 a } \text{cosec}^ { 2 } \frac { \theta } { 2 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { \theta } { 2 } } {/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 a } \text{cosec} ^ { 4 } \frac { \theta } { 2 }{/tex}
solution: According to the question, x = a ( {tex}\theta{/tex} - sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ), y = a (1 + cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ) Differentiating both sides of x and y w.r.t {tex}\theta{/tex} , {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d x } { d \theta } = a ( 1 - \cos \theta ){/tex} and {tex}\frac { d y } { d \theta } = - a \sin \theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \left( \frac { d y } { d \theta } \right) } { \left( \frac { d x } { d \theta } \right) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { - a \sin \theta } { a ( 1 - \cos \theta ) } = \frac { - \sin \theta } { 1 - \cos \theta }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { - 2 \sin \frac { \theta } { 2 } \cos \frac { \theta } { 2 } } { 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { \theta } { 2 } } ={/tex} {tex}- \cot \frac { \theta } { 2 }{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } \left( - \cot \frac { \theta } { 2 } \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( - \cot \frac { \theta } { 2 } \right) \times \frac { d \theta } { d x }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \text{cosec} ^ { 2 } \frac { \theta } { 2 } \times \frac { 1 } { a ( 1 - \cos \theta ) }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 a } \text{cosec}^ { 2 } \frac { \theta } { 2 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { \theta } { 2 } } {/tex} {tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 a } \text{cosec} ^ { 4 } \frac { \theta } { 2 }{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question,  x = a ({tex}\theta{/tex} - sin {tex}\theta{/tex}), y = a (1 + cos {tex}\theta{/tex})
Differentiating both sides of x and y w.r.t {tex}\theta{/tex},
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d x } { d \theta } = a ( 1 - \cos \theta ){/tex} and {tex}\frac { d y } { d \theta } = - a \sin \theta{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { \left( \frac { d y } { d \theta } \right) } { \left( \frac { d x } { d \theta } \right) }{/tex}{tex}= \frac { - a \sin \theta } { a ( 1 - \cos \theta ) } = \frac { - \sin \theta } { 1 - \cos \theta }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { - 2 \sin \frac { \theta } { 2 } \cos \frac { \theta } { 2 } } { 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { \theta } { 2 } } ={/tex}{tex}- \cot \frac { \theta } { 2 }{/tex}
Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } \left( - \cot \frac { \theta } { 2 } \right){/tex}
{tex}= \frac { d } { d \theta } \left( - \cot \frac { \theta } { 2 } \right) \times \frac { d \theta } { d x }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \text{cosec} ^ { 2 } \frac { \theta } { 2 } \times \frac { 1 } { a ( 1 - \cos \theta ) }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 a } \text{cosec}^ { 2 } \frac { \theta } { 2 } \times \frac { 1 } { 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { \theta } { 2 } } {/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 4 a } \text{cosec} ^ { 4 } \frac { \theta } { 2 }{/tex}

question_html: Find the value of a for which the function f is defined as f(x) ={tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { a \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } ( x + 1 ) , } & { x \leq 0 } \\ { \frac { \tan x - \sin x } { x ^ { 3 } } , } & { x > 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0.
question_text: Find the value of a for which the function f is defined as f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { a \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } ( x + 1 ) , } & { x \leq 0 } \\ { \frac { \tan x - \sin x } { x ^ { 3 } } , } & { x > 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0.
explanation: According to the question,we are given f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { a \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } ( x + 1 ) , } & { x \leq 0 } \\ { \frac { \tan x - \sin x } { x ^ { 3 } } , } & { x > 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0, and we have to find value of a. Therefore, LHL = RHL = f(0)....(i) Now, LHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { - } } a \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } ( x + 1 ){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { LHL } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } a \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } ( - h + 1 ){/tex} {tex} = a \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } = a{/tex} ..........(ii) Also,f(0) = {tex} a \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } {/tex} = a.......(iii) Now, we need to evaluate RHL at x = a [since {tex}{/tex} LHL = f(0) = a and from this,it is not possible to find the value of a] Now, RHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } \frac { \tan x - \sin x } { x ^ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad R H L = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \tan h - \sin h } { h ^ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \frac { \sin h } { \cos h } - \sin h } { h ^ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin h - \sin h \cos h } { h ^ { 3 } \cos h }{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin h (1 - \cos h ) } { h ^ { 3 } \cos h }{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin h } { h } \cdot \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 1 - \cos h } { h ^ { 2 } } \cdot \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 1 } { \cos h }{/tex} {tex} = 1 \times \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 1 - \cos h } { h ^ { 2 } } \times 1{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 1 - \cos h } { h ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { h } { 2 } } { h ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because 1 - \cos x = 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 2 \times \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { h } { 2 } } { \left( \frac { h ^ { 2 } } { 4 } \times 4 \right) }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \left( \frac { \sin \frac { h } { 2 } } { \frac { h } { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 1{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin x } { x } = 1 \right]{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { RHL } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} ..........(iv) Therefore, on substituting the values from Eqs. (ii), (iii) and (iv) to Eq. (i), we get, {tex} a = \frac { 1 } { 2 } = a{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow a = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}

Therefore, LHL = RHL = f(0)....(i) Now, LHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { - } } a \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } ( x + 1 ){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { LHL } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } a \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } ( - h + 1 ){/tex} {tex} = a \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } = a{/tex} ..........(ii) Also,f(0) = {tex} a \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } {/tex} = a.......(iii) Now, we need to evaluate RHL at x = a [since {tex}{/tex} LHL = f(0) = a and from this,it is not possible to find the value of a] Now, RHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } \frac { \tan x - \sin x } { x ^ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad R H L = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \tan h - \sin h } { h ^ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \frac { \sin h } { \cos h } - \sin h } { h ^ { 3 } }{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin h - \sin h \cos h } { h ^ { 3 } \cos h }{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin h (1 - \cos h ) } { h ^ { 3 } \cos h }{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin h } { h } \cdot \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 1 - \cos h } { h ^ { 2 } } \cdot \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 1 } { \cos h }{/tex} {tex} = 1 \times \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 1 - \cos h } { h ^ { 2 } } \times 1{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 1 - \cos h } { h ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { h } { 2 } } { h ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because 1 - \cos x = 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } \right]{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 2 \times \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { h } { 2 } } { \left( \frac { h ^ { 2 } } { 4 } \times 4 \right) }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \left( \frac { \sin \frac { h } { 2 } } { \frac { h } { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 1{/tex} {tex} \left[ \because \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin x } { x } = 1 \right]{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { RHL } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} ..........(iv) Therefore, on substituting the values from Eqs. (ii), (iii) and (iv) to Eq. (i), we get, {tex} a = \frac { 1 } { 2 } = a{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow a = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question,we are given f(x) ={tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { a \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } ( x + 1 ) , } & { x \leq 0 } \\ { \frac { \tan x - \sin x } { x ^ { 3 } } , } & { x > 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0, and we have to find value of a.
Therefore, LHL = RHL = f(0)....(i)
Now, LHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { - } } a \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } ( x + 1 ){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { LHL } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } a \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } ( - h + 1 ){/tex}
{tex} = a \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } = a{/tex}..........(ii)
Also,f(0) = {tex} a \sin \frac { \pi } { 2 } {/tex}= a.......(iii)
Now, we need to evaluate RHL at x = a [since{tex}{/tex} LHL = f(0) = a and from this,it is not possible to find the value of a]
Now, RHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } \frac { \tan x - \sin x } { x ^ { 3 } }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad R H L = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \tan h - \sin h } { h ^ { 3 } }{/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \frac { \sin h } { \cos h } - \sin h } { h ^ { 3 } }{/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin h - \sin h \cos h } { h ^ { 3 } \cos h }{/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin h (1 - \cos h ) } { h ^ { 3 } \cos h }{/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin h } { h } \cdot \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 1 - \cos h } { h ^ { 2 } } \cdot \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 1 } { \cos h }{/tex}
{tex} = 1 \times \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 1 - \cos h } { h ^ { 2 } } \times 1{/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 1 - \cos h } { h ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { h } { 2 } } { h ^ { 2 } }{/tex}{tex} \left[ \because 1 - \cos x = 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { x } { 2 } \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 2 \times \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { h } { 2 } } { \left( \frac { h ^ { 2 } } { 4 } \times 4 \right) }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \left( \frac { \sin \frac { h } { 2 } } { \frac { h } { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 1{/tex}{tex} \left[ \because \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin x } { x } = 1 \right]{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad \mathrm { RHL } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}..........(iv)
Therefore, on substituting the values from Eqs. (ii), (iii) and (iv) to Eq. (i), we get,
{tex} a = \frac { 1 } { 2 } = a{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow a = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}

question_html: For what values of {tex} \lambda{/tex} is the function f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { c } { \lambda \left( x ^ { 2 } - 2 x \right) , \text { if } x \leq 0 } \\ { 4 x + 1 , \text { if } x > 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex}is continuous at x = 0?
question_text: For what values of {tex} \lambda{/tex} is the function f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { c } { \lambda \left( x ^ { 2 } - 2 x \right) , \text { if } x \leq 0 } \\ { 4 x + 1 , \text { if } x > 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0?
explanation: If, f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \lambda \left( x ^ { 2 } - 2 x \right) , } & { \text { if } x \leq 0 } \\ { 4 x + 1 , } & { \text { if } x > 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0, We shall use definition of continuity to find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} . Since f(x) is continuous at x=0,therefore, {tex}{/tex} (LHL) x=0 = (RHL) x=0 = f(0).........(i) {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} f(0) = {tex}\lambda{/tex} [0-0] = 0, {tex}LHL= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f ( 0 - h ){/tex} {tex} = \lambda \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \left[ ( 0 - h ) ^ { 2 } - 2 ( 0 - h ) \right]{/tex} {tex} = \lambda \times 0 = 0{/tex} {tex}RHL= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f ( 0 + h ){/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } 4 ( 0 + h ){/tex} + 1 = 1 {tex} \because{/tex} LHL {tex} \ne{/tex} RHL, which is contradiction to Equation (i) {tex} \therefore{/tex} There is no value of {tex} \lambda{/tex} for which f(x) is continuous at x = 0.
solution: If, f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \lambda \left( x ^ { 2 } - 2 x \right) , } & { \text { if } x \leq 0 } \\ { 4 x + 1 , } & { \text { if } x > 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0, We shall use definition of continuity to find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} . Since f(x) is continuous at x=0,therefore, {tex}{/tex} (LHL) x=0 = (RHL) x=0 = f(0).........(i) {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} f(0) = {tex}\lambda{/tex} [0-0] = 0, {tex}LHL= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f ( 0 - h ){/tex} {tex} = \lambda \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \left[ ( 0 - h ) ^ { 2 } - 2 ( 0 - h ) \right]{/tex} {tex} = \lambda \times 0 = 0{/tex} {tex}RHL= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f ( 0 + h ){/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } 4 ( 0 + h ){/tex} + 1 = 1 {tex} \because{/tex} LHL {tex} \ne{/tex} RHL, which is contradiction to Equation (i) {tex} \therefore{/tex} There is no value of {tex} \lambda{/tex} for which f(x) is continuous at x = 0.
solution_html:
If,  f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \lambda \left( x ^ { 2 } - 2 x \right) , } & { \text { if } x \leq 0 } \\ { 4 x + 1 , } & { \text { if } x > 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0,

We shall use definition of continuity to find the value of {tex}\lambda{/tex}.

Since f(x) is continuous at x=0,therefore,
{tex}{/tex} (LHL)x=0 = (RHL)x=0 = f(0).........(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} f(0) = {tex}\lambda{/tex}[0-0] = 0,
{tex}LHL= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f ( 0 - h ){/tex}
{tex} = \lambda \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \left[ ( 0 - h ) ^ { 2 } - 2 ( 0 - h ) \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \lambda \times 0 = 0{/tex}
{tex}RHL= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } f ( 0 + h ){/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } 4 ( 0 + h ){/tex}+ 1 = 1
{tex} \because{/tex} LHL {tex} \ne{/tex} RHL, which is contradiction to Equation (i)
{tex} \therefore{/tex} There is no value of {tex} \lambda{/tex} for which f(x) is continuous at x = 0.

question_text: Discuss the continuity of the function f(x) at x = 1/2, when f(x) is defined as follows: f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 / 2 + x , } & { 0 \leq x < 1 / 2 } \\ { 1 , } & { x = 1 / 2 } \\ { 3 / 2 + x , } & { 1 / 2 < x \leq 1 } \end{array} \right.{/tex}
explanation: According to the question, f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 / 2 + x , } & { 0 \leq x < 1 / 2 } \\ { 1 , } & { x = 1 / 2 } \\ { 3 / 2 + x , } & { 1 / 2 < x \leq 1 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} Then, we have to check continuity of f(x) at {tex}x =\frac{ 1}{2}{/tex} We shall make use of the continuity of function to check whether f(x) is continuos at {tex}x=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} . Now, LHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 2 } ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 2 } ^ { - } } \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } + x \right){/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } - h \right)=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}=1{/tex} and RHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 2 } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 2 } ^ { + } } \left( \frac { 3 } { 2 } + x \right){/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \left( \frac { 3 } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } + h \right){/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( 2 + h ){/tex} = 2 {tex} \because{/tex} LHL {tex} \ne{/tex} RHL at x = 1/2. {tex} \therefore{/tex} {tex}f(x){/tex} is discontinuous at x = {tex} \frac 12{/tex}
solution: According to the question, f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 / 2 + x , } & { 0 \leq x < 1 / 2 } \\ { 1 , } & { x = 1 / 2 } \\ { 3 / 2 + x , } & { 1 / 2 < x \leq 1 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} Then, we have to check continuity of f(x) at {tex}x =\frac{ 1}{2}{/tex} We shall make use of the continuity of function to check whether f(x) is continuos at {tex}x=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} . Now, LHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 2 } ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 2 } ^ { - } } \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } + x \right){/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } - h \right)=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}=1{/tex} and RHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 2 } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 2 } ^ { + } } \left( \frac { 3 } { 2 } + x \right){/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \left( \frac { 3 } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } + h \right){/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( 2 + h ){/tex} = 2 {tex} \because{/tex} LHL {tex} \ne{/tex} RHL at x = 1/2. {tex} \therefore{/tex} {tex}f(x){/tex} is discontinuous at x = {tex} \frac 12{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, f(x)  = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 / 2 + x , } & { 0 \leq x < 1 / 2 } \\ { 1 , } & { x = 1 / 2 } \\ { 3 / 2 + x , } & { 1 / 2 < x \leq 1 } \end{array} \right.{/tex}
Then, we have to check continuity of f(x) at {tex}x =\frac{ 1}{2}{/tex}
We shall make use of the continuity of function to check whether f(x) is continuos at {tex}x=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}.
Now, LHL ={tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 2 } ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 2 } ^ { - } } \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } + x \right){/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } - h \right)=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}=1{/tex}
and RHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 2 } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 2 } ^ { + } } \left( \frac { 3 } { 2 } + x \right){/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } \left( \frac { 3 } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } + h \right){/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( 2 + h ){/tex} = 2
{tex} \because{/tex} LHL {tex} \ne{/tex} RHL at x = 1/2.
{tex} \therefore{/tex} {tex}f(x){/tex} is discontinuous at x ={tex} \frac 12{/tex}

question_html: If y = a sin x + b cos x, then prove that y2 +{tex}\left( \frac { d { y } } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex}= a2 + b2.
question_text: If y = a sin x + b cos x, then prove that y 2 + {tex}\left( \frac { d { y } } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex} = a 2 + b 2 .
explanation: Given, y {tex}^2{/tex} + {tex}\left( \frac { d { y } } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex} = a 2 + b 2 .......(i) We shall first find {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} and then use it in L.H.S of (i). Now, y = a sin x + b cos x..........(ii) Therefore,on differentiating both sides of Eq.(ii) w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = a cos x - b sin x Now, let us take LHS of Eq.(i), Here, LHS = y 2 + {tex}\left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex} Therefore,on putting the value of y and dy/dx, we get LHS = (a sin x + b cos x) 2 + ( a cos x - b sin x) 2 = a 2 sin 2 x + b 2 cos 2 x + 2ab sin x cos x + a 2 cos 2 x - b 2 sin 2 x +2ab sin x cos x = a 2 sin 2 x + b 2 cos 2 x + a 2 cos 2 x - b 2 sin 2 x = a 2 (sin 2 x + cos 2 x) + b 2 (sin 2 x + cos 2 x) = a 2 + b 2 = RHS
solution: Given, y {tex}^2{/tex} + {tex}\left( \frac { d { y } } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex} = a 2 + b 2 .......(i) We shall first find {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} and then use it in L.H.S of (i). Now, y = a sin x + b cos x..........(ii) Therefore,on differentiating both sides of Eq.(ii) w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = a cos x - b sin x Now, let us take LHS of Eq.(i), Here, LHS = y 2 + {tex}\left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex} Therefore,on putting the value of y and dy/dx, we get LHS = (a sin x + b cos x) 2 + ( a cos x - b sin x) 2 = a 2 sin 2 x + b 2 cos 2 x + 2ab sin x cos x + a 2 cos 2 x - b 2 sin 2 x +2ab sin x cos x = a 2 sin 2 x + b 2 cos 2 x + a 2 cos 2 x - b 2 sin 2 x = a 2 (sin 2 x + cos 2 x) + b 2 (sin 2 x + cos 2 x) = a 2 + b 2 = RHS
solution_html:
Given,  y{tex}^2{/tex}  +{tex}\left( \frac { d { y } } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex}= a2 + b2.......(i)

We shall first find {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} and then use it in L.H.S of (i).
Now, y = a sin x + b cos x..........(ii)
Therefore,on differentiating both sides of Eq.(ii) w.r.t x, we get,
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = a cos x - b sin x
Now, let us take LHS of Eq.(i),
Here, LHS = y2 + {tex}\left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) ^ { 2 }{/tex}
Therefore,on putting the value of y and dy/dx, we get
LHS = (a sin x + b cos x)2 + ( a cos x - b sin x)2
= a2 sin2 x + b2 cos2 x + 2ab sin x cos x  + a2 cos2 x - b2 sin2 x +2ab sin x cos x
= a2 sin2 x + b2 cos2 x + a2 cos2 x - b2 sin2 x
= a2 (sin2 x + cos2 x) + b2 (sin2x + cos2 x)
= a2 + b2
= RHS

question_html: If x = a ({tex} \theta {/tex} - sin {tex} \theta {/tex}) and y = a (1 + cos {tex} \theta {/tex}), then find {tex} \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} at {tex} \theta = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex}.
question_text: If x = a ( {tex} \theta {/tex} - sin {tex} \theta {/tex} ) and y = a (1 + cos {tex} \theta {/tex} ), then find {tex} \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} at {tex} \theta = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex} .
explanation: If x = a ( {tex} \theta {/tex} - sin {tex} \theta {/tex} ) and y = a (1 + cos {tex} \theta {/tex} ), then,we have to find find {tex} \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} at {tex} \theta = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex} . Now, x = a ( {tex} \theta {/tex} - sin {tex} \theta {/tex} ) and y = a (1 + cos {tex} \theta {/tex} ) On differentiating both sides w.r.t {tex} \theta{/tex} , we get {tex} \frac { d x } { d \theta } = a ( 1 - \cos \theta ){/tex} and {tex} \frac { d y } { d \theta } = - a \sin \theta{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d y / d \theta } { d x / d \theta } = \frac { - a \sin \theta } { a ( 1 - \cos \theta ) }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { - 2 a \sin \frac { \theta } { 2 } \cos \frac { \theta } { 2 } } { a \times 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { \theta } { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = - \cot \frac { \theta } { 2 }{/tex} On putting {tex} \theta = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex} , we get {tex} \left[ \frac { d y } { d x } \right] _ { \theta = \frac { \pi } { 3 } } = - \cot \frac { \pi } { 6 } = - \sqrt { 3 }{/tex} Hence, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } \text { at } \theta = \frac { \pi } { 3 } \text { is } - \sqrt { 3 }{/tex} .
