Now, x = a ( {tex} \theta {/tex} - sin {tex} \theta {/tex} ) and y = a (1 + cos {tex} \theta {/tex} ) On differentiating both sides w.r.t {tex} \theta{/tex} , we get {tex} \frac { d x } { d \theta } = a ( 1 - \cos \theta ){/tex} and {tex} \frac { d y } { d \theta } = - a \sin \theta{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d y / d \theta } { d x / d \theta } = \frac { - a \sin \theta } { a ( 1 - \cos \theta ) }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { - 2 a \sin \frac { \theta } { 2 } \cos \frac { \theta } { 2 } } { a \times 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { \theta } { 2 } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = - \cot \frac { \theta } { 2 }{/tex} On putting {tex} \theta = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex} , we get {tex} \left[ \frac { d y } { d x } \right] _ { \theta = \frac { \pi } { 3 } } = - \cot \frac { \pi } { 6 } = - \sqrt { 3 }{/tex} Hence, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } \text { at } \theta = \frac { \pi } { 3 } \text { is } - \sqrt { 3 }{/tex} .
solution_html:
If x = a ({tex} \theta {/tex} - sin {tex} \theta {/tex}) and y = a (1 + cos {tex} \theta {/tex}), then,we have to find find {tex} \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} at {tex} \theta = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex}.

Now, x = a ({tex} \theta {/tex} - sin {tex} \theta {/tex})
and y = a (1 + cos {tex} \theta {/tex})
On differentiating both sides w.r.t {tex} \theta{/tex}, we get
{tex} \frac { d x } { d \theta } = a ( 1 - \cos \theta ){/tex} and {tex} \frac { d y } { d \theta } = - a \sin \theta{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d y / d \theta } { d x / d \theta } = \frac { - a \sin \theta } { a ( 1 - \cos \theta ) }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { - 2 a \sin \frac { \theta } { 2 } \cos \frac { \theta } { 2 } } { a \times 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { \theta } { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = - \cot \frac { \theta } { 2 }{/tex}
On putting {tex} \theta = \frac { \pi } { 3 }{/tex}, we get
{tex} \left[ \frac { d y } { d x } \right] _ { \theta = \frac { \pi } { 3 } } = - \cot \frac { \pi } { 6 } = - \sqrt { 3 }{/tex}
Hence, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } \text { at } \theta = \frac { \pi } { 3 } \text { is } - \sqrt { 3 }{/tex}.

question_html: Find the relationship between a and b, so that the function f defined by f(x)= {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l } { a x + 1 , \text { if } x \leq 3 } \\ { b x + 3 , \text { if } x > 3 } \end{array} \right.{/tex}is continuous at x = 3.
question_text: Find the relationship between a and b, so that the function f defined by f(x)= {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l } { a x + 1 , \text { if } x \leq 3 } \\ { b x + 3 , \text { if } x > 3 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 3.
explanation: {tex}{/tex} According to the question,we have to find the relationship between a and b, so that the function f defined by f(x)= {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l } { a x + 1 , \text { if } x \leq 3 } \\ { b x + 3 , \text { if } x > 3 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 3. Therefore , LHL = RHL = f(3).........(i) Now, LHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { - } } ( a x + 1 ){/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ a ( 3 - h ) + 1 ]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} LHL = 3a + 1 and RHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { + } } ( b x + 3 ){/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ b ( 3 + h + 3 ]{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( 3 b + b h + 3 ){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} RHL = 3b + 3 From Eq. (i), we have LHL = RHL {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 3a + 1 = 3b + 3 Therefore, 3a - 3b = 2, which is the required relation between a and b.
solution: {tex}{/tex} According to the question,we have to find the relationship between a and b, so that the function f defined by f(x)= {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l } { a x + 1 , \text { if } x \leq 3 } \\ { b x + 3 , \text { if } x > 3 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 3. Therefore , LHL = RHL = f(3).........(i) Now, LHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { - } } ( a x + 1 ){/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ a ( 3 - h ) + 1 ]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} LHL = 3a + 1 and RHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { + } } ( b x + 3 ){/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ b ( 3 + h + 3 ]{/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( 3 b + b h + 3 ){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} RHL = 3b + 3 From Eq. (i), we have LHL = RHL {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} 3a + 1 = 3b + 3 Therefore, 3a - 3b = 2, which is the required relation between a and b.
solution_html:
{tex}{/tex}According to the question,we have to find the relationship between a and b, so that the function f defined by f(x)= {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l } { a x + 1 , \text { if } x \leq 3 } \\ { b x + 3 , \text { if } x > 3 } \end{array} \right.{/tex}is continuous at x = 3.

Therefore , LHL = RHL = f(3).........(i)
Now, LHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { - } } ( a x + 1 ){/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ a ( 3 - h ) + 1 ]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} LHL = 3a + 1
and RHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { + } } ( b x + 3 ){/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ b ( 3 + h + 3 ]{/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( 3 b + b h + 3 ){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} RHL = 3b + 3
From Eq. (i), we have
LHL = RHL{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex}  3a + 1 = 3b + 3
Therefore, 3a - 3b = 2, which is the required relation between a and b.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Differentiate the function with respect to x: {tex}x^{x \cos x}+\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex}
question_text: Differentiate the function with respect to x: {tex}x^{x \cos x}+\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}\mathrm{y}=x^{x \cos x}+\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} Also, Let u = x x cos x and {tex}\mathrm{v}=\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} ...(i) Now, u = x x cos x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = log (x x cos x ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = x cos x log x Differentiating both sides with respect to x, {tex}\frac{1}{u} \frac{d u}{d x}=\cos x \log x \frac{d}{d x}(x)+x \log x \frac{d}{d x}(\cos x)+x \cos x \frac{d}{d x}(\log x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=u\left[\cos x \log x+x(-\sin x) \log x+x \cos x\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = x x cos x (cos x log x - x sin x log x + cos x) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = x x cos x [cos x (1 + log x) - x sin x log x] ...(ii) Again, {tex}\mathrm{v}=\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log v = log(x 2 + 1) - log(x 2 - 1) Differentiating both sides with respect to x, {tex}\frac{1}{v} \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2 x}{x^{2}+1}-\frac{2 x}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=v\left[\frac{2 x\left(x^{2}-1\right)-2 x\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)\left(x^{2}-1\right)}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}\left[\frac{-4 x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)\left(x^{2}-1\right)}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-4 x}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} ...(iii) From (i), (ii) and (iii), we obtain {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = x x cos x [cos x(1 + log x) - x sin x log x] - {tex}\frac{4 x}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}\mathrm{y}=x^{x \cos x}+\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} Also, Let u = x x cos x and {tex}\mathrm{v}=\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} ...(i) Now, u = x x cos x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = log (x x cos x ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = x cos x log x Differentiating both sides with respect to x, {tex}\frac{1}{u} \frac{d u}{d x}=\cos x \log x \frac{d}{d x}(x)+x \log x \frac{d}{d x}(\cos x)+x \cos x \frac{d}{d x}(\log x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=u\left[\cos x \log x+x(-\sin x) \log x+x \cos x\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = x x cos x (cos x log x - x sin x log x + cos x) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = x x cos x [cos x (1 + log x) - x sin x log x] ...(ii) Again, {tex}\mathrm{v}=\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log v = log(x 2 + 1) - log(x 2 - 1) Differentiating both sides with respect to x, {tex}\frac{1}{v} \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2 x}{x^{2}+1}-\frac{2 x}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=v\left[\frac{2 x\left(x^{2}-1\right)-2 x\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)\left(x^{2}-1\right)}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}\left[\frac{-4 x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)\left(x^{2}-1\right)}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-4 x}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} ...(iii) From (i), (ii) and (iii), we obtain {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = x x cos x [cos x(1 + log x) - x sin x log x] - {tex}\frac{4 x}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}\mathrm{y}=x^{x \cos x}+\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex}
Also, Let u = xx cos x and {tex}\mathrm{v}=\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y = u + v
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} ...(i)
Now, u = xx cos x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = log (xx cos x)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = x cos x log x
Differentiating both sides with respect to x,
{tex}\frac{1}{u} \frac{d u}{d x}=\cos x \log x \frac{d}{d x}(x)+x \log x \frac{d}{d x}(\cos x)+x \cos x \frac{d}{d x}(\log x){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}=u\left[\cos x \log x+x(-\sin x) \log x+x \cos x\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = xx cos x (cos x log x - x sin x log x + cos x)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = xx cos x[cos x (1 + log x) - x sin x log x] ...(ii)
Again, {tex}\mathrm{v}=\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log v  =  log(x2 + 1) - log(x2 - 1)
Differentiating both sides with respect to x,
{tex}\frac{1}{v} \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{2 x}{x^{2}+1}-\frac{2 x}{x^{2}-1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=v\left[\frac{2 x\left(x^{2}-1\right)-2 x\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)\left(x^{2}-1\right)}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}\left[\frac{-4 x}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)\left(x^{2}-1\right)}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{-4 x}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} ...(iii)
From (i), (ii) and (iii), we obtain
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = xx cos x[cos x(1 + log x) - x sin x log x] - {tex}\frac{4 x}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex}

question_html: Find {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}, when: y = xx cos x + {tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}\right){/tex}.
question_text: Find {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} , when: y = x x cos x + {tex}\left(\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}\right){/tex} .
explanation: For that let us assume u = (x) x cos x and v = {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} u = (x) x cos x Taking log both sides log u = x cos x log x Differentiate both side with respect to x, {tex}\frac{1}{u} \times \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\cos x\left(x\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)+\ln x\right){/tex} + x log x (-2in x) {tex}\frac{du}{dx}{/tex} = (cos x (1 + log x) - x log x sin x) {tex}\times{/tex} u {tex}\frac{du}{dx}{/tex} = (cos (1 + log x) - x log x sin x) {tex}\times{/tex} (x cos x log x) ......(i) For v we do not have to take log just simple differentiate it, {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(x^{2}-1\right)(2 x)-\left(x^{2}+1\right)(2 x)}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} [using division rule] {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 x(-2)}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} .....(ii) from (i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = (cos x (1 + tan x) - x log x sin x) (x cos x log x) + {tex}\frac{2 x(-2)}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex}
solution: For that let us assume u = (x) x cos x and v = {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} u = (x) x cos x Taking log both sides log u = x cos x log x Differentiate both side with respect to x, {tex}\frac{1}{u} \times \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\cos x\left(x\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)+\ln x\right){/tex} + x log x (-2in x) {tex}\frac{du}{dx}{/tex} = (cos x (1 + log x) - x log x sin x) {tex}\times{/tex} u {tex}\frac{du}{dx}{/tex} = (cos (1 + log x) - x log x sin x) {tex}\times{/tex} (x cos x log x) ......(i) For v we do not have to take log just simple differentiate it, {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(x^{2}-1\right)(2 x)-\left(x^{2}+1\right)(2 x)}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} [using division rule] {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 x(-2)}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} .....(ii) from (i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = (cos x (1 + tan x) - x log x sin x) (x cos x log x) + {tex}\frac{2 x(-2)}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
For that let us assume u = (x)x cos x and v = {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} 
u = (x)x cos x 
Taking log both sides
log u = x cos x log x
Differentiate both side with respect to x,
{tex}\frac{1}{u} \times \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\cos x\left(x\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)+\ln x\right){/tex} + x log x (-2in x)
{tex}\frac{du}{dx}{/tex} = (cos x (1 + log x) - x log x sin x) {tex}\times{/tex} u
{tex}\frac{du}{dx}{/tex} = (cos (1 + log x) - x log x sin x) {tex}\times{/tex} (x cos x log x) ......(i)
For v we do not have to take log just simple differentiate it,
{tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(x^{2}-1\right)(2 x)-\left(x^{2}+1\right)(2 x)}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} [using division rule]
{tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 x(-2)}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} .....(ii) 
from (i) and (ii)
{tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = (cos x (1 + tan x) - x log x sin x) (x cos x log x) + {tex}\frac{2 x(-2)}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex}

question_html: If y = (x)x + (sin x)x, then find {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}.
question_text: If y = (x) x + (sin x) x , then find {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} .
explanation: Given, {tex} y = (x)^x + (sin x)^x{/tex} Let {tex}u = (x)^x{/tex} and {tex} v = (sin x)^x{/tex} Then, given equation becomes,y = u + v Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d u } { d x } + \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} .........(i) Consider, {tex}u = x^x{/tex} Taking log both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow log \ u = log x^x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow log\ u = x log x{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( \log x ) + \log x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( x ){/tex} [ using product rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = x \cdot \frac { 1 } { x } + log x . 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = 1 + log x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d u } { d x } = u( 1 + log x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d u } { d x } = x^x(1 + log x){/tex} ........(ii) Consider, v = (sin x) x Taking log both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow log v = log(sin x)^x{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { v } \frac { d v } { d x } = x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \log ( \sin x ) + \log ( \sin x ) \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( x ){/tex} [using product rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { v } \frac { d v } { d x } = x \cdot \frac { 1 } { \sin x } \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( \sin x ) + log \ sin x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { v } \frac { d v } { d x } = x \cdot \frac { 1 } { \sin x } \cdot \cos x + log\ sin x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { v } \frac { d v } { d x }= x cot x + log sin x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} = v(x cot x + log sin x) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} = (sin x) x (x cot x + log sin x).......(iii) Now, from Eqs.(i), (ii) and (iii), we get {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = x x (1 + log x) + (sin x) x (x cot x + log sin x).
solution: Given, {tex} y = (x)^x + (sin x)^x{/tex} Let {tex}u = (x)^x{/tex} and {tex} v = (sin x)^x{/tex} Then, given equation becomes,y = u + v Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d u } { d x } + \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} .........(i) Consider, {tex}u = x^x{/tex} Taking log both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow log \ u = log x^x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow log\ u = x log x{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( \log x ) + \log x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( x ){/tex} [ using product rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = x \cdot \frac { 1 } { x } + log x . 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = 1 + log x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d u } { d x } = u( 1 + log x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d u } { d x } = x^x(1 + log x){/tex} ........(ii) Consider, v = (sin x) x Taking log both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow log v = log(sin x)^x{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { v } \frac { d v } { d x } = x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \log ( \sin x ) + \log ( \sin x ) \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( x ){/tex} [using product rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { v } \frac { d v } { d x } = x \cdot \frac { 1 } { \sin x } \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( \sin x ) + log \ sin x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { v } \frac { d v } { d x } = x \cdot \frac { 1 } { \sin x } \cdot \cos x + log\ sin x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { v } \frac { d v } { d x }= x cot x + log sin x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} = v(x cot x + log sin x) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} = (sin x) x (x cot x + log sin x).......(iii) Now, from Eqs.(i), (ii) and (iii), we get {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = x x (1 + log x) + (sin x) x (x cot x + log sin x).
solution_html:
Given,{tex} y = (x)^x + (sin x)^x{/tex}
Let {tex}u = (x)^x{/tex}
and{tex} v = (sin x)^x{/tex}
Then, given equation becomes,y = u + v
Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, 
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d u } { d x } + \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex}.........(i)
Consider, {tex}u = x^x{/tex}
Taking log both sides, 
{tex}\Rightarrow log \ u = log x^x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow log\ u = x log x{/tex}
Differentiating both sides w.r.t x,
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( \log x ) + \log x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( x ){/tex}[ using product rule of derivative]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = x \cdot \frac { 1 } { x } + log x . 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = 1 + log x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d u } { d x } = u( 1 + log x){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d u } { d x } = x^x(1 + log x){/tex}........(ii)

 Consider, v = (sin x)x
Taking log both sides,
{tex}\Rightarrow log v = log(sin x)^x{/tex}
Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { v } \frac { d v } { d x } = x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \log ( \sin x ) + \log ( \sin x ) \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( x ){/tex}[using product rule of derivative]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { v } \frac { d v } { d x } = x \cdot \frac { 1 } { \sin x } \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( \sin x ) + log \ sin x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { v } \frac { d v } { d x } = x \cdot \frac { 1 } { \sin x } \cdot \cos x + log\ sin x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { v } \frac { d v } { d x }= x cot x + log sin x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex}= v(x cot x + log sin x)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} = (sin x)x(x cot x + log sin x).......(iii)
Now, from Eqs.(i), (ii) and (iii), we get
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = xx(1 + log x) + (sin x)x(x cot x + log sin x).

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If y = ex sin x, then prove that  {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 y = 0{/tex}.
question_text: If y = e x sin x, then prove that {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 y = 0{/tex} .
explanation: According to the question, if y = e x sin x, then,we have to prove that {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 y = 0{/tex} We shall use product rule of differentiation to prove the required result. Now,we have, y = e x sin x.....................(i) Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = e ^ { x } \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( \sin x ) + \sin x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \left( e ^ { x } \right){/tex} [by using product rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = e x .cos x + sin x.e x {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} =e x (cos x + sin x).........(ii) Again, differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = e ^ { x } \cdot \frac { d } { d x }{/tex} (cos x + sin x) + (cos x + sin x). {tex}\frac { d } { d x } \left( e ^ { x } \right){/tex} [by using product rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} = e x ( - sin x + cos x) + (cos x+ sin x). e x = e x [-sin x + cos x + cos x + sin x] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} = 2 cos xe x ...................(iii) Now, consider, LHS {tex}= \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 y{/tex} = 2e x cos x - 2e x (cos x + sin x) + 2e x sin x [From Eqs.(i), (ii) and (iii)] = 2e x cos x - 2e x cos x- 2e x sin x + 2e x sin x = 0 = RHS
solution: According to the question, if y = e x sin x, then,we have to prove that {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 y = 0{/tex} We shall use product rule of differentiation to prove the required result. Now,we have, y = e x sin x.....................(i) Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = e ^ { x } \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( \sin x ) + \sin x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \left( e ^ { x } \right){/tex} [by using product rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = e x .cos x + sin x.e x {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} =e x (cos x + sin x).........(ii) Again, differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = e ^ { x } \cdot \frac { d } { d x }{/tex} (cos x + sin x) + (cos x + sin x). {tex}\frac { d } { d x } \left( e ^ { x } \right){/tex} [by using product rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} = e x ( - sin x + cos x) + (cos x+ sin x). e x = e x [-sin x + cos x + cos x + sin x] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} = 2 cos xe x ...................(iii) Now, consider, LHS {tex}= \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 y{/tex} = 2e x cos x - 2e x (cos x + sin x) + 2e x sin x [From Eqs.(i), (ii) and (iii)] = 2e x cos x - 2e x cos x- 2e x sin x + 2e x sin x = 0 = RHS
solution_html:
According to the question,  if y = ex sin x, then,we have to prove that  {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 y = 0{/tex}

We shall use product rule of differentiation to prove the required result.
Now,we have, y = ex sin x.....................(i)
Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get,
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = e ^ { x } \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( \sin x ) + \sin x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \left( e ^ { x } \right){/tex}[by using product rule of derivative]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}= ex.cos x + sin x.ex
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} =ex(cos x + sin x).........(ii)
Again, differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get,
{tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = e ^ { x } \cdot \frac { d } { d x }{/tex}(cos x + sin x) + (cos x + sin x).{tex}\frac { d } { d x } \left( e ^ { x } \right){/tex}[by using product rule of derivative]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} = ex( - sin x + cos x) + (cos x+ sin x). ex
= ex[-sin x + cos x + cos x + sin x]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} = 2 cos xex...................(iii)
Now, consider,
LHS  {tex}= \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 y{/tex}
= 2ex cos x - 2ex(cos x + sin x) + 2ex sin x [From Eqs.(i), (ii) and (iii)]
= 2ex cos x - 2excos x-  2ex sin x + 2ex sin x
= 0 = RHS

question_html: If y = cos-1{tex} \left[ \frac { 2 x - 3 \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } { \sqrt { 13 } } \right]{/tex}, then find {tex} \frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}
question_text: If y = cos -1 {tex} \left[ \frac { 2 x - 3 \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } { \sqrt { 13 } } \right]{/tex} , then find {tex} \frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}
explanation: According to the question, y = cos -1 {tex} \left[ \frac { 2 x - 3 \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } { \sqrt { 13 } } \right]{/tex} Put x = sin {tex} \theta{/tex} , {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex} \theta{/tex} = sin -1 x {tex}\therefore \quad y = \cos ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 2 \sin \theta - 3 \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } } { \sqrt { 13 } } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \cos ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 2 \sin \theta - 3 \cos \theta } { \sqrt { 13 } } \right]{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } = \sqrt { \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } = \cos \theta \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y = \cos ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 13 } } \sin \theta - \frac { 3 } { \sqrt { 13 } } \cos \theta \right]{/tex} Now, let {tex} \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 13 } } = \cos \alpha{/tex} , then {tex} \frac { 3 } { \sqrt { 13 } } = \sin \alpha{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad y = \cos ^ { - 1 } [ \sin \theta \cos \alpha - \cos \theta \sin \alpha ]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y = \cos ^ { - 1 } [ \sin ( \theta - \alpha ) ]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y = \cos ^ { - 1 } \cos \left[ \frac { \pi } { 2 } - ( \theta - \alpha ) \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y = \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \theta + \alpha{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y = \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \sin ^ { - 1 } x + \alpha{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = 0 - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} + 0 {tex} \therefore \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { - 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
solution: According to the question, y = cos -1 {tex} \left[ \frac { 2 x - 3 \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } { \sqrt { 13 } } \right]{/tex} Put x = sin {tex} \theta{/tex} , {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex} \theta{/tex} = sin -1 x {tex}\therefore \quad y = \cos ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 2 \sin \theta - 3 \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } } { \sqrt { 13 } } \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \cos ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 2 \sin \theta - 3 \cos \theta } { \sqrt { 13 } } \right]{/tex} {tex}\left[ \because \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } = \sqrt { \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } = \cos \theta \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y = \cos ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 13 } } \sin \theta - \frac { 3 } { \sqrt { 13 } } \cos \theta \right]{/tex} Now, let {tex} \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 13 } } = \cos \alpha{/tex} , then {tex} \frac { 3 } { \sqrt { 13 } } = \sin \alpha{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad y = \cos ^ { - 1 } [ \sin \theta \cos \alpha - \cos \theta \sin \alpha ]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow y = \cos ^ { - 1 } [ \sin ( \theta - \alpha ) ]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y = \cos ^ { - 1 } \cos \left[ \frac { \pi } { 2 } - ( \theta - \alpha ) \right]{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y = \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \theta + \alpha{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad y = \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \sin ^ { - 1 } x + \alpha{/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = 0 - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex} + 0 {tex} \therefore \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { - 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, y = cos-1{tex} \left[ \frac { 2 x - 3 \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } } { \sqrt { 13 } } \right]{/tex}
Put x = sin {tex} \theta{/tex}, {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex} \theta{/tex} = sin-1x
{tex}\therefore \quad y = \cos ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 2 \sin \theta - 3 \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } } { \sqrt { 13 } } \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad y = \cos ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 2 \sin \theta - 3 \cos \theta } { \sqrt { 13 } } \right]{/tex}{tex}\left[ \because \sqrt { 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } = \sqrt { \cos ^ { 2 } \theta } = \cos \theta \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow y = \cos ^ { - 1 } \left[ \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 13 } } \sin \theta - \frac { 3 } { \sqrt { 13 } } \cos \theta \right]{/tex}
Now, let {tex} \frac { 2 } { \sqrt { 13 } } = \cos \alpha{/tex}, then {tex} \frac { 3 } { \sqrt { 13 } } = \sin \alpha{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad y = \cos ^ { - 1 } [ \sin \theta \cos \alpha - \cos \theta \sin \alpha ]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow y = \cos ^ { - 1 } [ \sin ( \theta - \alpha ) ]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad y = \cos ^ { - 1 } \cos \left[ \frac { \pi } { 2 } - ( \theta - \alpha ) \right]{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad y = \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \theta + \alpha{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad y = \frac { \pi } { 2 } - \sin ^ { - 1 } x + \alpha{/tex}
Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = 0 - \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}+ 0
{tex} \therefore \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { - 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - x ^ { 2 } } }{/tex}

question_html: For what value of k, is the function defined by f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l } { k \left( x ^ { 2 } + 2 \right) , \text { if } x \leq 0 } \\ { 3 x + 1 , \quad \text { if } x > 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} continuous at x = 0? Also, find whether the function is continuous at x = 1.
question_text: For what value of k, is the function defined by f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l } { k \left( x ^ { 2 } + 2 \right) , \text { if } x \leq 0 } \\ { 3 x + 1 , \quad \text { if } x > 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} continuous at x = 0? Also, find whether the function is continuous at x = 1.
explanation: Let f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{aligned} k \left( x ^ { 2 } + 2 \right) , & \text { if } x \leq 0 \\ 3 x + 1 , & \text { if } x > 0 \end{aligned} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0. Then, LHL = RHL = f(0).......(i) Here, LHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { - } } k \left( x ^ { 2 } + 2 \right){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } k \left[ ( 0 - h ) ^ { 2 } + 2 \right] {/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } k \left( h ^ { 2 } + 2 \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} LHL = 2k and RHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } ( 3 x + 1 ){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ 3 ( 0 + h ) + 1 ]{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( 3 h + 1 ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} RHL = 1 From Eq. (i), we have LHL=RHL {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2k = 1 {tex}\therefore \quad k = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} Now, let us check the continuity of the given function f(x) at x = 1. Consider, {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 1 } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 1 } (3 x + 1){/tex} = 4 = f(1) Therefore, f(x) is continuous at x = 1.
solution: Let f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{aligned} k \left( x ^ { 2 } + 2 \right) , & \text { if } x \leq 0 \\ 3 x + 1 , & \text { if } x > 0 \end{aligned} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0. Then, LHL = RHL = f(0).......(i) Here, LHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { - } } k \left( x ^ { 2 } + 2 \right){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } k \left[ ( 0 - h ) ^ { 2 } + 2 \right] {/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } k \left( h ^ { 2 } + 2 \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} LHL = 2k and RHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } ( 3 x + 1 ){/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ 3 ( 0 + h ) + 1 ]{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( 3 h + 1 ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} RHL = 1 From Eq. (i), we have LHL=RHL {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2k = 1 {tex}\therefore \quad k = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} Now, let us check the continuity of the given function f(x) at x = 1. Consider, {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 1 } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 1 } (3 x + 1){/tex} = 4 = f(1) Therefore, f(x) is continuous at x = 1.
solution_html:
Let f(x) ={tex} \left\{ \begin{aligned} k \left( x ^ { 2 } + 2 \right) , & \text { if } x \leq 0 \\ 3 x + 1 , & \text { if } x > 0 \end{aligned} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0.
Then, LHL = RHL = f(0).......(i)
Here, LHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { - } } k \left( x ^ { 2 } + 2 \right){/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } k \left[ ( 0 - h ) ^ { 2 } + 2 \right] {/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } k \left( h ^ { 2 } + 2 \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} LHL = 2k
and RHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } ( 3 x + 1 ){/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ 3 ( 0 + h ) + 1 ]{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( 3 h + 1 ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} RHL = 1

From Eq. (i), we have
LHL=RHL
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}2k = 1
{tex}\therefore \quad k = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
 Now, let us check the continuity of the given function f(x) at x = 1.
Consider, {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 1 } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 1 } (3 x + 1){/tex}
= 4 = f(1)
Therefore, f(x) is continuous at x = 1.

question_html: Differentiate the function (sin x – cos x(sin x – cos x), {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}<x<\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex} w.r.t to x.
question_text: Differentiate the function (sin x – cos x (sin x – cos x) , {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}<x<\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex} w.r.t to x.
explanation: We have, y = (sin x - cos x) (sin x - cos x) , {tex} \frac { \pi } { 4 } < x < \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} , Therefore, on taking logarithm both sides, we get, log y = log (sin x - cos x) (sin x - cos x) , {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} log y= (sin x - cos x). log(sin x - cos x) Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex} \frac { 1 } { y } \cdot \frac { d y } { d x } = ( \sin x - \cos x ) \times{/tex} {tex} \frac { d } { d x } \log ( \sin x - \cos x ){/tex} + log(sin x - cos x) {tex} \times\frac { d } { d x } ( \sin x - \cos x ){/tex} [By using product rule of derivative] {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { y } \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = (sin x - cos x) {tex} \frac { 1 } { ( \sin x - \cos x ) }{/tex} {tex} \frac { d } { d x } ( \sin x - \cos x ){/tex} + log(sin x - cos x).(cos x + sin x) {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { y } \frac { d y } { d x } = ( \sin x - \cos x ) \frac { 1 } { ( \sin x - \cos x ) }{/tex} (cos x + sin x) + log(sin x - cos x).(cos x + sin x) {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { y } \frac { d y } { d x } = ( \cos x + \sin x ){/tex} +(cos x + sin x) + log(sin x - cos x) {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = y(cos x + sin x)[ 1+log(sin x - cos x)] {tex} \therefore \quad \frac { d y } { d x } {/tex} = (sin x - cos x) (sin x - cos x) (cos x + sin x)[ 1+log(sin x - cos x)]
solution: We have, y = (sin x - cos x) (sin x - cos x) , {tex} \frac { \pi } { 4 } < x < \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 }{/tex} , Therefore, on taking logarithm both sides, we get, log y = log (sin x - cos x) (sin x - cos x) , {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} log y= (sin x - cos x). log(sin x - cos x) Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex} \frac { 1 } { y } \cdot \frac { d y } { d x } = ( \sin x - \cos x ) \times{/tex} {tex} \frac { d } { d x } \log ( \sin x - \cos x ){/tex} + log(sin x - cos x) {tex} \times\frac { d } { d x } ( \sin x - \cos x ){/tex} [By using product rule of derivative] {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { y } \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = (sin x - cos x) {tex} \frac { 1 } { ( \sin x - \cos x ) }{/tex} {tex} \frac { d } { d x } ( \sin x - \cos x ){/tex} + log(sin x - cos x).(cos x + sin x) {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { y } \frac { d y } { d x } = ( \sin x - \cos x ) \frac { 1 } { ( \sin x - \cos x ) }{/tex} (cos x + sin x) + log(sin x - cos x).(cos x + sin x) {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { y } \frac { d y } { d x } = ( \cos x + \sin x ){/tex} +(cos x + sin x) + log(sin x - cos x) {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = y(cos x + sin x)[ 1+log(sin x - cos x)] {tex} \therefore \quad \frac { d y } { d x } {/tex} = (sin x - cos x) (sin x - cos x) (cos x + sin x)[ 1+log(sin x - cos x)]
solution_html:
We have, y = (sin x - cos x)(sin x - cos x), {tex} \frac { \pi } { 4 } < x < \frac { 3 \pi } { 4 }{/tex},

Therefore, on taking logarithm both sides, we get,
log y = log (sin x - cos x)(sin x - cos x),
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} log y= (sin x - cos x). log(sin x - cos x)
Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get,
{tex} \frac { 1 } { y } \cdot \frac { d y } { d x } = ( \sin x - \cos x ) \times{/tex}{tex} \frac { d } { d x } \log ( \sin x - \cos x ){/tex}+ log(sin x - cos x){tex} \times\frac { d } { d x } ( \sin x - \cos x ){/tex}[By using product rule of derivative]
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { y } \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}= (sin x - cos x){tex} \frac { 1 } { ( \sin x - \cos x ) }{/tex}{tex} \frac { d } { d x } ( \sin x - \cos x ){/tex}+ log(sin x - cos x).(cos x + sin x)
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { y } \frac { d y } { d x } = ( \sin x - \cos x ) \frac { 1 } { ( \sin x - \cos x ) }{/tex}(cos x + sin x) + log(sin x - cos x).(cos x + sin x)
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { 1 } { y } \frac { d y } { d x } = ( \cos x + \sin x ){/tex}+(cos x + sin x) + log(sin x - cos x)
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}= y(cos x + sin x)[ 1+log(sin x - cos x)]
{tex} \therefore \quad \frac { d y } { d x } {/tex}= (sin x - cos x)(sin x - cos x)(cos x + sin x)[ 1+log(sin x - cos x)]

explanation: According to the question,if y = cosec -1 x, x> 1, then we have to show that {tex} x \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \left( 2 x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex} . Now, we have , y= cosec -1 x Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { - 1 } { x \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } \cdot \frac { d y } { d x } = - 1{/tex} Again,differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get, {tex} \left( x \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } \right) \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) +{/tex} {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \left( x \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } ( - 1 ){/tex} [ using product rule of derivative] {tex} \Rightarrow x \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } \cdot \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } +{/tex} {tex} \frac { d y } { d x } \left\{ x \times \frac { d } { d x } \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 }\right.{/tex} {tex} + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } \times \frac { d } { d x } ( x ) \} = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } +{/tex} {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } \left\{ \frac { x } { 2 \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } } \frac { d } { d x } \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right)\right.{/tex} {tex} + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } \times 1 \} = 0{/tex} [ using chain rule of derivative] {tex} \Rightarrow x \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { d y } { d x } \left\{ \frac { x \cdot 2 x } { 2 \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } } + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } \right\} = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { d y } { d x } \left\{ \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } { \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } } \right\} = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \left( x ^ { 2 } + x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex} {tex} \therefore \quad x \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \left( 2 x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex}

1 } } + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } \right\} = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad x \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { d y } { d x } \left\{ \frac { x ^ { 2 } + x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \left( 2 x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question,if y = cosec-1x, x> 1, then  we have  to show that {tex} x \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \left( 2 x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex}.

Now, we have , y= cosec-1x
Therefore,on differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get,
{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { - 1 } { x \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } \cdot \frac { d y } { d x } = - 1{/tex}
Again,differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get,
{tex} \left( x \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } \right) \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { d y } { d x } \right) +{/tex}{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \left( x \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } \right) = \frac { d } { d x } ( - 1 ){/tex}[ using product rule of derivative]
{tex} \Rightarrow x \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } \cdot \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } +{/tex}{tex} \frac { d y } { d x } \left\{ x \times \frac { d } { d x } \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 }\right.{/tex}{tex} + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } \times \frac { d } { d x } ( x ) \} = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } +{/tex}{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } \left\{ \frac { x } { 2 \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } } \frac { d } { d x } \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right)\right.{/tex}{tex} + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } \times 1 \} = 0{/tex}[ using chain rule of derivative]
{tex} \Rightarrow x \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { d y } { d x } \left\{ \frac { x \cdot 2 x } { 2 \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } } + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } \right\} = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { d y } { d x } \left\{ \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } } + \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } \right\} = 0{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { d y } { d x } \left\{ \frac { x ^ { 2 } + x ^ { 2 } - 1 } { \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } - 1 } } \right\} = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad x \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \left( x ^ { 2 } + x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex}
{tex} \therefore \quad x \left( x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \left( 2 x ^ { 2 } - 1 \right) \frac { d y } { d x } = 0{/tex}

question_html: Find {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex}, If y = (cos x)x + (sin x)1/x.
question_text: Find {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} , If y = (cos x) x + (sin x) 1/x .
explanation: Given, {tex} y = (cos x)^x + (sin x)^{1/x}{/tex} Let {tex}u = (cos x)^x\ \ and\ \ v = (sin x)^{1/x}{/tex} Then, given equation becomes y = u + v Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d u } { d x } + \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} .......(i) Taking log both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow log u = log(cos x)^x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow log u = x log(cos x){/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \log ( \cos x ) + \log ( \cos x ) \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( x ){/tex} [ using product rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = x \cdot \frac { 1 } { \cos x } (- sin x) + log \ cos x.1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = - x tan x + log(cos x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d u } { d x }= u[-x \ tan x + log \ cos x]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d u } { d x } = (cos x)^x[-x\ tan x + log\ cos x]{/tex} ........(ii) Now, consider v = (sin x) 1/x log v = log(sin x) 1/x {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log v = \frac { 1 } { x } \log \sin x{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex}\frac { 1 } { v } \cdot \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { x } \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( \log \sin x ){/tex} {tex}+ \log \sin x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { 1 } { x } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { v } \cdot \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { x } \cdot \frac { 1 } { \sin x }{/tex} cos x + log sin x {tex}\left( - \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { v } \cdot \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { \cot x } { x } - \frac { \log ( \sin x ) } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d v } { d x } = v \left( \frac { \cot x } { x } - \frac { \log ( \sin x ) } { x ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d v } { d x } = ( \sin x ) ^ { 1 / x } \left[ \frac { \cot x } { x } - \frac { \log ( \sin x ) } { x ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex} ...........(iii) Now, from Eqs.(i), (ii) and (iii), we get {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = (cos x) x [ -x tan x + log cos x] + (sin x) 1/x {tex}\left[ \frac { \cot x } { x } - \frac { \log ( \sin x ) } { x ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex}
solution: Given, {tex} y = (cos x)^x + (sin x)^{1/x}{/tex} Let {tex}u = (cos x)^x\ \ and\ \ v = (sin x)^{1/x}{/tex} Then, given equation becomes y = u + v Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d u } { d x } + \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} .......(i) Taking log both sides, {tex}\Rightarrow log u = log(cos x)^x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow log u = x log(cos x){/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \log ( \cos x ) + \log ( \cos x ) \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( x ){/tex} [ using product rule of derivative] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = x \cdot \frac { 1 } { \cos x } (- sin x) + log \ cos x.1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = - x tan x + log(cos x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d u } { d x }= u[-x \ tan x + log \ cos x]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d u } { d x } = (cos x)^x[-x\ tan x + log\ cos x]{/tex} ........(ii) Now, consider v = (sin x) 1/x log v = log(sin x) 1/x {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log v = \frac { 1 } { x } \log \sin x{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get {tex}\frac { 1 } { v } \cdot \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { x } \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( \log \sin x ){/tex} {tex}+ \log \sin x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { 1 } { x } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { v } \cdot \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { x } \cdot \frac { 1 } { \sin x }{/tex} cos x + log sin x {tex}\left( - \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { v } \cdot \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex} = {tex}\frac { \cot x } { x } - \frac { \log ( \sin x ) } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d v } { d x } = v \left( \frac { \cot x } { x } - \frac { \log ( \sin x ) } { x ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d v } { d x } = ( \sin x ) ^ { 1 / x } \left[ \frac { \cot x } { x } - \frac { \log ( \sin x ) } { x ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex} ...........(iii) Now, from Eqs.(i), (ii) and (iii), we get {tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = (cos x) x [ -x tan x + log cos x] + (sin x) 1/x {tex}\left[ \frac { \cot x } { x } - \frac { \log ( \sin x ) } { x ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Given,{tex} y = (cos x)^x + (sin x)^{1/x}{/tex}
Let {tex}u = (cos x)^x\ \  and\ \ v = (sin x)^{1/x}{/tex}
Then, given equation becomes
y = u + v
Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x,
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d y } { d x } = \frac { d u } { d x } + \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex}.......(i)
Taking log both sides, 
{tex}\Rightarrow log u = log(cos x)^x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow log u = x log(cos x){/tex} 
Differentiating both sides w.r.t x, 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \log ( \cos x ) + \log ( \cos x ) \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( x ){/tex}[ using product rule of derivative]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = x \cdot \frac { 1 } { \cos x } (- sin x) + log \ cos x.1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { u } \frac { d u } { d x } = - x tan x + log(cos x){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d u } { d x }= u[-x \ tan x + log \ cos x]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d u } { d x } = (cos x)^x[-x\ tan x + log\ cos x]{/tex}........(ii)
Now, consider v = (sin x)1/x
log v = log(sin x)1/x
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \log v = \frac { 1 } { x } \log \sin x{/tex}
On differentiating both sides w.r.t x, we get
{tex}\frac { 1 } { v } \cdot \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { x } \cdot \frac { d } { d x } ( \log \sin x ){/tex}{tex}+ \log \sin x \cdot \frac { d } { d x } \left( \frac { 1 } { x } \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { v } \cdot \frac { d v } { d x } = \frac { 1 } { x } \cdot \frac { 1 } { \sin x }{/tex}cos x + log sin x{tex}\left( - \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { v } \cdot \frac { d v } { d x }{/tex}= {tex}\frac { \cot x } { x } - \frac { \log ( \sin x ) } { x ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d v } { d x } = v \left( \frac { \cot x } { x } - \frac { \log ( \sin x ) } { x ^ { 2 } } \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { d v } { d x } = ( \sin x ) ^ { 1 / x } \left[ \frac { \cot x } { x } - \frac { \log ( \sin x ) } { x ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex}...........(iii)
 Now, from Eqs.(i), (ii) and (iii), we get
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x }{/tex} = (cos x)x[ -x tan x + log cos x] + (sin x)1/x{tex}\left[ \frac { \cot x } { x } - \frac { \log ( \sin x ) } { x ^ { 2 } } \right]{/tex}

question_html: Find all points of discontinuity of f where f is defined as follows,f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{aligned} | x | + 3 , & x \leq - 3 \\ - 2 x , & - 3 < x < 3 \\ 6 x + 2 , & x \geq 3 \end{aligned} \right.{/tex}
question_text: Find all points of discontinuity of f where f is defined as follows,f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{aligned} | x | + 3 , & x \leq - 3 \\ - 2 x , & - 3 < x < 3 \\ 6 x + 2 , & x \geq 3 \end{aligned} \right.{/tex}
explanation: Given function is f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { c c } { | x | + 3 , } & { x \leq - 3 } \\ { - 2 x , } & { - 3 < x < 3 } \\ { 6 x + 2 , } & { x \geq 3 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { c } { - x + 3 , x \leq - 3 } \\ { - 2 x , - 3 < x < 3 } \\ { 6 x + 2 , x \geq 3 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} First, we verify continuity at x = - 3 and then at x = 3 Continuity at x = -3 LHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow ( - 3 ) ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow ( - 3 ) ^ { - } } ( - x + 3 ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { LHL } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ - ( - 3 - h ) + 3 ]{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( 3 + h + 3 ){/tex} = 3 + 3 = 6 and RHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow ( - 3 ) ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow ( - 3 ) ^ { + } } ( - 2 x ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad R H L = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ - 2 ( - 3 + h ) ]{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( 6 - 2 h ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} RHL = 6 Also, f(-3) = value of f(x) at x = - 3 =-(-3) + 3 = 3 + 3 = 6 {tex}\because{/tex} LHL = RHL = f(-3) {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) is continuous at x = - 3 So, x = - 3 is the point of continuity. Continuity at x = 3 LHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { - } } [ - ( 2 x ) ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad L H L = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ - 2 ( 3 - h ) ]{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( - 6 + 2 h ){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} LHL = -6 and RHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { + } } ( 6 x + 2 ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { RHL } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ 6 ( 3 + h ) + 2 ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} RHL = 20 {tex}\because \quad \mathrm { LHL } \neq \mathrm { RHL }{/tex} {tex} \therefore{/tex} f is discontinuous at x = 3 Now, as f (x) is a polynomial function for x < -3 , -3< x < 3 and x > 3 so it is continuous in these intervals. Hence, only x = 3 is the point of discontinuity of f(x).
solution: Given function is f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { c c } { | x | + 3 , } & { x \leq - 3 } \\ { - 2 x , } & { - 3 < x < 3 } \\ { 6 x + 2 , } & { x \geq 3 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { c } { - x + 3 , x \leq - 3 } \\ { - 2 x , - 3 < x < 3 } \\ { 6 x + 2 , x \geq 3 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} First, we verify continuity at x = - 3 and then at x = 3 Continuity at x = -3 LHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow ( - 3 ) ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow ( - 3 ) ^ { - } } ( - x + 3 ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { LHL } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ - ( - 3 - h ) + 3 ]{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( 3 + h + 3 ){/tex} = 3 + 3 = 6 and RHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow ( - 3 ) ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow ( - 3 ) ^ { + } } ( - 2 x ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad R H L = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ - 2 ( - 3 + h ) ]{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( 6 - 2 h ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} RHL = 6 Also, f(-3) = value of f(x) at x = - 3 =-(-3) + 3 = 3 + 3 = 6 {tex}\because{/tex} LHL = RHL = f(-3) {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(x) is continuous at x = - 3 So, x = - 3 is the point of continuity. Continuity at x = 3 LHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { - } } [ - ( 2 x ) ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad L H L = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ - 2 ( 3 - h ) ]{/tex} {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( - 6 + 2 h ){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} LHL = -6 and RHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { + } } ( 6 x + 2 ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { RHL } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ 6 ( 3 + h ) + 2 ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} RHL = 20 {tex}\because \quad \mathrm { LHL } \neq \mathrm { RHL }{/tex} {tex} \therefore{/tex} f is discontinuous at x = 3 Now, as f (x) is a polynomial function for x < -3 , -3< x < 3 and x > 3 so it is continuous in these intervals. Hence, only x = 3 is the point of discontinuity of f(x).
solution_html:
Given function is
f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { c c } { | x | + 3 , } & { x \leq - 3 } \\ { - 2 x , } & { - 3 < x < 3 } \\ { 6 x + 2 , } & { x \geq 3 } \end{array} \right.{/tex}= {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { c } { - x + 3 , x \leq - 3 } \\ { - 2 x , - 3 < x < 3 } \\ { 6 x + 2 , x \geq 3 } \end{array} \right.{/tex}

First, we verify continuity at x = - 3 and then at x = 3
Continuity at x = -3
LHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow ( - 3 ) ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow ( - 3 ) ^ { - } } ( - x + 3 ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { LHL } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ - ( - 3 - h ) + 3 ]{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( 3 + h + 3 ){/tex}
= 3 + 3 = 6
and RHL = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow ( - 3 ) ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow ( - 3 ) ^ { + } } ( - 2 x ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad R H L = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ - 2 ( - 3 + h ) ]{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( 6 - 2 h ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} RHL = 6
Also, f(-3) = value of f(x) at x = - 3
=-(-3) + 3
= 3 + 3 = 6
{tex}\because{/tex} LHL = RHL = f(-3)
{tex}\therefore{/tex}f(x) is continuous at x = - 3 So, x = - 3 is the point of continuity.
Continuity at x = 3
LHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { - } } [ - ( 2 x ) ]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad L H L = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ - 2 ( 3 - h ) ]{/tex}
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } ( - 6 + 2 h ){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} LHL = -6
and RHL = {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ x \rightarrow 3 ^ { + } } ( 6 x + 2 ){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { RHL } = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{ h \rightarrow 0 } [ 6 ( 3 + h ) + 2 ]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} RHL = 20
{tex}\because \quad \mathrm { LHL } \neq \mathrm { RHL }{/tex}
{tex} \therefore{/tex} f is discontinuous at x = 3 Now, as f (x) is a polynomial function for x < -3 , -3< x < 3 and x > 3 so it is continuous in these intervals.
Hence, only x = 3 is the point of discontinuity of f(x).

question_html:
Show that the function defined as follows, is continuous at x = 1, x = 2 but not differentiable at x = 2.
f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { 3 x - 2 , } & { 0 < x \leq 1 } \\ { 2 x ^ { 2 } - x , } & { 1 < x \leq 2 } \\ { 5 x - 4 , } & { x > 2 } \end{array} \right.{/tex}
question_text: Show that the function defined as follows, is continuous at x = 1, x = 2 but not differentiable at x = 2. f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { 3 x - 2 , } & { 0 < x \leq 1 } \\ { 2 x ^ { 2 } - x , } & { 1 < x \leq 2 } \\ { 5 x - 4 , } & { x > 2 } \end{array} \right.{/tex}
explanation: According to the question, we have to show that the function defined is continuous at x = 1, x = 2 but not differentiable at x = 2. f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { 3 x - 2 , } & { 0 < x \leq 1 } \\ { 2 x ^ { 2 } - x , } & { 1 < x \leq 2 } \\ { 5 x - 4 , } & { x > 2 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} We shall use of definition of continuity and differentiability to prove the above result. First, we show that continuity of above function at x = 1 and x = 2. Continuity at x = 1: LHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 1 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 1 ^ { - } } ( 3 x - 2 ){/tex} = {tex} \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } [ 3 ( 1 - h ) - 2 ]{/tex} {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } ( 3 - 3 h - 2 ){/tex} = 1 RHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 1 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 1 ^ { + } } {/tex} (2x 2 - x) {tex} \Rightarrow \mathrm { RHL } = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } {/tex} [2 ( 1 + h ) 2 - (1 + h)] {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } {/tex} [2(1 + h 2 + 2h) - (1 + h)] {tex}= \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 }{/tex} [2 + 2h 2 + 4h - 1 - h] {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } {/tex} (2h 2 + 3h + 1) {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} RHL = 1 Also, f(1) = 3(1) - 2 = 3 - 2 = 1 {tex} \therefore{/tex} f(x) is continuous at x = 1. Continuity at x = 2 LHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { - } }{/tex} (2x 2 - x) {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} LHL = {tex} \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 }{/tex} [2(2 - h) 2 - (2 - h)] {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 }{/tex} [2(4 + h 2 - 4h) - (2 - h)] {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 }{/tex} ( 8 + 2h 2 - 8h - 2 + h) {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} LHL = 8 - 2 = 6 and RHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { + } } ( 5 x - 4 ){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { RHL } = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 }{/tex} [5(2 + h) - 4] {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 }{/tex} (10 + 5h - 4) = {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 }{/tex} (5h + 6) {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} RHL = 6 Also, f(2) = 2(2) 2 - 2 = 8 - 2 = 6 Since, LHL = RHL = f(2) {tex} \therefore{/tex} f(x) is continuous at x = 2. We will now check the differentiabililty of the given function at x = 2. Differentiability at x = 2 LHD = {tex} \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { f ( 2 - h ) - f ( 2 ) } { - h }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { LHD } = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \left[ 2 ( 2 - h ) ^ { 2 } - ( 2 - h ) \right] - [ 8 - 2 ] } { - h }{/tex} {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 2 \left( 4 + h ^ { 2 } - 4 h \right) - ( 2 - h ) - 6 } { - h }{/tex} {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 8 + 2 h ^ { 2 } - 8 h - 2 + h - 6 } { h }{/tex} {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { h ( 2 h - 7 ) } { - h } = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 }{/tex} -(2h - 7) {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} LHD = 7 and RHD = {tex} \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { f ( 2 + h ) - f ( { 2) } } { h }{/tex} {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { [ 5 ( 2 + h ) - 4 ] - [ 8 - 2 ] } { h }{/tex} {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { ( 6 + 5 h ) - ( 6 ) } { h } = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 5 h } { h }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} RHD = 5 Since, LHD {tex} \ne{/tex} RHD Therefore, f(x) is not differentiable at x = 2. Therefore, f(x) is continuous at x = 1 and x = 2 but not differentiable at x=2.
solution: According to the question, we have to show that the function defined is continuous at x = 1, x = 2 but not differentiable at x = 2. f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { 3 x - 2 , } & { 0 < x \leq 1 } \\ { 2 x ^ { 2 } - x , } & { 1 < x \leq 2 } \\ { 5 x - 4 , } & { x > 2 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} We shall use of definition of continuity and differentiability to prove the above result. First, we show that continuity of above function at x = 1 and x = 2. Continuity at x = 1: LHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 1 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 1 ^ { - } } ( 3 x - 2 ){/tex} = {tex} \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } [ 3 ( 1 - h ) - 2 ]{/tex} {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } ( 3 - 3 h - 2 ){/tex} = 1 RHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 1 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 1 ^ { + } } {/tex} (2x 2 - x) {tex} \Rightarrow \mathrm { RHL } = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } {/tex} [2 ( 1 + h ) 2 - (1 + h)] {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } {/tex} [2(1 + h 2 + 2h) - (1 + h)] {tex}= \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 }{/tex} [2 + 2h 2 + 4h - 1 - h] {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } {/tex} (2h 2 + 3h + 1) {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} RHL = 1 Also, f(1) = 3(1) - 2 = 3 - 2 = 1 {tex} \therefore{/tex} f(x) is continuous at x = 1. Continuity at x = 2 LHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { - } }{/tex} (2x 2 - x) {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} LHL = {tex} \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 }{/tex} [2(2 - h) 2 - (2 - h)] {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 }{/tex} [2(4 + h 2 - 4h) - (2 - h)] {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 }{/tex} ( 8 + 2h 2 - 8h - 2 + h) {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} LHL = 8 - 2 = 6 and RHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { + } } ( 5 x - 4 ){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { RHL } = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 }{/tex} [5(2 + h) - 4] {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 }{/tex} (10 + 5h - 4) = {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 }{/tex} (5h + 6) {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} RHL = 6 Also, f(2) = 2(2) 2 - 2 = 8 - 2 = 6 Since, LHL = RHL = f(2) {tex} \therefore{/tex} f(x) is continuous at x = 2. We will now check the differentiabililty of the given function at x = 2. Differentiability at x = 2 LHD = {tex} \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { f ( 2 - h ) - f ( 2 ) } { - h }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { LHD } = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \left[ 2 ( 2 - h ) ^ { 2 } - ( 2 - h ) \right] - [ 8 - 2 ] } { - h }{/tex} {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 2 \left( 4 + h ^ { 2 } - 4 h \right) - ( 2 - h ) - 6 } { - h }{/tex} {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 8 + 2 h ^ { 2 } - 8 h - 2 + h - 6 } { h }{/tex} {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { h ( 2 h - 7 ) } { - h } = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 }{/tex} -(2h - 7) {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} LHD = 7 and RHD = {tex} \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { f ( 2 + h ) - f ( { 2) } } { h }{/tex} {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { [ 5 ( 2 + h ) - 4 ] - [ 8 - 2 ] } { h }{/tex} {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { ( 6 + 5 h ) - ( 6 ) } { h } = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 5 h } { h }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} RHD = 5 Since, LHD {tex} \ne{/tex} RHD Therefore, f(x) is not differentiable at x = 2. Therefore, f(x) is continuous at x = 1 and x = 2 but not differentiable at x=2.
solution_html:
According to the question, we have to show that the function defined is continuous at x = 1, x = 2 but not differentiable at x = 2.
f(x) = {tex} \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { 3 x - 2 , } & { 0 < x \leq 1 } \\ { 2 x ^ { 2 } - x , } & { 1 < x \leq 2 } \\ { 5 x - 4 , } & { x > 2 } \end{array} \right.{/tex}

We shall use of definition of continuity and differentiability to prove the above result.
First, we show that continuity of above function at x = 1 and x = 2.
Continuity at x = 1:
LHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 1 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 1 ^ { - } } ( 3 x - 2 ){/tex}
= {tex} \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } [ 3 ( 1 - h ) - 2 ]{/tex}
{tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } ( 3 - 3 h - 2 ){/tex} = 1
RHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 1 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 1 ^ { + } } {/tex}(2x2 - x)
{tex} \Rightarrow \mathrm { RHL } = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } {/tex}[2 ( 1 + h )2- (1 + h)]
{tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } {/tex}[2(1 + h2+ 2h) - (1 + h)]
{tex}= \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 }{/tex} [2 + 2h2 + 4h - 1 - h]
{tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } {/tex}(2h2+ 3h + 1)
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} RHL = 1
Also, f(1) = 3(1) - 2 = 3 - 2 = 1
{tex} \therefore{/tex} f(x) is continuous at x = 1.

Continuity at x = 2
LHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { - } }{/tex}(2x2 - x)
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex}LHL = {tex} \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 }{/tex} [2(2 - h)2 - (2 - h)]
{tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 }{/tex}[2(4 + h2 - 4h) - (2 - h)]
{tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 }{/tex}( 8 + 2h2 - 8h - 2 + h)
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex}LHL = 8 - 2 = 6
and RHL = {tex} \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 2 ^ { + } } ( 5 x - 4 ){/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { RHL } = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 }{/tex}[5(2 + h) - 4]
{tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 }{/tex}(10 + 5h - 4) = {tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 }{/tex}(5h + 6)
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} RHL = 6
Also, f(2) = 2(2)2 - 2 = 8 - 2 = 6
Since, LHL = RHL = f(2)
{tex} \therefore{/tex}f(x) is continuous at x = 2.

We will now check the differentiabililty of the given function at x = 2.
Differentiability at x = 2
LHD = {tex} \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { f ( 2 - h ) - f ( 2 ) } { - h }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { LHD } = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \left[ 2 ( 2 - h ) ^ { 2 } - ( 2 - h ) \right] - [ 8 - 2 ] } { - h }{/tex}
{tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 2 \left( 4 + h ^ { 2 } - 4 h \right) - ( 2 - h ) - 6 } { - h }{/tex}
{tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 8 + 2 h ^ { 2 } - 8 h - 2 + h - 6 } { h }{/tex}
{tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { h ( 2 h - 7 ) } { - h } = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 }{/tex}-(2h - 7)
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} LHD = 7
and RHD = {tex} \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { f ( 2 + h ) - f ( { 2) } } { h }{/tex}
{tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { [ 5 ( 2 + h ) - 4 ] - [ 8 - 2 ] } { h }{/tex}
{tex} = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { ( 6 + 5 h ) - ( 6 ) } { h } = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 5 h } { h }{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow{/tex} RHD = 5
Since, LHD {tex} \ne{/tex}RHD
Therefore, f(x) is not differentiable at x = 2.
Therefore, f(x) is continuous at x = 1 and x = 2 but not differentiable at x=2.

question_html: Determine the value of constant involved in the definition so that the given function is continuous: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ccc} \frac{k \cos x}{\pi-2 x} & , & x<\frac{\pi}{2} \\ 3 & , & x=\frac{\pi}{2} \\ \frac{3 \tan 2 x}{2 x-\pi} & , & x>\frac{\pi}{2} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
question_text: Determine the value of constant involved in the definition so that the given function is continuous: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ccc} \frac{k \cos x}{\pi-2 x} & , & x<\frac{\pi}{2} \\ 3 & , & x=\frac{\pi}{2} \\ \frac{3 \tan 2 x}{2 x-\pi} & , & x>\frac{\pi}{2} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
explanation: Given function is,: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ccc} \frac{k \cos x}{\pi-2 x} & , & x<\frac{\pi}{2} \\ 3 & , & x=\frac{\pi}{2} \\ \frac{3 \tan 2 x}{2 x-\pi} & , & x>\frac{\pi}{2} \end{array}\right.{/tex} If f(x) is continuous at {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} , then {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{2}^{-}} f(x)=f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-h\right)=f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-h\right)=3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{k \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-h\right)}{\pi-2\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-h\right)}\right]=3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{k \sin h}{\pi-\pi+2 h}\right]=3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{k \sin h}{2 h}\right]=3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{k}{2} \lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{\sin h}{h}\right]=3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{k}{2}=3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 2(3) = 6 Therefore, k = 6
solution: Given function is,: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ccc} \frac{k \cos x}{\pi-2 x} & , & x<\frac{\pi}{2} \\ 3 & , & x=\frac{\pi}{2} \\ \frac{3 \tan 2 x}{2 x-\pi} & , & x>\frac{\pi}{2} \end{array}\right.{/tex} If f(x) is continuous at {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} , then {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{2}^{-}} f(x)=f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-h\right)=f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-h\right)=3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{k \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-h\right)}{\pi-2\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-h\right)}\right]=3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{k \sin h}{\pi-\pi+2 h}\right]=3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{k \sin h}{2 h}\right]=3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{k}{2} \lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{\sin h}{h}\right]=3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{k}{2}=3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 2(3) = 6 Therefore, k = 6
solution_html:
Given function is,: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ccc} \frac{k \cos x}{\pi-2 x} & , & x<\frac{\pi}{2} \\ 3 & , & x=\frac{\pi}{2} \\ \frac{3 \tan 2 x}{2 x-\pi} & , & x>\frac{\pi}{2} \end{array}\right.{/tex}
If f(x) is continuous at {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}, then
{tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow \frac{\pi}{2}^{-}} f(x)=f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-h\right)=f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-h\right)=3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{k \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-h\right)}{\pi-2\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-h\right)}\right]=3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{k \sin h}{\pi-\pi+2 h}\right]=3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{k \sin h}{2 h}\right]=3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{k}{2} \lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{\sin h}{h}\right]=3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{k}{2}=3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 2(3) = 6
Therefore, k = 6

question_html: If {tex}y = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left\{ {x\sqrt {1 - x} - \sqrt x \sqrt {1 - {x^2}} } \right\}{/tex} find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}y = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left\{ {x\sqrt {1 - x} - \sqrt x \sqrt {1 - {x^2}} } \right\}{/tex} find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} .
explanation: We have {tex}y = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left\{ {x\sqrt {1 - x} - \sqrt x \sqrt {1 - {x^2}} } \right\}{/tex} and 0 < x <1, then find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} Put x = sin A and {tex}\sqrt x = \sin B{/tex} Therefore, {tex}y = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left\{ {\sin A\sqrt {1 - {{\sin }^2}B} - \sin B\sqrt {1 - {{\sin }^2}A} } \right\}{/tex} = sin -1 {sinAcosB - sinBcosA} = sin -1 {sin(A - B)} = A - B Thus {tex}y = {\sin ^{ - 1}}x - {\sin ^{ - 1}}\sqrt x {/tex} Differentiating w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} - \frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {{\sqrt {\left( x \right)} }^2}} }}.\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {\sqrt x } \right){/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} - \frac{1}{{2\sqrt x \sqrt {1 - x} }}{/tex}
solution: We have {tex}y = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left\{ {x\sqrt {1 - x} - \sqrt x \sqrt {1 - {x^2}} } \right\}{/tex} and 0 < x <1, then find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} Put x = sin A and {tex}\sqrt x = \sin B{/tex} Therefore, {tex}y = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left\{ {\sin A\sqrt {1 - {{\sin }^2}B} - \sin B\sqrt {1 - {{\sin }^2}A} } \right\}{/tex} = sin -1 {sinAcosB - sinBcosA} = sin -1 {sin(A - B)} = A - B Thus {tex}y = {\sin ^{ - 1}}x - {\sin ^{ - 1}}\sqrt x {/tex} Differentiating w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} - \frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {{\sqrt {\left( x \right)} }^2}} }}.\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {\sqrt x } \right){/tex} {tex} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} - \frac{1}{{2\sqrt x \sqrt {1 - x} }}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have {tex}y = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left\{ {x\sqrt {1 - x} - \sqrt x \sqrt {1 - {x^2}} } \right\}{/tex} and 0 < x <1, then find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} 
Put x = sin A and {tex}\sqrt x = \sin B{/tex} 
Therefore, {tex}y = {\sin ^{ - 1}}\left\{ {\sin A\sqrt {1 - {{\sin }^2}B} - \sin B\sqrt {1 - {{\sin }^2}A} } \right\}{/tex} 
= sin-1{sinAcosB - sinBcosA}
= sin-1{sin(A - B)} = A - B 
Thus {tex}y = {\sin ^{ - 1}}x - {\sin ^{ - 1}}\sqrt x {/tex} 
Differentiating w.r.t. x, we get 
{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} - \frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {{\sqrt {\left( x \right)} }^2}} }}.\frac{d}{{dx}}\left( {\sqrt x } \right){/tex} 
{tex} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt {1 - {x^2}} }} - \frac{1}{{2\sqrt x \sqrt {1 - x} }}{/tex}
