question_html: If x = sin {tex}\left(\frac{1}{a} \log y\right){/tex}, show that (1 - x2) y2 - xy1 - a2y = 0
question_text: If x = sin {tex}\left(\frac{1}{a} \log y\right){/tex} , show that (1 - x 2 ) y 2 - xy 1 - a 2 y = 0
explanation: Given, x = {tex}\sin \left(\frac{1}{a} \log y\right){/tex} (log y) = a sin -1 x {tex}y=e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}{/tex} ...(i) To prove: (1 - x 2 ) y 2 - xy 1 - a 2 y = 0 We notice a second-order derivative in the expression to be proved so first take the step to find the second-order derivative. Let's find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} As, {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} So, lets first find dy/dx {tex}\because y=e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}{/tex} Let t = asin -1 x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d t}{d x}=\frac{a}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \sin ^{-1} \mathrm{x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}}\right]{/tex} And y = e t {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d t} \frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=e^{t} \frac{a}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}=\frac{a e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}{/tex} ...(ii) Again differentiating with respect to x applying product rule: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\operatorname{ae}^{\operatorname{asin}^{-1} \mathrm{x}} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}^{2}}}\right)+\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\sqrt{\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{asin}^{-1} \mathrm{x}}{/tex} Using chain rule and equation (ii) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=-\frac{\operatorname{ae}^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{2\left(1-x^{2}\right) \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}(-2 x)+\frac{a^{2} e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}{/tex} [using {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(x^{n}\right)=n x^{n-1} \frac{d}{d x} \sin ^{-1} x=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}{/tex} ] {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{x a e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{\left(1-x^{2}\right) \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}+\frac{a^{2} e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right) \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\mathrm{a}^{2} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{asin}^{-1} \mathrm{x}}+\frac{\mathrm{xae}^{\mathrm{asin}^{-1} \mathrm{x}}}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}^{2}}}{/tex} Using equation (i) and equation (ii) {tex}\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right) \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\mathrm{a}^{2} \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} (1 - x 2 ) y 2 - xy 1 - a 2 y = 0 Hence proved.
solution: Given, x = {tex}\sin \left(\frac{1}{a} \log y\right){/tex} (log y) = a sin -1 x {tex}y=e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}{/tex} ...(i) To prove: (1 - x 2 ) y 2 - xy 1 - a 2 y = 0 We notice a second-order derivative in the expression to be proved so first take the step to find the second-order derivative. Let's find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} As, {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} So, lets first find dy/dx {tex}\because y=e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}{/tex} Let t = asin -1 x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d t}{d x}=\frac{a}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \sin ^{-1} \mathrm{x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}}\right]{/tex} And y = e t {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d t} \frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=e^{t} \frac{a}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}=\frac{a e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}{/tex} ...(ii) Again differentiating with respect to x applying product rule: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\operatorname{ae}^{\operatorname{asin}^{-1} \mathrm{x}} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}^{2}}}\right)+\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\sqrt{\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{asin}^{-1} \mathrm{x}}{/tex} Using chain rule and equation (ii) {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=-\frac{\operatorname{ae}^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{2\left(1-x^{2}\right) \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}(-2 x)+\frac{a^{2} e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}{/tex} [using {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(x^{n}\right)=n x^{n-1} \frac{d}{d x} \sin ^{-1} x=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}{/tex} ] {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{x a e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{\left(1-x^{2}\right) \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}+\frac{a^{2} e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right) \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\mathrm{a}^{2} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{asin}^{-1} \mathrm{x}}+\frac{\mathrm{xae}^{\mathrm{asin}^{-1} \mathrm{x}}}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}^{2}}}{/tex} Using equation (i) and equation (ii) {tex}\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right) \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\mathrm{a}^{2} \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} (1 - x 2 ) y 2 - xy 1 - a 2 y = 0 Hence proved.
solution_html:
Given,
x = {tex}\sin \left(\frac{1}{a} \log y\right){/tex}
(log y) = a sin-1x
{tex}y=e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}{/tex} ...(i)
To prove: (1 - x2) y2 - xy1 - a2y = 0
We notice a second-order derivative in the expression to be proved so first take the step to find the second-order derivative.
Let's find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}
As, {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex}
So, lets first find dy/dx
{tex}\because y=e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}{/tex}
Let t = asin-1x
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d t}{d x}=\frac{a}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \sin ^{-1} \mathrm{x}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}}\right]{/tex}
And y = et
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d t} \frac{d t}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=e^{t} \frac{a}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}=\frac{a e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}{/tex} ...(ii)
Again differentiating with respect to x applying product rule:
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\operatorname{ae}^{\operatorname{asin}^{-1} \mathrm{x}} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}^{2}}}\right)+\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\sqrt{\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}} \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{asin}^{-1} \mathrm{x}}{/tex}
Using chain rule and equation (ii)
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=-\frac{\operatorname{ae}^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{2\left(1-x^{2}\right) \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}(-2 x)+\frac{a^{2} e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}{/tex}
[using {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(x^{n}\right)=n x^{n-1} \frac{d}{d x} \sin ^{-1} x=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}}{/tex}]
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{x a e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{\left(1-x^{2}\right) \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}+\frac{a^{2} e^{a \sin ^{-1} x}}{\left(1-x^{2}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right) \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\mathrm{a}^{2} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{asin}^{-1} \mathrm{x}}+\frac{\mathrm{xae}^{\mathrm{asin}^{-1} \mathrm{x}}}{\sqrt{1-\mathrm{x}^{2}}}{/tex}
Using equation (i) and equation (ii)
{tex}\left(1-\mathrm{x}^{2}\right) \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\mathrm{a}^{2} \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{x} \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (1 - x2) y2 - xy1 - a2y = 0
Hence proved.

question_html: If y = 3e2x + 2e3x , prove that {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-5 \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 6y = 0.
question_text: If y = 3e 2x + 2e 3x , prove that {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-5 \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 6y = 0.
explanation: Given that y = 3e 2x + 2e 3x , Then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 6e 2x + 6e 3x = 6 (e 2x + e 3x ) Therefore, {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 12e 2x + 18e 3x = 6 (2e 2x + 3e 3x ) Hence {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-5 \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 6y = 6 (2e 2x + 3e 3x ) – 30 (e 2x + e 3x ) + 6 (3e 2x + 2e 3x ) = 0. So, the result is correct.
solution: Given that y = 3e 2x + 2e 3x , Then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 6e 2x + 6e 3x = 6 (e 2x + e 3x ) Therefore, {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 12e 2x + 18e 3x = 6 (2e 2x + 3e 3x ) Hence {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-5 \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 6y = 6 (2e 2x + 3e 3x ) – 30 (e 2x + e 3x ) + 6 (3e 2x + 2e 3x ) = 0. So, the result is correct.
solution_html:
Given that y = 3e2x + 2e3x,
Then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 6e2x + 6e3x
= 6 (e2x + e3x)
Therefore, {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 12e2x + 18e3x
= 6 (2e2x + 3e3x)
Hence {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-5 \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} + 6y = 6 (2e2x + 3e3x) – 30 (e2x + e3x) + 6 (3e2x + 2e3x) = 0.
So, the result is correct.

question_html: If y = A cos (log x) + B sin (log x), prove that {tex}x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=0{/tex}
question_text: If y = A cos (log x) + B sin (log x), prove that {tex}x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=0{/tex}
explanation: We have, y = A cos (log x) + B sin (log x) ...[equation(i)] Differentiating both side with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{1}{x} A \sin (\log x)+\frac{B}{x} \cos (\log x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = -A sin (log x) + B cos (log x) Again differentiating both side with respect to x, we get {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{d y}{d x}=-A \frac{\cos (\log x)}{x}-B \frac{\sin (\log x)}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = - {A cos (log x) + B sin (log x)} [from equation (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}=-y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=0{/tex}
solution: We have, y = A cos (log x) + B sin (log x) ...[equation(i)] Differentiating both side with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{1}{x} A \sin (\log x)+\frac{B}{x} \cos (\log x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = -A sin (log x) + B cos (log x) Again differentiating both side with respect to x, we get {tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{d y}{d x}=-A \frac{\cos (\log x)}{x}-B \frac{\sin (\log x)}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = - {A cos (log x) + B sin (log x)} [from equation (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}=-y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=0{/tex}
solution_html:
We have,
y = A cos (log x) + B sin (log x) ...[equation(i)]
Differentiating both side with respect to x, we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{1}{x} A \sin (\log x)+\frac{B}{x} \cos (\log x){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = -A sin (log x) + B cos (log x)
Again differentiating both side with respect to x, we get
{tex}x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{d y}{d x}=-A \frac{\cos (\log x)}{x}-B \frac{\sin (\log x)}{x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = - {A cos (log x) + B sin (log x)}   [from equation (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}=-y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=0{/tex}

question_html: If y = ex(sin x + cos x), then show that {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 y = 0{/tex}.
question_text: If y = e x (sin x + cos x), then show that {tex} \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 y = 0{/tex} .
explanation: According to the question, if y = e x (sin x + cos x).............(i) then,we are required to prove that, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 y = 0{/tex} ..........(ii) We shall make use of product rule of differentiation in order to prove the required result. Therefore,on differentiating both sides of Eq(i) w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = e ^ { x } \frac { d } { d x } ( \sin x + \cos x ){/tex} {tex}+ ( \sin x + \cos x ) \frac { d } { d x } \left( e ^ { x } \right){/tex} = e x (cos x - sin x) + (sin x + cos x)e x =e {tex}^x{/tex} [cos x - sin x + sin x + cos x] = e x (2 cos x) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = 2 e ^ { x } \cos x{/tex} ...............(iii) Therefore,on differentiating both sides of Eq(iii) w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \left( 2 e ^ { x } \right) \frac { d } { d x } ( \cos x ) + \cos x \frac { d } { d x } \left( 2 e ^ { x } \right){/tex} [ By using product rule of derivative] = 2e x (- sin x) + cos x.2e x = 2e x cos x - 2e x sin x.........(iv) Therefore, on putting the value of {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } \text { and } \frac { d y } { d x } {/tex} from Eq.(iv) and (iii) along with the value of y in LHS of Eq.(ii), we get, LHS = {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 y{/tex} = (2e x cos x - 2e x sin x) - 2(2e x cos x) + 2e x (sin x + cos x) = 2e x cos x - 2e x sin x - 4e x cos x + 2e x sin x + 2e x cos x = 4e x cos x - 4e x cos x = 0 = RHS
solution: According to the question, if y = e x (sin x + cos x).............(i) then,we are required to prove that, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 y = 0{/tex} ..........(ii) We shall make use of product rule of differentiation in order to prove the required result. Therefore,on differentiating both sides of Eq(i) w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = e ^ { x } \frac { d } { d x } ( \sin x + \cos x ){/tex} {tex}+ ( \sin x + \cos x ) \frac { d } { d x } \left( e ^ { x } \right){/tex} = e x (cos x - sin x) + (sin x + cos x)e x =e {tex}^x{/tex} [cos x - sin x + sin x + cos x] = e x (2 cos x) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = 2 e ^ { x } \cos x{/tex} ...............(iii) Therefore,on differentiating both sides of Eq(iii) w.r.t x, we get, {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \left( 2 e ^ { x } \right) \frac { d } { d x } ( \cos x ) + \cos x \frac { d } { d x } \left( 2 e ^ { x } \right){/tex} [ By using product rule of derivative] = 2e x (- sin x) + cos x.2e x = 2e x cos x - 2e x sin x.........(iv) Therefore, on putting the value of {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } \text { and } \frac { d y } { d x } {/tex} from Eq.(iv) and (iii) along with the value of y in LHS of Eq.(ii), we get, LHS = {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 y{/tex} = (2e x cos x - 2e x sin x) - 2(2e x cos x) + 2e x (sin x + cos x) = 2e x cos x - 2e x sin x - 4e x cos x + 2e x sin x + 2e x cos x = 4e x cos x - 4e x cos x = 0 = RHS
solution_html:
According to the question, if y = ex(sin x + cos x).............(i)

then,we are required to prove that,
 {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 y = 0{/tex}..........(ii)

We shall  make use  of product rule of differentiation  in order to prove  the required result.
Therefore,on differentiating both sides of Eq(i) w.r.t x, we get,
{tex}\frac { d y } { d x } = e ^ { x } \frac { d } { d x } ( \sin x + \cos x ){/tex}{tex}+ ( \sin x + \cos x ) \frac { d } { d x } \left( e ^ { x } \right){/tex}
= ex(cos x - sin x) + (sin x + cos x)ex
=e{tex}^x{/tex} [cos x - sin x + sin x + cos x]
= ex(2 cos x)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac { d y } { d x } = 2 e ^ { x } \cos x{/tex}...............(iii)
Therefore,on differentiating both sides of Eq(iii) w.r.t x, we get,
{tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } = \left( 2 e ^ { x } \right) \frac { d } { d x } ( \cos x ) + \cos x \frac { d } { d x } \left( 2 e ^ { x } \right){/tex}[ By using product rule of derivative]
= 2ex(- sin x) + cos x.2ex
= 2excos x - 2ex sin x.........(iv)
Therefore, on putting the value of {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } \text { and } \frac { d y } { d x } {/tex} from Eq.(iv) and (iii) along with the value of y in LHS of Eq.(ii), we get,
LHS = {tex}\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { d y } { d x } + 2 y{/tex}
= (2excos x - 2ex sin x) - 2(2excos x) + 2ex(sin x + cos x)
= 2excos x - 2ex sin x - 4excos x +  2exsin x + 2excos x
= 4excos x - 4excos x = 0 = RHS

question_html: Show that the function f(x) defined by f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \frac { \sin x } { x } + \cos x , } & { x > 0 } \\ { 2 , } & { x = 0 } \\ { \frac { 4 ( 1 - \sqrt { 1 - x } ) } { x } , } & { x < 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0.
question_text: Show that the function f(x) defined by f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \frac { \sin x } { x } + \cos x , } & { x > 0 } \\ { 2 , } & { x = 0 } \\ { \frac { 4 ( 1 - \sqrt { 1 - x } ) } { x } , } & { x < 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0.
explanation: To show that the given function is continuous at x = 0, we show that (LHL) x = 0 = (RHL) x = 0 = f(0) ....(i) Here, we have f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \frac { \sin x } { x } + \cos x , } & { x > 0 } \\ { 2 , } & { x = 0 } \\ { \frac { 4 ( 1 - \sqrt { 1 - x } ) } { x } , } & { x < 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} Now, LHL = {tex}\lim _ { x \rightarrow 0 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 0 ^ { - } } \frac { 4 ( 1 - \sqrt { 1 - x } ) } { x }{/tex} {tex}= \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 4 [ 1 - \sqrt { 1 - ( 0 - h ) } ] } { 0 - h }{/tex} {tex}= \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 4 [ 1 - \sqrt { 1 + h } ] } { - h }{/tex} {tex}= \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 4 [ 1 - \sqrt { 1 + h } ] } { - h } \times \frac { 1 + \sqrt { 1 + h } } { 1 + \sqrt { 1 + h } }{/tex} {tex}= \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 4 \left[ ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } - ( \sqrt { 1 + h } ) ^ { 2 } \right] } { - h [ 1 + \sqrt { 1 + h } ] }{/tex} {tex}= \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 4 [ 1 - ( 1 + h ) ] } { - h [ 1 + \sqrt { 1 + h } ] }{/tex} {tex}= \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { - h \times 4 } { - h [ 1 + \sqrt { 1 + h } ] }{/tex} {tex}= \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 4 } { 1 + \sqrt { 1 + h } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 4 } { 1 + \sqrt { 1 } } = \frac { 4 } { 2 }{/tex} = 2 and RHL = {tex}\lim _ { x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } \left( \frac { \sin x } { x } + \cos x \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { RHL } = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \left( \frac { \sin h } { h } + \cos h \right){/tex} {tex}= \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin h } { h } + \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \cos h{/tex} = 1 + cos 0 = 1 + 1 = 2 Also , given that x = 0, f(x) = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(0) = 2 Since, (LHL) x = 0 = (RHL) x = 0 = f(0) = 2 Therefore, f(x) is continuous at x = 0.
solution: To show that the given function is continuous at x = 0, we show that (LHL) x = 0 = (RHL) x = 0 = f(0) ....(i) Here, we have f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \frac { \sin x } { x } + \cos x , } & { x > 0 } \\ { 2 , } & { x = 0 } \\ { \frac { 4 ( 1 - \sqrt { 1 - x } ) } { x } , } & { x < 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} Now, LHL = {tex}\lim _ { x \rightarrow 0 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 0 ^ { - } } \frac { 4 ( 1 - \sqrt { 1 - x } ) } { x }{/tex} {tex}= \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 4 [ 1 - \sqrt { 1 - ( 0 - h ) } ] } { 0 - h }{/tex} {tex}= \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 4 [ 1 - \sqrt { 1 + h } ] } { - h }{/tex} {tex}= \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 4 [ 1 - \sqrt { 1 + h } ] } { - h } \times \frac { 1 + \sqrt { 1 + h } } { 1 + \sqrt { 1 + h } }{/tex} {tex}= \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 4 \left[ ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } - ( \sqrt { 1 + h } ) ^ { 2 } \right] } { - h [ 1 + \sqrt { 1 + h } ] }{/tex} {tex}= \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 4 [ 1 - ( 1 + h ) ] } { - h [ 1 + \sqrt { 1 + h } ] }{/tex} {tex}= \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { - h \times 4 } { - h [ 1 + \sqrt { 1 + h } ] }{/tex} {tex}= \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 4 } { 1 + \sqrt { 1 + h } }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { 4 } { 1 + \sqrt { 1 } } = \frac { 4 } { 2 }{/tex} = 2 and RHL = {tex}\lim _ { x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } \left( \frac { \sin x } { x } + \cos x \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { RHL } = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \left( \frac { \sin h } { h } + \cos h \right){/tex} {tex}= \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin h } { h } + \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \cos h{/tex} = 1 + cos 0 = 1 + 1 = 2 Also , given that x = 0, f(x) = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f(0) = 2 Since, (LHL) x = 0 = (RHL) x = 0 = f(0) = 2 Therefore, f(x) is continuous at x = 0.
solution_html:
To show that the given function is continuous at x = 0, we show that
(LHL)x = 0 = (RHL)x = 0 = f(0) ....(i)
Here, we have f(x) = {tex}\left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { \frac { \sin x } { x } + \cos x , } & { x > 0 } \\ { 2 , } & { x = 0 } \\ { \frac { 4 ( 1 - \sqrt { 1 - x } ) } { x } , } & { x < 0 } \end{array} \right.{/tex}
Now, LHL = {tex}\lim _ { x \rightarrow 0 ^ { - } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 0 ^ { - } } \frac { 4 ( 1 - \sqrt { 1 - x } ) } { x }{/tex}
{tex}= \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 4 [ 1 - \sqrt { 1 - ( 0 - h ) } ] } { 0 - h }{/tex}
{tex}= \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 4 [ 1 - \sqrt { 1 + h } ] } { - h }{/tex}
{tex}= \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 4 [ 1 - \sqrt { 1 + h } ] } { - h } \times \frac { 1 + \sqrt { 1 + h } } { 1 + \sqrt { 1 + h } }{/tex}
{tex}= \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 4 \left[ ( 1 ) ^ { 2 } - ( \sqrt { 1 + h } ) ^ { 2 } \right] } { - h [ 1 + \sqrt { 1 + h } ] }{/tex}
{tex}= \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 4 [ 1 - ( 1 + h ) ] } { - h [ 1 + \sqrt { 1 + h } ] }{/tex}
{tex}= \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { - h \times 4 } { - h [ 1 + \sqrt { 1 + h } ] }{/tex}
{tex}= \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { 4 } { 1 + \sqrt { 1 + h } }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 4 } { 1 + \sqrt { 1 } } = \frac { 4 } { 2 }{/tex} = 2
and RHL = {tex}\lim _ { x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } f ( x ) = \lim _ { x \rightarrow 0 ^ { + } } \left( \frac { \sin x } { x } + \cos x \right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm { RHL } = \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \left( \frac { \sin h } { h } + \cos h \right){/tex}
{tex}= \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \frac { \sin h } { h } + \lim _ { h \rightarrow 0 } \cos h{/tex}
= 1 + cos 0
= 1 + 1
= 2
Also , given that x = 0, f(x) = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}f(0) = 2
Since, (LHL)x = 0 = (RHL)x = 0 = f(0) = 2
Therefore, f(x) is continuous at x = 0.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}y = {\left( {\cos x} \right)^{{{\left( {\cos x} \right)}^{\left( {\cos x} \right)...\infty }}}}{/tex} , show that {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{y^2}\tan x}}{{y\log \cos x - 1}}{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}y = {\left( {\cos x} \right)^{{{\left( {\cos x} \right)}^{\left( {\cos x} \right)...\infty }}}}{/tex} , show that {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{{y^2}\tan x}}{{y\log \cos x - 1}}{/tex}
explanation: We have, {tex}y = {\left( {\cos x} \right)^{{{\left( {\cos x} \right)}^{\left( {\cos x} \right)...\infty }}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = {\left( {\cos x} \right)^y}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \log y = \log {\left( {\cos x} \right)^y}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log y = y\log \cos x{/tex} On differentiating w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{1}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = y.\frac{d}{{dx}}\log \cos x + \log \cos x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{y}{{\cos x}}.\frac{d}{{dx}}\cos x + \log \cos x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{y}{{\cos x}}.(-sinx) + \log \cos x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}\left[ {\frac{1}{y} - \log \cos x} \right] = \frac{{ - y\sin x}}{{\cos x}} = - y\tan x{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - {y^2}\tan x}}{{\left( {1 - y\log \cos x} \right)}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{{y^2}\tan x}}{{y\log \cos x - 1}}{/tex} Hence Proved.
solution: We have, {tex}y = {\left( {\cos x} \right)^{{{\left( {\cos x} \right)}^{\left( {\cos x} \right)...\infty }}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = {\left( {\cos x} \right)^y}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \log y = \log {\left( {\cos x} \right)^y}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log y = y\log \cos x{/tex} On differentiating w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{1}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = y.\frac{d}{{dx}}\log \cos x + \log \cos x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{y}{{\cos x}}.\frac{d}{{dx}}\cos x + \log \cos x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{y}{{\cos x}}.(-sinx) + \log \cos x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}\left[ {\frac{1}{y} - \log \cos x} \right] = \frac{{ - y\sin x}}{{\cos x}} = - y\tan x{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - {y^2}\tan x}}{{\left( {1 - y\log \cos x} \right)}}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{{y^2}\tan x}}{{y\log \cos x - 1}}{/tex} Hence Proved.
solution_html:
We have, {tex}y = {\left( {\cos x} \right)^{{{\left( {\cos x} \right)}^{\left( {\cos x} \right)...\infty }}}}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow y = {\left( {\cos x} \right)^y}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \log y = \log {\left( {\cos x} \right)^y}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \log y = y\log \cos x{/tex}

On differentiating w.r.t. x, we get

{tex}\frac{1}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = y.\frac{d}{{dx}}\log \cos x + \log \cos x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{y}{{\cos x}}.\frac{d}{{dx}}\cos x + \log \cos x.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{y}.\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{y}{{\cos x}}.(-sinx) + \log \cos x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}\left[ {\frac{1}{y} - \log \cos x} \right] = \frac{{ - y\sin x}}{{\cos x}} = - y\tan x{/tex}

{tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - {y^2}\tan x}}{{\left( {1 - y\log \cos x} \right)}}{/tex}

{tex} = \frac{{{y^2}\tan x}}{{y\log \cos x - 1}}{/tex} Hence Proved.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} when y = xcos x + (sin x)tan x
question_text: Find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} when y = x cos x + (sin x) tan x
explanation: We have, y = x cos x + (sin x) tan x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = e cos x log x + e tan x log sin x Differentiating with respect to x using chain rule, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{\cos x \log x}\right)+\frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{\tan x \log \sin x}\right){/tex} {tex}=e^{\cos x \log x} \frac{d}{d x}(\cos x \log x)+e^{\tan x \log \sin x} \frac{d}{d x}(\tan x \log \sin x){/tex} {tex}=e^{\log x^{\cos x}}\left[\cos x \frac{d}{d x}(\log x)+\log x \frac{d}{d x}(\cos x)\right]{/tex} {tex}+e^{\log (\sin x)^{\tan x}}\left[\tan x \frac{d}{d x} \log \sin x+\log \sin x \frac{d}{d x}(\tan x)\right]{/tex} {tex}=x^{\cos x}\left[\cos x\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)+\log x(-\sin x)\right]{/tex} {tex}+(\sin x)^{\tan x}\left[\tan x\left(\frac{1}{\sin x}\right) \frac{d}{d x}(\sin x)+\log \sin x\left(\sec ^{2} x\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}=x^{\cos x}\left[\frac{\cos x}{x}-\sin x \log x\right]{/tex} {tex}+(\sin x)^{\tan x}\left[\tan x\left(\frac{1}{\sin x}\right)(\cos x)+\sec ^{2} x \log \sin x\right]{/tex} {tex}=x^{\cos x}\left[\frac{\cos x}{x}-\sin x \log x\right]{/tex} + (sin x) tan x [1 + sec 2 x log sin x] The differentiation of the given function y is as above.
solution: We have, y = x cos x + (sin x) tan x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = e cos x log x + e tan x log sin x Differentiating with respect to x using chain rule, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{\cos x \log x}\right)+\frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{\tan x \log \sin x}\right){/tex} {tex}=e^{\cos x \log x} \frac{d}{d x}(\cos x \log x)+e^{\tan x \log \sin x} \frac{d}{d x}(\tan x \log \sin x){/tex} {tex}=e^{\log x^{\cos x}}\left[\cos x \frac{d}{d x}(\log x)+\log x \frac{d}{d x}(\cos x)\right]{/tex} {tex}+e^{\log (\sin x)^{\tan x}}\left[\tan x \frac{d}{d x} \log \sin x+\log \sin x \frac{d}{d x}(\tan x)\right]{/tex} {tex}=x^{\cos x}\left[\cos x\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)+\log x(-\sin x)\right]{/tex} {tex}+(\sin x)^{\tan x}\left[\tan x\left(\frac{1}{\sin x}\right) \frac{d}{d x}(\sin x)+\log \sin x\left(\sec ^{2} x\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}=x^{\cos x}\left[\frac{\cos x}{x}-\sin x \log x\right]{/tex} {tex}+(\sin x)^{\tan x}\left[\tan x\left(\frac{1}{\sin x}\right)(\cos x)+\sec ^{2} x \log \sin x\right]{/tex} {tex}=x^{\cos x}\left[\frac{\cos x}{x}-\sin x \log x\right]{/tex} + (sin x) tan x [1 + sec 2 x log sin x] The differentiation of the given function y is as above.
solution_html:
We have, y = xcos x + (sin x)tan x
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = ecos x log x + etan x log sin x
Differentiating with respect to x using chain rule,
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{\cos x \log x}\right)+\frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{\tan x \log \sin x}\right){/tex}
{tex}=e^{\cos x \log x} \frac{d}{d x}(\cos x \log x)+e^{\tan x \log \sin x} \frac{d}{d x}(\tan x \log \sin x){/tex}
{tex}=e^{\log x^{\cos x}}\left[\cos x \frac{d}{d x}(\log x)+\log x \frac{d}{d x}(\cos x)\right]{/tex} {tex}+e^{\log (\sin x)^{\tan x}}\left[\tan x \frac{d}{d x} \log \sin x+\log \sin x \frac{d}{d x}(\tan x)\right]{/tex}
{tex}=x^{\cos x}\left[\cos x\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)+\log x(-\sin x)\right]{/tex} {tex}+(\sin x)^{\tan x}\left[\tan x\left(\frac{1}{\sin x}\right) \frac{d}{d x}(\sin x)+\log \sin x\left(\sec ^{2} x\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}=x^{\cos x}\left[\frac{\cos x}{x}-\sin x \log x\right]{/tex} {tex}+(\sin x)^{\tan x}\left[\tan x\left(\frac{1}{\sin x}\right)(\cos x)+\sec ^{2} x \log \sin x\right]{/tex}
{tex}=x^{\cos x}\left[\frac{\cos x}{x}-\sin x \log x\right]{/tex} + (sin x)tan x[1 + sec2x log sin x]
The differentiation of the given function y is as above.

question_html: If x = a({tex}\theta{/tex} + sin {tex}\theta{/tex}) and y = a(1 - cos {tex}\theta{/tex}),find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} at {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}.
question_text: If x = a( {tex}\theta{/tex} + sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ) and y = a(1 - cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ),find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} at {tex}\theta=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} .
explanation: We have, x = a( {tex}\theta{/tex} + sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ) and y = a(1 - cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}{/tex} = a(1 + cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ) and {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}{/tex} = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(d y / d \theta)}{(d x / d \theta)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{a \sin \theta}{a(1+\cos \theta)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin \theta}{(1+\cos \theta)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \sin (\theta / 2) \cos (\theta / 2)}{2 \cos ^{2}(\theta / 2)}{/tex} = {tex}\tan \frac{\theta}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(\tan \frac{\theta}{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \sec ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2} \cdot \frac{d \theta}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2} \sec ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}\right) \times \frac{1}{a(1+\cos \theta)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)_{\theta=\frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex} ,= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \sec ^{2} \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{1}{a\left(1+\cos \frac{\pi}{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ( {tex}\sqrt2{/tex} ) 2 {tex}\frac {1} {a(1+0)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac 1 a{/tex}
solution: We have, x = a( {tex}\theta{/tex} + sin {tex}\theta{/tex} ) and y = a(1 - cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}{/tex} = a(1 + cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ) and {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}{/tex} = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(d y / d \theta)}{(d x / d \theta)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{a \sin \theta}{a(1+\cos \theta)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin \theta}{(1+\cos \theta)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \sin (\theta / 2) \cos (\theta / 2)}{2 \cos ^{2}(\theta / 2)}{/tex} = {tex}\tan \frac{\theta}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(\tan \frac{\theta}{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \sec ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2} \cdot \frac{d \theta}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2} \sec ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}\right) \times \frac{1}{a(1+\cos \theta)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)_{\theta=\frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex} ,= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \sec ^{2} \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{1}{a\left(1+\cos \frac{\pi}{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ( {tex}\sqrt2{/tex} ) 2 {tex}\frac {1} {a(1+0)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac 1 a{/tex}
solution_html:
We have,
x = a({tex}\theta{/tex} + sin {tex}\theta{/tex}) and y = a(1 - cos {tex}\theta{/tex})
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}{/tex} = a(1 + cos {tex}\theta{/tex}) and {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}{/tex} = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(d y / d \theta)}{(d x / d \theta)}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{a \sin \theta}{a(1+\cos \theta)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sin \theta}{(1+\cos \theta)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \sin (\theta / 2) \cos (\theta / 2)}{2 \cos ^{2}(\theta / 2)}{/tex} = {tex}\tan \frac{\theta}{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(\tan \frac{\theta}{2}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \sec ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2} \cdot \frac{d \theta}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2} \sec ^{2} \frac{\theta}{2}\right) \times \frac{1}{a(1+\cos \theta)}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}\right)_{\theta=\frac{\pi}{2}}{/tex},= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \sec ^{2} \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{1}{a\left(1+\cos \frac{\pi}{2}\right)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ({tex}\sqrt2{/tex})2 {tex}\frac {1} {a(1+0)}{/tex} = {tex}\frac 1 a{/tex}

question_html: If y = a cos (log x) + b sin (log x), prove that x2y2+ xy1 + y = 0.
question_text: If y = a cos (log x) + b sin (log x), prove that x 2 y 2 + xy 1 + y = 0.
explanation: We have y = a cos (log x) + b sin (log x) Differentiating both side w.r.t. to x y 1 = {tex}\frac{{ - a\sin (\log x)}}{x} {/tex} + {tex} \frac{{b\cos (\log x)}}{x}{/tex} xy 1 = - a sin (log x) + b cos (log x) Again differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, xy 2 + y 1 (1) = {tex} \frac{{ - 3\cos (\log x)}}{x} - \frac{{4\sin (\log x)}}{x}{/tex} x 2 y 2 + xy 1 = -3cos (log x) - 4 sin (log x) x 2 y 2 + xy 1 = -y x 2 y 2 + xy 1 + y = 0
solution: We have y = a cos (log x) + b sin (log x) Differentiating both side w.r.t. to x y 1 = {tex}\frac{{ - a\sin (\log x)}}{x} {/tex} + {tex} \frac{{b\cos (\log x)}}{x}{/tex} xy 1 = - a sin (log x) + b cos (log x) Again differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, xy 2 + y 1 (1) = {tex} \frac{{ - 3\cos (\log x)}}{x} - \frac{{4\sin (\log x)}}{x}{/tex} x 2 y 2 + xy 1 = -3cos (log x) - 4 sin (log x) x 2 y 2 + xy 1 = -y x 2 y 2 + xy 1 + y = 0
solution_html:
We have y = a cos (log x) + b sin (log x)
Differentiating both side w.r.t. to x
y1 = {tex}\frac{{ - a\sin (\log x)}}{x} {/tex} +  {tex} \frac{{b\cos (\log x)}}{x}{/tex} 
xy1 = - a sin (log x) + b cos (log x)
Again differentiating both sides w.r.t. x,
xy2 + y1 (1) = {tex} \frac{{ - 3\cos (\log x)}}{x} - \frac{{4\sin (\log x)}}{x}{/tex} 
x2y2 + xy1 = -3cos (log x) - 4 sin (log x)
x2y2 + xy1 = -y
x2y2 + xy1 + y = 0

question_html:
If y = (x)cos x + (sin x)tan x, prove that
{tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = xcos x {{tex}\frac{\cos x}{x}{/tex} - (sin x) log x} + (sin x)tan x {tex}\cdot{/tex} {1 + (log sin x) sec2 x}.
question_text: If y = (x) cos x + (sin x) tan x , prove that {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = x cos x { {tex}\frac{\cos x}{x}{/tex} - (sin x) log x} + (sin x) tan x {tex}\cdot{/tex} {1 + (log sin x) sec 2 x}.
explanation: Suppose that u = (x) cos x and v = (sin x) tan x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} u = (x) cos x Taking log both sides,we get log u = cos x log x differentiate w.r.t x {tex}\frac{1}{u} \times \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\cos x\left(\frac{1}{x}\right){/tex} + log x (-sin x) {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = ( {tex}\frac{\cos x}{x}{/tex} - log x sin x) {tex}\times{/tex} u {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = ( {tex}\frac{\cos x}{x}{/tex} - log x sin x) {tex}\times{/tex} (x) cos x ......(i) v = (sin x) tan x Take log both sides,we get log v = tan x {tex}\times{/tex} (log sin x) Differentiate,w.r.t x {tex}\frac{1}{v} \times \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = tan x {tex}\left(\frac{\cos x}{\sin x}\right){/tex} + log sin (sec 2 x) {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = (tan x {tex}\left(\frac{\cos x}{\sin x}\right){/tex} + log sin (sec 2 x) {tex}\times{/tex} v {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = (1 + log sin x (sec 2 x)) {tex}\times{/tex} (sin x) tan x .....(ii) From equation(i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = (1 + log sin x (sec 2 x)) {tex}\times{/tex} (sin x) tan x + ( {tex}\frac{\cos x}{x}{/tex} - log x sin x) {tex}\times{/tex} (x) cos x
solution: Suppose that u = (x) cos x and v = (sin x) tan x {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} u = (x) cos x Taking log both sides,we get log u = cos x log x differentiate w.r.t x {tex}\frac{1}{u} \times \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\cos x\left(\frac{1}{x}\right){/tex} + log x (-sin x) {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = ( {tex}\frac{\cos x}{x}{/tex} - log x sin x) {tex}\times{/tex} u {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = ( {tex}\frac{\cos x}{x}{/tex} - log x sin x) {tex}\times{/tex} (x) cos x ......(i) v = (sin x) tan x Take log both sides,we get log v = tan x {tex}\times{/tex} (log sin x) Differentiate,w.r.t x {tex}\frac{1}{v} \times \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = tan x {tex}\left(\frac{\cos x}{\sin x}\right){/tex} + log sin (sec 2 x) {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = (tan x {tex}\left(\frac{\cos x}{\sin x}\right){/tex} + log sin (sec 2 x) {tex}\times{/tex} v {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = (1 + log sin x (sec 2 x)) {tex}\times{/tex} (sin x) tan x .....(ii) From equation(i) and (ii) {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = (1 + log sin x (sec 2 x)) {tex}\times{/tex} (sin x) tan x + ( {tex}\frac{\cos x}{x}{/tex} - log x sin x) {tex}\times{/tex} (x) cos x
solution_html:
Suppose that u = (x)cos x and v = (sin x)tan x 
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} 
u = (x)cos x 
Taking log both sides,we get
log u = cos x log x
differentiate w.r.t x
{tex}\frac{1}{u} \times \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\cos x\left(\frac{1}{x}\right){/tex} + log x (-sin x)
{tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = ({tex}\frac{\cos x}{x}{/tex} - log x sin x) {tex}\times{/tex} u
{tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = ({tex}\frac{\cos x}{x}{/tex} - log x sin x) {tex}\times{/tex} (x)cos x ......(i)
v = (sin x)tan x 
Take log both sides,we get
log v = tan x {tex}\times{/tex} (log sin x)
Differentiate,w.r.t x
{tex}\frac{1}{v} \times \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = tan x {tex}\left(\frac{\cos x}{\sin x}\right){/tex} + log sin (sec2 x)
{tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = (tan x {tex}\left(\frac{\cos x}{\sin x}\right){/tex} + log sin (sec2 x) {tex}\times{/tex} v
{tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = (1 + log sin x (sec2 x)) {tex}\times{/tex} (sin x)tan x .....(ii)
From equation(i) and (ii)
{tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = (1 + log sin x (sec2 x)) {tex}\times{/tex} (sin x)tan x + ({tex}\frac{\cos x}{x}{/tex} - log x sin x) {tex}\times{/tex} (x)cos x

question_html:
For what value of k is the following function continuous at x = 2?

{tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} 2x + 1;\,\,x < 2 \hfill \\ k;\,\,x = 2 \hfill \\ 3x - 1;\,\,x > 2 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}
question_text: For what value of k is the following function continuous at x = 2? {tex}f(x) = \left\{ \begin{gathered} 2x + 1;\,\,x < 2 \hfill \\ k;\,\,x = 2 \hfill \\ 3x - 1;\,\,x > 2 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right.{/tex}
explanation: {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} 2x + 1{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left[ {2\left( {2 - h} \right) + 1} \right]{/tex} = 5 {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} \left( {3x -1} \right){/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} 3\left( {2 + h} \right) - 1{/tex} = 5 In given that question f(x) is continuous at x=2,therefore {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} f(x) = f(2) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} f(x){/tex} 5 = k {tex}\Rightarrow k = 5{/tex}
solution: {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} 2x + 1{/tex} {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left[ {2\left( {2 - h} \right) + 1} \right]{/tex} = 5 {tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} \left( {3x -1} \right){/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} 3\left( {2 + h} \right) - 1{/tex} = 5 In given that question f(x) is continuous at x=2,therefore {tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} f(x) = f(2) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} f(x){/tex} 5 = k {tex}\Rightarrow k = 5{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} 2x + 1{/tex}
{tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} \left[ {2\left( {2 - h} \right) + 1} \right]{/tex} = 5
{tex}= \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} f(x) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} \left( {3x -1} \right){/tex}
{tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{h \to 0} 3\left( {2 + h} \right) - 1{/tex} = 5
In given that question f(x) is continuous at x=2,therefore
{tex} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ - }} f(x) = f(2) = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to {2^ + }} f(x){/tex}
5 = k 
{tex}\Rightarrow k = 5{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Determine the values of a, b, c for which the function {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ccc} \frac{\sin (a+1) x+\sin x}{x} & , & \text { for } x<0 \\ c & , & \text { for } x=0 \\ \frac{\sqrt{x+b x^{2}}-\sqrt{x}}{b x^{3 / 2}} & , & \text { for } x>0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0.
question_text: Determine the values of a, b, c for which the function {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ccc} \frac{\sin (a+1) x+\sin x}{x} & , & \text { for } x<0 \\ c & , & \text { for } x=0 \\ \frac{\sqrt{x+b x^{2}}-\sqrt{x}}{b x^{3 / 2}} & , & \text { for } x>0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0.
solution_html:
The given function can be rewritten as:
{tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ccc} \frac{\sin (a+1) x+\sin x}{x} & , & \text { for } x<0 \\ c & , & \text { for } x=0 \\ \frac{\sqrt{x+b x^{2}}-\sqrt{x}}{b x^{3 / 2}} & , & \text { for } x>0 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{l} \frac{\sin (a+1) x+\sin x}{x}, \text { for } x<0 \\ c, \text { for } x=0 \\ \frac{\sqrt{1+b x}-1}{b x}, \text { for } x>0 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
We observe
(LHL at x = 0) = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(0-h)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(-h){/tex}
{tex}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{-\sin (a+1) h-\sin (-h)}{h}\right]=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{-\sin (a+1) h}{h}-\frac{\sin h}{h}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=-(a+1) \lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{\sin (a+1) h}{(a+1) h}\right]-\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin h}{h}=-a-2{/tex}
Now (RHL at x = 0) = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x) = \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac {\sqrt{1 + bx} -1} {bx} = \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac {b}{2b\sqrt{1+bx}} = \frac12{/tex}
(RHL at x = 0) = f(0) = c
If f(x) is continuous at x = 0, then
{tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x)=f(0){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -a - 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = c
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -a - 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} and c = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\frac{-5}{2}{/tex}, c = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\sqrt{1+b x}-1}{b x}{/tex}
{tex}b x \neq 0 \Rightarrow b \neq 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = {tex}\frac{-5}{2}{/tex} , b {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 and c = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}y=\{\log (x+\sqrt{x^{2}+1})\}^{2}{/tex}, show that {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}=2{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}y=\{\log (x+\sqrt{x^{2}+1})\}^{2}{/tex} , show that {tex}\left(1+x^{2}\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}=2{/tex}
explanation: Given, {tex}\mathrm{y}=(\log (\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}))^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=2(\log (x+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1})) \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}}\left(1+\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}}(2 x)\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=2(\log (\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1})) \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}}\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=2(\log (x+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1})) \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2(\log (x+\sqrt{x^{2}+1})}{\sqrt{x^{2}+1}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}=\frac{2\left({\displaystyle\frac{1+{\displaystyle\frac x{\sqrt{1+x^2}}}}{x+\sqrt{1+x^2\;}}}\right)\times\sqrt{1+x^2}-2(\log(x\;+\sqrt{1+\;x^2}))\times{\displaystyle\frac x{\sqrt{1+x^2}}}\;}{\left(\sqrt{1+x^2}\right)^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\frac{2\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}\right) \times \sqrt{1+x^{2}}-2(\log (\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}})) \times \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}}}{\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\frac{2-2(\log (\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}})) \times \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}}}{\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}{/tex} We have to find out , LHS = {tex}\left(x^{2}+1\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}=\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}+1\right) \times \frac{2-2(\log (\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}})) \times \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}}}{\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}+\mathrm{x} \times \frac{2(\log (\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1})}{\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}}{/tex} {tex}=2-2(\log (x+\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}})) \times \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}}+\frac{2 \mathrm{x}(\log (\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1})}{\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}}{/tex} = 2 = RHS Hence Proved.
solution: Given, {tex}\mathrm{y}=(\log (\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}))^{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=2(\log (x+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1})) \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}}\left(1+\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}}(2 x)\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=2(\log (\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1})) \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}}\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=2(\log (x+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1})) \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2(\log (x+\sqrt{x^{2}+1})}{\sqrt{x^{2}+1}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}=\frac{2\left({\displaystyle\frac{1+{\displaystyle\frac x{\sqrt{1+x^2}}}}{x+\sqrt{1+x^2\;}}}\right)\times\sqrt{1+x^2}-2(\log(x\;+\sqrt{1+\;x^2}))\times{\displaystyle\frac x{\sqrt{1+x^2}}}\;}{\left(\sqrt{1+x^2}\right)^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\frac{2\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}\right) \times \sqrt{1+x^{2}}-2(\log (\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}})) \times \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}}}{\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\frac{2-2(\log (\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}})) \times \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}}}{\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}{/tex} We have to find out , LHS = {tex}\left(x^{2}+1\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}=\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}+1\right) \times \frac{2-2(\log (\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}})) \times \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}}}{\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}+\mathrm{x} \times \frac{2(\log (\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1})}{\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}}{/tex} {tex}=2-2(\log (x+\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}})) \times \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}}+\frac{2 \mathrm{x}(\log (\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1})}{\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}}{/tex} = 2 = RHS Hence Proved.
solution_html:
Given,
{tex}\mathrm{y}=(\log (\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}))^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=2(\log (x+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1})) \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}}\left(1+\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}}(2 x)\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=2(\log (\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1})) \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}}\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}=2(\log (x+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1})) \frac{1}{\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2(\log (x+\sqrt{x^{2}+1})}{\sqrt{x^{2}+1}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}=\frac{2\left({\displaystyle\frac{1+{\displaystyle\frac x{\sqrt{1+x^2}}}}{x+\sqrt{1+x^2\;}}}\right)\times\sqrt{1+x^2}-2(\log(x\;+\sqrt{1+\;x^2}))\times{\displaystyle\frac x{\sqrt{1+x^2}}}\;}{\left(\sqrt{1+x^2}\right)^2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\frac{2\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}}\right) \times \sqrt{1+x^{2}}-2(\log (\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}})) \times \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}}}{\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}=\frac{2-2(\log (\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}})) \times \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}}}{\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}{/tex}
We have to find out , 
LHS = {tex}\left(x^{2}+1\right) \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}=\left(\mathrm{x}^{2}+1\right) \times \frac{2-2(\log (\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}})) \times \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}}}{\left(1+\mathrm{x}^{2}\right)}+\mathrm{x} \times \frac{2(\log (\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1})}{\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}}{/tex}
{tex}=2-2(\log (x+\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}})) \times \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{x}^{2}}}+\frac{2 \mathrm{x}(\log (\mathrm{x}+\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1})}{\sqrt{\mathrm{x}^{2}+1}}{/tex}
= 2
= RHS
Hence Proved.

question_html: If {tex}y=\tan ^{-1}\left\{\frac{\sqrt{1+x}-\sqrt{1-x}}{\sqrt{1+x}+\sqrt{1-x}}\right\}{/tex}, find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}.
question_text: If {tex}y=\tan ^{-1}\left\{\frac{\sqrt{1+x}-\sqrt{1-x}}{\sqrt{1+x}+\sqrt{1-x}}\right\}{/tex} , find {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} .
explanation: Here, {tex}\mathrm{y}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+x}-\sqrt{1-x}}{\sqrt{1+x}+\sqrt{1-x}}\right){/tex} Put x = cos 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+\cos 2 \theta}-\sqrt{1-\cos 2 \theta}}{\sqrt{1+\cos 2 \theta}+\sqrt{1-\cos 2 \theta}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{2 \cos ^{2} \theta}-\sqrt{2 \sin ^{2} \theta}}{\sqrt{2 \cos ^{2} \theta}+\sqrt{2 \sin ^{2} \theta}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{2}(\cos \theta-\sin \theta)}{\sqrt{2}(\cos \theta+\sin \theta)}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{\cos \theta-\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}}{\frac{\cos \theta+\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}}\right){/tex} [Dividing numerator and denominator by cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ] {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{\cos \theta}{\cos \theta}-\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}}{\frac{\cos \theta}{\cos \theta}+\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-\tan \theta}{1+\tan \theta}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\tan \frac{\pi}{4}-\tan \theta}{1+\tan \frac{\pi}{4} \times \tan \theta}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left[\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\theta\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}-\theta{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1} x{/tex} (Using x = cos 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} ) Differentiate it with respect to x, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=0-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} The differentiation of the given function y is as above.
solution: Here, {tex}\mathrm{y}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+x}-\sqrt{1-x}}{\sqrt{1+x}+\sqrt{1-x}}\right){/tex} Put x = cos 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+\cos 2 \theta}-\sqrt{1-\cos 2 \theta}}{\sqrt{1+\cos 2 \theta}+\sqrt{1-\cos 2 \theta}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{2 \cos ^{2} \theta}-\sqrt{2 \sin ^{2} \theta}}{\sqrt{2 \cos ^{2} \theta}+\sqrt{2 \sin ^{2} \theta}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{2}(\cos \theta-\sin \theta)}{\sqrt{2}(\cos \theta+\sin \theta)}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{\cos \theta-\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}}{\frac{\cos \theta+\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}}\right){/tex} [Dividing numerator and denominator by cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ] {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{\cos \theta}{\cos \theta}-\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}}{\frac{\cos \theta}{\cos \theta}+\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-\tan \theta}{1+\tan \theta}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\tan \frac{\pi}{4}-\tan \theta}{1+\tan \frac{\pi}{4} \times \tan \theta}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left[\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\theta\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}-\theta{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1} x{/tex} (Using x = cos 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} ) Differentiate it with respect to x, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=0-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} The differentiation of the given function y is as above.
solution_html:
Here, {tex}\mathrm{y}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+x}-\sqrt{1-x}}{\sqrt{1+x}+\sqrt{1-x}}\right){/tex}
Put x = cos 2{tex}\theta{/tex}
{tex}\therefore y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+\cos 2 \theta}-\sqrt{1-\cos 2 \theta}}{\sqrt{1+\cos 2 \theta}+\sqrt{1-\cos 2 \theta}}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{2 \cos ^{2} \theta}-\sqrt{2 \sin ^{2} \theta}}{\sqrt{2 \cos ^{2} \theta}+\sqrt{2 \sin ^{2} \theta}}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{2}(\cos \theta-\sin \theta)}{\sqrt{2}(\cos \theta+\sin \theta)}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{\cos \theta-\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}}{\frac{\cos \theta+\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}}\right){/tex} [Dividing numerator and denominator by cos {tex}\theta{/tex}]
{tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{\cos \theta}{\cos \theta}-\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}}{\frac{\cos \theta}{\cos \theta}+\frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-\tan \theta}{1+\tan \theta}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\tan \frac{\pi}{4}-\tan \theta}{1+\tan \frac{\pi}{4} \times \tan \theta}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left[\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\theta\right)\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}-\theta{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1} x{/tex} (Using x = cos 2{tex}\theta{/tex})
Differentiate it with respect to x,
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=0-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex}
The differentiation of the given function y is as above.

question_html: If x = a[cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + log tan ({tex}\theta{/tex} / 2)] and y = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex}, find {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} at {tex}\theta=(\pi / 4){/tex}.
question_text: If x = a[cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + log tan ( {tex}\theta{/tex} / 2)] and y = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} , find {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} at {tex}\theta=(\pi / 4){/tex} .
explanation: We have x = a{cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + log tan ( {tex}\theta{/tex} / 2)} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}{/tex} = {tex}a\left\{-\sin \theta+\frac{\sec ^{2}(\theta / 2)}{2 \tan (\theta / 2)}\right\}{/tex} = {tex}a\left\{-\sin \theta+\frac{1}{2 \sin (\theta / 2) \cos (\theta / 2)}\right\}{/tex} = {tex}a\left\{-\sin \theta+\frac{1}{\sin \theta}\right\}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{a\left(1-\sin ^{2} \theta\right)}{\sin \theta}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{a \cos ^{2} \theta}{\sin \theta}{/tex} And, y = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}=a \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\left(\frac{d y}{d \theta} \times \frac{d \theta}{d x}\right){/tex} = {tex}\left(a \cos \theta \cdot \frac{\sin \theta}{a \cos ^{2} \theta}\right){/tex} = tan {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{d y}{d x}\right]_{\theta=\pi / 4}{/tex} = tan {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} = 1
solution: We have x = a{cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + log tan ( {tex}\theta{/tex} / 2)} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}{/tex} = {tex}a\left\{-\sin \theta+\frac{\sec ^{2}(\theta / 2)}{2 \tan (\theta / 2)}\right\}{/tex} = {tex}a\left\{-\sin \theta+\frac{1}{2 \sin (\theta / 2) \cos (\theta / 2)}\right\}{/tex} = {tex}a\left\{-\sin \theta+\frac{1}{\sin \theta}\right\}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{a\left(1-\sin ^{2} \theta\right)}{\sin \theta}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{a \cos ^{2} \theta}{\sin \theta}{/tex} And, y = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}=a \cos \theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\left(\frac{d y}{d \theta} \times \frac{d \theta}{d x}\right){/tex} = {tex}\left(a \cos \theta \cdot \frac{\sin \theta}{a \cos ^{2} \theta}\right){/tex} = tan {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{d y}{d x}\right]_{\theta=\pi / 4}{/tex} = tan {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} = 1
solution_html:
We have
x = a{cos {tex}\theta{/tex} + log tan ({tex}\theta{/tex} / 2)}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d x}{d \theta}{/tex} = {tex}a\left\{-\sin \theta+\frac{\sec ^{2}(\theta / 2)}{2 \tan (\theta / 2)}\right\}{/tex} = {tex}a\left\{-\sin \theta+\frac{1}{2 \sin (\theta / 2) \cos (\theta / 2)}\right\}{/tex} 
= {tex}a\left\{-\sin \theta+\frac{1}{\sin \theta}\right\}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{a\left(1-\sin ^{2} \theta\right)}{\sin \theta}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{a \cos ^{2} \theta}{\sin \theta}{/tex} 
And, y = a sin {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d \theta}=a \cos \theta{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\left(\frac{d y}{d \theta} \times \frac{d \theta}{d x}\right){/tex} = {tex}\left(a \cos \theta \cdot \frac{\sin \theta}{a \cos ^{2} \theta}\right){/tex} = tan {tex}\theta{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\left[\frac{d y}{d x}\right]_{\theta=\pi / 4}{/tex} = tan {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} = 1

question_html: If y = (log x)cos x + {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex}, find {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex}.
question_text: If y = (log x) cos x + {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} , find {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} .
explanation: Let (log x) cos x = u and {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} = v. Then, u = (log x) cos x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = cos x {tex}\cdot{/tex} log (log x) ......(i) On differentiating both sides of (i) w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{1}{u} \cdot \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\cos x \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\{\log (\log x)\}{/tex} + {tex}\log (\log x) \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(\cos x){/tex} = {tex}\cos x \frac{1}{\log x} \cdot \frac{1}{x}{/tex} - (sin x) {tex}\cdot{/tex} log (log x) {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}u \cdot\left\{\frac{\cos x}{x \log x}\right.{/tex} - (sin x) {tex}\cdot{/tex} log (log x)} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = (log x)cos x {tex}\cdot{/tex} { {tex}\frac{\cos x}{x \log x}{/tex} - (sin x) {tex}\cdot{/tex} log (log x)} And, v = {tex}\frac{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(x^{2}-1\right) \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(x^{2}+1\right)-\left(x^{2}+1\right) \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(x^{2}-1\right)}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(x^{2}-1\right) \cdot 2 x-\left(x^{2}+1\right) \cdot 2 x}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-4 x}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = (log x)cos x {tex}\cdot{/tex} { {tex}\frac{\cos x}{x \log x}{/tex} - (sin x) {tex}\cdot{/tex} log (log x)} - {tex}\frac{-4 x}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex}
solution: Let (log x) cos x = u and {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} = v. Then, u = (log x) cos x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = cos x {tex}\cdot{/tex} log (log x) ......(i) On differentiating both sides of (i) w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{1}{u} \cdot \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\cos x \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\{\log (\log x)\}{/tex} + {tex}\log (\log x) \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(\cos x){/tex} = {tex}\cos x \frac{1}{\log x} \cdot \frac{1}{x}{/tex} - (sin x) {tex}\cdot{/tex} log (log x) {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}u \cdot\left\{\frac{\cos x}{x \log x}\right.{/tex} - (sin x) {tex}\cdot{/tex} log (log x)} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = (log x)cos x {tex}\cdot{/tex} { {tex}\frac{\cos x}{x \log x}{/tex} - (sin x) {tex}\cdot{/tex} log (log x)} And, v = {tex}\frac{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(x^{2}-1\right) \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(x^{2}+1\right)-\left(x^{2}+1\right) \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(x^{2}-1\right)}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(x^{2}-1\right) \cdot 2 x-\left(x^{2}+1\right) \cdot 2 x}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-4 x}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = u + v {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} = (log x)cos x {tex}\cdot{/tex} { {tex}\frac{\cos x}{x \log x}{/tex} - (sin x) {tex}\cdot{/tex} log (log x)} - {tex}\frac{-4 x}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let (log x)cos x = u and {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-1}{/tex} = v. Then,
u = (log x)cos x 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log u = cos x {tex}\cdot{/tex} log (log x) ......(i)
On differentiating both sides of (i) w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{1}{u} \cdot \frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\cos x \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\{\log (\log x)\}{/tex} + {tex}\log (\log x) \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(\cos x){/tex} 
= {tex}\cos x \frac{1}{\log x} \cdot \frac{1}{x}{/tex} - (sin x) {tex}\cdot{/tex} log (log x)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}u \cdot\left\{\frac{\cos x}{x \log x}\right.{/tex} - (sin x) {tex}\cdot{/tex} log (log x)}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}{/tex} = (log x)cos x {tex}\cdot{/tex}{ {tex}\frac{\cos x}{x \log x}{/tex} - (sin x){tex}\cdot{/tex} log (log x)}
And,  v = {tex}\frac{\left(x^{2}+1\right)}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(x^{2}-1\right) \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(x^{2}+1\right)-\left(x^{2}+1\right) \cdot \frac{d}{d x}\left(x^{2}-1\right)}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{dv}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\left(x^{2}-1\right) \cdot 2 x-\left(x^{2}+1\right) \cdot 2 x}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-4 x}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y = u + v
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d u}{d x}+\frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} 
= (log x)cos x {tex}\cdot{/tex}{ {tex}\frac{\cos x}{x \log x}{/tex} - (sin x){tex}\cdot{/tex} log (log x)} - {tex}\frac{-4 x}{\left(x^{2}-1\right)^{2}}{/tex}

question_html: Determine the value of the constant k so that the function {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl} \frac{\sin 2 x}{5 x}, & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\ k & , \text { if } x=0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0.
question_text: Determine the value of the constant k so that the function {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl} \frac{\sin 2 x}{5 x}, & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\ k & , \text { if } x=0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} is continuous at x = 0.
explanation: Given function is, {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl} \frac{\sin 2 x}{5 x}, & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\ k & , \text { if } x=0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} If f(x) is continuous at x = 0, then {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} f(x)=f(0){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin 2 x}{5 x}=k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 \sin 2 x}{5 \times 2 x}=k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2}{5} \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin 2 x}{2 x}=k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2}{5} \times 1=k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow k=\frac{2}{5}{/tex}
solution: Given function is, {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl} \frac{\sin 2 x}{5 x}, & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\ k & , \text { if } x=0 \end{array}\right.{/tex} If f(x) is continuous at x = 0, then {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} f(x)=f(0){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin 2 x}{5 x}=k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 \sin 2 x}{5 \times 2 x}=k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2}{5} \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin 2 x}{2 x}=k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2}{5} \times 1=k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow k=\frac{2}{5}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given function is, {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl} \frac{\sin 2 x}{5 x}, & \text { if } x \neq 0 \\ k & , \text { if } x=0 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
If f(x) is continuous at x = 0, then
{tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} f(x)=f(0){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin 2 x}{5 x}=k{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 \sin 2 x}{5 \times 2 x}=k{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2}{5} \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin 2 x}{2 x}=k{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{2}{5} \times 1=k{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow k=\frac{2}{5}{/tex}

question_html: Find the value of the constant k so that the given function is continuous at the indicated point: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} \frac{x^{2}-25}{x-5}, & x \neq 5 \\ k, & x=5 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex} at x = 5
question_text: Find the value of the constant k so that the given function is continuous at the indicated point: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} \frac{x^{2}-25}{x-5}, & x \neq 5 \\ k, & x=5 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex} at x = 5
explanation:
Given: {tex}f(x)=\left\{ egin{aligned}  rac{x^{2}-25}{x-5}, & x 
eq 5 \ k, & x=5 \end{aligned}ight.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=\left\{ egin{aligned}  rac{x^{2}-25}{x-5}, & x 
eq 5 \ k, & x=5 \end{aligned}ight.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=\left\{ egin{aligned}  rac{x^{2}-25}{x-5}, & x 
eq 5 \ k, & x=5 \end{aligned}ight.{/tex} If f(x) is continuous at x = 5, then {tex}\lim_\limits{x ightarrow 5} f(x)=f(5){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{x ightarrow 5}(x+5)=k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5 + 5 = k Hence, k = 10
solution:
Given: {tex}f(x)=\left\{ egin{aligned}  rac{x^{2}-25}{x-5}, & x 
eq 5 \ k, & x=5 \end{aligned}ight.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=\left\{ egin{aligned}  rac{x^{2}-25}{x-5}, & x 
eq 5 \ k, & x=5 \end{aligned}ight.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=\left\{ egin{aligned}  rac{x^{2}-25}{x-5}, & x 
eq 5 \ k, & x=5 \end{aligned}ight.{/tex} If f(x) is continuous at x = 5, then {tex}\lim_\limits{x ightarrow 5} f(x)=f(5){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{x ightarrow 5}(x+5)=k{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5 + 5 = k Hence, k = 10
solution_html:
Given: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} \frac{x^{2}-25}{x-5}, & x \neq 5 \\ k, & x=5 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} \frac{x^{2}-25}{x-5}, & x \neq 5 \\ k, & x=5 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} \frac{x^{2}-25}{x-5}, & x \neq 5 \\ k, & x=5 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex}
If f(x) is continuous at x = 5, then
{tex}\lim_\limits{x \rightarrow 5} f(x)=f(5){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 5}(x+5)=k{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  5 + 5 = k
Hence,  k = 10

question_html: Find the value of the constant k so that the given function is continuous at the indicated point:  {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} k x^{2}, & x \geq 1 \\ 4, & x<1 \end{array}\right.{/tex} at x = 1
question_text: Find the value of the constant k so that the given function is continuous at the indicated point: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} k x^{2}, & x \geq 1 \\ 4, & x<1 \end{array}\right.{/tex} at x = 1
explanation: Given function is: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} k x^{2}, & x \geq 1 \\ 4, & x<1 \end{array}\right.{/tex} We have (LHL at x = 1) = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(1-h)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} 4=4{/tex} (RHL at x = 1) = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(1+h)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} k(1+h)^{2}=k{/tex} If f(x) is continuous at x = 1, then {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} f(x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 4
solution: Given function is: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} k x^{2}, & x \geq 1 \\ 4, & x<1 \end{array}\right.{/tex} We have (LHL at x = 1) = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(1-h)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} 4=4{/tex} (RHL at x = 1) = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(1+h)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} k(1+h)^{2}=k{/tex} If f(x) is continuous at x = 1, then {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} f(x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 4
solution_html:
Given function is: {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} k x^{2}, & x \geq 1 \\ 4, & x<1 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
We have
(LHL at x = 1) = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(1-h)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} 4=4{/tex}
(RHL at x = 1) = {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(1+h)=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} k(1+h)^{2}=k{/tex}
If f(x) is continuous at x = 1, then
{tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 1^{+}} f(x){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = 4

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If y = x + tan x, show that {tex}\cos ^{2} x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-2 y+2 x=0{/tex}
question_text: If y = x + tan x, show that {tex}\cos ^{2} x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-2 y+2 x=0{/tex}
explanation: Given, y = x + tan x ...(i) As we have to prove: {tex}\cos ^{2} x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-2 y+2 x=0{/tex} In order to find the double derivative, we will do the differentiation two times. As {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} So lets first find dy/dx and differentiate it again. {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}(x+\tan x)=\frac{d}{d x}(x)+\frac{d}{d x}(\tan x){/tex} {tex}\left[\because \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\tan \mathrm{x})=\sec ^{2} \times \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{n}}\right)=\mathrm{nx}^{\mathrm{n}-1}\right]{/tex} = 1 + sec 2 x {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 1 + sec 2 x Differentiating again with respect to x: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(1+\sec ^{2} \mathrm{x}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(1)+\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\sec ^{2} \mathrm{x}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}{/tex} = 0 + 2 secx secx tanx [differentiated sec 2 x using chain rule, let t = secx and z = t 2 {tex}\therefore \frac{d z}{d x}=\frac{d z}{d t} \times \frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} ] {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 2 sec 2 x tanx ...(ii) As we got an expression for the second order, as we need cos 2 x term with {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} Multiply both sides of equation (i) with cos 2 x: We have, {tex}\cos ^{2} x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 2 cos 2 x sec 2 x tanx [ {tex}\because{/tex} cosx {tex}\times{/tex} secx = 1] {tex}\cos ^{2} x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=2 \tan x{/tex} From equation (i) tanx = y - x {tex}\therefore \cos ^{2} x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=2(y-x){/tex} {tex}\therefore \cos ^{2} x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-2 y+2 x=0{/tex} Hence proved
solution: Given, y = x + tan x ...(i) As we have to prove: {tex}\cos ^{2} x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-2 y+2 x=0{/tex} In order to find the double derivative, we will do the differentiation two times. As {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex} So lets first find dy/dx and differentiate it again. {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}(x+\tan x)=\frac{d}{d x}(x)+\frac{d}{d x}(\tan x){/tex} {tex}\left[\because \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\tan \mathrm{x})=\sec ^{2} \times \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{n}}\right)=\mathrm{nx}^{\mathrm{n}-1}\right]{/tex} = 1 + sec 2 x {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 1 + sec 2 x Differentiating again with respect to x: {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(1+\sec ^{2} \mathrm{x}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(1)+\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\sec ^{2} \mathrm{x}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}{/tex} = 0 + 2 secx secx tanx [differentiated sec 2 x using chain rule, let t = secx and z = t 2 {tex}\therefore \frac{d z}{d x}=\frac{d z}{d t} \times \frac{d t}{d x}{/tex} ] {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 2 sec 2 x tanx ...(ii) As we got an expression for the second order, as we need cos 2 x term with {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} Multiply both sides of equation (i) with cos 2 x: We have, {tex}\cos ^{2} x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 2 cos 2 x sec 2 x tanx [ {tex}\because{/tex} cosx {tex}\times{/tex} secx = 1] {tex}\cos ^{2} x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=2 \tan x{/tex} From equation (i) tanx = y - x {tex}\therefore \cos ^{2} x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=2(y-x){/tex} {tex}\therefore \cos ^{2} x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-2 y+2 x=0{/tex} Hence proved
solution_html:
Given, y = x + tan x ...(i)
As we have to prove: {tex}\cos ^{2} x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-2 y+2 x=0{/tex}
In order to find the double derivative, we will do the differentiation two times.
As {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right){/tex}
So lets first find dy/dx and differentiate it again.
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}(x+\tan x)=\frac{d}{d x}(x)+\frac{d}{d x}(\tan x){/tex} {tex}\left[\because \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(\tan \mathrm{x})=\sec ^{2} \times \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{n}}\right)=\mathrm{nx}^{\mathrm{n}-1}\right]{/tex}
= 1 + sec2x
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 1 + sec2x
Differentiating again with respect to x:
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{dx}}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(1+\sec ^{2} \mathrm{x}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}(1)+\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{dx}}\left(\sec ^{2} \mathrm{x}\right){/tex}
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2} \mathrm{y}}{\mathrm{dx}^{2}}{/tex} = 0 + 2 secx secx tanx
[differentiated sec2x using chain rule, let t = secx and z = t2 {tex}\therefore \frac{d z}{d x}=\frac{d z}{d t} \times \frac{d t}{d x}{/tex}]
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 2 sec2x tanx ...(ii)
As we got an expression for the second order, as we need cos2x term with {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}
Multiply both sides of equation (i) with cos2x:
We have,
{tex}\cos ^{2} x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 2 cos2x sec2x tanx [{tex}\because{/tex} cosx {tex}\times{/tex} secx = 1]
{tex}\cos ^{2} x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=2 \tan x{/tex}
From equation (i)
tanx = y - x   {tex}\therefore \cos ^{2} x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=2(y-x){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \cos ^{2} x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-2 y+2 x=0{/tex}
Hence proved

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If y = sin (log x), prove that {tex}x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=0{/tex}
question_text: If y = sin (log x), prove that {tex}x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=0{/tex}
explanation: Given, y = sin (log x) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\cos (\log x) \times \frac{1}{x}{/tex} [using chain rule] {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d y}{d x}=\cos (\log x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{d y}{d x}=-\sin (\log x) \times \frac{1}{x}{/tex} [using product rule & chain rule] {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}=-y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=0{/tex} Hence proved
solution: Given, y = sin (log x) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\cos (\log x) \times \frac{1}{x}{/tex} [using chain rule] {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d y}{d x}=\cos (\log x){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{d y}{d x}=-\sin (\log x) \times \frac{1}{x}{/tex} [using product rule & chain rule] {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}=-y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=0{/tex} Hence proved
solution_html:
Given, y = sin (log x)
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\cos (\log x) \times \frac{1}{x}{/tex}  [using chain rule]
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d y}{d x}=\cos (\log x){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+\frac{d y}{d x}=-\sin (\log x) \times \frac{1}{x}{/tex} [using product rule & chain rule]
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}=-y{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow x^{2} \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+x \frac{d y}{d x}+y=0{/tex}
Hence proved
