question_html: Differentiate w.r.t. x: {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}+\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}-\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\right)-1<x<1, x \neq 0{/tex}
question_text: Differentiate w.r.t. x: {tex}\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}+\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}-\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}\right)-1<x<1, x \neq 0{/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}+\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}-\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}}\right){/tex} Put {tex}x^{2}=\cos 2 \theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+\cos 2 \theta}+\sqrt{1-\cos 2 \theta}}{\sqrt{1+\cos 2 \theta}-\sqrt{1-\cos 2 \theta}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+2 \cos ^{2} \theta-1}+\sqrt{1-1+2 \sin ^{2} \theta}}{\sqrt{1+2 \cos ^{2} \theta-1}-\sqrt{1-1+2 \sin ^{2} \theta}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{2} \cos \theta+\sqrt{2} \sin \theta}{\sqrt{2} \cos \theta-\sqrt{2} \sin \theta}\right)=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\sqrt{2}(\cos \theta+\sin \theta)}{\sqrt{2}(\cos \theta-\sin \theta)}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\cos \theta+\sin \theta}{\cos \theta-\sin \theta}\right)=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{\cos \theta+\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}}{\frac{\cos \theta-\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1+\tan \theta}{1-\tan \theta}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1} \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\theta\right) ~~~...\left[\because \tan (a+b)=\frac{\tan a+\tan b}{1-\tan a \cdot \tan b}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}+\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1} x^{2}{/tex} ... {tex}\left[\because 2 \theta=\cos ^{-1} x^{2} \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1} x^{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)+\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1} x^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=0+\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^{4}}} \cdot \frac{d}{d x} x^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{-2 x}{\sqrt{1-x^{4}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-x}{\sqrt{1-x^{4}}}{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}+\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}-\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}}\right){/tex} Put {tex}x^{2}=\cos 2 \theta{/tex} {tex}\therefore y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+\cos 2 \theta}+\sqrt{1-\cos 2 \theta}}{\sqrt{1+\cos 2 \theta}-\sqrt{1-\cos 2 \theta}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+2 \cos ^{2} \theta-1}+\sqrt{1-1+2 \sin ^{2} \theta}}{\sqrt{1+2 \cos ^{2} \theta-1}-\sqrt{1-1+2 \sin ^{2} \theta}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{2} \cos \theta+\sqrt{2} \sin \theta}{\sqrt{2} \cos \theta-\sqrt{2} \sin \theta}\right)=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\sqrt{2}(\cos \theta+\sin \theta)}{\sqrt{2}(\cos \theta-\sin \theta)}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\cos \theta+\sin \theta}{\cos \theta-\sin \theta}\right)=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{\cos \theta+\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}}{\frac{\cos \theta-\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1+\tan \theta}{1-\tan \theta}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1} \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\theta\right) ~~~...\left[\because \tan (a+b)=\frac{\tan a+\tan b}{1-\tan a \cdot \tan b}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}+\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1} x^{2}{/tex} ... {tex}\left[\because 2 \theta=\cos ^{-1} x^{2} \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1} x^{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)+\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1} x^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}=0+\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^{4}}} \cdot \frac{d}{d x} x^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{-2 x}{\sqrt{1-x^{4}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{-x}{\sqrt{1-x^{4}}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+x^{2}}+\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{\sqrt{1+x^{2}-\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}}\right){/tex}
Put {tex}x^{2}=\cos 2 \theta{/tex}
{tex}\therefore y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+\cos 2 \theta}+\sqrt{1-\cos 2 \theta}}{\sqrt{1+\cos 2 \theta}-\sqrt{1-\cos 2 \theta}}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+2 \cos ^{2} \theta-1}+\sqrt{1-1+2 \sin ^{2} \theta}}{\sqrt{1+2 \cos ^{2} \theta-1}-\sqrt{1-1+2 \sin ^{2} \theta}}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{2} \cos \theta+\sqrt{2} \sin \theta}{\sqrt{2} \cos \theta-\sqrt{2} \sin \theta}\right)=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{\sqrt{2}(\cos \theta+\sin \theta)}{\sqrt{2}(\cos \theta-\sin \theta)}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\cos \theta+\sin \theta}{\cos \theta-\sin \theta}\right)=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{\cos \theta+\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}}{\frac{\cos \theta-\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1+\tan \theta}{1-\tan \theta}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\tan ^{-1} \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\theta\right) ~~~...\left[\because \tan (a+b)=\frac{\tan a+\tan b}{1-\tan a \cdot \tan b}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\pi}{4}+\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1} x^{2}{/tex}    ...{tex}\left[\because 2 \theta=\cos ^{-1} x^{2} \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1} x^{2}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)+\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1} x^{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}=0+\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^{4}}} \cdot \frac{d}{d x} x^{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{-2 x}{\sqrt{1-x^{4}}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{-x}{\sqrt{1-x^{4}}}{/tex}

question_html: Differentiate {tex}\sqrt{\frac{(x-3)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{3 x^{2}+4 x+5}}{/tex} w.r.t. x.
question_text: Differentiate {tex}\sqrt{\frac{(x-3)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{3 x^{2}+4 x+5}}{/tex} w.r.t. x.
explanation: Let y = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{(x-3)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{\left(3 x^{2}+4 x+5\right)}}{/tex} Taking logarithm on both sides, we have log y = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} [log (x – 3) + log (x 2 + 4) – log (3x 2 + 4x + 5)] Now, differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{(x-3)}+\frac{2 x}{x^{2}+4}-\frac{6 x+4}{3 x^{2}+4 x+5}\right]{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y}{2}\left[\frac{1}{(x-3)}+\frac{2 x}{x^{2}+4}-\frac{6 x+4}{3 x^{2}+4 x+5}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}~\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{(x-3)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{3 x^{2}+4 x+5}}\left[\frac{1}{(x-3)}+\frac{2 x}{x^{2}+4}-\frac{6 x+4}{3 x^{2}+4 x+5}\right]{/tex}
solution: Let y = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{(x-3)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{\left(3 x^{2}+4 x+5\right)}}{/tex} Taking logarithm on both sides, we have log y = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} [log (x – 3) + log (x 2 + 4) – log (3x 2 + 4x + 5)] Now, differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{(x-3)}+\frac{2 x}{x^{2}+4}-\frac{6 x+4}{3 x^{2}+4 x+5}\right]{/tex} or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y}{2}\left[\frac{1}{(x-3)}+\frac{2 x}{x^{2}+4}-\frac{6 x+4}{3 x^{2}+4 x+5}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}~\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{(x-3)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{3 x^{2}+4 x+5}}\left[\frac{1}{(x-3)}+\frac{2 x}{x^{2}+4}-\frac{6 x+4}{3 x^{2}+4 x+5}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Let y = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{(x-3)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{\left(3 x^{2}+4 x+5\right)}}{/tex}
Taking logarithm on both sides, we have
log y = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}[log (x – 3) + log (x2 + 4) – log (3x2 + 4x + 5)]
Now, differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
{tex}\frac{1}{y} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{(x-3)}+\frac{2 x}{x^{2}+4}-\frac{6 x+4}{3 x^{2}+4 x+5}\right]{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{y}{2}\left[\frac{1}{(x-3)}+\frac{2 x}{x^{2}+4}-\frac{6 x+4}{3 x^{2}+4 x+5}\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}~\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{(x-3)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{3 x^{2}+4 x+5}}\left[\frac{1}{(x-3)}+\frac{2 x}{x^{2}+4}-\frac{6 x+4}{3 x^{2}+4 x+5}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Show that the function f(x) = 2x - |x| is continuous at x = 0.
question_text: Show that the function f(x) = 2x - |x| is continuous at x = 0.
explanation: We have, f(x) = 2x - |x| {tex}=\left\{\begin{aligned} 2 x-x, & \text { if } x \geq 0 \\ 2 x-(-x), & \text { if } x<0 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} x, & \text { if } x \geq 0 \\ 3 x, & \text { if } x<0 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex} Now, (L.H.L at x = 0) {tex}=\lim _\limits{x\rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=\lim_\limits{x \rightarrow 0} 3 x=3 \times 0=0{/tex} (R.H.L at x = 0) {tex}=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} x=0{/tex} and, f(0) = 0 {tex}\therefore\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x)=f(0){/tex} Therefore, f(x) is continuous at x = 0
solution: We have, f(x) = 2x - |x| {tex}=\left\{\begin{aligned} 2 x-x, & \text { if } x \geq 0 \\ 2 x-(-x), & \text { if } x<0 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} x, & \text { if } x \geq 0 \\ 3 x, & \text { if } x<0 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex} Now, (L.H.L at x = 0) {tex}=\lim _\limits{x\rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=\lim_\limits{x \rightarrow 0} 3 x=3 \times 0=0{/tex} (R.H.L at x = 0) {tex}=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} x=0{/tex} and, f(0) = 0 {tex}\therefore\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x)=f(0){/tex} Therefore, f(x) is continuous at x = 0
solution_html:
We have,
f(x) = 2x - |x| {tex}=\left\{\begin{aligned} 2 x-x, & \text { if } x \geq 0 \\ 2 x-(-x), & \text { if } x<0 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} x, & \text { if } x \geq 0 \\ 3 x, & \text { if } x<0 \end{aligned}\right.{/tex}
Now,
(L.H.L at x = 0) {tex}=\lim _\limits{x\rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=\lim_\limits{x \rightarrow 0} 3 x=3 \times 0=0{/tex}
(R.H.L at x = 0) {tex}=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} x=0{/tex}
and, f(0) = 0
{tex}\therefore\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x)=f(0){/tex}
Therefore, f(x) is continuous at x = 0

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Discuss the continuity of the function f(x) given by {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} 2 x-1 & , \text { if } x<0 \\ 2 x+1 & , \text { if } x \geq 0 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
question_text: Discuss the continuity of the function f(x) given by {tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll} 2 x-1 & , \text { if } x<0 \\ 2 x+1 & , \text { if } x \geq 0 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
explanation: When x < 0, we have f(x) = 2x - 1 Clearly, f(x) is a polynomial function for x < 0. So, f(x) is continuous for all x < 0. When x > 0, we have f(x) = 2x + 1 Clearly, f(x) is a polynomial function for x > 0. So, it is continuous for all x > 0. Let us now consider the point x = 0. At x = 0, we have {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0}(2 x-1)=-1{/tex} and, {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0}(2 x+1)=1{/tex} {tex}\therefore \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x) \neq \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x){/tex} So, f(x) is not continuous at x = 0. Hence, f(x) is everywhere continuous except at x = 0.
solution: When x < 0, we have f(x) = 2x - 1 Clearly, f(x) is a polynomial function for x < 0. So, f(x) is continuous for all x < 0. When x > 0, we have f(x) = 2x + 1 Clearly, f(x) is a polynomial function for x > 0. So, it is continuous for all x > 0. Let us now consider the point x = 0. At x = 0, we have {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0}(2 x-1)=-1{/tex} and, {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0}(2 x+1)=1{/tex} {tex}\therefore \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x) \neq \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x){/tex} So, f(x) is not continuous at x = 0. Hence, f(x) is everywhere continuous except at x = 0.
solution_html:
When x < 0,
we have f(x) = 2x - 1
Clearly, f(x) is a polynomial function for x < 0.
So, f(x) is continuous for all x < 0.
When x > 0, we have f(x) = 2x + 1
Clearly, f(x) is a polynomial function for x > 0. So, it is continuous for all x > 0.
Let us now consider the point x = 0. At x = 0, we have
{tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0}(2 x-1)=-1{/tex} and, {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x)=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0}(2 x+1)=1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x) \neq \lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x){/tex}
So, f(x) is not continuous at x = 0.
Hence, f(x) is everywhere continuous except at x = 0.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Differentiate the function with respect to x: {tex}\log \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}\right){/tex}
question_text: Differentiate the function with respect to x: {tex}\log \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}\right){/tex}
explanation: Let, {tex}y=\log \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}\right){/tex} . Put, {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}=v, \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}\right)=\tan v=u{/tex} , we get y = log u, u = tan v and {tex}v=\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{d y}{d u}=\frac{1}{u}, \frac{d u}{d v}=\sec ^{2} v{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d u} \times \frac{d u}{d v} \times \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{u} \times \sec ^{2} v \times \frac{1}{2}=\frac{1}{\tan v} \sec ^{2} v \times \frac{1}{2}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} u = tan v] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2 \sin v \cos v}=\frac{1}{\sin 2 v}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}+x\right)}=\frac{1}{\cos x}=\sec x{/tex} {tex}\left[\because v=\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = secx
solution: Let, {tex}y=\log \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}\right){/tex} . Put, {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}=v, \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}\right)=\tan v=u{/tex} , we get y = log u, u = tan v and {tex}v=\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{d y}{d u}=\frac{1}{u}, \frac{d u}{d v}=\sec ^{2} v{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d u} \times \frac{d u}{d v} \times \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{u} \times \sec ^{2} v \times \frac{1}{2}=\frac{1}{\tan v} \sec ^{2} v \times \frac{1}{2}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} u = tan v] {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2 \sin v \cos v}=\frac{1}{\sin 2 v}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}+x\right)}=\frac{1}{\cos x}=\sec x{/tex} {tex}\left[\because v=\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = secx
solution_html:
Let, {tex}y=\log \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}\right){/tex}.
Put,  {tex}\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}=v, \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}\right)=\tan v=u{/tex}, we get
y = log u, u = tan v and {tex}v=\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{d y}{d u}=\frac{1}{u}, \frac{d u}{d v}=\sec ^{2} v{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d u}{d x}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Now, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d y}{d u} \times \frac{d u}{d v} \times \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{u} \times \sec ^{2} v \times \frac{1}{2}=\frac{1}{\tan v} \sec ^{2} v \times \frac{1}{2}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} u = tan v]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2 \sin v \cos v}=\frac{1}{\sin 2 v}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}+x\right)}=\frac{1}{\cos x}=\sec x{/tex} {tex}\left[\because v=\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{x}{2}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = secx

question_html: If y = A cos nx + B sin nx, show that {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+n^{2} y=0{/tex}
question_text: If y = A cos nx + B sin nx, show that {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+n^{2} y=0{/tex}
explanation: We have, y = A cos nx + B sin nx ....equation (i) On differentiating with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = - An sin nx + Bn cos nx ....equation (ii) On differentiating again with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -An 2 cos nx - Bn 2 sin nx = -n 2 (A cos nx + B sin nx) = -n 2 y [from equation(i)] {tex}\therefore\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+n^{2} y=0{/tex}
solution: We have, y = A cos nx + B sin nx ....equation (i) On differentiating with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = - An sin nx + Bn cos nx ....equation (ii) On differentiating again with respect to x, we get {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -An 2 cos nx - Bn 2 sin nx = -n 2 (A cos nx + B sin nx) = -n 2 y [from equation(i)] {tex}\therefore\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+n^{2} y=0{/tex}
solution_html:
We have,
y = A cos nx + B sin nx ....equation (i)
On differentiating with respect to x, we get
{tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = - An sin nx + Bn cos nx   ....equation (ii)
On differentiating again with respect to x, we get
{tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = -An2 cos nx - Bn2 sin nx = -n2 (A cos nx + B sin nx) = -n2y [from equation(i)]
{tex}\therefore\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}+n^{2} y=0{/tex}

question_html: Find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex}, where y = {tex}\log \left(\frac{x^{2}}{e^{2}}\right){/tex}
question_text: Find {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} , where y = {tex}\log \left(\frac{x^{2}}{e^{2}}\right){/tex}
explanation: We have ​​y = {tex}\log \left(\frac{x^{2}}{e^{2}}\right){/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{x^{2} / e^{2}} \times \frac{1}{e^{2}} \times 2 x=\frac{2}{x}{/tex} Again differentiating both sides w.r.t. x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=2\left(\frac{-1}{x^{2}}\right)=\frac{-2}{x^{2}}{/tex}
solution: We have ​​y = {tex}\log \left(\frac{x^{2}}{e^{2}}\right){/tex} Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{x^{2} / e^{2}} \times \frac{1}{e^{2}} \times 2 x=\frac{2}{x}{/tex} Again differentiating both sides w.r.t. x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=2\left(\frac{-1}{x^{2}}\right)=\frac{-2}{x^{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have ​​y = {tex}\log \left(\frac{x^{2}}{e^{2}}\right){/tex}
Differentiating both sides w.r.t. x
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{x^{2} / e^{2}} \times \frac{1}{e^{2}} \times 2 x=\frac{2}{x}{/tex}
Again differentiating both sides w.r.t. x
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}=2\left(\frac{-1}{x^{2}}\right)=\frac{-2}{x^{2}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If y = ae2x + be-x, show that, {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\frac{d y}{d x}-2 y=0{/tex}
question_text: If y = ae 2x + be -x , show that, {tex}\frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\frac{d y}{d x}-2 y=0{/tex}
explanation: Given that, y = ae 2x + be -x differentiating w.r.t. x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 2 ae 2x + be -x (-1) = 2 ae 2x - be -x ....(i) differentiating w.r.t. x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 2 ae 2x (2) - be -x - be -x (-1) = 4 ae 2x + be -x Adding and subtracting be -x on RHS {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 4 ae 2x + 2 be -x - be -x = 2(ae 2x + be -x ) + 2 ae 2x - be -x = 2y + {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} [from (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\frac{d y}{d x}-2 y=0{/tex}
solution: Given that, y = ae 2x + be -x differentiating w.r.t. x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 2 ae 2x + be -x (-1) = 2 ae 2x - be -x ....(i) differentiating w.r.t. x {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 2 ae 2x (2) - be -x - be -x (-1) = 4 ae 2x + be -x Adding and subtracting be -x on RHS {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 4 ae 2x + 2 be -x - be -x = 2(ae 2x + be -x ) + 2 ae 2x - be -x = 2y + {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} [from (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\frac{d y}{d x}-2 y=0{/tex}
solution_html:
Given that,
y = ae2x + be-x
differentiating w.r.t. x
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} = 2 ae2x + be-x (-1) = 2 ae2x - be-x    ....(i)
differentiating w.r.t. x
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 2 ae2x (2) - be-x - be-x (-1) = 4 ae2x + be-x   
Adding and subtracting be-x on RHS
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 4 ae2x + 2 be-x - be-x 
= 2(ae2x + be-x) + 2 ae2x - be-x 
= 2y + {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}  [from (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} y}{d x^{2}}-\frac{d y}{d x}-2 y=0{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If f(x) = {tex}x\left( {\sqrt x - \sqrt {x + 1} } \right),{/tex} then
question_text: If f(x) = {tex}x\left( {\sqrt x - \sqrt {x + 1} } \right),{/tex} then

options.text: f (x) is not differentiable at x = 0
options.html: f (x) is not differentiable at x = 0
options.explanation: If f(x) is differential at a point x = a then f(x) is also continuous at x = and left hand limit at that point is equal to right hand limit at that point. And if left hand limit is not equal to right hand limit then function is discontinuous and hence not differentiable at that point. {tex}f(x)=x(\sqrt{x}-\sqrt{x+1}){/tex} We will find the left and right hand limit at x = 0 to check if function f(x) is continuous at x = 0. (L.H.L at x = 0) {tex}=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x){/tex} {tex}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(-h){/tex} {tex}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0}-h(\sqrt{-h}-\sqrt{-h+1}){/tex} As, we can see that square-root of negative number is not defined. So, limit does not exist. (R.H.L at x = 0) {tex}=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x){/tex} {tex}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(h){/tex} {tex}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} h(\sqrt{h}-\sqrt{h+1}){/tex} = 0 So. here left hand limit at x = 0 is not equal to right hand limit at x = 0. So, the function is discontinuous and also not differentiable at x = 0 Hence, f(x) is not differential at x = 0.

options.text: f (x) is continuous but not differentiable at x = 0
options.html: f (x) is continuous but not differentiable at x = 0

options.text: f (x) is not continuous but differentiable at x = 0
options.html: f (x) is not continuous but differentiable at x = 0

So, limit does not exist. (R.H.L at x = 0) {tex}=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x){/tex} {tex}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} f(h){/tex} {tex}=\lim _\limits{h \rightarrow 0} h(\sqrt{h}-\sqrt{h+1}){/tex} = 0 So. here left hand limit at x = 0 is not equal to right hand limit at x = 0.
